text: The Complete Works of the Imperial Library Category Twelve: Miscellaneous Trifles of the Northern Dream Subcategory One: Miscellaneous Stories【Miscellaneous Affairs】Summary We officials respectfully submit that Trifles of the Northern Dream consists of twenty volumes and was written by Song Dynasty's Sun Guangxian. Sun Guangxian, courtesy name Mengwen, self-proclaimed Baoguangzi. The Ten Kingdoms Spring and Autumn records him as a native of Guiping, but his own inscription refers to him as Fuchun. Upon examining Sun Guangxian's preface, which states that he was born in the region around Mount Emei and Mount Min, it is evident that he should be considered a native of Shu. The reference to Fuchun is actually a reference to his clan name. He served as a judge in Lingzhou during the Tang Dynasty, then served under Gao Jixing in Jingnan as an official. Later, he advised Gao Jichong to surrender three states to the Song Dynasty, which Emperor Taizu praised. He was appointed as the governor of Huangzhou, and his career is clearly recorded in the "Jingnan Biographies" of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period. Previously, it was mistakenly believed that he lived during the Five Dynasties period. He wrote other works such as the "Jingtai Collection," "Juzhai Collection," "Wanbichen Collection," "Gonghu Compilation," "Wancan Book," and "Continued Tongli," but these have been lost since the Song Dynasty. Only this book has survived. The title "Trifles of the Northern Dream" is derived from a passage in the Zuo Zhuan, which mentions a dream about tilling land in the south of the river, while Jingzhou is located north of the Yangtze River, hence the name. It was written during his service to the Gao family. The content of the book includes anecdotes about officials from the Tang and Five Dynasties periods, and each entry often mentions a specific person.

What is said to show evidence is based on the example of the *Du Yang* compilation. Its records are quite disordered, and its sequence is also quite redundant. However, the remnants and trivial words can often be used for verification. Therefore, during the Song Dynasty, Li Fang and others compiled *Tai Ping Guang Ji* and drew much from its text. Chao Gongwu's *Reading Records* mentions Guangxian's continuation of the *Tong Li* in ten volumes, which compiled events from the Tang and Five Dynasties to continue Ma Zong's book, and included references to Huang Chao, Li Maodun, Liu Shouguang, and An Baqin, as well as events from Wu, Tang, Min, Guang, Wu-Yue, and the two Shus. Emperor Taizu, because much of what it recorded was not accurate, ordered the book to be destroyed. But this book has never been criticized, so it is not very false, which can be known. There are two versions in circulation; one is the edition by Ming's Shang Weijun in *Bai Hai*, with many omissions and errors, making it nearly unreadable. Recently, a new edition from Yangzhou is a collection by Yuan's Hua Ting Sun Daoming, still somewhat complete and orderly as the Song Dynasty's Shaanxi edition. Therefore, the Yangzhou edition is recorded, and the Shang edition is not used. March of the 43rd year of Qianlong, respectfully submitted for proofreading.

Chief Compiler [Minister] Ji Yun, [Minister] Lu Xixiong, [Minister] Sun Shiyi Chief Proofreader [Minister] Lu Feichi Preface to *North Dream Trifles* From the time of the chaos during the Guangming era, secret and rare books were lost and scattered. After the reign of Emperor Wuzong, there was a period of silence and obscurity, and the lingering fragrance of the court and countryside could not be spread. I was born in Min and E, and served in Jing and Ying, where I often felt ashamed of my ignorance. In my travels and stays, I dedicated myself to seeking out information. Recently, I met the former Deputy Prefect of Fengxiang, Yang Pin, who often spoke of the old customs and traditions of the Qin region, which I always kept in mind. Later, at the Palace by the Lake, I met Yuan Cheng, the Middle Scholar, and had friendly and intimate conversations with him. Many of his stories matched what I had heard before. Yuan Gong said to a few of his family members: "You all were born in Chang'an, but you did not hear as much as those in Fuchun. This is where the value of asking questions and thus expanding one's knowledge lies." Thereafter, whenever I heard something, I did not dare to trust it alone, but carefully verified and cross-checked it multiple times before writing it down. Not only did I aim to record these events, but also to use them to offer advice and encouragement. Over three decades, I gathered and compiled all the words and deeds of eminent Tang scholars, arranging them in order of their importance. Any events that were similar in nature, from the Tang dynasty to the Later Tang, Later Liang, Shu, and Southern dynasties, that I had heard or known, I added to the end. The total number of compiled events amounted to twenty volumes. The *Yugong* (The Tribute of Yu) describes the geography and administration of ancient China, and there is a dream of hunting in the south of the Yangtze River. I have been serving in the north of the Jing River, and titled this work *North Dream Trifles*. It is trivial and detailed, but the main points can be understood. Although it is not a work of great depth, it is hoped that it will encourage future generations and make them look up to past events. It is also like the coarse grass among silk and hemp. May those who understand not criticize too harshly.

The Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature Trifles of the Northern Dream, Volume One By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty Emperor Xuanzong's Appreciation for Jinshi Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty was fond of learning and culture. Whenever he conversed with the scholars in the palace, he would often discuss the rise and fall of past dynasties. He took a keen interest in the imperial examinations and once inscribed on the palace pillars, "Jinshi Li from the local examination." When a chancellor left office, he would compose poetry to send him off, the verses being both elegant and refined. Whenever he discussed state affairs with the chancellors, he would be engrossed for the entire day. By the time of Emperor Xizong, who enjoyed playing football and cockfighting for amusement, he boasted about his skill in "buddai jinshi" (a type of scholar) and said to the court jester Shi Yezhu, "Even if I were to become a jinshi, I should be eligible for the title of Zhuangyuan (top scholar)." Shi Yezhu replied, "If you were to meet the sage-kings Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang as the head of the Ministry of Rites, Your Majesty would still be destined to fail." The emperor laughed and left it at that. From this, one can discern the difference in their preferences and understand the state of governance. The Bravery of Vice-Chancellor Li Deyu When Li Deyu, the Vice-Chancellor, was young, he was exceptionally intelligent and quick-witted. Emperor Xianzong was so impressed that he seated him on his knees. Li Jifu, Li Deyu's father, often boasted about his son's cleverness to his colleagues. Wu Yuanheng, the chancellor, summoned Li Deyu and asked him, "What books does your son enjoy at home? I wish to understand his aspirations." Li Deyu remained silent. The next day, Wu Yuanheng informed Li Jifu of the incident and joked, "You are indeed quite foolish!" Li Jifu returned home and scolded Li Deyu. Li Deyu replied, "Wu Gong, as the emperor's advisor, should not ask about the books my son enjoys but rather inquire about the state of governance and the balance of yin and yang. Asking about books is the responsibility of the Ministry of Rites, and his question was inappropriate. That is why I did not respond." Li Jifu then reported this to Wu Yuanheng, who was deeply embarrassed. From that day on, Wu Yuanheng's reputation grew.

Viceroy Zheng Guang's Tax Exemption During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, his maternal uncle Zheng Guang was granted two estates in Yunyang and Hu counties, and was exempted from taxes. The chancellors submitted a petition stating that this was not appropriate. The emperor replied, "I wish to show special favor to Guang because he is my maternal uncle, but I did not think carefully at the beginning. This tax exemption is granted. You all must always offer advice with the utmost loyalty and integrity. It is difficult to discuss matters involving relatives. If they do not love me, how could they offer good advice? If everything can be handled like this, what worries would there be in governing the world? There must be a beginning and an end, and we must all adhere to it." Soon after, the chancellors were dismissed. Zheng Guang's son, who was a fellow official, once bought an estate and was pleased that it was tax-free. He then said, "There is no property in the world that is not taxed." His fellow official refused to sell to him with a private note. Later, Zheng Guang's descendants served as county magistrates and set the taxes, leaving no time to seek blessings. Even a maternal uncle like Zheng Guang is so, what about common officials? Reviving Buddhism Again After Emperor Wuzong succeeded to the throne, Emperor Xuanzong, who was his uncle, secretly traveled to foreign lands or stopped at famous mountains in southern Jiangnan, meeting many high-ranking Taoist and Buddhist monks. When he first heard of politics, he said to the chancellors, "Buddhism, though a foreign religion, deeply supports the fundamental principles of governance. It is possible to preserve without discussing. I do not want to excessively criticize and harm good virtue." He then issued an edict to restore ancient temples and abandoned sites, entrusting local officials to select high-ranking monks to reside there. Only those who have taken monastic vows are not to be ordained indiscriminately. After Emperor Yizong ascended the throne, he devoted himself solely to promoting Buddhism. Chancellor Xiao Fang and Pei Tan, who were serving as Attendants and Advisors at the time, submitted a memorial with strong advice, the main points being: "We hear that the original..."

The Way of the Ancestor uses benevolence and frugality as its foremost principles, while the style of the Simple King is characterized by righteousness. Passed down through a hundred generations, it serves as a model for a thousand years, being the most sage and the most wise, and cannot be changed. As for the Buddha, born in Tianzhu (India), leaving the royal palace, he cut off the deepest attachments, achieving the extraordinary after attaining Nirvana, with fame returning beyond the realm and principles emerging from the mundane. This is not something that emperors can aspire to. Extensively citing the meanings of what is futile and harmful, much of which is not recorded, the style is gentle and smooth, similar to Han Yu's petition to remove the Buddha bones during the Yuanhe period. The wise emperor, though he listened and praised, could not stop it. In his later years, when the Buddha bones arrived in the capital, he soon passed away. Those who understand consider it a sign of great mourning. The daughter of the Zheng family built a thatched hut over her father's grave. In the Dazhong period of the Tang dynasty, the governor of Yan state reported that Zheng Shen Zuo, who was sent to guard the camp in Qingzhou, died in battle. His daughter, aged twenty-four, had already been betrothed to Li Yuanqing, a soldier in the Right Xiangxiong unit, but had not yet received the betrothal gifts. After learning that her father Zheng Shen Zuo had died in battle, she divorced Li Yuanqing, shaved her head, went to Huai'an in the north of Qingzhou to retrieve her father's remains, and buried them together with her mother in the Jinxiang area of Xuxiu County, Yan state, then built a hut in the cemetery. Those who understand said that the woman's act of going to the border to retrieve her father's remains and burying them together with her mother is both brave and filial, truly commendable. Building a hut over a grave is a recent custom, but it cannot be prohibited by the state, as it is not wrong. Extensively citing the rites to prove it.

**Go Game with the Japanese Prince** During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty, a Japanese prince visited China and was skilled at playing Go. The emperor ordered the imperial advisor Gu Shiyan to play against him. The prince brought Go pieces made of cold and warm jade, with the green ones resembling the color of pine jade. The cold and warm jade pieces were said to be warm in winter and cool in summer, though some may have exaggerated this. The prince reached the 33rd move, and Gu Shiyan, fearing to dishonor the emperor's command, was sweating and trembling before finally making a move. The prince was also intensely focused and tense, and after several moves, he conceded defeat. He then said to the official in charge of foreign affairs, "This is just a defensive move." The official replied, "If you beat the third player, you can see the second. If you beat the second, you can see the first." The prince wished to see the strongest player. The official said, "If you beat the third, you can see the second. If you beat the second, you can see the first." The prince then said, "A small country like Japan cannot match a great country like China in three aspects, let alone the Chinese scholars." **The Story of Deng** Bao Guangzi said, "Governor An Zhongba of Jianzhou in Shu was greedy for wealth and called Deng, a skilled Go player, to play against him. He made Deng stand by and only allowed him to make a move after he had calculated his own moves, so that throughout the day, only a few moves were played. The next day, he called Deng again. Someone advised Deng, 'This governor likes bribes. He doesn't play Go; why don't you offer him something and ask to leave?' Deng agreed and paid ten ingots of gold to avoid further humiliation, which is truly laughable."

Refutation of Du Yu During the Dazhong era, the Minister of Works Chen Shang established debates on Emperor Wen of Han's abolition of the guard and the *Spring and Autumn Annals and Zuo Commentary* scholarship. Confucius revised the classics, clearly distinguishing between praise and criticism, which is associated with Legalist thought. Zuo Qiuming, as the historian of Lu, recorded contemporary politics, lamenting the loss of loyal and virtuous individuals and fearing the decline of good and evil. He recorded events by days and months, detailing the roles of officials, which is fundamentally different from supporting the words of the sages or embellishing the classics. It is of the historian school. The *Spring and Autumn Annals* are clear and practical, while the *Zuo Zhuan* is complex and lacks evidence. Du Yu never considered that Confucius' work as the classics should be placed alongside the *Shujing*, *Shijing*, and *Yijing*, and that Zuo Qiuming's work as history should be placed alongside those of Sima Qian and Ban Gu. He combined these two distinct ideas in a way that is inconsistent and incomplete, leading to gaps in his explanations and a lack of coherence in his writings, with many passages omitted. Additionally, Lu Guimeng from Wu also cited the works of Dan Zhu and Zhao Kuang as evidence, aligning with Chen Shang's arguments. My colleague Wang Zhenfan, who specializes in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, has refuted many of Du Yu's errors, presenting a well-organized and detailed critique that has surprised many. Only I, a humble person, have privately agreed with the arguments of Chen Shang, Lu Guimeng, Dan Zhu, and Zhao Kuang, not out of blind agreement, but because the matter is right.

Li Deyu Suppressed Bai Juyi Bai Juyi, the Right Minister, was renowned for his literary talent but did not attain a high official position. Before this, during the Taihe era, Liu Yuxi, serving as a guest official, visited Li Deyu, the Right Minister, and asked, "Have you recently received the collection of Bai Juyi's writings?" Li Deyu replied, "I have repeatedly shown it to you and stored it separately, but I have not yet opened it. Today, I will look through it for you." When he took it out, it was full of dust. After opening it and then rolling it up again, he said to Liu Yuxi, "I find this person unworthy of keeping for long. His writing is so exquisite, why bother to read it? I fear it will turn my heart." Thus, he was suppressed in this way. The gentlemen of the literati class all envied him, saying that he had the talent of a scholar but lacked the vision of a prime minister. In his responses to imperial edicts, one could see his ability to manage state affairs, but he was suppressed by the times. This is similar to Jia Yi, who served under Emperor Wen of Han but did not become a high-ranking official, and people felt sorry for him. Bao Guangzi said, "Li Deyu's suppression of Bai Juyi was based on this reason." Initially, Emperor Wenzong ordered Li Deyu to mention Yang Yuqing and Niu Sengru as leaders of factions in the court. Yang and Niu were close friends of Bai Juyi, and his reluctance to support them is due to this. It is not suppressing his writing; he feared that they would form alliances and hinder his actions.

Niu Sengru, a Remarkable Scholar The chancellor Niu Sengru, whose courtesy name was Sianan, was said to be descended from Niu Xianke. He lived in the region between Wan and Ye, where he was born into a poor family but dedicated himself to learning with great diligence and had bold and unconventional aspirations. During the Yongzhen period, he was selected as a Jinshi (imperial scholar). At the time, he and his peers passed through the Hall of State Affairs, where the prime minister said, "Cleanse and prepare for our arrival." Niu Sengru alone stepped forward and said, "I dare not." The others were astonished by his boldness. In the early Yuanhe period, he passed the imperial examinations for specialized positions and held various high-ranking offices, including those in the central government, such as the Office of the Imperial Censor, the Secretariat, and the position of Left Chancellor. He also served as the governor of Yangzhou and Jianzhou, and as the governor of the Eastern Capital (Luoyang). Before his death, he wrote the *Zhouqin Xingji*, a historical or travelogue-style account of the Zhou and Qin dynasties. Li Deyu sharply criticized this work. In the early Dazhong period, Niu Sengru died without receiving a posthumous title. Later, after Bai Minzhong became chancellor, he proposed the posthumous title "Jian" for Niu Sengru. Bai Juyi and Bao Guangzi commented, "Niu Sengru rose to prominence before Li Deyu, and his talents could not be suppressed. Comparing Niu's abilities and achievements with Li's, it is clear to see the merits and faults of both. Moreover, the *Zhouqin Xingji* was not appropriate, and Li Deyu's criticism of it was justified, while others found it shocking. Do not say that Li Deyu, the Duke of Wei, was jealous of talent and envious of goodness. Niu Sengru narrowly escaped a great calamity, which was fortunate indeed."

**Pre-selection of Liu Huchao for the Imperial Examination** At the end of the Dazhong era, the chancellor Lü Tuó resigned from his post, and his son Huchao was preparing to take the Jinshi examination before his father had stepped down. Huchao was prematurely selected for the examination and passed. The Counselor-in-Advisory, Cui Xuan, submitted a memorial stating that Huchao was abusing his father's power and influence, swaying the entire empire. He argued that the examination papers were required to be submitted by October, but how could it be acceptable for the son to privately select his father's name, interfering with the chief examiner, mocking and manipulating the legal procedures, allowing deceit and corruption to prevail? Huchao should isolate himself in seclusion, and the matter should be investigated by the Censorate. However, the memorial was not acted upon. The Preserver of Light commented: During the early Dazhong era, Lü Tuó was involved in the downfall of Li Taiwei, solely because he was aligned with Li Sen, who had killed Wu Xiang. He also altered the history of the Yuanhe period and accused officials of bribing eunuchs. This was far from the deeds of Deyu, who established his reputation through his contributions to the state and his frugal lifestyle. By focusing on minor flaws and ignoring his great virtues, when he held high office, he had no other merits. The advice from the censors is evident. Comparing his actions to those of Zhu Yaya, they are hardly comparable. **Liu Sanfu's Account of Three Lifetimes**

During the Tahe era of the Tang dynasty, Li De Yu was stationed in Zhe West, and there was a man named Liu San Fu who, despite growing up poor and hardworking in his studies, possessed remarkable talent and insight. At the time, a messenger carrying imperial edicts arrived to bestow upon Li De Yu. Li De Yu tested Liu San Fu's abilities and said, "You can draft a memorial for me. Can you complete it immediately?" Liu San Fu replied, "The essence of writing lies in precision, not speed." Li De Yu thought he had a point. Liu San Fu then asked, "The songs of fishermen and woodcutters are all said to be inspired by your writings. I wish to see your collection of writings." Li De Yu handed him several scrolls, and Liu San Fu then composed a memorial based on them. Li De Yu praised him and sent him to the imperial court to take the examination. Indeed, he passed the examination and served in various high-ranking positions. Liu San Fu could recall three lifetimes' worth of experiences. He once was a horse that often longed for the post station and neighed. If its hoof was hurt, it felt pain deeply. Later, when Liu San Fu rode a horse over rough terrain, he would slow down the pace. If there were stones on the road, he would remove them. His family did not install thresholds on their doors, out of concern for hurting the horse's hooves. His son Yi was granted the top rank in the imperial examination and served in the imperial court. He submitted a memorial to clear Li De Yu's name and returned the sacred coffin of Zhu Ya to be buried in Luoyang, to repay the kindness of his ancestors. Scholars and officials praised him. --- A Bald-Headed Rhinoceros Du Bin Gong Cong, the grandson of Duke You, whose father was named Cong Yu, served in various local administrative positions. He married Princess Qiyang of the Xianzong era and held many important positions. He served in the imperial court again but had no other talents. He never received poor and uneducated scholars and only enjoyed good food and held a position without doing much. A scholar wrote a letter to Cong, saying, "You have the virtue of being large and solid, living in a time of civilization. Direct your wise counsel, frequenting high-ranking positions. I strongly criticized him, but the text is too long to record." People called him a bald-headed rhinoceros. Whenever he governed a frontier region, he never punished or imprisoned anyone, not even inquiring about the dead. It is appropriate to blame him. Holding a high position and obstructing the talented, enjoying a generous salary to enrich oneself, having no merit for the country and no virtue for the people, to die in wealth and honor, what kind of person is this? His descendants did not enjoy the same, for what reason?

The Emperor Wenzong of Tang, addressing the chancellors, said: "Emperor Taizong received Wei Zheng, who gathered and corrected omissions to perfect sage governance. Now I have gained Wei Mo, who will offer the strongest possible remonstrance in ambiguous situations. Though I dare not hope to reach the level of the Zhenguan era, I hope to avoid making mistakes. Today, I appoint Wei Mo as the Right Censor and instruct the Secretary to craft appropriate words for him. I then asked Wei Mo, 'What books do you have at home?' He replied, 'I have no books at all, only the tablet of the Right Censor Wei, the Marquis of Wen.' The Emperor ordered it brought in. Zheng Tan, standing nearby, said, 'It is in the person, not in the tablet.' The Emperor said, 'You do not understand at all. The meaning of the banyan tree is not dependent on the tablet.' --- **North Dream Trifles, Volume One** **Imperially Approved Four Treasuries** **North Dream Trifles, Volume Two** **By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty** **Pi Rishou Submits a Written Proposal** During the Xiantong era, the Jinshi Pi Rishou submitted two written proposals. The first proposed making Mencius a subject of study, stating: "I have heard that the principles of sage rulers do not exceed the classics. The principles of the classics do not exceed the histories, and the principles of the histories do not exceed the philosophical works. The one who does not diverge from the path is Mencius. Those who diverge from the path in their philosophical works are certainly to be condemned as enemies of the classics and histories." And so on. The text is too long to include here, but he proposed abolishing the works of Zhuangzi and Liezi in favor of Mencius, and making those who understand its meaning eligible for the same selection as those who pass the classics. The second proposal suggested enshrining Han Yu in the Imperial Academy, stating: "I have heard that the principles of sage rulers do not exceed the pursuit of usefulness. Their usefulness during their lifetime can be known at the time, and their usefulness after their death can be known for all time. And so on. He further said that Mencius and Xunzi helped to spread Confucius's teachings, and that the teachings of Wen Ziqi are vast and far-reaching."

Only Han Yu was entrusted with the task of guarding and transmitting the teachings, trampling the teachings of Yang and Mo and the doctrines of Buddhism and Daoism, thus making Confucian teachings shine brightly like the sun and stars. Since the Tang dynasty, Han Yu alone has achieved this. If Han Yu is not included among the Twenty-One Sages, the ritual ceremonies are not complete. Li Yu was originally named Yi Shao and later named Xi Mei, from Xiangyang and Jingling. He devoted himself to literature, lived in seclusion in Lumen Mountain, and called himself "Jianqi" (a rare talent). During the Dazhong imperial examination, he did not pass, and the Minister of Rites Zheng Yu, mocking his appearance, said, "Your talent and learning are very rich, but why only one day?" Li Yu replied, "Minister, you cannot abandon me in one day; two days mean you should not discard my words because of my appearance." All scholars admired and respected him. He became a Doctor of the Imperial Academy, lived in Suzhou, and was a literary friend of Lu Guimeng. He wrote ten volumes of "Wen Su" and three volumes of "Pi Zi." His son was a chancellor under the King of Wu and Yue. During the reign of Emperor Xuānzōng, the Chancellor Lìng Hú Tóng received the most favor and held the highest power, especially jealous of those who surpassed him. Because his son Hào did not understand and failed the imperial examination, Zhang Yún, Liu Tuì, and Cui Xuān successively submitted memorials to protect him. Emperor Xuānzōng showed leniency towards Tóng and sent him to govern Weiyang. Tóng submitted a memorial to plead for his son's injustice, stating: "Since the previous emperor, I have been in the office of the Secretariat for a long time. Those who receive my favor say I am kind, while those who do not receive my favor say I am weak. I am not made of fine wine and meat, how could I satisfy everyone's mouths? At times, I am criticized for my shortcomings, and some say I once consulted Wēn Qí about historical matters. He replied that the matter originated from the *Nanhua Jing* (Tao Te Ching) and said it was not a rare book. Perhaps the chancellor should take the time to read ancient texts."

The emperor was increasingly angered by this and reported that Qi had talent but lacked proper conduct, making him unsuitable for promotion. He also mentioned that Emperor Xuānzōng's private actions offended Wen Qi, so Qi was appointed as the military officer in Fāngchéng. Therefore, in Qi's poem, he wrote: "I now understand this resentment; people accumulate regret from reading the second chapter of the *Nánhuá*." Li Shangyin, whose father was an old official of the state of Chu, was not shown favor by Táo, and Li Shangyin resented him. Therefore, he inscribed a poem in the guardhouse, the last line of which said: "Young man, despite your high status, you cannot enter the eastern pavilion again." The official position only allowed for a low rank. Luò Yǐn from Jiangdong was also known to Táo, but despite this, he achieved no success. He wrote a poem mourning the chancellor, saying: "Deep favor cannot be repaid; what matter is this humble dwelling?" Because of the resentment of the three talented scholars, it is known that Táo neglected talented people. Lù Shānrén informed Wang Tíngcuō. During the reign of Táng Tián Hóngzhèng, he led the three armies of Zhènzhōu, killed someone, and established Wang Tíngcuō. Wang Tíngcuō was a relative of Wáng Wǔjùn. He was born in a villa, and once several dozen birds gathered in the courtyard trees in the morning and under the eaves in the evening. A villager named Lù Débō found it strange. When he grew up, he had broad shoulders and was skilled in the books of Yin符 and Gu鬼. He served in military positions and gained the trust of the soldiers. Once, he was sent to Héyáng, returned on the way, got drunk, and slept in a corner of the road. Suddenly, a person carrying a basket passed by and looked at him carefully, saying: "He should be a fiefdom holder, not an ordinary person." The servant woke up and told Wang Tíngcuō. Wang Tíngcuō rode several miles to catch up with him, showed respect, and asked. The man introduced himself as Lù Shānrén from Jǐyuán, saying: "I saw that your nose breath was like this."

### Final Translation The dragon on the right is like a tiger, the dragon and tiger qi (vital energy) intersect, and the king will reign in this autumn. The family courtyard should have a big tree, and the tree reaching the hall is a sign of this. That year, indeed, the three armies supported him as the acting governor, and he returned to his villa. The courtyard trees sway and darken the northern shed. In the west of the villa, the god of the Flying Dragon Mountain, Court Gather, went to worship it. As he was about to reach the temple, a hundred steps away, someone wearing a hat and robe bowed to him; when he entered the temple, the god was sitting sideways, facing east, and the structure still stands to this day. Court Gather was clear, thrifty, fair, loyal to the court, and diligent in serving the military and civilians. His descendants succeeded him as the commander of the garrison, and during the time of Zhu Liang, Wang Rong was enfeoffed as the Prince of Zhao, but was killed by his subordinate Zhang Wenli. **Appointing officials leads to trouble.** The Chancellor Ma Zhi of Tang once governed An Nan, pacified the military and civilians, treated the barbarians and savages kindly, abolished the pearl pool, and maintained frugality and simplicity. Li Zhuo later governed this region, using harsh laws, and the military city was far away and belonged to the southern barbarians, leading to labor and military service for six or seven years. In the seventh year of Xiantong, Gao Pian restored it; previously, between Jing and Xu, the conscription and resistance to the barbarians were very difficult. A candidate heard that two thousand soldiers had died in the barbarian territory, and wrote a poem to criticize, saying: The southern regions do not select officials properly, leading to the downfall of our troops. For three or four years, we have suffered humiliation. The weak retreat when they fight, and the brave become more brutal. Military forces fill the land, and generals amass wealth and jewels, carving the wounds of the common people into the salaries of the brave. The mighty army of Xu Chang, led by the loyal and valiant, departs like a thousand horses, stopping like a river of flesh. Sometimes, the foot soldiers return, and the gates and doors of a thousand households cry out, their lamentations echoing through the streets and alleys, filling the valleys with resentment. Who can bear to listen to the drumbeats, unable to look at the golden arrowheads? Thinking of this, tears flow endlessly. The green waters of the River Yi, reciting this poem, reveal the suffering caused by poor appointments, which is the cause of great hardship in the eastern regions. ### Final Translation The dragon on the right is like a tiger, the dragon and tiger qi (vital energy) intersect, and the king will reign in this autumn. The family courtyard should have a big tree, and the tree reaching the hall is a sign of this. That year, indeed, the three armies supported him as the acting governor, and he returned to his villa. The courtyard trees sway and darken the northern shed. In the west of the villa, the god of the Flying Dragon Mountain, Court Gather, went to worship it. As he was about to reach the temple, a hundred steps away, someone wearing a hat and robe bowed to him; when he entered the temple, the god was sitting sideways, facing east, and the structure still stands to this day. Court Gather was clear, thrifty, fair, loyal to the court, and diligent in serving the military and civilians. His descendants succeeded him as the commander of the garrison, and during the time of Zhu Liang, Wang Rong was enfeoffed as the Prince of Zhao, but was killed by his subordinate Zhang Wenli. **Appointing officials leads to trouble.** The Chancellor Ma Zhi of Tang once governed An Nan, pacified the military and civilians, treated the barbarians and savages kindly, abolished the pearl pool, and maintained frugality and simplicity. Li Zhuo later governed this region, using harsh laws, and the military city was far away and belonged to the southern barbarians, leading to labor and military service for six or seven years. In the seventh year of Xiantong, Gao Pian restored it; previously, between Jing and Xu, the conscription and resistance to the barbarians were very difficult. A candidate heard that two thousand soldiers had died in the barbarian territory, and wrote a poem to criticize, saying: The southern regions do not select officials properly, leading to the downfall of our troops. For three or four years, we have suffered humiliation. The weak retreat when they fight, and the brave become more brutal. Military forces fill the land, and generals amass wealth and jewels, carving the wounds of the common people into the salaries of the brave. The mighty army of Xu Chang, led by the loyal and valiant, departs like a thousand horses, stopping like a river of flesh. Sometimes, the foot soldiers return, and the gates and doors of a thousand households cry out, their lamentations echoing through the streets and alleys, filling the valleys with resentment. Who can bear to listen to the drumbeats, unable to look at the golden arrowheads? Thinking of this, tears flow endlessly. The green waters of the River Yi, reciting this poem, reveal the suffering caused by poor appointments, which is the cause of great hardship in the eastern regions.

High Pian Opened the Sea Route [Wang Shenji Also Opened the Sea Route] Initially, there was a sea route between Jiaozhi (in modern-day northern Vietnam) and the South China Sea, but many large ships sank in this area. High Pian, the governor of Annam, went to inspect the situation and discovered hidden rocks in the water. He then submitted a petition to the court requesting the excavation of the sea route to facilitate trade with the South China Sea. His memorial stated: "People are risking their lives to navigate these treacherous waters, and the rocks are a major obstacle. If we could clear these rocks, it would greatly benefit the people of the region." The emperor granted his request, and High Pian hired workers and offered them substantial rewards to clear the rocks. After the rocks were removed, the sea route became safe and navigable, benefiting the people of the region for generations. Some say that High Pian used his skills to summon lightning and thunder to clear the rocks, though the details are unclear. Bao Guangzi, a historian, once heard that Wang Shenji, the ruler of Min, was troubled by rocks along the coast that obstructed maritime traffic. One night, he dreamed of Wu Zixu (also known as Wu An Wang), who promised to help clear the rocks. Wang Shenji then ordered his official Liu Shanfu to perform a ritual to seek divine assistance. As soon as the ritual was completed, a fierce storm arose, and Liu Shanfu watched from a high place as a yellow object, measuring thousands of feet in length, attacked the rocks. After three days of intense activity, the storm subsided, and the rocks were cleared, revealing a clear and navigable channel. The area was then recorded and named "Gantang Port," and it was also called "Tianwei Road," signifying the divine intervention that had made it possible.

During the Xiantong era, the Minister of Rites, Gao Shi, was in charge of the imperial examination and promoted three isolated and poor scholars. Among them, Gong Cheng Yi composed three hundred poems, which many people wrote on the walls of houses. Xu Tang had a poem about Dongting Lake that was especially skillful, and people called him Xu Dongting. The most remarkable among them was Nie Yizhong, from Zhongdu in Henan, who was poor when young and excelled in ancient poetry. He wrote a poem about a nobleman's house that said, "Planting flowers in the west garden, the flowers bloom along the green pavilion road. Removing them to become weeds." He also wrote a poem about a farmer's family that said, "The father tills the fields on the plain, the son clears the mountains below. In June, the grain has not yet ripened, but the government has already built granaries." Another poem said, "Working the fields at noon, sweat drips onto the soil beneath the crops. Who thinks about the effort put into each grain on the plate?" Yet another poem said, "Selling new silk in February and new grain in May, healing a surface wound by cutting out the heart. I wish the king's heart could transform into a bright candle, not illuminating the luxurious banquets but only the houses of the fleeing." These poems are close in words but far in meaning, combining the essence of the Three Hundred Poems. Promoting three people showed Gao Shi's fairness. Bao Guangzi once had a colleague show him a line from a poem collection during the examination period, saying, "Pine trees are guarded by flowers." He criticized this by saying, "Jia Langxian said, 'In an empty courtyard, there are only bamboo; in idle land, pine trees are planted.' Your son and Jia Sheng are like spring orchids and autumn chrysanthemums." One day, attending a peony banquet for a high official, there were two pine trees planted opposite each other in the garden, and they were ordered to be cut down because they guarded the flowers. This official at the banquet had a smug look, and I remained silent and did not dare to answer, which can be understood.

Emperor Wenzong of Tang highly regarded Wang Qi. Wang Qi served three terms as a frontier governor and held various government positions. He was posthumously granted the title of Taifu (Grand Commandant). The emperor highly esteemed him, once writing a poem on the tablet of the Crown Prince to praise him. He had his portrait painted in a private hall, and teachers and friends regarded him as the contemporary Confucius. Although he served in frontier regions, his family had no surplus wealth. The emperor knew he was very poor and ordered that the monthly salary of 500 guan be given to him by the musicians of the Xianshao Academy. Wang Qi was ignorant in managing his household, and all his salary went to his servants and concubines. In his old age, he suffered from cold and hunger, so additional support was provided. At that time, those who understood the situation criticized Wang Qi for not expressing humility and for sharing his salary with musicians, criticizing him for his opportunistic gain, which was a flaw. Bao Guangzi said that scholars' families are ashamed of accumulating wealth. Down to carrying baskets, holding plows, tending gardens, and selling vegetables, there is no salary that can replace farming. How can one live without means? How can one forget the sweetness and bitterness and neglect morning and evening care? In today's society, salaries are small and people cannot be frugal. If they have a little more, they indulge their dogs and pigs. If they are a little short, they suffer their wives and children. And they claim to be content with poverty, but I find no value in that. Only clothing and food are essential for survival. If one does not aspire to be a high-ranking official, one should still think about being frugal and sufficient.

**Translation:** Volume II of *North Dream and Tales* Part of the *Four Treasuries* Volume III of *North Dream and Tales* Compiled by Sun Guangxian of the Tang dynasty Lu Zhao as the Top Scorer in the Imperial Examination During the Tang dynasty, Chancellor Li Deyu, the Duke of Weiwu, suppressed superficial and shallow individuals while rewarding and promoting those who were poor and humble. At the time, powerful officials formed cliques, and the military officials broke their power. As a result, he made enemies and was cut off from the protection of powerful officials, leaving no guests. Only the scholar Lu Zhao, from Yichun, who had extraordinary talent, was allowed to take off his robe and be at ease during each meeting. According to the old custom, the Ministry of Rites first reported to the court before announcing the results of the imperial examination. Fearing that there might be relatives who would recommend candidates in the third year of Fanchang, Chancellor Wang was in charge of the imperial examination and first reported to the military officials. He said, "I do not recommend anyone, but I congratulate you on having a top scorer in this year's results." Wang did not understand the meaning of the phrase and sent a trusted official to inquire at the chancellor's residence. The official reported, "The chancellor has only had long and relaxed interactions with Lu Zhao among the candidates." Wang replied, "Indeed, he is here." That year, Lu Zhao became the top scorer and passed the examination. At the time, people said that although Lu was known by the military officials, it did not affect the fairness of the examiners.

### Translation **Playful Name Change for Bi Xian** During the Tang Dynasty, Bi Xian, a man from Wu Xiang, was renowned for his literary talent and noble bearing, surpassing his peers. He was selected as a Jinshi but did not achieve his aspirations. He once visited a respected official who was knowledgeable and influential, hoping for a name change to ensure success. This official mocked him for being the son of a salt merchant and suggested changing his name to "諴." The official, delighted, accepted and thanked him for the name. Bi Xian eventually passed the imperial examination and reached the position of prime minister. The previous official felt a mix of shame and regret. **Duan Xiang Treads on the Golden Lotus (with Xiahou Xiang appended)** Duan Xiang, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, resided in Jingling. In his youth, he was poor and struggled to find enough food. He often heard the bell of Zengkou Temple during temple services and would visit to eat. The temple monks grew tired of this and began ringing the bell after the service, hoping he would arrive too late to get food. Later, he rose to a high position, serving as the military governor of Jingnan. He wrote a poem titled "Zengkou Temple," which mentioned encountering a monk who rang the bell after the meal, alluding to his past experiences. After achieving wealth and status, he used a golden lotus-shaped basin to wash his feet. Xu Xiangshang wrote a letter advising him, saying, "Why should one block first and then open? What is the point of life if it does not fulfill one's aspirations?" Xiahou Zhi, another high-ranking official, was once a wandering scholar, traveling on a slow donkey that fell into a well. He often visited the homes of officials and stayed in inns, but frequently encountered disputes. People called him "Unlucky Scholar." Later, he rose to the rank of general and prime minister. Some say that Wang Bo, another high-ranking official, was similarly unlucky and wrote a poem about a temple in Yangzhou, which was also attributed to Duan Xiang. Both accounts are preserved.

Chancellor Li Guan was misled by Liu Biao. During the Tang Dynasty, Li Guan, the chancellor, was born in a village near Fengxiang. He was of noble character and had not yet learned the customs of the court when he first took the imperial examinations. He stayed at the residence of his in-laws, the Liu family, in the capital. The various members of the Liu family, who were his in-laws, often teased him because he was unfamiliar with human affairs. They instructed him to learn the proper etiquette of bowing and kneeling, and waited until he bowed deeply. They secretly wrote words on his black headband, saying, "There is a house to rent here." The chancellor did not understand and left. The court officials saw and laughed at him. Xu Mengrong, who held the position of Attendant in Ordinary, looked down on this official title and called it a lowly official. He could not enhance Li Guan's reputation for others. The chancellor began to seek advice on his studies from the various members of the Liu family. The various members of the Liu family guided him to the right places for his studies and first instructed him to pay respects. Xu Mengrong, the chancellor, indeed went to the office of the Marquis of Gaoyang, who apologized with shame, saying, "My official position is extremely idle and cold, not worthy of enhancing a gentleman's reputation." Although it was already in his heart, and he saw the words on his black headband, knowing that he was simple and honest. Soon after, in the following year, Xu Mengrong knew the examination hall, and Li Guan, the chancellor, was the top scorer and passed the examination. Thus, it is known that the Liu family's teasing and mockery led to Li Guan's quick success.

Du Bin Gong, reaching the highest rank among ministers and enjoying unparalleled wealth, once spoke with his colleagues about three things in life that did not go as he expected. The first was his appointment as Prefect of Lizhou, the second was his demotion to the position of Grand Minister of Agriculture, and the third was when he moved from the Western Circuit to Guangling, and his ship was anchored at Qutang Gorge, where he was surrounded by terrifying waves, too busy to call out, and extremely thirsty, he drank watered-down tea himself. When he was stationed in Jingzhou, many of his relatives and sisters-in-law stayed temporarily in the palace in Jingzhou, where they were extremely poor. The Duke never helped them and did not give them any gifts or favors during festivals and holidays. A person riding in a sedan chair arrived at the government office and scolded, but he did not inquire or investigate. Whenever he governed a military district, he did not deal with lawsuits. In Fengxiang and later in the Western Circuit, all prisoners were released or dealt with, whether they were punished lightly or severely, it was left to them to decide. Someone picked up a document wrapped in lacquerware at the Sword Gate, which was a complete record of court cases in Chengdu. He was completely indifferent or unmoved. What kind of heart does he have? [Note: "秃角犀" is a metaphorical term, possibly referring to a rhinoceros with no horns, symbolizing someone who is lacking in certain qualities. The exact meaning is uncertain, but it is retained in the translation with a brief note indicating the uncertainty.]

Li Guangyan, the Grand Tutor, Selected a Suitable Son-in-Law Grand Tutor Li Guangyan, having achieved great feats that contributed to the stability and prosperity of the country, held a high rank and prestige. His daughter was still unmarried, and his advisors believed he would certainly select an excellent son-in-law. In casual conversation, they highly praised a scholar named Zheng, who was of noble family background, known for his elegance and charm, and hoped that the Grand Tutor would marry his daughter to him. On another occasion, they spoke of this again. The Grand Tutor thanked his advisors and said, "Li Guangyan is merely a soldier who has experienced many difficulties and achieved minor accomplishments. How could I unreasonably seek a prestigious family?" He had already selected an excellent son-in-law, whom none of the distinguished scholars had seen. He summoned a low-ranking officer from the guesthouse and pointed to him, saying, "This is the match for my daughter. He will be promoted to a higher military rank and given a generous amount of gold and silk." Xu Dang, an official, commented, "Grand Tutor Li has established a great achievement in stabilizing the country. He harbors concerns about being forgotten or betrayed after his success. Focusing solely on maintaining peace and security within the borders, he does not seek alliances with prestigious families. This is not his intention. How far removed this is from the case of Hou Jing, who sought to marry into the Wang and Xie families." [Note: The Wang and Xie families were close relatives of the Grand Tutor's family, and he often mentioned this.]

### Translation **Wang Wen Gong Sleeping with Crossed Hands [Appendix: Sikong Tu]** Wang Wen Gong Ning, a man of great moral integrity and ascetic practice, stood out among his contemporaries. He would always sleep with his hands crossed, fearing that he might encounter the spirits of his ancestors in his dreams. He ate no more than eighteen pieces of steamed bread, showing his frugality. Once, he served as the governor of Jiaozhou, and at that time, Sikong Tu, a serving scholar-official, was preparing for the imperial examination. Sikong Tu traveled from his villa to the prefecture to pay respects to Wang Wen Gong Ning. Afterward, he did not visit any relatives or acquaintances. The gatekeeper quickly informed Sikong Tu that he had left the city. If Sikong Tu entered the city to visit relatives or acquaintances, he would not enter the prefectural office. Wang Wen Gong Ning learned of this and praised Sikong Tu for his dedication and respect, which he found even more admirable. When it came time for the examination, Sikong Tu ranked fourth among those who passed. His contemporaries were surprised by his obscure name and rapid success, and some even mocked him, calling him "Sikong Kong." Wang Wen Gong Ning, aware of this, called together the top scholars and declared before them, "I have the honor of overseeing the examination this year, and the entire list is dedicated to Sikong Tu, a single individual." Thus, Sikong Tu's reputation grew even stronger. Later, he became a censor and served under the former prime minister Lu Xie, who visited him and left a poem: "The Sikong family is noble, and the office of censor is powerful. Old man, I am still here, but it is not yet time to lament the end of the road. His name and virtue are indeed highly esteemed." **Farewell Feast for Duke Liu Zhan in Hezhong** Duke Liu Zhan, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, had an ancestor named Liu Jing, who was from Lianzhou. In his youth, Liu Jing served as an official under a high-ranking official in Hennan, responsible for drafting documents. He inscribed his name on the stone and water features beside the Shangshan Post Station. The people of Xingyang found this remarkable and encouraged him to continue his studies, leading him to change into a simple robe and present himself as a humble scholar. Later, he was recommended and passed the imperial examination, serving in various high-ranking positions. Duke Liu Zhan, though poor and isolated, was talented. Although he passed the examination, he did not engage in the political intrigues of the time. As a judicial official, he often lacked even basic food. Once, he visited a temple where he met a monk and was invited to a meal. He left several scrolls of his writings with the monk. After retiring, a high-ranking official named Liu Junrong visited the temple and saw these writings, finding them remarkable and moved by Liu Zhan's poverty. He provided him with substantial assistance and support.

### Translation Knowing that the person from Lianzhou (or a mountain) had no powerful supporters in court, he told the monk that although he was idle, he could still make this person a chancellor. Later, when appointed as the Deputy Governor of Hedong, some arrogant and superficial nobles in his entourage despised him. One day, he was summoned to the capital, and the Deputy Governor of Pu held a farewell banquet for him. The superficial and arrogant entourage members called the chancellor 'Governor' and asked, 'What position will you hold upon returning to court?' The chancellor replied, 'As soon as I get a chance, I will become chancellor.' This man laughed at him, and there were also some who found his words unusual. From then on, he successively rose from Water Department Censor to Chief of Drafting Imperial Edicts, then into the Hanlin Academy, and finally received a high-ranking appointment. [As told by Master Wang Wukang.] **The Lucky槐Tree of the Li Family [with an additional story about the枣Tree of Zhao Linggong]** Duke Li Fu of the Tang Dynasty, who lived in Yongle, Hedong, had a courtyard with a槐tree that grew three branches, two of which reached over the house, but one did not. The chancellor had three brothers named Shi and Cheng, who all reached high positions, while Fu only held the position of military governor. Recently, during the Shizhou era, Zhao Linggong Ying had a枣tree in his family courtyard that was unusually graceful and visible from afar. A fortune-teller visited the neighborhood and asked people if this family would produce a chancellor. The elderly man said there was no such thing, but the fortune-teller suggested that Zhao Linggong's son, who was nicknamed 'Chancellor,' might fulfill this prophecy. The fortune-teller said that the auspicious signs were strong and would manifest in his descendants. Later, Zhao Linggong rose from a judge in Taiyuan to become a high-ranking official and a chancellor, proving the fortune-teller's prediction correct. [This version is also correct.] Scholars who achieve success do so through various paths, either through talent or fate. The rise and fall of fortunes also follow people, and the unusual growth of the槐and枣trees must be a sign of auspiciousness. Otherwise, why would they grow so luxuriantly and uniquely? [The story of the Li family was obtained from Grand Tutor Li Zairen, and the story of Zhao Linggong was obtained from the magistrate of Changyang, Kang Zhang, who provided detailed accounts.]

### Final Translation **Gao Taiwei Decides the Fate of Buddhist Monks** During the Tang Dynasty, Gao Pian, the Taiwei and Prince of Bohai, was stationed in Shu. While on a border inspection tour, he arrived at Zizhong Prefecture and stayed at the Prefectural Office. Across the mountain top from the prefectural office stood the Kaiyuan Buddhist Temple. One evening, as the sun set, the monks inside the temple were engaged in their ritual chants. Gao Pian, feeling repulsed by the sight, ordered his soldiers to capture and chain the monks. The next morning, he whipped them on the back and expelled them. He then summoned the generals and officials and told them, "These monks, though they have no fault in their devotion, will cause chaos in ten years. There will be thousands of tonsured men who will rebel, and I am repulsed by this." Indeed, later, the local people, all tonsured and armed, were called "Big Tonsured" and "Small Tonsured," and they used the temple as a base to threaten the prefectural officials, fulfilling Gao Pian's prophecy. [This account was obtained from Wang Tiao, a recluse from Zizhong.] **Wang Zhongling Dodo Resists Huang Chao** During the Tang Dynasty, Wang Zhongling, the Zhongling Military Governor, was highly respected and famous for his virtue. He held a prominent position and was known for his elegant demeanor, but he lacked the talent to quell rebellions. He was stationed at the Palace of Zhubing as the overall commander to defend against Huang Chao's rebel forces, which were gradually advancing southward. Before going to his post, he brought his concubines with him. His wife had not yet left the capital, and he was naturally jealous. Suddenly, he received news that his wife was leaving the capital and was on her way. He said to his attendants, "Huang Chao is gradually advancing southward, and my wife is coming from the north. How can I find peace in such a situation?" One of his attendants jokingly replied, "Why not surrender to Huang Chao?" Wang Zhongling laughed heartily at this. Later, when Jingzhou fell, he took control of Tongguan. Huang Chao sent someone to convey a message to him, saying, "Your Excellency, a scholar, is not my match. Please withdraw yourself."

To avoid defiling the sharp blade, he abandoned his post and followed Emperor Xian's retreat to Shu, where he was once again appointed as Grand Marshal. However, his great achievement was not accomplished, and he ultimately suffered an untimely fate. Contemporary opinion said: "When Huang Chao crossed the Yangtze River, General Gao could not resist or defend. Could Wang Zhongling, being a scholar, cope with the situation?" After being demoted from the position of Grand Marshal, there was a poem, and its main content was: "The imperial decree [or demotion] has been heard coming from the capital, but the military edict has not yet reached all the troops. He also had aspirations within it." [Huang Chao rose in Guangzhou and called himself the Grand Marshal of the Hundred Million Righteous Army, submitting a memorial first stating his intention to attack the capital, promising that if one were to surrender easily, there would certainly be promotion and reward, which was quite laughable for the court.] Lu Shizong's Headwear During the Tang Dynasty, Lu Shizong, known for his handsome appearance, was widely recognized. When he governed Chengdu, he entrusted administration to the official Bì Xián and accompanied himself with musicians and courtesans. He held banquets by the riverside, and the people and women of the city admired him as if they wanted to throw fruits at him, even though Wei Jie and Pan Yue could not compare to him. He was skilled in headwear, and the people of Shu followed his example; later, he cut the ends of his silk cap to distinguish himself from others. In the alleys, anyone who dressed up and combed their hair would be ridiculed, saying, "Are you not Lu Shizong?" Once, when passing a pork shop, he saw a pig dealer tell the butcher [or live pig] that this pig was well-formed and not inferior to Lu Shizong, which was quite laughable. He had ten official courtesans named Xingyun serving him at banquets. When he moved to Zhubiao Palace, he held farewell banquets at Hejiang Pavilion and gave gifts to Xingyun and others, who expressed gratitude with many poems, one of which said, "Where can the wandering soul be severed? At the misty banks of the Yangtze River in Jiangnan," which has since become popular in the entertainment circles. Li Xun, the Minister of Works, was filled with anger [with Zhao Wen's text attached].

During his tenure as governor of Yuzhou, Xue Neng treated those who took the imperial examinations with special respect. Li Xun, a respected official, had an elder brother who served as a military officer at the government office. The younger brother, a talented but humble individual, aspired to improve himself through study and eventually retired to the countryside. Within a year, he wore mourning clothes and presented his work to the general, explaining his actions. The general praised him for his clear and handsome appearance and admired his writings, holding him in high regard. He then gave him a postal patrol certificate, allowing him to be relieved of his duties and live in seclusion, fearing it might hinder his son's advancement. Eventually, the younger brother achieved success in the imperial examinations, held high-ranking positions, and was appointed as the governor of Yuzhou. Zheng Yu, a high-ranking official, was known for his fondness for luxury and wore a half-sleeved brocade garment. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, Zheng Yu, a native of Guangzhou, was a man of great talent and scholarship. He was promoted to the rank of Jinshi and held high-ranking positions, earning a famous and illustrious reputation. However, by nature, he was fond of luxury and wore a half-sleeved garment made of brocade. Cui Wei, a high-ranking official, was stationed in Jingnan, and Xingyang was appointed as the governor of Guangnan. During his visit, Cui Wei treated Xingyang with normal courtesy, as he had never presented his writings to Hui Gong. On this occasion, Xingyang wore mourning clothes and presented his work in the guest quarters. Hui Gong read the beginning of his manuscript and praised him repeatedly without tiring, saying, "Truly, he is worthy of a half-sleeved brocade garment." Since Hui Gong was a former chancellor, he held a military ceremony in the palace and reluctantly granted him the position. Hui Gong said, "The way of both literature and martial arts is fully demonstrated," and expressed his admiration through words and expressions. Some say that Xingyang, while sleeping after drinking, saw a white pig, which is a legend about a snake spitting out a white pig, a metaphor for a good omen.

Wei Zong, the Man of Abundant Grain During the Tang Dynasty, Wei Zong, a high-ranking official, was skilled in managing wealth. He owned a large estate in the east of Jiangling Prefecture, with fertile land and valuable properties. The land was so rich that it was known as the most fertile in the region, and the rice piled up like small hills, all of which were surplus grain. In the early Dazhong era, he was appointed as the Governor of Guangzhou. Emperor Xuanzong warned him about the dangers of greed, mentioning the "greedy spring" (贪泉) in Fanyu. The Chancellor responded calmly, saying, "The estates in Jiangling still have seventy piles of grain, so there is no need for me to be greedy." The emperor replied, "This can be called a man of abundant grain." Li Dang, the Man in the Bamboo Cage [with Cui Jue's Two Sons Attached] During the Tang Dynasty, Li Dang, a high-ranking official, governed the region of Nanliang. There were many officials' estates within his jurisdiction, and their descendants lived as guests there. Some of these descendants were immoral and imitated each other in wrongdoing. Previous officials could not stop them because they had various excuses. Li Dang, known for his strictness and decisiveness, issued a lenient order to weave bamboo cages and summoned the worst offenders. He questioned their family backgrounds and connections in court, saying, "Your family lineage is so prestigious, yet your behavior is so disgraceful. Are you not bringing shame to your ancestors? Today, what I am punishing will surely be praised by your noble relatives. [Or, 'will surely be approved by your noble relatives.'] Be encouraged. I immediately ordered them to be placed in the bamboo cages and thrown into the Han River. From then on, the others were on guard and each tried to control themselves. Cui Jue, a high-ranking official, had two sons who were very wicked. The military officer Liu, holding the rank of duiwei, judged them, saying, "Cui's two sons are the three great evils of Jingnan and cannot escape punishment."

Wu Xinglu, Warming the Urinal [Lì Tú Nán Attached] During the Tang Dynasty, Wu Xinglu, a Shangshu (Minister) from Pengzhou, was a young man who served the eunuch Xi Men with caution and respect. Every night, he would warm the urinal and serve it to him, deeply pleasing the eunuch's intention. One day, when the eunuch was washing his feet, he showed Wu Xinglu the pattern on his feet and said, "With such a pattern, how could one not become a Ten Army Commander?" Wu Xinglu bowed and said, "This is not proof enough; I also have it." He performed the duties of a servant, removing his shoes and presenting them. The eunuch sighed and said, "You are just loyal and filial; I will eventually help you achieve this." Later, he was given a military position, appointed as the Governor of Pengzhou, and served as the Xunjun Sima (Military Governor) of Western Shu under Lu Dān, the Xianggong (Governor). He was successful in subduing the barbarians and served in the Western Shu, Eastern Shu, and three regions in the south. He was promoted to the position of Shangshu, and the imperial edict said, "He is the hero of the age, a harbinger of good fortune for the people." This was a criticism of him. Lì Tú Nán, as the Deputy Governor of Western Shu, was dismissed from office along with the governor. Wu Xinglu wanted to recruit him, but Lì Tú Nán had always despised Wu Xinglu. When he heard about this, he laughed and said, "I cannot shave my head and tattoo my face to serve as a bodyguard." He rode out of the government office directly to the northern suburb without returning to his private residence. His family hurriedly dressed him and chased after him. Zhang Yun, the resident magistrate of Chengdu, often spoke carelessly and was poisoned and killed by Wu Xinglu.

### Final Translation **Feng Shizhong Examines the Prison Records** During the Tang Dynasty, Feng Shizhong, the Chancellor, revered Buddhism and rarely ate meat and blood. He personally handled the records of executions, never bending the law even for monks who committed crimes. Before the execution, he would consider the prisoners with a warm face and compassionate heart, fully understanding their situations. For those sentenced to death, he would first show them the judgment, give them wine and food, and then hand them over to the law. He governed Western Sichuan for three years, eating mostly vegetables. He feasted the officials with dumplings and other dishes made from taro, dyed to make colors, and used to imitate pork shoulder, lamb, and other delicacies, all very realistic. People compared him to Emperor Wu of Liang, but he frequently had puppet shows in his residence, allowing soldiers and civilians to watch without any restrictions. However, his involvement in palace politics tarnished his noble virtues, alas. **Liu Tui, the Secretary, Did Not Worship His Ancestors** Liu Tui, a Secretary from Tonglu, early in life entered the imperial examination through literature. His father advised him, saying, "Let you pursue your advancement, whether poor or rich. I do not expect anything from you. After I die, do not worship me." Then he took a small boat to fish and enjoy himself, finally unknown where he went. Purple Micro, as a high-ranking official, served in Shangyu, and his grief over the loss of parents was profound. On his deathbed, he also warned his son as his father had. Shu's Minister of Rites, Zuan, was his son. Once he spoke with his colleagues about it. Gentlemen say, "Families bound by the norms of propriety value funerals and ancestral worship." What kind of person was Liu's father? If he were a recluse, what harm would it do to the simple rites of filial piety? Purple Micro, as a Confucian scholar, advanced to a high rank, following his father's advice, but disrupted the old customs of the ancients. Could the respect due to ancestors be easily abandoned? Perhaps there is a reason, but people did not understand.

During the fourth year of the Dazhong era, Feng Fang, a successful candidate in the imperial examinations, was the most renowned among his peers. That year, the Kingdom of Siam built a tower and presented valuable silk and cloth, requesting a record to be written, which brought him great honor. Initially, he served as a military officer in the capital region, where his benefactor was Du Shentuan, a prominent official. Du had been appointed to govern Jiangxi, and before the imperial decree was issued, he summoned Longle Gong privately to discuss the matter. Du promised him an appointment to serve in Nanchang and instructed him not to reveal the news. Longle Gong bowed and thanked Du before leaving hastily. On his way home, he met his friend Zheng Cong on a main road, who noticed his joyful expression and stopped to inquire. Longle Gong immediately revealed the promise of appointment from Du. Zheng Cong then went to the capital region to congratulate Du, stating that he had learned the news from Feng Fang. Du was deeply disappointed and disapproved of Feng Fang's lack of discretion. When the imperial decree was issued, Feng Fang was not included in the list of appointees, leaving him in a state of anxiety and doubt, unsure of what to do. On the day the decree was announced, Longle Gong traveled from Fang Bridge in a sedan chair, accompanied by his students. Longle Gong bowed to Du and said, "Be diligent." From then on, Feng Fang's reputation for being arrogant and superficial spread among the literati, and he never achieved prominence. Later, he became involved in dealings with influential eunuchs, which attracted more criticism. He held positions such as Minister of Rites, Governor of Meizhou, and eventually became a Grand Censor in Shu.

The Three Transformations of Unworthy Sons In the Xiantong era of the Tang dynasty, in Jingzhou, there was a scholar named Tang Wujing, who was highly learned and well-versed in the Five Classics. He was truly a great scholar, and his interests were very high, earning him respect from others. He gathered five hundred disciples, supporting himself through teaching, and lived a leisurely life, enjoying the cultural atmosphere of Xihé and Jǐnán. Many of his associates were close friends, and he often said to others: "Unworthy sons undergo three transformations: the first is into locusts, meaning they sell their estates to eat; the second is into bookworms, meaning they sell their books to eat; the third is into tigers, meaning they sell their slaves and maidservants to eat. In every generation, there are those who fall into these three categories." Xue Baosun's Superficiality Xue Baosun was a son of a distinguished family, relying on his talent and family background. All evaluations of scholars were based on his opinions, and he was known for being superficial and shallow. Chancellor Xiahou Zi, particularly disliked him, and his younger brother was named Baohou to distinguish him. Because of this, they were not on good terms. His wife, Lu氏, shared similar character and values with him. When her uncle Xuejian came to visit, Lu氏 took precautions and waited until he left before ordering water to clean the threshold. Xuejian found out about this and became very angry. The chancellor criticized him, and as a result, Baosun was demoted to the position of司马 in Lǐzhou, where he remained for seven years without being replaced. When Xiahou Zi was appointed to govern Wei, the chancellor Mò was promoted, but Baosun died in the prefecture. I have seen some of Xue's writings.

One of the pieces says: "To see off friends at Bashi, I stayed at an inn and saw several objects resembling people. When I questioned them, their mouths moved, and they all said they were officials from the states and counties of Jianghuai and Lingnan. Alas, Heaven has created people to be beaten by these kinds. I also saw actors wearing red and black costumes performing a dance, and everyone laughed except for Baosun, who did not take it lightly; all these things are like this." Lu Fang, after finishing his term in Fangzhou, went directly to Lizhou to visit his sister-in-law and returned to a post office, looking back and laughing, saying, "Who would have thought that Xue Baosun would suddenly meet Military Judge Li and play with willow branches?" (As told by the old military officer Zhou Ai from Lizhou who once served there.) Chen Fang's Essay on the Mantis No scholar in Shu does not sell wine, admiring the example of Xiangru who sold pots. Chen Fang, the imperial physician, whose family sold wine, was beaten by officials who did not sweep the street. His mother was very virtuous and encouraged him to study, but he did not allow her to stop him from returning home to achieve fame. Every year, food, paper, pens, clothes, horses, and servants were sent from Chengdu. Chen Fang's eight-verse essay on the mantis became widely known, and he passed the imperial examination in the first year of Taihe. Prime Minister Li, upon seeing the announcement, ordered the removal of the wine sign and closed the door, but his family still refused, and eventually congratulated him on passing the examination, attributing it to his mother's encouragement. Later, he became the son-in-law of Bai Zhongling, served as a deputy governor of Xishu, and successively governed Peng and Han prefectures until his death.

**Liu Chonggui's Litchi Painting** During the Tang Dynasty, Liu Chonggui, a high-ranking official, lived a life of simplicity and frugality, which greatly attracted public opinion. He once invited fellow officials to a meal of bitter kale [or horse] and steamed flatbread. Some court officials knew this was a show of humility and secretly asked a young servant, "What did the chancellor eat for breakfast?" The servant replied, "He ate it all up." The court officials laughed at this. Later, when Liu Chonggui was stationed in Fanyang, he emulated Wu Yin's integrity. His relatives and acquaintances in the capital who were poor awaited his help, but he only drew a painting of litchis and composed a poem to give as a gift. After his death in the southern regions, his family escorted his coffin through Zhuzhou Palace. His family members sold sea pearls and jade in the market, which was criticized by the public. **Zhao Chongning, Called the Uninscribed Stele (with Zhang Fang attached)** During the Tang Dynasty, Zhao Chongning, a high-ranking official, was clear and upright, and his household had no unworthy visitors. He admired the style of Wang Meng and Liu Zhenzhang. He was tall and dignified and did not write articles, so he was called the Uninscribed Stele. Each time he was promoted, the usual practice was to nominate someone to replace him, but he never nominated anyone, saying that there was no one in the court who could replace him. The world also criticized him for this. Liang Xiang Zhang Fang once became a monk and then returned to the secular world to take the imperial examinations. Zhao Chongning looked down on him. Someone asked, "Wasn't Liu Ke and Cai Jing monks?" Zhao Chongning replied, "Even if Liu Ke and Cai Jing became monks, they were not well-known. Suddenly presenting themselves in the imperial examinations, how could that be acceptable?" Zhang Fang was a member of the aristocracy who, for no reason, left the secular world and did not engage in meditation or seeking enlightenment, but instead presented himself in the imperial examinations in the hope of gaining favor. Such behavior could not be tolerated by the public. Zhang Fang, a member of the aristocracy, relied on the Eastern Prince to seek favor, because he was rejected by Tian Shui, and eventually became a high-ranking official in the Liang Dynasty.

Scattered Notes of the Northern Dream, Volume Three Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries Scattered Notes of the Northern Dream, Volume Four By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty Governor Zhao Kangning's Red Brush During the Tang dynasty, Governor Zhao Kangning of Xiang Prefecture, a man of great merit and title, was renowned for his handsome appearance and his meticulous grooming. He would hang a mirror to adjust his hat and comb, often using a family taboo to punish someone. When Prime Minister Cui Gong Rong was transferred to govern Hunan, he met Zhao Kangning at Xianshou and hosted a banquet. The prime minister casually and subtly intervened, saying, "I have heard that Lord Gong punishes people with words, which is very meaningless. Upon hearing this, I felt uneasy and only showed frowns. How can one beat or scold others?" Soon after, a courtier used a red brush to sweep over the black hat. The prime minister then said, "This is especially unacceptable," and the attendants only bowed their heads. Later, in the land of Tianshui, after the loss of Han South, Zhao Kangning fled to Wu, passing through Xiakou where Du Hong met him at the outskirts and yielded the main seat. Zhao Kangning quickly occupied the position, not knowing how to act appropriately, as shown in this example. He ultimately suffered misfortune in Huai Prefecture, and it is appropriate.

The Son of the Xue Family Prepared Military Attire The Tang dynasty official Xue, known for his literary talent, was frequently posted to military garrisons and often sighed with regret. He once wrote a poem thanking Huainan for sending him Tianzhu tea, with the lines: "A lower-ranking official is given something; truly discarded, relying on my poetic reputation to receive it." He meant that as a military commander, he was considered a low-ranking official. When he was stationed in Xuchang, all the officials gathered, and he ordered his son to prepare military attire to protect the guests. The guests were surprised and alarmed, and the Eighth Seat (a high-ranking official) said, "Make him avoid disaster." At the time, people thought he was superficial, but it was because he did not hold a proper official position and used this as a way to express his intentions, not because he was superficial. The Right Minister Sun Wò Was Open and Simple The Right Minister Sun Wò was open and simple, not engaging in artificial or exaggerated behavior. He once said to his friends and relatives, "When one holds oneself, one must strive for moderation. Though one's conduct may not be perfect, it does not need to be overly strict. Use one's strengths to highlight others' shortcomings, and one's clarity to highlight others' obscurity. It is best not to do this." After being exiled to Hengshan, he had a calm and open heart and did not feel sorrow because of his exile. Whenever he met guests, the servants and laborers would quarrel and fight in front of him, but he remained calm as if seeing nothing. He said to the guests, "If one meets such people with anger, one's own mind will be disturbed." His character and demeanor were all like this. Once, he traveled to Shu and visited Master Du Guangting to receive a talisman. He said, "I once met a sage who spoke of current affairs and often made plans for a high retreat. Afterward, even though I reached a high position, I was eventually exiled to Nanyue. I wrote a poem to Master Du, with the key lines: 'It is indeed difficult to meet in Shu; the heart in Chu is even more sorrowful. My journey is like that of Fan Li, and my teacher is like Xu Fu. Someday, our meeting place will be in the Ten Isles.'" In the late Tang dynasty, many officials suffered the disaster at Gushui and Baima Pavilion, but only Right Minister Sun Wò was spared.
