**Liu Pin, the Tang Dynasty Official, Honors Mou Xun** During the Tang Dynasty, Liu Pin, an official known for his upright, clear, and virtuous character, was feared both inside and outside the court. He was demoted to serve as the governor of Luzhou. Before taking up his new post, he visited the Eastern Shu court with his ceremonial weapons. The chief general, Gu Xiang Yanlang, firmly refused to receive him. A high-ranking official, referred to as "subordinate," said, "The regions of Ba and Shu are troubled, and local strongmen are rising up. This is the son of a military officer, but he alone is able to admire virtue. If not encouraged to advance, he will then withdraw his ambition. By my praise, people will certainly honor him. This could benefit the region by reducing the number of bandits, which would be good." The younger generation secretly laughed but respected him.

In the late Tang Dynasty, the imperial court failed to capture Taiyuan, so Chancellor Zhang Jun was appointed as the supreme commander, with Han Jian as his deputy. Sun Kui, the Shangshu of Zelü, led the campaign with troops from his region. Yang Fugong, the military commander, did not agree with Zhang Jun and delayed his troops, causing them Cui Yun's Armpit Mark During the Tang Dynasty, when Cui Yun, the prime minister, was conducting an inspection in Zhe West, there was a monk from Waguan Temple who held the *Lotus Sutra* and had disciples. A fortune-teller told Cui Yun that his facial expression indicated he would have a noble child. When asked where the child would be, neither his wife nor his concubines could see any sign. Cui Yun pondered slowly and then summoned a former attendant who had once served as a court entertainer and showed her to the fortune-teller, saying, "Indeed, it is here." On the day of the child's birth, there was a mark on the child's armpit, clearly resembling the name of the monk from Waguan Temple. Cui Yun then named the child "Zi Lang." At seven years old, the child still did not eat meat. One day, a monk came to see him, and Cui Yun slapped the child's cheek and said, "Since you love the position of official, why don't you eat meat?" From then on, the child began to eat meat. This child was none other than Cui Yun. Another account suggests that the monk was from Mount Zhongnan, and both accounts exist.

### The Preferences of Respected Individuals During the Tang Dynasty, Li Jing, the Grand Councillor of Zhu崖, was known for his close relationships with his colleagues. When someone inquired about his preferences, he replied, "I enjoy hearing something new," and mentioned a new book by Cui Wei, a high-ranking official, which he particularly liked for its fresh content. He also enjoyed eating new cakes, considering them delicious and precious. Before a banquet, he would ensure that new cakes were prepared by arriving at the office the day before to request them. Du You, another high-ranking official, preferred to have steamed rice and dried meat for breakfast. Cui Anqian, a high-ranking official, enjoyed watching bullfights. Despite their individual preferences, none of these officials were driven by immediate gain, unlike those who were obsessed with gambling tokens, betting on horses, or accumulating wealth. Bi Xian, a high-ranking official, came from a humble background. His family in the region near the Wei River was originally poor, and he himself had been a low-ranking official in Wuxi County, often referred to as a "伍伯" (a guard or low-ranking official). The Grand Councillor was ashamed of this and wanted him to quit his menial duties. He repeatedly sent messages to improve his position but received no response. Finally, he appointed Yang Zai, a local official, to govern the county. Bi Xian personally invited Yang Zai to his private residence, sat down with him, and discussed the matter, hoping to elevate his nephew's status. He promised to ensure that Yang Zai would receive favorable treatment and generous conditions if he could secure a government position for his nephew. Upon arriving in the county, Yang Zai informed the low-ranking official of Bi Xian's intentions. The official, however, was surprised and asked, "How could I, a lowly person, have a nephew who is a high-ranking official?" Yang Zai encouraged him, saying, "Each year, you receive sixty units of currency and customary fees. If you perform your duties well, you will receive favorable treatment for life. What specific position does the Grand Councillor wish to secure for you?" --- ### Footnotes: 1. **唐朱崖李太尉** - Li Jing, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty. [4] 5. **崔侍郎安潜** - Cui Anqian, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty. [5] 6. **毕相諴** - Bi Xian, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty. [6] 7. **杨会** - Yang Hui, a local official. Yang Lingsheng reported all this to the Chancellor, who admired and praised him, and found his words to be true, ultimately not changing his mind. Recently, Governor Fu Su of Shu and his younger brother, Ning Ji, who had once served as the magistrate of Tangxing County in Shuzhou, deeply missed a local official named Yang Fang, who was a close relative of the Yu family. When they repeatedly held power in Shu, they appointed Yang Fang to a high-ranking position as a reward. Yang Fang said, "My service as an official is known far and wide; I humbly accept this post, but would not hide the truth from the people. How could I take on the responsibility of thousands of households to gain a mere title?" Even if forced to take a military position, he was appointed as a temporary official but finally did not leave his post in the county, completing his duties as a relative. Yang Wei, the governor of Yangzhou, served three terms in the region. Daoist Chen Xiufu, who often stayed at the Purple Extreme Palace, greatly desired to meet him, but Yu Hongnong always left for other places. Yu Hongnong then told the道士, "If he comes again, please report it to me." One day, Chen Xiufu arrived twice in quick succession, and soon the state official arrived with his banner. Yu Hongnong finally greeted him, saying, "I have long admired you from afar. I am happy to serve you. May I ask about your future career?" Yu Hongnong called him "卿" and said to the prefect, "You have served three terms as a prefect; what more can you say?" The prefect was displeased, thinking that he had previously governed two counties and now served three terms in the same region. From then on, he considered his current position as his final resting place. After completing his term without incident, he did not understand his words. Shortly after, he was appointed to this county again, serving his term until he died. Thus, the saying that he served three terms as a prefect was indeed true. Yang Gong's younger brother, Pin, told me about this.

A False Claim to Imperial Authority During the Tang Dynasty, the powerful eunuch Tian Lingzi, who held the title of Army Commander, wielded immense influence and power. He once wrote to Xu Chang, requesting that his brother Chen Jingxuan be appointed to the position of Commander of Cavalry. The high-ranking official Cui Shizhong, serving as Secretary of State, refused the request. Later, Cui was transferred to govern the western region (Sichuan). Chen Jingxuan, along with generals Shili, Niuan, and Luo Yuan, competed for control of the three major rivers (symbolizing power and influence). Chen emerged victorious and was appointed as the Governor of the Western Region, succeeding Cui. This news shocked the court and the people. A report was circulated claiming that Chen had been appointed by the Chancellor, but no one knew who had issued the order. In Qingcheng County, a group of followers of the Maitreya sect (a Buddhist millenarian movement) saw an opportunity in the chaos and began to spread false claims, pretending to be the Chancellor's emissaries. They claimed that a rebellion had broken out in the east, and the emperor was planning to flee to Sichuan. They urged Chen to ride swiftly to take up his new post and even set up a false leader to assist him. The military authorities were confused and sent officials to greet Chen at a nearby inn. These officials were instructed to provide four white horses, which they suspected were unusual. They detained the officials and kept them under surveillance. Soon after, the real Chen, accompanied by his entourage, arrived at the inn. The false leaders were then captured and bound, awaiting further orders. They were secretly executed in Yingchuan. Knowledgeable people remarked that Chen's claim to the title of "Imperial Teacher" (or "Chancellor") was a fabrication, achieved through the influence of eunuchs, and had no positive impact. Those who sought power through such means were ultimately doomed to self-destruction, a fate that was not entirely unexpected.

### Translation Crying for Ma and the Matter of Liu Shèrén During the Tang Dynasty, Li Shi, the chancellor, was a man of extraordinary talent and profound learning, surpassing all his peers. However, he was slandered by political factions and eventually promoted to a high position, which seemed to be achieved through improper means. Although his promotion was ostensibly based on his talent and merit, it was widely seen as a result of political maneuvering. On the day the edict was issued, Liu Shèrén, a scribe, cried upon hearing the news. Chancellor Li Shi criticized Liu Shèrén's ancestors for their corruption and submitted a detailed memorial to the emperor, accusing them of accepting bribes and drinking poison. He wrote a poem titled "Parrot Cup," filled with harsh and critical words that deeply offended the court. Knowledgeable officials read his memorial and said, "Why say so much? If only you had not mentioned the disrespectful actions in front of the emperor, there would be no need to punish the son of the Pei family." At the time, this view was generally accepted. The Promotion of the Gu Brothers by Cai Jing During the Tang Dynasty, Cai Jing, a high-ranking official, served as the military governor of Tian De. He had a keen eye for talent and often recognized the potential of those around him. One day, he invited his son, Shu Xiang, and other officials to a banquet at a mountain pavilion, where he called upon two young men named Gu Yanlang and Gu Yanhui, who were known for their integrity. He instructed them not to stand up and asked them to remain seated. The two young men were confused and did not understand his intentions. Cai Jing encouraged them, saying, "Both of you have the appearance of future marquises. Take good care of yourselves, and in the future, I hope to rely on your descendants." He then promoted their ranks. When the Yellow Turban rebels invaded the capital, Gu Yanlang led his troops and restored order, earning the title of governor of Dongchuan and being appointed as an imperial envoy. Shu Xiang and his brothers went to rely on him and requested that Shu Xiang be appointed as the deputy military governor. They treated him with great respect, as if he were a father figure, and all major military and administrative matters were discussed with him. After Gu Yanlang's death, his brother Gu Yanhui succeeded him and also reached the rank of imperial envoy.

Lu Yi, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, passed the imperial examination in the sixth month. During the reign of Emperor Xizong, he followed the emperor to Liangyang and stayed at his temporary residence during the time of fleeing and laboring. At that time, it was the sixth month, and after passing the examination, he became a high-ranking official. He later served in the Hanlin Academy, where he suffered from the heat and humidity during the summer months. His colleagues mocked him, saying, "It's a good day to make the imperial list," implying that his success was untimely. However, the official was renowned for his literary talent and noble character, and he was one of the three prominent Lus in the court, known as the "Three Lus." The other two were Lu Xi and Lu Wei. Lu Guangqi, the son of Lu Yi, was executed, and his brothers prepared themselves to take the examination. They told their close friends, "This is our first attempt," indicating their initial effort. Lu Guangqi was known for his cautious and meticulous nature, and he pursued various paths to success. He submitted a single scroll of his work and achieved success in many endeavors. After passing the examination, he served in various positions in the imperial court and was known for his ability to write detailed and separate documents for each matter. This style of writing, with eight lines of text and separate sheets of paper, became a model for officials to follow, and it was Lu Guangqi who first established this practice. During the late Tang Dynasty, candidates for the imperial examination did not focus on their moral conduct or literary skills but rather on diligently visiting and networking with influential people, a practice known as "jingqie." This was also the model set by the Prince of Fanyang. Lu Guangqi's nephew, Junmai, also had the spirit of his clan, known for their prominence and influence.

Wu Rong, a senior official and renowned literary figure of the Tang Dynasty, sought the favor of Premier and Grand Commandant Wei Zhaodu through his literary skills. Every time he submitted his drafts first, they did not meet the emperor's expectations. Wu Rong then begged a close friend of the military commander to convey his sincerity, saying, "I am fortunate to be among the guests, and I dare not fail to contribute. I have not heard that it is satisfactory, and I am very worried." The friend laughed and said, "Wu Rong is indeed a talented scholar. Every time he is asked, it is his own literary work, not mine. From then on, he revised his work, and it finally met the emperor's expectations." In Jingzhou, a region known for its scholars, most candidates did not become famous, and it was called the "heavenly wasteland" examination. Liu Tuo, a scholar, passed the examination in Jingzhou, and it was called "breaking the precedent of failing to pass the imperial examinations." Since then, many scholars from Jingzhou have achieved success. Guan Tu, a military officer, passed the examination and returned home. The local officials held a banquet in his honor. During the banquet, someone pointed to the sauce bowl on the table and joked with the old officer, "Pull out a soldier to do it." The officer replied with an acetic jar, "This is also for the soldiers." The guests laughed loudly. Guan Tu's wife, Chang Xiu's sister, was a woman of great talent and thought. Her memorial text for her husband is known in the world.

**Wú Róng's Literary Ability** After gaining some reputation, the Tang official Wú Róng, serving as a侍郎 (attendant official), once sought recognition through his literary talent by attaching himself to Wéi Zhāodù, the Prime Minister and Grand General. Every time he drafted a document, he would first present it to Wéi, but it never met his approval. Wú then sought the help of his close associate Zhang Wu to convey his sincerity, saying, "I am fortunate to be among the guests of your court, and I have only ever gained your favor through my writings. Though I feel ashamed of my crude and unrefined skills, I dare not fail to strive for merit. Yet I have heard no word of approval, and I am instead filled with fear." Zhang Wu laughed and said, "Wú the compiler (校书) is indeed a talented scholar. Whenever he is invited to contribute, it is his own writing, not mine. Therefore, you should change your style." He did so, and it finally met the approval of the high-ranking official. Later, he was demoted from office and took residence in Jiangling. There, he composed a preface to a collection of poems by the Buddhist monk Guàn Xiū, stating that since the Tang dynasty, only Yuan and Bai (the famous poets Yuan Zhen and Bai Juyi) and Guàn Xiū himself had been worthy of praise. In his eulogy for Lù Guīmēng, he wrote, "Within the realm, literary talent is only Lǔ Wàng (Lu Guimeng)." These contradictory statements did not go unnoticed and were inevitably criticized by those who understood the matter. --- **Breaking the "Tianhuang" Examination** In the Tang dynasty, Jingzhou was a region known for its gentry and scholars. Each year, candidates from the area were sent to take the imperial examinations, but few succeeded. This region was known as the "tianhuang" (unbroken wilderness), a term for a place that had never produced a successful examinee. Liu Tuì, the Secretary (舎人), was the first to pass the examination from Jingzhou, and thus was called the one who "broke the tianhuang." Since then, I have known of Gǔ Guāntú and Cháng Xiū, who were residents of Jingzhou. Cháng Xiū was known for his high literary talent and had repeatedly passed the highest-level examinations. Guāntú was the son of a military officer stationed before the government office (衙前将校). After passing the examination and returning to his hometown, the local military commander and his subordinates hosted a banquet in his honor. During the feast, he pointed to a sauce bowl on the table and joked with the old officer, saying, "The one who is in charge [or: the one who is promoted] is the one who does it." The person serving the sauce replied, "This is also the one who is in charge [or: promoted] who does it." The guests at the table laughed heartily. Gǔ Guāntú's wife was Cháng Xiū's sister, a woman of literary talent. She composed a eulogy for her husband, which became known in the world.

Cheng Linggong was suddenly surrounded by a giant snake. During the Tang Dynasty, Cheng Linggong, the Commander-in-Chief of Jingzhou, led troops from Cai Prefecture to garrison Jingling. He was targeted for assassination by the military governor Zhang Qian and fled with his troops to Zigui County. One night, a large snake wrapped around his body, preventing him from dying. He said, "If there is something owed, life and death are determined by fate." He hesitated, and the snake also disappeared. Afterward, he recruited and settled households, trained soldiers, and stabilized the region at Zhu Gong. He was soon granted a ceremonial staff, comforted and supported the devastated, and strove diligently for good governance. In the early years, the population was only seventeen households, but by the end of his tenure, it had grown to ten thousand. He served the king and the state diligently, facilitating trade and agriculture, earning praise from the court. He was known as "North Han and South Guo" (Han refers to the surname of the official from Huazhou, originally surnamed Guo, who later returned to his original surname). There was a registrar named He Yin, who was also a former monk, and was upright, sincere, thrifty, and frugal. He became a trusted advisor, and all administrative shortcomings relied on his prevention and assistance. After He Yin's death, Cheng Linggong became more assertive and boastful, often challenging others, and was criticized by those who knew him. His father-in-law, Zhu Zhizhang, a baker, often spoke disrespectfully. Cheng Linggong even killed his eldest son for a minor fault, leaving no heir. During the naval campaign, his advisors remained silent, leading to the defeat at the Battle of Ezh渚, a regrettable outcome.

The Chancellor Zhang Jun's Defeat of the Rebels During the Tang Dynasty, Huang Chao's forces attacked the imperial capital, and Emperor Xizong took refuge in Shu. At the time, Zhang Jun, who was still a commoner without official rank, lived in Yongle Village in Hedong. One day, while walking along a village road, he encountered a Daoist priest who appeared in different guises—sometimes wearing a coarse linen garment and other times a Daoist robe. This Daoist was not easily approachable. Suddenly, the Daoist called out to Zhang, saying, "Zhang San Shi Si Lang, await me in the imperial court to defeat the rebels." Zhang turned around and saw that it was the same Daoist. He said, "I am just a commoner; how could I possibly defeat the rebels?" The Daoist encouraged him to go to Shu, where he would meet with the emperor. However, Zhang was unable to make the journey south due to his mother's illness. The Daoist then gave him two pills and said, "By taking these, you can remain healthy for ten years." Zhang took the medicine, and his mother's illness was cured. Later, Zhang Jun rose to become a chancellor and successfully defeated the rebels. The Daoist, however, was never seen again, and the prophecy of Zhang's victory proved to be remarkably accurate.

薛澄州弄笏【罗九臯附】 唐薛澄州昭纬，即薛保逊之子，恃才傲物，亦有父风。每入朝省，弄笏而行，旁若无人。好唱浣溪纱词。知举后，有一门生辞归乡里，临岐献防曰：“侍郎重德，某乃受恩，尔后请不弄笏与唱浣溪纱，即某幸也。”时人谓之至言。有小吏常学其行步揖逊，公知之，乃召谓曰：“试于庭前学得似，则恕尔罪。”于是下亷拥姬妾而观之，小吏安详傲然，举动酷似，笑而舎之。路侍中岩在西蜀，尝夏日纳凉于毬场防中使院。小吏罗九臯巾裹步履，有似裴条郎中，大貂遥见，促召衫带，遍视方知其非，因笞之。 西岳神毙张簴 唐张防早为僧，败道归俗，后为梁相。先在华山云台观修业，观侧有庄。其弟簴亦轻易道教，因脱防服挂于天尊臂上，云：“借此公为我掌之。”须臾，精神恍忽，似遭殴撃，痛呌狼狈，或顿或起，如有人拖曵之状，归至别业而卒。斯人也，必党于释氏而轻侮道尊，人之无礼，自贻隂殛，非不幸也。与嘉州崔使君开尹真君石函事同【开石函为防官所録夺算见宣室志】。李载仁郎中目睹为愚话之。

Liu Zhongying, the high-ranking official, was governing Qiancheng when one of his maids, disappointed and out of favor, was sold to Chengdu by a female intermediary. Gai Juyuan, a prominent military leader in the Western Shu region, repeatedly governed powerful prefectures and had his residence in Kuzhuxi. The female intermediary described the maid to Gai Juyuan, who decided to take her into his household. The tools and materials for women's work followed her. Every day and night, Gai Juyuan admired her skills and craftsmanship. One day, while Gai Juyuan was looking out of his street-facing window, the maid was attending to him. On the street below, a silk peddler passed by. Gai Juyuan called the maid to his residence and, among the silk bolts, unfolded, rolled, and examined them, assessing their thickness and bargaining with the peddler. The maid suddenly lost her voice and collapsed, as if suffering from a stroke. She was ordered to be carried away and taken back to the female intermediary's home. The next day, she recovered. When questioned about her suffering, the maid, a low-ranking servant, said, "I once was a fine maid of the Liu family. If I die, I die, but how could I serve a silk peddler?" The people of Shu heard of this and all sighed in admiration. Families of high status generally follow the path of propriety, but Gai Juyuan, suddenly becoming wealthy, did not know the customs of scholars and was mocked by maids and servants. It is appropriate.

Zhao Shishu and Liu Bin's Mutual Poetry During the Tang Dynasty, Liu Bin, the governor of Luzhou, was traveling upstream when his boat passed through Ma Xiao Town. A local leader named Zhao Shishu, who had established a camp on high ground and had the authority to decide on crimes, lawsuits, and life or death matters, was granted a military position by the local authority. When the governor's entourage passed by, Zhao Shishu rowed a small boat, wearing a rough cloth robe and holding a cane, and presented a petition stating, "The commoner Zhao Shishu, deputy governor, is not under the jurisdiction of the local area." Liu Bin found this strange and refused to accept it. Zhao Shishu explained, "I am a deputy governor and not under the jurisdiction of the local area." Liu Bin was pleased and received him happily, staying in the camp and setting up a residence, providing abundant supplies and becoming even more respectful and courteous. Zhao Shishu also composed poems expressing his true intentions, which Liu Bin responded to in kind. Liu Bin praised Zhao Shishu for understanding his limits and being sincere.

Ancestral Lineage Chart for Jinshi (Imperial Examination Degree Holders) --- In the Zen tradition, there is an ancestral lineage chart that lists those who have received the essence of the Buddha's teachings. Among the imperial examination degree holders, those who possess the talent of generals and prime ministers are all compiled in the records. However, the discrepancy between names and reality, and the mixing of the good with the bad, often misleads future generations, which is truly astonishing. During the Tang dynasty, Yuwen Hong, a scholar from a distinguished family, lacked literary talent but had a strong passion for the highest level of the imperial examinations. He had a daughter who reached the age of marriage and was truly a beauty of the state. The nobles' sons could not obtain her, and at the time, Dou Fan, who was over the age of 60 and planning to remarry, had his brother, a censor, who had influence and could help others pass the imperial examination. Yuwen Hong betrothed his daughter to Dou Fan. Dou Fan spoke about it to his elder brother, who indeed gained something. The Chancellor Wei, a relative by marriage, found this very despicable. Because of the fire at the residence of the Shu Prefect of Huaizhou, the ancestral tomb was burned. The servants said that a mouse's tail dragged the fire into the storage room, causing the fire to spread. The Governor of Jingzhao told Yuwen Hong that when a fish transforms into a dragon, lightning strikes its tail. Recently, even mice have had their tails burned. This was used to mock him. Bao Guangzi once met a monk who was well-versed in false doctrines. One day, he gathered followers and preached, claiming to have transcended the world. How can one know that he will not be included in the lineage chart in the future? Thus, how can one know that Yuwen Hong's descendants will be included in the imperial examination records? Alas!

Wen Tingyun and Li Shangyin were equally famous, and they were collectively known as "Wen and Li." Wen Tingyun, whose courtesy name was Fei Qing, was also known by the name Yu, and he was famous for his brilliant and elegant literary talents, particularly in writing short, lyrical poems. Whenever he took the imperial examinations, he could complete an eight-rhyme poem in just eight hand movements, a feat that earned him the nickname "Rescuer of Many," as he often helped others by providing them with his work. However, Wen Tingyun had moral flaws, and he was looked down upon by the literati and officials. Li Shangyin once remarked, "I recently obtained a couplet: 'Far compared to Zhao Gong, who served as chancellor for thirty-six years, I have not yet found a matching line.'" Wen Tingyun replied, "Why not say, 'Recently the same as Guo Ling, who served as chancellor for twenty-four years'?" Emperor Xuanzong once composed a poem with the line "Golden hairpin," but he could not find a fitting continuation. He asked an unsuccessful candidate to complete it, and Wen Tingyun responded with "Jade bracelet," which pleased the emperor. Another time, the emperor composed a poem that began with the medicine name "Baotouweng" (white-haired old man), and Wen Tingyun continued it with "Cang'erzi" (hedgehog seed). The emperor loved the poem "Pusamanzan" (Pusamanzan) and asked the chancellor, Dinghufu, to secretly present Wen Tingyun's new composition. He warned them not to reveal it, but they spoke of it to others, leading to Wen Tingyun's distrust. Wen Tingyun also remarked, "In the chancellery, a general sits," criticizing the chancellor's lack of learning. Emperor Xuanzong enjoyed incognito walks and once met Wen Tingyun at an inn. Wen Tingyun did not recognize the emperor's face and boldly questioned him, saying, "Are you not the Grand Marshal?"

The emperor said, "No, it is not so." Then he asked again, "Is it not the case that you are one of the high-ranking officials such as Grand Counselors, Secretaries, or County Magistrates?" The emperor replied, "No, it is not so." Wen Tingyun was then demoted to be the County Magistrate of Fangcheng. The decree stated: "The Confucian school values moral conduct above all else, and considers literary talent as secondary. You, who lack moral conduct, how can your literary talent compensate for this? You merely possess exceptional talent that is not bound by convention, but you are rarely used at the right time." Thus, Wen Tingyun lived a life of wandering and eventually died. Du You, from the region of Western Sichuan, was appointed to the position of Governor of Huaihai. Wen Tingyun visited the Du family's garden in Weiqu and left behind a poem: "Before the tavern of the Zuo family, golden-threaded willows sway; on the dike of the Sui dynasty, the wind carries the sails of luxurious boats. Greedy to bring rain to two places, I do not see the lotus flowers reflected in the water." Du You heard this and sent a thousand bolts of silk to Wen Tingyun. Wu Xing's Shen Hui said that Wen Tingyun's uncle was once scolded by his relatives in Jianghuai, and as a result, Wen Tingyun changed his name. Wen Tingyun also often relied on other candidates to help him in the annual imperial examinations. Shen Xun, the High Official in charge of the exams, assigned Wen Tingyun to a table separate from the other scholars. The next day, before the exams began, Shen Xun told Wen Tingyun, "Those who have been guarding the names are all entrusting their essays to a scholar. This year, there are no scholars who are pretending. Please do your best." He then sent Wen Tingyun away, and as a result, Wen Tingyun was not pleased.

A Woman from the Cui Family and Her Fate with Zhou Ba [With the Story of a Nun from the Lu Family Attached] The wife of Zhou Ba, the Chancellor of Zhexi, was a lady from the Cui family of Boling. She was the sister of the prime minister during the Dazhong period of the Tang Dynasty. When she was young, she became a female Taoist nun, or perhaps a widow who wore a crown and veil, living a secluded life. Zhou Ba was known for his boldness and chivalry. Upon learning of her beauty, he secretly took her by leaping over the wall. Her family had no knowledge of her fate. Later, when Zhou Ba rose to power in Zhexi and established his own state, he prepared a grand procession to bring her back to the Cui family. He said, "In the past, when I held a low position, I dared not speak of this matter. Now, honored with your presence, I do not wish to cause you shame. The chancellor has no choice but to accept you." [This matter was told in great detail by Yang Shao Yin, the Deputy Prefect of Fengxiang. In recent times, the wife of Zhong Ling, the magistrate of Jiangnan, was the daughter of Lu Zhao, a scholar-official. She lost her virtue due to chaos and turmoil, and her brothers, who held official positions, were shamed by it. They said, "Why didn't you commit suicide, young lady, and marry a man who is not a real man?" The *Xianzhuan* (Biography of Immortals) tells of Xu Xianggu, who lived on the Hengshan mountain and was teased by the monks. She fell down by herself, which was said to be protected by the spirits of the Taoist deities.] A nun from Moshan opened a hall to teach the Dharma, and the Zen master Deng Yinfeng was present.

A person of virtue tests his adherence to principles, carrying a knife into a meditation hall at midnight with the intent to commit a violent act. The nun, fearing death, loses her resolve, and he takes her away to a hidden peak, removes her robe, gathers the monks to inform them, and his followers immediately disperse. During the reign of Liu Bei, his general Zhang Qiang is ruthless and tyrannical, whipping someone's chest, and governing Meizhou, there is a young nun with beautiful and wise appearance who preaches the Infinite Life Sutra. Zhang Qiang attempts to force her into breaking her vows, but she refuses and scolds him, causing him to break her teeth. He then drowns her and her father together in the waters of the Maoyi River. The daughter of the Cui family, Mo Shan Nun, preserves herself by compromising out of fear and cowardice, while Xu Priestess uses her spiritual power to stop the violence. The nun who preached the sutra dies because she upheld her vows, showing that women practicing Daoism also face certain obstacles. Thus, it is known that those who cultivate beauty to seduce others also face challenges. Sun Qi, a scholar and official, wrote *North Gate Records*, which describes the leisure activities of the sons of officials in the city. Its intent seems to say that the daughter of a noblewoman lost her virtue to her nephew, the son of the Zheng family, and was subsequently married to him, killing her family to cover up the scandal. It is known that the leisure activities in the city do not harm the young. Cui Yuchang did not know about oxen.

In the early days before Zhu Wen founded the Later Liang dynasty, Cui Yuchang was selected as a Jinshi and had a villa in the jurisdiction of Bianzhou. Cui Yuchang was quick-witted and skilled in social interactions. When he first arrived in Bianzhou, he flattered Zhu Wen by proposing to perform the ritual of honoring his hometown. Zhu Wen was very pleased with this gesture because Cui Yuchang did not come across as overly familiar or disrespectful. He was well taken care of and often invited to be a guest at Zhu Wen's gatherings, sometimes even accompanying him in entertainment. Zhu Wen, knowing that Cui Yuchang owned a large estate, asked if he had any oxen to lend. He inquired, "Do you have any oxen in your estate?" Cui Yuchang replied, "I don't know how to handle an ox," implying that he did not have any. This was a colloquial expression meaning he did not have any oxen. Zhu Wen became very angry, saying, "How can there be someone who doesn't know how to handle an ox? Am I a village fool? If you know how to handle an ox, then I don't know how to handle one. Such disrespect is unforgivable and could lead to dire consequences." Later, when someone explained the situation, Zhu Wen gradually calmed down. During the Tang dynasty, Zhang Shu, a Right Censor, was the son of Liang Jiong, the Minister of Personnel, and the nephew of Yi. Zhang Shu was known for his beautiful prose and quick wit, and was highly regarded at the time. His mother, who often wore a jade Tianzun (a Taoist deity), was lost during the chaos of the Yellow Turban Rebellion. Her remains were later found, but Zhang Shu, out of respect for his mother's memory, did not seek to advance his career through the imperial examinations, which was seen as a sign of his integrity. Eventually, he passed the imperial examinations under the auspices of Pei Zhi, a high-ranking official, and became a Right Censor. Once, he playfully teased his fellow Jinshi, Du Xunhe, saying, "It is a great honor to be a Jinshi with you, Du Xunhe." Du Xunhe responded, "It is a great honor to be a Jinshi with Zhang Shu." He added, "Everyone knows of Du Xunhe's name, but who knows of Zhang Shu's?" They both laughed heartily, realizing that a false reputation is not enough to determine someone's true worth. Zhang Shu was also known for his literary works, including the *Ji Oufu* (击瓯赋), which contained the lines, "Dongfang Chengqing, the phoenix startled, pecking open the pearl net; the Red-Jawed Horse of the Emperor, breaking through the jade fields." He also wrote the *Hu Jiao Fu* (鄠郊赋), which narrated the turmoil in Chang'an and expressed sorrow for the chaos in Jiangnan and Ganling, showing that even Du Xunhe's reputation could not match his literary achievements.

**Trivial Tales of the North, Volume Four** **Imperially Approved Complete Library of Four Treasuries** **Trivial Tales of the North, Volume Five** **Tang Dynasty, compiled by Sun Guangxian** **The Secret Memorial of Duke Linghu** (附: Wooden Headpieces) During the reign of Emperor Wenzong in the Taiping era, the eunuchs acted with unrestrained power, and as a result of the Ganlu Incident, officials such as Wang Ya were all caught in the ensuing disaster and remained unexonerated. After Emperor Xuangong ascended the throne, he severely curtailed their power. In his later years, he once entrusted defense to Chancellor Linghu Gong. Duke Linghu wished to exterminate all the eunuchs but, considering their injustice, he secretly submitted a memorial, saying, "Do not spare those with crimes, do not fill vacancies, and naturally there will be no remnants left." Later, this document was seen by the eunuchs, and thus the relationship between the north and south became even more antagonistic. By the end of Emperor Zhaozong's reign, Cui Shizhong was able to carry out his wishes, but in doing so, both good and bad eunuchs were indiscriminately punished. After the Danyu period, palace ladies all wore wooden headpieces, and people from all directions imitated this trend. Only the inner officials each had their own style. Craftsmen remarked, "Chopping military heads and special heads, it turned out to be true."

Li Yuan Mocks Cao Tang In the Tang Dynasty, Cao Tang, a Jinshi (imperial examination graduate), was renowned for his ethereal and transcendent talent in fairy poetry. Li Yuan, a Yungwa (a type of official) from Yueyang, often recited Cao Tang's poetry and thought of him. One day, Cao Tang visited Li Yuan. Li Yuan eagerly received him, saying, "I have not yet seen your appearance, and I would have thought you worthy of riding a celestial bird. Now that I meet you, I wonder if even a strong water buffalo could bear your weight." The people of the time found this amusing. [It was said that poetry was too pure, and prose was too distant; both were deemed unnecessary, as they lacked literary brilliance and moral instruction.] The Affair of the Ethnic Officials in the Imperial Secretariat According to Li Zhao's *Guoshi Bu* (Supplement to the History of the Tang Dynasty), at the end of the Zhenyuan era, there were four officials who, having served as military governors, were granted purple robes and appointed to the Imperial Secretariat. They were jokingly called the "Four Gentlemen" by their colleagues. From the Dazhong era to the Xiantong era, Bai Zhongling (a title for Bai Juyi) was appointed as a chancellor, followed by Bi Xian, Cao Quan, and Luo Shao, who were also appointed as chancellors. Cui Shenyu, a chancellor, said, "It is time to retire. Recently, the Imperial Secretariat is full of ethnic officials, as Bi, Bai, Cao, and Luo are all of ethnic descent." When Jiang Shen was appointed as a chancellor, Li Jingxun, the Shangshu (a high-ranking official) of Xichuan, saw the appointment notice and sighed, "I cannot serve such people." He then feigned illness and left his post, composing a poem: "Ten thousand households in Chengdu are thrown away like a single feather." This was compared to the saying about Bo Ling. In recent times, Wu Rong, a Shilang (a type of official), was a student of Zhao Chong, a Daifu (a high-ranking official). On the day Zhao Chong passed away, Wu Rong, from Tianshui, said, "I expected to treat him as an equal, but how disappointing it is that he did not attain the highest rank."

**Translation:** Xu Xiang Critiques Cheng Zhongling During the reign of Emperor Zhaozong in the late Tang Dynasty, Cheng Zhongling, a prominent figure in Jingnan, had once been a monk who seized control of a palace and was later officially recognized. In his later years, he was known for his vigorous debates and distinctive manner. Initially, the region of Li and Lang, which was under his jurisdiction, was seized by a local strongman named Lei Man. Cheng Zhongling requested that the region be transferred to the jurisdiction of Xu Yanrui, a high-ranking official, but Xu Yanrui, while serving as chancellor, did not take action on the request, leading to Cheng Zhongling's resentment. When Xu Yanrui was sent to govern Fanyu, his route passed through Cheng Zhongling's territory. Although Cheng Zhongling extended hospitality to Xu Yanrui, he was often discontent. After a meal, the seating arrangements were changed, and Cheng Zhongling engaged in sharp, deceptive argumentation. Xu Yanrui remarked, "As a high-ranking official, you compare yourself to Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Wen of Jin, yet Lei Man is merely a local bandit. How could you not use force against him if it were not for your resentment toward the court?" Cheng Zhongling was embarrassed and humbled. Xu Yanrui's refined and eloquent discourse deeply engaged Cheng Zhongling, who, despite being greatly respectful and fearful, made a playful remark about the tropical diseases prevalent in the southern regions: "May you take care of yourself, as the diseases of the south are no easy matter." Xu Yanrui humorously responded, "In the southern regions, even these diseases cannot harm a former monk." Cheng Zhongling was ultimately ashamed.

### Translation **Wei Shangshu Jian Lu Xiang** During the early part of Emperor Xuanzong's reign of the Tang Dynasty, Lu Xie passed the imperial examination for the jinshi degree. He was unremarkable in appearance and had an awkward speech, which led to him being nicknamed "Hui" (comet), implying that he was a fleeting or unusual person due to his speech impediment. His brothers all mocked him, but only Wei Xi, the Shangshu (Minister), showed respect. Wei Xi said to his brothers, "Although Lu is unremarkable in character, his articles have a beginning and an end. Such a person can be predicted to achieve great things in the future." Indeed, Lu Xie eventually reached the highest levels of government, was promoted to the position of Governor of Jingzhao, and later became the Governor of Fujian. His former brothers who had mocked him never reconciled or showed favor, which is what is meant by losing people because of appearance. Is it not the case with the Wei brothers? **Xue Feng Appreciates Wang Zhuo** During the early part of Emperor Xuanzong's reign, a man from Weicheng County in Mianzhou, Wang Zhuo, passed the imperial examination for the jinshi degree. He had unusual and remarkable writing, continuing the legacy of Li Bai and Chen Ziang. Once, he wrote an inscription for the Daoist temple in Weicheng County, which was eloquent and well-structured. At that time, Xue Feng, who was governing Mianzhou, saw and appreciated Wang Zhuo's work. Because Wang Zhuo was from his region, Xue Feng invited and met him, and as a result, Wang Zhuo changed his name to Zhuo and his courtesy name to Cizan. Xue Feng found his writing as impressive as Wang Bo's. Wang Zhuo's mother built the inscription, and Xue Feng listed his official titles on the back of the stele to enhance the inscription. Even though there was war and destruction, the dragon-headed top of the stele remained standing. Those who appreciated such things would stop to read it. Later, Wang Zhuo became blind and disabled, and was unknown in the world. Thanks to Hedong Gong (a title for a prominent figure) who raised his status, his descendants were honored. His son, Wang Pu, served in Shu up to the position of Hanlin Scholar.

**Chen Tao's Obsession with Books** During the Dazhong era, there was a recluse named Chen Tao from Hongzhou, who possessed exceptional talent. His poetry seemed to reflect either supernatural techniques or strategies of kingship and hegemony. Even scholars of literature found it difficult to fully understand his poetry, which reflected the teachings of Confucius. Some of his poems include: - "The depth of the river is not enough to show the might of the whale." - "Drinking ice, the wolf's child becomes thin; thinking of the鹧鸪, the cold day is long." - "The Central Plains are not devoid of dragons and phoenixes, but the royal net is too loose." - "A pot of male and female gold liquid fire; ten years of cold and heat in a deer skin robe. Send it to the Dong River, let Ban Gu face the corner and fly the iron shuttle." **Yangshuo and Lipu's Natural Beauty** Wang Zan, a famous official, had a chance encounter with Yang Qu, a scholar from Hongnong, who had visited the southern regions. Yang Qu spoke highly of the natural beauty of Yangshuo and Lipu, saying, "Have you seen the natural beauty of Yangshuo and Lipu?" Wang Zan replied, "I have never seen such beauty." They both laughed. Soon after, Yang Qu was appointed to a position in the south and moved his family there. He was also considered a high-ranking local official.

### Translation **Imperial Edict to Resolve Conflicts in Huai and Zhe Regions** Emperor Xizong of Tang, exiled in Shu, had not yet gathered his court officials, and there were no scholars capable of drafting imperial edicts. Although officials like Le Penggui and Hou Hei resided in the Hanlin Academy, they lacked the talent to resolve difficulties and conflicts. The high-ranking military officer stationed in Huaihai, wielding troops, did not advance and was at odds with Zhou Bao of western Zhejiang. Petitions and reports were submitted in succession, each detailing grievances. The court intended to issue an edict to mediate, but the draft by the scholar was unsatisfactory to the emperor. A promising candidate, Li Duan, known for his eloquent writing, was summoned by the military commander Tian Lingzi, who was aware of his abilities. Tian Lingzi engaged him in conversation and entrusted him with the task of drafting the edict. Li Duan requested a drink of wine, and after several cups, he completed the edict in one stroke. Beyond the elegance of his writing, he also demonstrated remarkable eloquence, earning high praise from the emperor. He was appointed as the official responsible for drafting imperial edicts during the emperor's temporary residence and eventually rose to the position of provincial official. According to tradition, Li Sen, the chancellor, had recommended a deputy with a purple robe, but the emperor repeatedly refused. One candidate, aware of Li Sen's literary skills, requested him to draft a memorial. The memorial stated: "The region under your command oversees eight states, with a powerful military force. A deputy wearing a purple robe is not appropriate for the position." The chancellor was pleased and indeed granted the candidate an imperial favor. The general who defeated the rebels of Zhaoyi drafted the imperial edict himself and submitted it to the Hanlin Academy. Similarly, Zheng Wengong drafted an edict for the high-ranking military officer, all aiming to achieve their objectives without overstepping their roles. **Fate and Fortune: The Tale of Wu Rong** Qian Shangfu, the warlord, began by killing Dong Chang and gained control over the two Zhe regions, fulfilling his ambitions. However, people were ashamed of his actions. Wu Lang, a scholar from Xiaoshan County in Yue Prefecture, had a reputation for his literary skills in the imperial examinations but was repeatedly detained and could not pursue his studies. He managed to escape westward, nearing the boundary of Suzhou, when he looked back and saw two officials with purple robes chasing him. Wu believed he would be captured and decided to avoid them by not turning around. He hurried forward and reached a ferry, where he called for a boat and asked Wu to join him. Upon reaching the shore, the pursuers were nowhere to be seen. Thus, he escaped. Later, he was appointed to a high official position, demonstrating that those whose fate is determined are aided by divine intervention.

Notable Figures from the Shen and Jiang Families Shen Xun, the Lang, was exceptionally refined and beautiful, a person among immortals. He was appointed to a high position in the northern mountains, and in the capital, people recited the immortal poetry of Cao Tang, saying, "The imperial edict newly appoints Shen Lang, who then divides the land and leads the east. Where does he roam tonight? His attendants all ride white phoenixes, revealing his divine appearance." Jiang Ning, the Lang, was also a notable figure. Whenever he appeared, the officials and their families regarded it as an auspicious omen, calling him the Water Moon Guanyin. The ancient figures Pan Anren and Wei Shubao could not compare to him. In the late Tang dynasty, there were distinguished officials known as the Jade Bamboo Class (玉笋班), among whom Shen Xun, with his character resembling that of his ancestors, was said to have continued their excellence. The Jade Bamboo Class was also known for its elegance and refinement. Zhang Jun, Le Penggui, and Tian Lingzi and Their Involvement in Internal and External Affairs According to an old rule, scholars were not supposed to associate with eunuchs. Tian Lingzi, the military commander, possessed immense power, likened to the ability to reverse the heavens. During the reign of Emperor Xizong, the emperor relocated to Yangyuan, and the officials had not yet gathered, with a lack of someone to handle official documents. Le Penggui, the Lang, also reached the emperor's location and visited the eunuch, requesting matters both internal and external. As a result, he was recommended to serve as a scholar in the Hanlin Academy. Zhang Jun, the chancellor, was promoted from a hermit to a court official, also emerging from the school of Zi Fang, and all showed respect for both internal and external matters. When the emperor arrived in Shu, the officials gathered. One day, the eunuch hosted a banquet for the chancellor, and scholars and Zhang Jun were both invited. Zhang Jun, embarrassed by the presence of others, decided to bow first.

A visit to the Central Commander to show respect through the offering of wine surprised the Central Commander. Soon after, the host and guests took their seats. The Central Commander said to the other officials, "I and the others have different social standings. I have already been honored by both the inner and outer courts; how could I be afraid of changing? Today, in a humble position, offering wine is not appropriate." Zhang Gong was filled with shame and fear. From then on, he was greatly disrespected by the scholarly elite. Le Gong, when he first took the imperial examination, submitted a petition to Li Zhaoshi, a high-ranking official, and recommended himself. He wrote, "Except for the Nine Classics, the Laozi and Zhuangzi, and the Eight Capital Assignments, I wrote eight hundred volumes of books. Please allow me to be tested, for I truly have knowledge." However, he was not very simple in his official documents, and some people of the time did not approve of it. The story of Xue Shao, the Junior Tutor, rejecting the inner and outer courts: During the late Tang Dynasty, Xue Tinggui, the Junior Tutor, was a prominent figure from a noble family who served during a declining era. The First Emperor of Later Liang, Li Keyong, grew stronger in military power, causing the court to be shaken and gradually become more arrogant, which made the whole world fear him. Xue Tinggui, as the envoy for the four commanderies, was approached by Liu Han, a guest officer from Yimen, who came to prevent him from submitting to the inner and outer courts. Xue Tinggui pretended not to know and told the attendant, "I am unworthy; how dare I accept the Grand Marshal's obeisance?" He refused to bow. After accepting the throne, he served Later Liang with dedication, and the First Emperor of Later Liang treated him with leniency, allowing him to live a long life until his death. During this period, he was ordered to confer the title of Grand Tutor on the First Emperor of Shu. His old illness flared up in the palace, and the people of Shu sent a doctor named Yang Pu to treat him. Xue Tinggui wrote a letter of thanks, and at the end of the letter, he asked for a sedan chair to return to the capital to seek medical treatment. The First Emperor of Shu was surprised and said, "Fortunately, there is effective medicine."

Without waiting for recovery, he firmly requested and stopped the carriage. The official said to the envoy, "Last night, when I asked this doctor, he was completely illiterate. How can I entrust my life to him?" Eventually, he did not take medicine and returned north. Wang Jian served in the Later Liang as a Censor of the Secretariat and served as an assistant to the governor in Qiantang (Hangzhou). Before that, Wang Jian had established himself in one region. He often had to tolerate the Later Liang ruler because the regions north of the Yellow River and west of the pass were powerful enemies. He also frequently suffered defeats in the Huai River and Yangtze River area, which greatly relied on the Two Zhe regions for support and tribute. Whenever envoys were sent far across the vast sea, they hoped for generous gifts and feared not being treated properly, so they all bowed to him. Those who understood it found it remarkable. Eventually, he received a high-ranking position in the imperial court. --- **Yang Sheng, the Adopted Mother [An Shi Jian Attached]** During the Tang Dynasty, Yang Sheng initially served under Li Changfu, the military governor of Fengxiang, and repeatedly established military achievements. However, Li Changfu began to suspect him and secretly intended to harm him. Li Changfu's beloved concubine, Zhou, pitied Yang Sheng's innocence and secretly informed him. Thus, Yang Sheng managed to escape and was saved. Later, he became the commander of the Fifty-Fourth Army and was appointed as the military governor of Weisheng Commandery, establishing his command in Pengzhou. He governed the people with kindness and invited respected guests, including monks and Taoists, treating them with great courtesy. The people deeply revered him. After Li Changfu's defeat, he ordered the search for Zhou, who was then treated as a mother figure. Zhou, being young and beautiful, feared that there might be inappropriate requests, so she remained cautious.

**Translation:** He swore to heaven and earth that he would never enter into an improper match. Every morning before attending to his duties, he would perform the ritual of asking for well-being. Even when besieged, he never neglected these rituals. The newly established regime's military forces were not yet fully prepared, and it was suddenly attacked by the ruler of Shu (Liu Bei) on a besieged city. The rescue forces did not arrive in time, and the city was destroyed within ten days, leading to the death of the commander. There was a horse and foot officer named An Shijian, who was a trusted advisor to Yang. When the city was captured, he was taken prisoner. The ruler of Shu, knowing his loyalty and valor, hoped to use him and wished to treat him leniently. An Shijian said, "I have been promoted by Yang, the Grand Tutor, and dare not hesitate to die." The ruler sighed and executed him, setting up a memorial service and burying him. **Cheng Lingong and the Hezhou Zai** During the Tianyou period of the Tang Dynasty, the Huai army besieged Wuchang without relief. Du Hong, the official, requested assistance from the King of Liang. The King of Liang, being friendly with Jingzhou, advised Cheng to lead troops to rescue. Following the hegemon's desire to personally lead the campaign, he ordered the construction of a large ship using resources from five prefectures. It took three years to complete, and it was named "Hezhou Zai." The ship was equipped with defensive measures and had a structure resembling a government office, and it was also named "Qishan Jiehai," meaning "Cutting through the Sea." Its splendor and grandeur can be imagined. He concealed faults and rejected advice, making decisions based on his own will. The officials in the government were unable to speak up, and only the clerk Yang Hou supported him. The ship was attacked and burned by Wu forces at Pujun Mountain, leading to the drowning of the commander and the scattering of the soldiers. Previously, the name was changed to "Rui," which means "inside the water," and his death in the water was seen as a premonition. The armies of Hunan and Langzhou entered Jingzhou, capturing soldiers, civilians, and various officials, leading them to Changsha. Changing the name "Rui" and the story of "Hezhou Zai" were all predestined.

Wei Zhaodu, the Imperial High Commissioner, led an expedition against Western Shu. During the Tang Dynasty, Chen Jingxuan occupied Chengdu and defied the central government. Wei Zhaodu was appointed as the Imperial High Commissioner to lead the campaign against him, mobilizing troops from Eastern Shu. At that time, Wang Shu, the future founder of the Shu Kingdom, was a bandit leader who had been plundering various counties. He rallied his troops and submitted to Wei Zhaodu, becoming the Commander of the Inner Guard and serving as one of Wei Zhaodu's key lieutenants. Wang Shu then petitioned to be granted control over several states in Western Shu, with authority established in the region of Linjiang. Wang Shu was known for his humility and wisdom, and he served Wei Zhaodu with great caution. However, Wei Zhaodu realized that Wang Shu could not effectively manage military affairs, as this was not his strength. Whenever Wei Zhaodu planned to attack a city, he would request to wear military attire and lead the troops into battle, but he feared being hit by arrows or stones and would not approach the front lines. Wei Zhaodu would then say, "How could soldiers dare to be disrespectful?" He would call out to a notorious figure from Eastern Shu known as "Tang the Human-Eater" and warn him, saying, "How is human flesh different from pork or mutton?" He would then give the figure one thousand coins to compensate for the "meat price," and others would follow this example. After two years of siege, the city of Shu was already in dire straits and would soon fall. One day, there was a commotion outside the city gates, as the troops were running out of food. A soldier seized and dragged one of Wei Zhaodu's close aides, Cao the Magistrate, and cut him into pieces, eating him. Fearing that he would suffer the same fate, Wei Zhaodu feigned illness and handed over the banner of Western Shu to Wang Shu, returning to the imperial court. Although he was not known for his bravery, he had used his intelligence to save himself.

### Translation **Zhang Lufeng's Misfortune** In times of great difficulty, it is often the case that gentlemen encounter misfortune. During the late Tang Dynasty, the Jinshi Zhang Lufeng, who was renowned alongside Luo Yin, both from Zhejiang, frequently failed the imperial examinations despite their fame. The powerful warlord Qian Chu, known as "Qian Shangfu," rose to prominence and controlled the eight districts of Qiantang. After defeating Dong Chang, he completely occupied Hangzhou and Yue. Thus, Zhang and Luo were caught in his net. However, they came from humble origins and were not familiar with the affairs of the world. They valued county magistrates more than imperial court officials, once saying, "Such a person is not capable of being a county magistrate, only fit to be an imperial court official." Consequently, Zhang Lufeng was appointed as a low-ranking official responsible for drafting and managing official documents. He refused and was beaten. Luo Yin was appointed as the magistrate of Qiantang and obeyed orders out of fear of death. Zhang and Luo considered this a disgrace, while Qian Chu took pride in it. Both good and bad were destroyed together; alas, how pitiful! It is said that later, Zhang Lufeng served as the governor of Suzhou and wrote three volumes titled *Zhangzi*, which circulated in the world. Luo Yin was highly regarded by the imperial court, and Qian Chu eventually appointed him as a high-ranking official, the Grand Secretary, and he lived a comfortable life until his death. **The Second Marriage of the Pei Family (with the addition of Guī Dēng Shàngshū)** Pei Qiu, the Duke of Jin, was frugal and meticulous. During his time in Jiangxi, he kept all household items and books sealed and stored, never using them. Whenever there was a banquet, he would borrow them from officials' homes. In Fanyu, he had a daughter whom he deeply loved and chose Zheng Jinshi of荥yang as her husband. However, due to the urgency of the autumn recommendation, he could not avoid the examination. Soon after arriving in the capital, he received news of his father's death. The five teachings (five moral principles) dictated that his daughter and her husband were overwhelmed with grief, but Zheng, the husband, laughed, indicating a lack of deep affection. Generally, women from noble families do not remarry after their husbands' deaths. The daughter, being young and unable to forget her feelings, summoned the doorkeeper to arrange a second marriage.

A Former Official Informed Him [or: A Former Official Told Him] that Remarriage Involving Incestuous Relations Began in Hedong (河东). Yuán Zhēn (元祯), a Junior Supervisor (少监), Sū Yá (苏涯), a Middle Chancellor (中丞) granted purple robes, Yáng Pín (杨玭), a Junior County Magistrate (少尹), and the Five Teachings' close officials, including the Chief of the County (别驾), all agreed. Guī Dēng (归登), a Grand Secretary (尚书), always cleared the area around him when bathing and sat in a large basin for a while. Sometimes, someone peeping saw a giant turtle blowing water. He was very stingy in nature. Once he spoiled a lamb's liver, cut it, and ate it immediately, sealing the leftovers. One day, his wife cut and ate the sealed leftovers, and he became very angry, leading him to never eat meat again. This is better than the likes of He Qiao. --- **Closing Doors to Avoid Barbarians [Wang Xianzhu Attached]** Western Sichuan (西川) has been free from war since the rebellion of Liu Pi (刘辟) after the Tang dynasty. People are not accustomed to fighting. Each year, troops are sent to garrison at the Dadu River (大渡河). As soon as the barbarian flag is raised, they flee at the sight of it. During the Xiantong period (咸通), the barbarians advanced directly to the city, and the residents closed their doors to resist, and the barbarians did not dare to knock on the doors. Once, a barbarian got lost and entered Guangdu County (广都县). Hundreds of villagers chased him, and as soon as he turned back, he fled like a collapsing wall. From morning till night, they could not capture him, showing how cowardly he was. During the reign of Wang Xianzhu (王先主), the Yunnan barbarians invaded Sichuan. The Sichuan army was brave and eager to conquer them, capturing and eating them without regarding them as enemies, showing a hundredfold increase in courage compared to before. --- **High Magistrate's Cunning Deceit**

During the Xiantong period, the southern barbarians surrounded the Western Shu region. The court ordered Grand Marshal Gao Pian, Duke of Bohai, to move from Tianping Army to govern Chengdu. Before the military vehicles arrived, he first wrote military orders on silk banners, drew a symbol on them, and posted them at post stations to boost morale. The Nanzhao chieftain, having been punished by the defeat at Jiaozhi, fled upon seeing the post station. Previously, the city had no outer walls; when the southern invaders arrived, it was quickly reduced to ashes. The common people had no long-term plan for safety. Duke Gao planned the layout, drew maps, and began construction. Worried that the construction would be disrupted, he ordered the monk Jingshan to enter Nanzhao on a mission. He declared that Gao was personally inspecting the border and personally overseeing the construction of the city, raising beacons all the way to the Dadu River. In 93 days, the city walls rose, and the flags did not move; the Nanzhao army was too frightened to act. This is what it means to win through deception. Zhang Daogu's Epitaph During the Tianfu period of the Tang dynasty, Zhang Daogu from Putaixian, Cangzhou, was promoted to the position of Left Censor after passing the imperial examination. He was highly learned but eccentric and aloof. He submitted a memorial on five dangers and two disorders and was demoted to serve as a magistrate in Shizhou. Afterward, he went to Shu (modern Sichuan). In his earlier memorial, he said that Liu Bei and Sun Quan have already been born in this world. He feared being resented by the Shu ruler and had no place to settle. When the new dynasty began, he hoped to be summoned to serve in the court but was rejected and ultimately met a tragic fate. He once divined an ominous hexagram and prepared a tomb, inscribing it as the grave of Zhang Daogu, Left Censor of the Tang dynasty. Indeed, he was later killed and buried. Someone found a letter from the Shu ruler, criticizing the officials for concealing his talents and not recommending him, and saying that he was a person who clung to the past and brought disaster upon himself. (The Left Censor was deeply knowledgeable in the *I Ching* and wrote several volumes titled *Yi Ti*, which were circulated in the world.)

### Translation #### Preface to the Collection of Chaogujizi During the Zhenyuan period of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man from Zigui named Tan Zhengfu, who lived in the mountains of Lushan. He was summoned by a high-ranking official, Fu Zai, to write for him, but he eventually disappeared in the regions of Ba and Wu. Someone once showed me a few of his writings, which had a unique style and a severe yet dignified tone, truly embodying the craftsmanship of Wudu's knife and ruler. He was given the title "Chaogujizi," and his collection consisted of twenty volumes. I wrote to Li Wan, an official in the prefectural administration of Guizhou, asking him to search for the collection. However, three days before the letter reached him, a local resident who had a complete set of his works accidentally set fire to them, burning them to ashes. Alas, if I had been able to transcribe and disseminate them, it would have brought glory to his reputation. What a great misfortune! #### Luo Gun's Refusal of the Appointment in Western Sichuan [with Appendices on Li Pin and Huang Fegong] Luo Gun, an official from Chengdu, was a native of Linqiong. He took the imperial examination for the jinshi degree and was known for his excellent literary skills and high moral character. During the Dashi period of the Tang Dynasty, after passing the examination, he did not return home. At a time of chaos and disorder, the court was full of troubles, and he faced military hardships, enduring all kinds of poverty and cold. The ruler of Shu sent a letter to the Hanlin Academy, asking the scholar-official Lühu and the junior minister Wu to select one person to serve as a secretary and appoint him as a junior minister in the region of Guiyang. Luo Gun, addressing his close friend, said, "I swear to block the main road with horses, wear ragged clothes, and wait for the imperial court. I will no longer return west; I will be like the Eastern neighbor of Confucius." Eventually, he became a member of the imperial court and died as a junior minister of the Rites Department in the Later Liang Dynasty. In Shu, the only person with ambition was this junior minister. Yang Xiong's two sons died, leaving no heir, and he was buried in Shu. How far is this from the example of Master Ji of Yanling? In recent times, Li Pin and Huang Fegong were both from the southern regions. Li Pin abandoned his wife and married into a noble family, while Huang did not return home for thirty years, during which his wife and mother were still alive. How could he have the heart to do so?

Gao Ce's Memorial [with Han Zhao's Commentary] Gao Ce of Pengzhou was a man of great intelligence and broad knowledge, his literary skills extensive and versatile. He was skilled in astronomy and calendar calculations, as well as the four traditional arts: playing the zither, chess, painting, and calligraphy. He was also adept at playing the flute and the huqin. He was compared to Zhu Yi of the Liang dynasty. Once, he paid homage to Gao Yan and presented a memorial, in which he summarized his abilities: "I have read ten thousand volumes and can drink a hundred cups." Gao Yan said, "Ten thousand volumes are not easily verified [or, 'one does not have time to verify']; but a hundred cups of wine can be tested." He then offered Gao Ce wine, and indeed, Gao Ce drank a hundred cups as he claimed. Later, when Emperor Xian of the Han dynasty fled to Shu, Gao Ce presented his writings to the court and was appointed to the position of Secretarial Officer. He died while serving as the Military Governor's Secretary in Weisheng. Han Zhao, who served in Shu, reached the rank of Minister of Rites and Grand Master of the Palace of Literary Thought. He had some literary talent, but his skills were varied and not particularly strong, like a sock's thread that has no single strong strand. At the time, a court official named Li Taigao said, "Han the Eighth Seat's skills and arts are like the threads of a sock—none of them are particularly strong." Contemporaries agreed with this assessment.

Fu Zai (符载), originally from Shu, was a man of extraordinary talent. He initially associated with Yang Heng and Song Ji, residing in Qingcheng Mountain to study. Yang Heng was promoted to the rank of Jinshi (进士), while Song Ji died old without achieving anything. Only Fu Gong (符公) considered himself capable of great achievements and was ashamed of routine appointments, holding high aspirations. Wei Nankang (韦南康), while governing Shu, appointed him as a deputy officer. Although he was recognized, he still behaved haughtily. Wei Gong set up an alchemical ritual to invite him to write a斋词 (斋词) at the Maha Pool. Fu Gong accompanied by drinking at the Maha Pool, left the table to wash his hands and mouth, and ordered twelve clerks to hold the inkstone. Each person took turns walking around the pool and recited poetry on the spot, praising his quick and agile nature. Liu Pi (刘辟) was a military officer at the time and relied on Wei Gong. He especially asked Fu Gong to write a true praise, which read: "Majestic and powerful, with a noble and heroic spirit, like a dragon emerging from the water and an eagle riding the wind. Acting with righteousness is firm, assisting benevolence is smooth. In the future, meeting in the Hall of the Unicorns. When the troubles in Jingzhao occurred, Pengcheng appointed him to a temporary post, arising the intention to dominate. Fu Gong for him."

In all the matters he handled, he was even more submissive and respectful, especially during Liu Pi's defeat. Many officials in the office suffered the disaster, but only Fu Sheng presented a box of rough drafts of his memorials to Gao Chongwen, the chancellor, and then respectfully departed. He lived in seclusion on Mount Lu, and it can be said that he had foresight, as evidenced by the previous true praise. He lived comfortably in the two forests of Jixian and passed the year there. The army of Nanchang requested him to serve as a deputy and be appointed as a ritual official, but he did not accept the appointment. A young servant carried a scroll and presented it to Xiangyang, asking for a million coins to buy a mountain. People from all directions invited him, and his door was filled with offerings. In his thatched cottage, he had twenty female entertainers to serve and entertain him. His reputation was very famous at the time, and those who adhered to the right path and followed conventions were called wicked people. [I once read the complete works of Fu Gong. His writing is concise and elegant, entering the inner chambers of his art. Only Jianping Tan Zhengfu could truly appreciate it. As for Song Ji, though he had literary talent, his writing was verbose and superficial, not in the same league as Fu's. Zhan Ben died in the tomb inscription of Pengshan, which is written in the style of Song.] During the Guangqi period of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Hou He from Chengdu. He had a dignified and beautiful appearance, like an ice pot, and was selected or promoted.

Cui, who came from a family of officials in Binning, served as a subordinate during the displacement of Emperor Xian. He was later promoted to the positions of Zhongshu Shèrén and Hanlin Xueshi, and was able to complete several test questions immediately during an imperial examination. The old aristocratic scholars secretly admired him. After Emperor Xian returned to the capital, he was appointed to a prefecture but did not take up the post. Instead, he retired to a villa in Daojiang and named it "Wulong Pavilion." When Wang Jian, who sought to establish a base in Sichuan, wanted to invite Cui to join his entourage, he first sent his chief secretary Feng Fang to assess Cui's suitability. Feng Fang, known for his literary talent, wrote the letter inviting Cui. Cui stayed in Chengdu and was highly respected by Hou Gong Zhen. Hou Gong Zhen wrote a letter of thanks to Wang Jian, expressing his confidence in his ability to draft official documents and memorials. Zhao Fang, from Yanting County in Zizhou, was well-versed in military strategy and practical governance. Both he and his wife had strong moral principles and did not accept invitations from other officials. He wrote ten volumes of "The Long and Short Strategies," which discussed the principles of kingship and hegemony and were widely circulated.

**Translation:** Trivial Records of the Northern Dream, Volume Five <Subsection, Novel Class, Miscellaneous Records, Trivial Records of the Northern Dream> Imperially Approved Complete Library of Four Treasuries Trivial Records of the Northern Dream, Volume Six By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty The Affair of Wu Xiang [with Liu Hanhong attached] Tang Dynasty's Li Shen was known for his integrity and straightforwardness. In the central government, he was close with Li Deyu, and those who opposed them were deeply resentful. When Li Shen governed Huaihai, Wu Xiang was the Magistrate of Jiangdu. At that time, there was a fallen noblewoman of the Yan family living in Guangling, who was known for her beauty. The chancellor wanted to take her as a concubine, and Wu Xiang forcibly betrothed her. This angered him greatly. Taking advantage of the lavish dowry during the wedding, he fabricated charges and investigated, finding that Wu Xiang had additional decorative items beyond his salary. He was charged with embezzlement and executed, supposedly as punishment for disrespect. When Emperor Xuanzong was still a commoner, he fully understood Wu Xiang's injustice. After ascending the throne, those who were not in harmony with the two Lis led them to file a petition for redress. Li Deyu was exiled to Zhu Ya for this. Passing through Lishui, he told the officials residing in the capital, "Li Twenty wronged me." Ma Zhi, who was once feared by Li Deyu, was sent out as an external official. The matter of Wu Xiang was investigated by the Censorate, and when Fuwu was serving as the Censor-in-Chief, he was able to carry out his intentions. Wu Xiang was from Lishui, and the woman from the Yan family returned to Lishui, dying a widow. The old saying that Zhe Dong is difficult to govern holds true. Li Shen understood five or six parts of public affairs, while Liu Hanhong understood seven parts. Others understood three or four parts. Because his talent was rare.

The Story of Pei Xiang Gong's Birth in the Kingdom of Yutian [with Master Shuangfeng, Daoist Wen Ruhai] During the Tang Dynasty, Pei Xiang Gong devoted his attention to Buddhism and was proficient in Zen and monastic rules. He wrote the preface for the "Chán Quán" (Zen Commentary on the Dharmadhātu) by Master Mi of Gui Peak, who attained the direct transmission of Dharma from Bodhidharma. Pei Xiang Gong often wore a robe made of coarse wool and held a begging bowl, begging for alms in a place where singers and courtesans performed. He claimed that he was not tainted by worldly desires and could teach the Dharma to others. He often made vows to be a king in future lives and to promote and protect Buddhism. Later, a son was born to the king of Yutian, and the boy had the name of Pei Xiang Gong written on his hands. This news reached the imperial court, and his descendants wanted to bring him back to China, but the king of Yutian refused. Master Shuangfeng gathered a thousand disciples, and the depth of his discussions on metaphysics was unparalleled. However, he became entangled with a common woman and thus lost his way, confirming that lust is indeed a great punishment. Initially, Li Gong Wei had a good relationship with the master and only appointed him as a minor official. Daoist Wen Ruhai wrote extensive commentaries on the *Zhuangzi*, earnestly requesting a minor official position, just like Tang Huixiu and Liao Guangxuan, whose aspirations and status were equally humble. Alas! The Story of Wei Shi's Daughter Marrying Liu Qian Chancellor Wei Gong Zhou was stationed in the South Sea and had a subordinate named Liu Qian, who was of low rank but had a distinguished appearance. Wei Gong Zhou married his daughter to Liu Qian as a concubine. His wife, fearing that this would bring criticism, advised against it, but Wei Gong Zhou said, "This man is not an ordinary person. In the future, my descendants may rely on him." Liu Qian was later promoted to the position of governor of Fengzhou due to his military achievements. Wei Gong Zhou's wife gave birth to two sons, Liu Fang and Liu Yan. Liu Fang became the governor of Guangzhou, and Liu Yan succeeded him, ruling over the four prefectures of the Lingnan region. They established the Kingdom of Han, changed their name to Fang, and reigned for 24 years before Liu Fang's younger son succeeded him. The governor of Jingzhao's ability to recognize talent was indeed accurate.

**Translation:** **The Biography of Tian Bu, Minister** When the Duke of Wei, Cui Wei, the Prime Minister of the Tang Dynasty, was stationed in Huaiyang, Lu Dan, the Chancellor, and Zhang Duo, the Langzhong, both of whom had been dismissed from their posts in Zhe West and Changzhou respectively, passed through Huaiyang to pay homage to the Duke. On a leisurely day, the Duke privately entertained the two guests while playing chess. At this time, a woman carrying a report stated that a female sorceress had arrived with Minister Tian Bu and was staying at an inn near a certain pavilion. The Duke was greatly surprised by the sorceress's arrival, as it was unusual. Soon after, the sorceress said that her manifestation was different from that of other sorceresses and requested to be moved to the Duke's residence. The Duke then hurriedly summoned the sorceress, who met with the spirit and bowed, saying, "Thank you, Minister." The Duke asked, "Why do you thank me?" The spirit replied, "Tian has an unworthy son who is corrupt and insatiable, causing the affairs of the prefecture to be in disarray. He should be punished with death, but thanks to your hidden virtue, he has been spared. It is your grace that allows the ancestral temple of Tian's family to continue receiving offerings." The Duke was startled and said, "Strange indeed! While I have been a minister, I have never used confidential matters to benefit my family." One day, the military governor of Xia Prefecture reported that Tian Kuang, the governor of Yin Prefecture, had committed embezzlement and privately forged armor to exchange for border horses and cloth. The emperor was furious and said, "Embezzlement is a separate matter, but he was entrusted with a border prefecture where he should have been guarding against theft. Using armor to aid the enemy is tantamount to rebellion. I have ordered the chancellor to punish him according to the law, which may even lead to the extermination of his entire clan." The next day, the Duke calmly said to the emperor, "Tian Kuang's embezzlement is already subject to the law, but he is the grandson of Tian Hongzheng and the son of Tian Bu. Tian Hongzheng was the first to request to pay homage to the emperor from the region north of the Yellow River and served as an official. Tian Bu also followed his father's example and was later summoned to fight in Huai West, where he died with loyalty and filial piety. If we now punish him according to the law to strengthen the borders, it would be better to show leniency and encourage loyalty and valor."
