The emperor's anger subsided, and he only demoted Tian Kuang to be a magistrate in a distant prefecture, without any intervention from the Duke's relatives or close friends.

Having almost forgotten it, now the spirit's words are indeed the truth of the matter, he then ordered it to be inscribed on a tablet and displayed. The official said to the spirit, "You died for righteousness and valor, how can you be so influenced by a foolish woman?" The spirit replied in a mournful tone, "I often owe this old woman eighty thousand coins, and now I am enduring shame to repay her; it is just an old debt." The official, along with two guests and the military governor's staff, jointly repaid the remaining amount, paying it on their behalf. He claimed that the matter was not verified, and the spirit then departed. The chancellor Li Gong Qi of the Liang dynasty recorded this matter and said, "Alas, a remarkable man, dying for the debt owed to a woman is already like this; how much more so for a great national debt? Stealing the ruler's salary without reporting, stealing the ruler's authority and being disloyal, is it appropriate? And Chancellor Cui served as a general and prime minister for nearly thirty years, it is indeed appropriate." --- **The Request to Repair the Temple of Duke Liang of Di** Li Deyu, the Grand Vizier, had not yet left the academy when he was renowned for his eloquence and literary talent but was not fond of taking the imperial examinations. Ji Fu, the chancellor, urged his relatives to encourage him, and Zhang Wu said, "Enjoying fine horses and not entering the ranks of officials, one can still advance in rank through merit." Ji Fu and Wu Yuanheng, the chancellor, were colleagues, and their affairs often did not align. Whenever they retired from court, Ji Fu's demeanor was displeased. Zhang Wu then said, "What is difficult about this? Why not ask to repair the Temple of Duke Liang of Di?" Thus, Wu Yuanheng gradually sought to leave the capital and take up a military governorship, and his plans were known early on. I once read the Grand Vizier's record of his three court submissions and rejections, which truly shows his remarkable talent, yet he ultimately suffered from factional strife, a result of his exceptional qualities.

### Translation **Princess Tongchang's Story** Emperor Xuanzong hoped for a long life without an heir, and many high-ranking officials were disobedient or rebellious. Emperor Yizong, while living in his princely mansion, often felt fear or anxiety. Later, a concubine named Guo gave birth to a daughter who died within a month, just as Emperor Yizong was in a state of sorrow. The imperial princess, who had survived, was treasured by Emperor Yizong after he ascended the throne. She was bestowed the title of Princess Tongchang and received incomparable favor from Wei Baocheng. However, due to her illness, the medicinal treatments were ineffective, and she died. Several families of medical officials, including Han Zongzhao, Kang Shoushang, and others, were all exterminated. Chancellor Liu submitted a petition to admonish Emperor Yizong, but he did not listen. Emperor Yizong once visited the left army and saw a Bodhisattva Guanyin statue that sank into the ground four feet. He asked those around him, and they replied that he was the emperor of China, while the Bodhisattva was a monk from the borderlands. The emperor was pleased. When bandits invaded the capital, Concubine Guo did not manage to rush to the imperial residence and had to beg for food in the capital city, and people at the time mourned her. [The lavish life of Princess Tongchang is described in Su E's *Du Yang Za Bian*] **Memorial by Hou Changye** After the Guangming era, eunuchs gained power, setting up positions for the north and south, and the military commander Tian Lingzi had the power to reverse the heavens. The court was filled with fear, and while Wang Xianzhi and Huang Chao were plundering the Jianghuai region, the court was deeply concerned. Left Censor Hou Changye submitted a memorial, strongly criticizing the current situation, but it was not presented to the emperor. He was ordered to be executed within the palace. Later, it was said that the content of Hou Changye's memorial was inappropriate, particularly the phrase "please open the Dati Daochang to dissipate the military threat," which seemed to be written by a mediocre monk. If Hou Changye had made such a statement, it was fitting that he should have been punished.

Li Changshi's Encounter with Daoist Arts Li Fu, the son of Li Changshi, the Grand Attendant from Longxi, was known for his simple and tranquil nature. During the Guanghua period, he fled to Liangchuan with other officials to escape the turmoil. Known as "Xiaodie," he often visited a neighboring temple to relieve his worries. There, he met a monk named Shuang Gong, who, due to their karmic connection, became acquainted with Li Fu. Every morning, the monk would leave the temple, either to attend a meal or to practice his spiritual duties. If Li Fu was in the temple, the monk would not lock his room, allowing Li Fu to stay and rest. Over time, their friendship deepened, and the monk said, "How could I, being in the temple, close the door on Li Changshi?" One day, the monk calmly asked Li Fu, "The world speaks of the 'yellow-white arts.' Do you believe in them? Do you desire them?" Li Fu replied, "Though I have never paid much attention, how could I doubt their existence? Yet, how could I dare to desire them?" The monk, who was known as "Pendao," explained that he left the temple every dawn to cultivate virtue, and his respect for Li Changshi's virtue made him unwilling to keep such knowledge secret. Despite Li Fu's repeated polite refusals, the monk eventually shared his Daoist arts with him. Afterward, Li Fu was most favored by Prince Sanfeng Hu and Chancellor Cui. Whenever these two officials celebrated their birthdays, Li Fu would present them with silver medicinal vessels. Despite his family's frequent financial struggles, Li Fu never indulged himself and never passed on these Daoist arts to his descendants. Li Qu, the Grand Administrator and Li Fu's direct descendant, once told me about this story. Master Du Guangting of Guangcheng often said, "There is no one who has not cultivated Dao and yet hoped to obtain Daoist arts. If one obtains them, disaster will surely follow. Only those renowned for their integrity and purity often attain them." This is precisely what Li Fu exemplified.

**Translation:** **Lu Xiang's Experience with Drinking [With Zhu Xiucai, the Drunken Scholar, and Liu Xubai of Donggao Attached]** When Lu Xiang was serving as an official in Yiling, a scholar came to pay respects to the chancellor. They conversed in a relaxed manner, and Lu Xiang then ordered wine and urged the scholar to drink. The scholar replied, "By nature, I do not drink alcohol." Lu Xiang said, "If indeed as you say, then you have already reduced your weaknesses by half." He meant that if one has a lifelong aversion to drinking, it is as if one has ten parts of regret, and being troubled by alcohol would naturally reduce it by half. Zhu Xiucai, from Suining, passed the imperial examinations. He loved drinking and wrote a poem that was considered the best, especially his *Farewell to Yang Guifei from the Bright Emperor*. However, he was also known for his drunkenness. When he failed the examination, he went to pay respects to Gong Xian, the governor of Longzhou, who was a general from Shu. Gong Xian, who was also fond of drinking, had recently cast a new sword and said, "How can I find someone to test it?" Zhu Xiucai, being a fellow villager, was invited to try it. Zhu Xiucai stretched his neck, and soon his head and body were separated. Alas, he died in a place not befitting his talents. This was indeed a playful remark by Lu Xiang, but it was also wise words. **Liu Xubai of Donggao, whose name was Wang Ji, with the courtesy name Wugong, wrote a *Memorial to the Du Kang Temple* and a *Record of the Drunken Land*, both of which elaborately praised the virtues of wine. Liu Xubai, from Jingling, passed the imperial examinations and loved drinking. He wrote a poem that said, "Knowing that in the land of drunkenness there is no tax on households, let him indulge in it and neglect his lower dantian." Those who love drinking, if they are followers of Confucius, would not go against the warnings?" **The Story of Pei and Zheng Supporting the Prince** During the reign of Emperor Xizong of Tang, when he visited Liangyang again, Zhu Mei supported the prince. Chancellors Xiao Guo, Pei Che, and Zheng Kuangtu, among others, supported him as well. When the false ruler was defeated and Emperor Xizong was restored, Pei and Zheng, among others, all suffered the death penalty. Initially equipped with guards, surrounded by spears and halberds, Chancellor Pei still joked, "The emperor's defenses are formidable, even though they may seem weak or ineffective." Chancellor Xiao Guo was given poison in Hezhong, held in hand, hoping for a reduced punishment due to the emperor's past favor. The poison caused his hand to rot, and he eventually drank it and died.

田军容檄韦太尉 唐太尉韦昭度出身于显赫世家，名位非同小可，然而沙门僧澈因其承蒙皇恩而暗中结交宫廷。京兆尹与几位当时宰相皆因他而被提拔。悟达国师知元是僧澈的师父，曾被诸宰相轻视，每当在西川行宫谒见悟达国师时，他们皆行大礼。国师以礼相待，并邀请僧澈共饮。后来，田军容掌管军事事务，讨伐成都，他发布了一封檄文说：“太尉相国韦昭度，近日因和尚僧澈才得以显达。他在中书省开设官职买卖，在翰林院则借他人之笔。此即所指也。” 朱李骤进 唐李师望是皇族成员，自负才华，欲以一方为己任。他游历邛蜀，了解南蛮的勇怯。于是上书请求分割西川数州，在临邛郡建立定边军节度使。诏书批准了他的请求，他从凤翔少尹被提拔担任此职。当时西川大将嫉妒他分割自己的辖区，于是暗中与南诏通信。因此，蛮军在附近乡绅的引导下侵犯了蜀川。元戎窦滂未能阻止，李师望不久也被贬谪。陇西（又说是因为在华阳捕贼）。光化年间，朱朴从毛诗博士升为宰相，依仗其口才，认为可以迅速实现太平，由藩邸推荐而闻名。

Emperor Zhaozong was appointed to this position and, on the day of his appointment, he personally presented several matters of state. Each time he said that he would bring it to the Emperor. When he took on great power, he could not implement his plans. From then on, the emperor's favor gradually diminished, and there was turmoil both inside and outside the court. The eunuchs said that the performer Mu Dao Ling was reciting scriptures. When he reached the emperor's presence, he said, "If Zhu is a prime minister, then he is not a prime minister." The next day, when he was dismissed from office, people said, "It has always been possible for a scholar to be made prime minister." A gentleman does not despise words that are not spoken, but despises actions that fall short. Especially in the late Tang, when the country was in turmoil and everyone was armed, even with extraordinary talent, one could not plan effectively. Zhu Gong, a mere scholar, tried to restore order with his limited eloquence and only brought himself disgrace. Before he could fully express his ideas, he was criticized by knowledgeable people for having his young attendants play the hululu. The Frivolous Affairs of Li Qunyu [with references to Wei Hang and Li Qu] Li Qunyu, a scholar from Lizhou, had a reputation for poetry and was not fond of taking the imperial examinations. His friends and relatives forced him to take it once. He was known to the chancellor of Hedong, Pei Gongxiu, who promoted his reputation. He presented poems and was appointed as a scholar in the Hall of Imperial Literature, and he died in Jing and Xiang regions. However, he often associated with drunkards, and it was suspected that he was like Zhang Hu. Li Shao, who was exiled to Lizyang, knew all about his behavior. He recorded it, saying that he was a person of high moral integrity and not a reckless one. It was suspected that he was slandered by his peers, or it was said that he had served as an officer in Jing and had borrowed books to present to the magistrate of Lizhou, Ai Shi. Li said to Ai, "I am in dire straits."

May the governor kindly rescue it, the governor should admonish for its recklessness, the benefit is not great. In recent years, Wei Hang from Jingzhao, a member of an old, prestigious family, also studied classical literature but was unsuccessful and died without achieving much. Li Qu from Longxi, a great-grandson of someone with a good fortune, often served as the magistrate of Xiangzhou Xiangle County, and people from Jingzhao frequently disturbed him. Li Qu would always serve them with respect and patience. One day, a guest said to Li Qu, "There is someone who encourages you to seek a position to save yourself from poverty and hunger." His wife heard this and became very angry, saying, "If they call you 'Lord,' then they can call me 'Lady.' That is unacceptable." Li Qu heard this and felt both amused and disdainful. Using lyrics for entertainment [with references to Wei Zhuang and He Ning]. Before this, Li Yuan once wrote a poem: "Human affairs are settled over three cups of wine, and a lifetime is played out in one game of chess." Emperor Xuanzong of Tang believed that he lacked the talent to govern and did not discuss it with the prefects or chancellors. However, he eventually agreed. When Wei Zhuang took the imperial exams, he encountered a bandit attack and wrote "Qin Woman's Lament," which includes the lines, "The inner treasury burned to ashes, and the bones of the nobles were trodden underfoot on the imperial street." After this, many nobles were surprised and criticized him. Wei Zhuang then avoided mentioning it, and people called him "Qin Woman's Lament Scholar." Later, in his family precepts, he forbade hanging screens with "Qin Woman's Lament" on them, but this was too late to stop the criticism. He Ning, when young, enjoyed writing lyrics and distributed them in Bian and Luo. When he became chancellor, he entrusted others to collect and burn them. However, the chancellor was heavy and virtuous, and he was ultimately tainted by his love for elegant poetry. When the Khitans entered Yimen, he was called "Lyric Chancellor." The saying goes, "Good deeds don't spread far, but bad deeds travel a thousand miles." Scholars and gentlemen should be cautious about this [another version says, "Long days are spent playing one game of chess, both versions are kept"].

In the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the administration of state affairs was entrusted to Prime Minister Linghu Gong, and the relationship between the ruler and minister was harmonious, with no gap between them. Dong Shiren, a court official, frequently criticized Linghu Gong's shortcomings and submitted secret reports to the emperor, who kept the reports and only instructed the Prime Minister about the matters without mentioning the names of the individuals involved. During this period, the son of the Chancellor was selected for an examination, but the process was loosely handled, and it was said that he was called 'the unsolvable jinshi' (a degree candidate), though in reality, he had something to be heard about. He also submitted a report about his sons and nephews accepting bribes, stating that they were seen taking gold but not people in broad daylight. The Chancellor resented Dong Shiren and appointed a man to be his secretary, treating him with great care. The purple robe (a symbol of high office) was entrusted to him as a trusted advisor, and the Chancellor had no doubts about him. The official who was supposed to be a model of integrity received a bribe of ten thousand coins, and this was reported by the secretary, leading to his demotion. The gentleman said, 'The Duke of Pengcheng intends to discipline others but must first cleanse himself. How can he criticize others for corruption when he himself is corrupt? It is appropriate that he did not reach a high position.' Before this, Linghu, the Chancellor, being of a single lineage, often tried to expand his clan to rival the powerful families of Cui and Lu. He often introduced wealthy families into the imperial register, and those who could not obtain official positions wanted to submit petitions to change their surname to Linghu. At the time, people criticized him for this.

Lu Guimeng, posthumously honored as Lu Wàng, was of an old and distinguished family. His father, Bīn Yú, passed the highest imperial examination and served as an imperial censor in Zhejiang East. Lu Guimeng lived in Suzhou, where he dedicated himself to the Six Classics and began studying literature at the age of twenty. He formed close friendships with scholars such as Yan Rao, Pi Rihou, Luo Yin, and Wu Rong. Known for his high moral integrity, he was poor at home and thought about earning a salary to support his parents. He served as deputy governor of Wu and Lujiang prefectures with Zhang Bo and compiled works such as *Fuxing Shilu* (40 volumes), *Songling Ji* (10 volumes), and *Lizhe Congshu* (5 volumes). Chancellor Li Wei and Grand Tutor Lu Xie highly respected him. Luo the Censor sent a poem to Lu Guimeng, which read, "The Dragon Pavilion Chancellor once admired your lofty writings; now the Yellow Gate has no master, and the green mountains remain unburned." This indicates that he had been considered for a position of honor. At the end of the Tang dynasty, he was appointed as Left Censor by imperial decree.

On that day, Wu Rong, the Right Censor, died suddenly. In the third year of Guanghua, he was posthumously granted the title of Right Censor. Wei Zhuang composed an epitaph for him, and Lu Xisheng wrote an epitaph. Yan Rao wrote the inscription on his tombstone. Pi Rihou, the Doctor, composed a poem. When Pi Rihou died in Zhejiang, Fang Gan's poetry was famous in Wu region, but Lu Xisheng did not approve of it. One day, he suddenly composed fifty poems, arranged as new works by Fang Gan. Contemporaries praised and admired them. Lu Xisheng said, "These are the works of the underling imitating Fang Gan." Fang Gan's poems were in the model, and those with a keen eye and discernment also agreed. Shi Ji, the Minister of Personnel, was able to take poetry as his own responsibility. He returned Liu Deren's scroll with a poem that said, "A hundred poems are like one; the beginning is like the end." He criticized Liu for not being able to change his style, which was similar to Lu Xisheng. Yan Jifu's Tombstone Inscription Yan Jifu Rao was exiled and died outside Lake. He once wrote his own tombstone inscription. He was impatient and could not tolerate others. His inscription said, "He resided in the East Wu region and had a friendship with Lu Guimeng of Wu Prefecture based on poetry and discourse. A decade without exceeding. When Lu Guimeng died, he helped him to the grave, and emotional and ceremonial respect remained intact. After that, the former Adviser to the Imperial Council, Gao Gong, and the former Chancellor, Lu Gong Yi, two gentlemen, remained unchanged with Rao until his death. The rest of his face-to-face friends were like hands being held while passing through the market, and upon seeing profit, they would release their hands and go their separate ways. Nobody understands me. There are also the Minister of Personnel, Shi Ji, the Deputy Minister of War, Yu Jing, and the Secretary of the Central Secretariat, Zheng Gong, who wrote it. These three gentlemen have not changed since today, but I do not know what will happen in the future when I see my lonely and unprotected family."

Biography of Li Qi [with Liang Buqu Attached] Sikong Tu, the Attendant in the Secretariat, composed the biography of Li Qi, noting that he possessed exceptional talent which was envied by his contemporaries, leading to unjust misfortune. Throughout his life, he wrote extensively, producing a collection of writings that distilled the essence of various schools of thought, a thirty-volume anthology, a five-volume work on social ranks and classifications, a three-volume work on the essence of the *I Ching*, a commentary on the *Analects*, and a two-part work on the concept of "wu wei," along with a single work on the interpretation of principles. In a moment of crisis, his works were destroyed in a fire, and as a result, they are no longer accessible to the public. Alas! "Yang Chun Bai Xue" (highbrow art) is appreciated by only a few; is this not a true statement? Bao Guangzi said: The writings of Tang Dynasty figures such as Han Yu, Liu Zongyuan, Li Ao, Li Guan, and Huangfu Shi surpassed Xunzi and Mencius, and were superior to the works of Yan and Xie. The only figure they revered was Liang Buqu, who served as their mirror. Yet Liang's reputation was silent and unacknowledged, was he not of the same caliber as those who created "Yang Chun Bai Xue"? Thus, one should be wary of the empty praise bestowed upon popular figures. Tombstone Inscription for Bai Juyi [with Lu and Zheng Attached] Bai Juyi, the Attendant in the Secretariat, was close friends with Yuan Zhen, and both were famous for their poetry, earning them the nickname "Yuan Bai." In his collection, there is a poem mourning Yuan Zhen, in which he writes: "We both shed tears, unable to speak, and I wonder about the sorrowful events that have transpired since our parting. I imagine the trees on the original plains of Xianyang have already grown three zhang tall, with white willow branches." In his own tombstone inscription, Bai Juyi mentions his friendship with Liu Yuxi of Pengcheng but does not mention Yuan Zhen, leading people to suspect that there was a rift between them. Zheng Wengong Tian and Lu Xiang were related by family ties, sharing similar family backgrounds and literary accomplishments. They served together in the central government, but due to a disagreement over official matters, they engaged in a verbal altercation, accidentally spilling ink from an inkstone. It was said that this was a conflict between prime ministers, but it was not serious enough to warrant such an accusation. Ultimately, one of them was dismissed from office as a result.

Eunuchs Reforming Their Duties [With Dou the Censor Attached] In ancient times, there were many eunuchs who usurped power and ruled autocratically, yet some also exhibited loyalty and filial piety, which are recorded in historical annals. Since the An Lushan Rebellion, military disasters repeatedly occurred, forcing the emperor to flee. The command of the emperor's personal guard was entrusted to powerful eunuchs such as Yu Chao'en and Dou Wentang, who were the most prominent among them. Later, positions such as the Left Army, Twelve Guards, Military Inspectorate, Chancellery, and Palace Secretariat were established, comparable to the four chancellors. Sixteen palace officials, all eunuchs, were appointed to perform the duties of the ministries, acting as formal members of the bureaucracy. Officials with purple belts, who served in the imperial court as military commanders, were under the command of Yang Fugong, who allowed them to announce or guide using ceremonial robes and tablets, a role that was changed from the original. Yan Zunmei was the best eunuch for internal court affairs. He once commanded the military and retired at the end of the Tang Dynasty, living in Shu Prefecture as a common old man. Occasionally, he was approached or befriended. His son served in Shu as a Gatekeeper, and once, a monk presented him with a purple robe. When the monk came to thank him, the words of thanks recorded in the official documents were unclear due to the heat and sweat. He bowed and said nothing about the military commander, slapping his palm and looking at it for a long time. He said, "Ugly and of low status, I am unable to do anything." The eunuch replied, "Not dare." He laughed loudly after stepping back. After Yan's death, the Shu court bestowed posthumous honors upon him, appointing him as a Censor. Dou Yong refused to accept the posthumous appointment, even during the era of divided rule, he did not compromise. Many scholars admired him.

The Rise and Fall of Luo and Gu [with Fang Gan appended] During the Tang dynasty, Luo Geshizhong and Gu Boshi were both favored by Chancellor Liang Hui. Although Gu was the son of a salt merchant, he possessed a refined and dignified demeanor. Luo, also from Qiantang, had a strange accent. Whenever the chancellor's children hosted banquets, Gu alone could engage in graceful and harmonious conversation, making it hard to distinguish him as a person of humble origin. Gu's literary works were highly regarded in his time, and he was eager for fame. He once submitted a request to his patrons, hoping to rank just above the lowest candidate in the civil service examination. [Liang Hui mentioned this to Xue Shi, who passed on the story to Liang Gong. I heard it from Liang Gong.] Luo, however, frequently faced setbacks and grew resentful. Eventually, he was ostracized by the aristocratic youth and, after a falling out, returned eastward. After the Yellow Turban Rebellion was quelled, court officials discussed recalling Luo, but Wei Yifan opposed it, saying, "I once shared a boat with him, though we were not acquainted. The boatman told me, 'There is a government official on board.' I asked, 'What kind of official?' Luo replied, 'What kind of official? I can hold my pen between my feet and match several of you.' If he were to pass the examination and enter officialdom, we would be mere chaff." Thus, Luo was not recalled. The poet Fang Gan was also from Wu. Wang Gui, a high-ranking official, valued his talent and invited him into his inner chambers. Fang bowed twice in succession, and the vice-premier responded calmly. Before Fang could rise, he offered another bow. At the time, he was nicknamed "Fang Sanbai" (Fang Three Bows).

Li Qiqiang, the chancellor of the Later Liang dynasty, gained fame through his literary talent and served as a censor during the late Tang dynasty. During the reign of Emperor Zhaozong, when the imperial court was relocated and the literati dispersed, he and Yang Bin from Hongnong also sought refuge in the regions of Jing and Chu. Yang Bin later became the ruler of Shu. Li Qiqiang lingered in Qingjiang of Yidao, concealing his identity and calling himself Li Changguan of Huayuan. His elder brother Guangfu served as the governor of Yidu and often despised him, leaving Li Qiqiang feeling lonely. He often sat by the water, picking leaves and trying to draft imperial edicts, exclaiming with disappointment and frustration before throwing them into the water. When Zhu Wen, the founder of the Later Liang dynasty, accepted the imperial throne, Li Qiqiang was summoned to serve as a翰林学士 and soon entered the imperial court. Later, Yidu's son Bin, a traveler in Zhubu, visited the chancellor and counted the shortcomings of his father, eventually sending him away.

Du Xunhe entered the Hanlin Academy [with Ping Zeng and Jia Dao attached]. During the Tang Dynasty, Du Xunhe once traveled to Li Xian, the first emperor of the Later Liang Dynasty, and presented him with thirty poems, all of which were easy to understand. Because of this, Li Xian treated him generously. Upon receiving the throne, Du Xunhe was appointed as a翰林学士 and died five days later. Li Yiji, the military governor of Zhuang, promoted talented individuals from humble backgrounds, often using his own sons for the role of掌诰. He said, "They are knowledgeable about historical stories and can be useful in emergencies." For example, someone like Jingzhao could write a single poem or essay that would be considered a national masterpiece, which was beyond the capabilities of others. Fortunately, Du Xunhe passed away, sparing him from bringing shame to Li Yiji. Ping Zeng and Jia Dao were demoted for their obscure and difficult talents, which were deemed useless, and this was typical of the situation. The musician Guan Xiaohong [with Shi Cong attached]. During the reign of Emperor Zhaozong of Tang, when the court officials were forcibly relocated, many famous courtesans and musicians fell into the hands of powerful feudal lords. Guan Xiaohong, a female musician known for playing the pipa, was called "Guan Biejia" and was a nickname. Zhu Wen, the founder of the Later Liang Dynasty, sought her out. When she arrived, he asked, "Can you play 'Yangxia Saisang'?" Guan Xiaohong bowed and played it. When she left, he ordered those close to him to make her play and serve wine. As a result, she lost her favor and soon died. There was also a pipa player named Shi Cong, known as "Shi Sima," who claimed that he had been praised by Linghu Chu, the chancellor of the Tang Dynasty, and was allowed to perform with his sons, including Huanshen and Lian. After the chaos, he entered Shu and was not listed in the music registry. He was often invited by high-ranking officials and treated as a guest. One day, several military officers were drinking, and Shi Cong played the huqin, dominating the scene. Those present, who were not playing different instruments, were noisy and laughing, paying no attention to him. Shi Cong then struck the instrument and cursed, saying, "I was once a court official serving as a chancellor. Today, I am playing with these soldiers and am not being listened to. How bitter is this!" At the time, those who recognized him also expressed their surprise. Since the chaos, the social order has been inverted, and there are countless unfortunate events. Is it only the lowly musicians who suffer such misfortunes?

**孙内子【萧惟香附】** The wife of Meng Changqi, a scholar from the Tang dynasty, was skilled in composing poetry. One day, she burned all her collections, thinking that poetic talent was not a woman's business. From then on, she dedicated herself solely to fulfilling her role as a wife and managing the household. She wrote a poem about a candle, which read: "The light surpasses silver candle and the fragrance compares to orchids. A piece of white jade that makes one feel cold. On a spring night in the imperial palace, carefully read the heavenly script after being drunk." She also wrote a poem about a zither, which read: "The fingers of jade pluck the strings with a clear sound. The sad and resentful feelings of Xiangfei are the hardest to listen to. At first, it sounds like a cool breeze blowing. It seems like a drizzling evening rain. It sounds like a stream flowing from a green mountain. It sounds like a stream flowing down a blue cliff from afar. After playing at night, one feels melancholic. The dew moistens the clusters of orchids, and the moon fills the courtyard." She wrote another poem about receiving clear wine, which read: "Thank you for sending clear wine to a sorrowful person. Clear, sweet, and fragrant, the scent is true. It is best on a moonlit night in a green window. A cup of wine stirs up the spring in one's heart." In the village of Pan Nian in Taizhou, there was a woman named Xiao Weixiang, who had talent and creativity. Before marriage, she often conversed with the scholar Wang Yuanyan through the window. She ran to Langya and indulged in unrestrained behavior. Wang left her in an inn and went away. She secretly met travelers and, having nowhere to turn, hanged herself. There were hundreds of poems in the inn. It is indeed true that poetic talent is not a woman's business. This story was heard from Liu Shanfu.

Random Notes of the Northern Dream, Volume Six Imperially Sanctioned Complete Library Random Notes of the Northern Dream, Volume Seven Compiled by Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty Meng Haoran and Zhao Guo Lost Their Way in Poetry During the Tang Dynasty, Meng Haoran of Xiangyang was a close friend of Li Bai. When Emperor Xuanzong summoned Li Bai to join the Hanlin Academy, Meng Haoran, hoping to be recommended by his friend, waited for news in the capital for a long time. One day, Emperor Xuanzong summoned Li Bai for a private audience. Li Bai took the opportunity to mention Meng Haoran, saying, "Your Majesty, I have a good friend, Meng Haoran, who is currently staying at my residence. I humbly request that he be summoned to present his poetry." The emperor agreed and summoned Meng Haoran to present his verses. Meng Haoran recited his poem: "Stop submitting petitions at the northern gate, Return to my humble home in the south. An unworthy person is abandoned by a wise ruler, Due to illness, old friends have become distant." The emperor was displeased and said, "I have never seen Meng Haoran present his poetry to the court. Why did he not say something like 'The mist rises over the Yunmeng Marsh, the waves shake the walls of Yueyang City'? Because of this, he did not receive any favor, and he remained a commoner until the end." Later, Emperor Xuanzong requested Zhao Guo's poetry. The first poem in Zhao Guo's collection was titled "On Qin Shi Huang" and contained the lines: "Only knowing that the six states fell to the axe, No scholars were able to determine right from wrong." The emperor was displeased with this. [Some say that the passage refers to Meng Jiao and Wang Wei in the Hanlin Academy, and both interpretations are preserved here.]

Vang Qi's Poem as Chancellor [with Li Cheng attached] Although Vang Qi, the chancellor of the Tang Dynasty, had a reputation as a poet, he had no aspirations for high office. He often served as the governor of Lu Prefecture, where he was a low-ranking official under Yang Xingmi, the Wu King. Because of an oversight, he was punished with a beating. However, he was valued for his scholarly, mild-mannered, and cautious nature. During the reign of Emperor Zhaozong, when Wu Xiong controlled the Huaihai region, the court indulged in leniency and spoke highly of Vang Qi's virtues, leading to his appointment, which shocked both inside and outside the court. At the time, the imperial order had been disrupted, and there were many troubles throughout the land. The chancellor had no room to act and was indecisive in his decisions. When the troops from Taiyuan arrived near the Wei River, the emperor was terrified and eager for a way to repel the enemy. The chancellor proposed adding the character "哲" (meaning "wise") to Confucius' posthumous name. His failure to address the current problems was typical of such situations. His colleagues, who felt he held his position undeservedly, often mocked him. The chancellor then wrote a poem on the wall of the Secretariat. The poem read: "Side slopes, tiny worms, Kunlun ants, all competing to drag; one day, white rain falls, no dull, no weak, it seems the times are declining."

Even with talent and intelligence, one cannot achieve prosperity or rescue; there must be concern for the possibility of both good and bad being destroyed together. The chancellor agreed and commented on the poem of the old monk: "The sun illuminates the snow on the western mountains, the old monk's door is not open. A frozen bottle sticks to the pillar base, the ashes of the overnight fire remain. The child is sick and returns home, a young deer enters in the cold." It is often said that this poem's couplets can weigh heavy and light equally, without bias. Someone asked the chancellor if he had recently written new poetry. The chancellor replied, "My poetic inspiration comes from a snowy bridge while riding a donkey. How did I come to this insight? This is what I say about my lifelong effort." Li Cheng was promoted for his essay on the five colors of the sun and became the governor of Henan. He tested candidates daily, and among them was Hao, whose scroll contained the poem on the five colors of the sun. The chancellor was greatly surprised, fearing that his own excellence would be overshadowed. Upon further examination, he admired his talent and beauty. In the final lines of the poem, it invades the late water with a sharp movement, looking down at the cold mountain and revealing its beauty. The chancellor laughed loudly and said, "Li Cheng's poem is already in the auspicious sun; why is its beauty revealed at night?" Therefore, Hao's poem could not surpass it.

**Translation:** **Lai Peng's Poetry [with Shen Guang and Yang Jingzhi Attached]** Lai Peng, a Tang Dynasty Jinshi (imperial scholar), was known for his clear and beautiful poetic thoughts. The Fujian Shangshu (minister) Wei Xiu loved his talent and once intended to marry his daughter to him, but the marriage did not materialize. Later, Lai Peng traveled to Shu, where in his summer exam scrolls, he wrote a poem that read, "A night wind cuts through the green lotus leaves, tricking the autumn rain into not forming pearls." Those who understood it saw this as an omen of misfortune. That year, he did not participate in the autumn exams and died at the rank of Tongyi Lang (a junior official). Shen Guang, a Jinshi, wrote the "Dongting Lake Ode." Wei Baizuo Xiu, a high-ranking official, told the court scholars, "This ode is a perfect piece of music and poetry." He was later appointed as an official in Fujian. Hongnong Yang Jingzhi wrote the "Mount Hua Ode," which General Li of Zhuya often placed on his desk, reading it while sitting or standing. The ode roughly reads: It seems as if a thousand acres of land are within arm's reach, As if a city with a thousand雉 walls is within a small enclosure, As if a hundred-mile lake is contained in a cup of water, As if a nine-story building is as small as an ant's mound. Flies move back and forth between the east and west of Zhou, Tiny insects are in a hurry, and the Qin state has passed, Honeycombs are connected, forming the Afang Palace, Soon it was rebuilt as the Jianzhang Palace, The small stars are bright, burning down Xianyang, Countless cocoons and chestnuts are hidden by the first emperor. After ten years, changes and prosperity, carrying sorrow and hardship, follow it. [Yang's family is a prominent clan in Huayin, and this ode is intended to express the grandeur of Mount Hua and the true talents of the world.] **Li Xueshi's Prophecy [with Liu Changmei and Gou Wei Attached]**

During the reign of Emperor Xizong in the Tang Dynasty, Liu Changmei governed a state or region. At this time, the summer floods in the Xialao Gorge were dangerous due to rapid currents. The local saying went that if the Yanyu (a rock) appeared as large as a horse, the Qutang Gorge was too dangerous to navigate. As a result, travelers stopped rowing and waited for the water level to drop before continuing their journey. There was a scholar-official from the imperial court, Li Rao, who was traveling with his family from Sichuan to Jiangling. The water conditions were extremely bad, and he hoped to stay a little longer to find a safer crossing. However, he was from Longxi and was in a hurry, nearly getting into trouble. He was summoned by someone and firmly requested to proceed eastward. Despite his attempts to stop him, he set off, rowing and bowing respectfully as he departed. The state official watched him leave, and as he did, the swirling, turbulent waters split open, breaking the boat and causing the entire family of Li to drown. Only one woman (likely a servant or attendant) was washed ashore and survived. Before this, a kitchen worker from Yongan named Chen Xiaonu was rowing an empty boat down the Qutang Gorge. He saw a person dressed in white and black, holding an iron weapon, asking about Li's journey. The person claimed to be waiting for the woman who survived and told the governor that Li had arrived at an official residence, where officials greeted him. It was heard that this journey did not include the name of the woman, so she was sent to the water's edge. At that time, the entire incident was reported to the emperor. Since then, the Qutang Gorge has been considered a water temple, and it is customary to offer sacrifices there during the spring and autumn seasons. In recent literary works from Longxi, there is a piece called "Gold Hairpin Falls into the Well," which may have been prophetic. It is said that righteous people who die become officials in the underworld, and these officials are often those with virtue and status in the human world, known as "Yin Xian" (underworld immortals). Recent stories written by officials from Zhu Ya and Zhang Du also mention the concept of judgment in the underworld. Liu Changmei twice governed a state or region, and a monk from Yun'an County, Yuanwu, had connections with military officers from Sichuan.

**Translation:** Wang Shangshu set aside three hundred thousand of his own salary to repair the Guanyin Temple. [This statue has a spirit.] He kept thirty thousand for himself. One day, he passed away. After seven days, he was on the verge of decay but suddenly revived. Medicine was administered to aid his recovery, and he said, "At first, I arrived at an official office and met General Liu [that is, Changmei]. He said, 'Why did you misuse the merit money?' Since we had met before, I was released and told to return the money. Yuan Wu then instructed his disciples to sell their robes and begging bowls to repay the debt. Soon after, he passed away." Gou Wei, a clean and upright official in the Western Sichuan region, was once a member of the Wang family from Mianzhu County. When the family's son was gravely ill and entered the afterlife, he returned to life and saw the ghostly official, who said, "I am Gou Wei, the magistrate. I live in the West Market of Chengdu. I once borrowed three thousand taels of silver from someone and have not repaid it. Now that you are back, please tell this to my family." The Wang family son later visited Gou Wei's son and told him the name of the creditor. The debt was indeed repaid. **Lu Shi San Yue** During the Tang Dynasty, Lu Yantang specialized in poetry and passed the imperial examination after twenty-five attempts. In one of his works, he wrote, "A fox runs across the official road, and a dog bumps into the shop door." Zhang Jun, the Prime Minister, personally witnessed this and often praised it. He also wrote, "A hungry cat crouches near the mouse hole, and a greedy dog guards the fish on the chopping block." These lines were admired by Cheng Zhongling. Additionally, he wrote, "Chestnuts explode, burning the felt and breaking it; a cat jumps and touches the overturned cauldron." These lines were praised by Wang Xianzhu. Lu once said to others, "Throughout my life, I visited high-ranking officials, but I did not expect to gain recognition from cats and dogs." People heard this and laughed. Lu once wrote, "Not the same as literary compositions, easy to be this one or that one," and entered the Hanlin Academy. His peers teased him, saying, "Not the same as literary compositions, easy to be this one or that one." Eventually, he was dismissed from the academy after a few days for being incompetent.

### Li Shi's Collection of Writings [Including Works by Pi Rihou and Zhuang Bu] Li Shi was the son of Li Shi (李渷), a chancellor of the Tang Dynasty. He was profoundly skilled in literature, far surpassing his contemporaries. During his time taking the imperial examinations, his writings circulated under the guidance of Mr. Ming Yi. He also wrote a letter in response to Mr. Ming Yi. Officials who read his works could not fathom their depth, and from this, one can infer the quality of his other writings. However, he relied on his talent and rushed forward, ultimately suffering misfortune. Later, Li Shi (李渷) was posthumously honored with the title of Grand Tutor and the posthumous name "Wen" (文), as recorded in the biography written by Sima Kui. Before this, Liu Chonggui (刘崇龟), a secretary, wrote a memorial for Li Shi (李渷), which led to his demotion. Li Shi (李渷) wrote the "Parrot Cup Ode" in response to the historical figure Da Peng (大彭), whose ancestors committed corruption and took poison. In his response, he wrote: "Jade dogs bark at the celestial gate, colorful children cry for divine officials. A red envelope contains a petition about earthly matters. The world's pillars collapse, the scales of the four seas' dragons boil. The evening star is cold and bright, the star of literature is dim and lacking in vitality. The sun or moon does not rise again, stench is buried in yellow sand. Request the emperor to send a true official to open the golden treasury and restore the Purple Extreme position in the Qinghua Palace. Ancient rain and dew grace cannot be borrowed or cherished, and the desire to shed blood until the evil stars are sated. Very meaningful." Pi Rihou (皮日休) once visited the Minister of Works Gui Rong (归融) but was not seen. He then wrote the "Snake and Turtle Ode" to criticize Gui Rong for not showing himself, and Gui Rong's son wrote the "Leather Shoe Ode" in response, leading to mutual slander and ridicule. Zhuang Bu (庄布) later wrote a long letter to criticize Pi Rihou, which circulated widely. Zheng Zhun (郑准) collected military documents. ---

During the Tang Dynasty, Zheng Zun from Xingyang relied on his literary skills to depend on the Governor of Jingzhou, often wanting to be on equal footing with Chen and Ruan. He compiled his own works into three volumes titled "Military Records of Liu Biao." Although he had a broad mind, the style of his writing was not elegant. In a letter congratulating the court officials, he wrote: "The Attendant to the Secretary of the Central Secretariat said 'drafting the edict,' and the Attendant to the Imperial Secretary said 'submitting for approval.'" In a letter congratulating Zhao, the successor of the Governor of Xiangzhou, he wrote: "Without bathing and wearing jade, there is a favorable omen; without climbing a mountain to obtain a talisman, there is no concern for the seal." This was a clear indication of his lowly status in the midst of celebration. The respect shown by neighboring regions was not as great as this. On the day of the imperial examination, he titled a poem about a water buffalo "Preventing the Calf from Standing, Meeting People, Reveal Tail, and Jump," which the officials of the court laughed at. Zheng Zun criticized Chen Yong. During the Tang Dynasty, Chen Yong, a candidate for the imperial examinations from Qingshen, Meizhou, had a reputation for poetry and was skilled at playing chess. When Emperor Zhaozong was forced to relocate and set up his court in the suburbs of Shaanxi, that year, he gained fame and returned to Shu (Sichuan). Wei Shu, the secretary, congratulated him with a poem. A local named Tuo Shan composed a poem in response to Wei Shu's poem. The poem said: "Yielding virtue has been heard by many scholars, but seeking fame has also been heard by the world." It criticized Chen Yong for being like a pot-washer and wine-seller. It mistakenly attributed the poem to Feng Fushi, because Feng often wrote humorous poems. Some say that Tuo Shan from Shu wrote this poem, using Feng's name. Yingchuan, who once boasted of his poetic skills, visited Zheng Zun, the Governor of Jingzhou. Zheng Zun also considered himself a master of writing. He told Yingchuan, "Today there are many affairs, I have no time to write." He asked Yingchuan to write three or four pieces of writing for him. Yingchuan worked from morning until evening but could not finish the drafts, because he wanted to show off his own abilities. There is a couplet at the beginning of his poetry collection: "A water buffalo on the other side of the river with its nose floating across the stream, birds on the bank of the stream walking with their heads raised." The Mr. Du Guangting of Jingzhao said, "Your excellent lines are numerous, why use this as the opening of the collection?" Yingchuan said, "It was once praised by a high-ranking official, so I engraved it at the beginning of the collection." It was because he wanted to gain false praise and sell his work.

Wang Chao's Memorials [Shi Qinru and Xu Cun Attached] In the late Tang Dynasty, Wang Chao, an official in Fengxiang, supported Li Maozhen and wielded the power of Cao Cao and Ma Chao, submitting memorials and official documents with unrestrained freedom. When the ruler of Shu, Liu Bei, first captured Chengdu, Feng Juan, the governor, appointed Wang Chao to draft his memorials. After a long time, Wang Chao was appointed to oversee the drafting of memorials, and Wei Zhuang, a senior official, was assigned to handle matters of strategy. However, Wang Chao's performance was not entirely satisfactory in these matters. Wang Baohui of Langzhou had literary talent but lacked a formal style. His writing was frank and straightforward, easy to understand, and he was appointed to draft memorials in Fengxiang. Wang Chao, from an old and prestigious family, sought to align with the grandeur of the times, and every time he submitted a memorial with false but eloquent arguments, the ruler of Shu, Liu Bei, loved him. Liu Bei showed the drafts of the memorials written by the two Wangs to the Prince of Changle, who wrote a letter of humility and apology, praising Wang Chao greatly. The key point of the letter was: "To have eyes that have not seen and ears that have not heard," criticizing Wang Chao for being out of touch with reality and relying on his military strength to lose his ability to serve or leave a position appropriately. Wang Chao later became the acting governor of Xingyuan and was killed. He had a collection of thirty volumes titled "Fengming Ji" that circulated in the world. Later, there was a man named Shi Qinru who imitated Wang Chao's style and served as an official under Liu Zhijun. He followed and surrendered to Shu but was unable to remain humble and avoid harm. Both the host and guest revealed their sharpness, and they were eventually executed together. Reading his sealed memorials and official documents, step by step, it is appropriate that he was feared and suffered misfortune. Xu Cun initially defected from Jingzhou Governor Cheng Zhongling to Shu. The ruler of Shu had the intention to kill him, but a close official named Liu Xiuye advised him to remain humble and quiet. Every time he established a great achievement, he would feign illness, and thus he escaped being killed during the reign of the ruler of Shu. He was like an old official from Pengcheng, not as good as a minor official from Gaoyang. Wang Chao's complete collection of thirty volumes is now only known from three volumes heard by Lu Qinghong.

**Translation:** Li Shangyin Drafted a Memorial for the Presentation of a Sword (With an Appendix on Gyu Chuanzhang from Sichuan) Li Shangyin, the external official, relied on Linghu Chu of Pengyang to draft a memorial through his official correspondence, as the Chancellor was in a critical situation. There was a valuable sword that had been specially bestowed by the late emperor. The imperial decree ordered Li to draft the memorial, but he was not satisfied with the purpose. He then composed the following verses orally: This sword, a divine weapon from the armory, was specially bestowed by the late emperor. It is inappropriate to return it to the afterlife, nor should it remain in the human realm. At the time, people admired his concise and appropriate handling of the matter. Linghu Tong, the son of Linghu Chu, later held the position of Grand Secretary, seemingly distant from the region of Longxi, and never had the opportunity to show favor. On the Double Ninth Festival, Li Shangyin visited Linghu Chu's residence and left a poem at the guardhouse, which read: "Ten years without any news from the afterlife, something on my mind while drinking in front of the wine cup. Young man, with your high-ranking position, you have the area in front of your residence where horses are stabled. The eastern hall offers no reason or opportunity to allow another look. Upon seeing it, the Chancellor felt ashamed and sad, and locked up this guardhouse, never to stay there again." Gyu Chuanzhang, an official from Sichuan, initially served as a judge in Yonghe Prefecture. He had sharp and abundant literary talent but was marred by redundancy and complexity. Because he was waiting to meet the Chancellor, Zhang Gong, who had a reason and could not be seen, Gyu became angry and returned, drafting a memorial that was only several thousand characters long. He handed it to the messenger and left in a huff. Another day, the Chancellor told the officials, "Gyu showed a long memorial that cannot be excessive."

Although I have heard that the draft is extensive, it is criticized for lacking brevity, appropriateness, and essentiality. [The manuscript has the addition: "What is short has its limitations, and what is long has its strengths; I have heard this from ancient people."] At the Yellow Talisman ritual site, the stars are all in their proper places. Gu Yun, the doctor, wrote a斋词 for Gao Yan Gong, saying that if the sky is calm, the stars can be picked. Can such unusual and dangerous phrases be used in the most solemn contexts? During the chaotic period at the end of the Tang Dynasty, there was a great need for methods to save the times. Whenever officials submitted sealed memorials, Kong Wei, the chancellor, did not have time to read them thoroughly; he would only say, "I know about the rise and fall of past and present." He did not examine the main points of the proposed benefits and harms. He looked down on them for not understanding changes. Yu Hui Zeng, the director of the Imperial Academy, submitted a memorial to Cui Xiangguo Gong, which was thousands of words long, covering everything from Yao and Shun to the Sui and Tang dynasties, with the rise and fall of each dynasty clearly recorded. However, at the end, it became scattered and disorganized, far from being concise. Therefore, those among scholars who understand change are few. [The manuscript has the addition: "Pei Du, the chancellor, submitted a memorial about the jade belt given by the late emperor before his death. The matter with Linghu Gong is similar, but it is unclear which version is correct. Old court officials often say that Li Shangyin wrote the memorial about the sword, and Linghu Gong said that today he did not have time to write it carefully and simply improvised it."]

Gao Chongwen, the High Minister, on Snow Gao Chongwen, the High Minister of the Tang Dynasty, originally from Jizhou, was a military officer who, for his meritorious service in suppressing Liu Pi, was appointed as the military governor of Xichuan. One day, during a heavy snowfall, the officials gathered to recite and appreciate poems. The people of Bohai often called others "儿" (likely a typographical error, possibly "姣," meaning "beautiful person," but the context suggests it might be a derogatory term). At this banquet, Gao Chongwen addressed the guests, saying, "Although I am merely a soldier, I too have a poem." He then recited it spontaneously: "Gao Chongwen, valuing martial prowess, not valuing literary skills, carries the spear out to the frontier, known as a general. That beautiful person shoots down a goose, white feathers falling in a flurry." All agreed that his poem was comparable to the works of Ao Cao of the Northern Qi. Dai Wei Piang, the Grand Marshal, was his great-grandson. When stationed in Shu, he faced unrest from the barbarians and pirates, so he built a defensive wall forty li in circumference. Although the court granted him favors, they also suspected him of fortifying his position. One day, upon hearing the sound of music, he knew there was a change, so he inscribed a message on a kite, saying, "At night, the sound of the kite echoes in the clear sky, the pentatonic scale trusts the wind to come and go, the melody seems like a song, yet it shifts to a different tune." Within ten days, he reported the move to Zhugong.

Poems about Dongting Lake [By Li Dong, Bao He, Lu Yanrang, and Gu Kuang] The Xiang River flows northward to Yueyang, reaching the Sichuan River. After the summer floods, the Sichuan River rises significantly, blocking the Xiang River, causing it to overflow and form Dongting Lake. The lake broadens to hundreds of miles, with Junshan Mountain gracefully lying in the water during autumn. When the autumn waters recede, the mountain returns to land, and only the Xiang River remains. The transformation of sea into land can be seen here. Xu Tang's poetry about Dongting Lake was the most outstanding, and no one has followed in his footsteps since. The poet Qiji, staying in Balin, wanted to write a poem about Dongting Lake but could not find satisfaction. A military official named Cai, whose name is forgotten, jokingly told Qiji, "The poem about Dongting Lake is the best, and others should not write about it." Qiji firmly requested, and the official recited, "How pitiful is Dongting Lake, with no beard in the third winter, because it does not form a lake." The monks laughed at him. Scholar Li Dong admired Jia Dao and wanted to emulate him, often thinking of Jia Dao's poetry. There is also Bao He, who often writes crude and vulgar verses, such as "bitter bamboo shoots sprout green pegs, and pomegranate trees hang small fruits."

A bottle is described as a mist-covered mountain hat, a boat as a water shoe. It is said that the oars move the boat, the wind moves the bamboo, and the chest beats. Even if something good is attributed to it, it is still based on a foundation that may not be entirely solid, akin to "wind entering a hole." During the reign of Emperor Ling, Lu Yanrang composed a poem lamenting the death of a border general, which said, "It is sand and gravel that has caused the injury, not stones. There are many documents and duties on the body, a large wound on the back." People called this a "spine-beating poem." A popular poem says, "Sometimes guests stay overnight at the window, and there is nothing to do in the room but accompany a monk to sleep." It is called a "self-falling advantageous poem." Gu Kuang, a scholar wearing a scholar's robe in Mount Mao, had a talented scholar who recited, "Stop the horse on the mountain, think for a long time without finding a solution. Why not say, 'The wind brings the smell of feces?'" The scholar replied, "A scholar should not be rude." Gu Kuang said, "It is me." The scholar was ashamed and retreated. In my early years, I once composed a poem about the Southern Yue region, which said, "In the morning kitchen, clams are cooked, in the spring, mulberry silk is woven." The Hanlin Academy read it and fell to the ground, not understanding its meaning. The Hanlin Academy said, "You only know the name, but do not know that clams are not elegant things." Later, I understood it. Those who study poetry should take this as a warning.

High Chán established his reputation through poetry. [Attached: Hu Zēng and Lóu Yìng] The poet Gāo Chán's poetic thoughts were clear but deliberately strange and risky, with sparse ideas and weak reasoning, making him a culprit against the elegance of the Book of Songs. Xú Zhōu, a high-ranking official, once said to others, "If one were to see this person, they would want to give them a slap on the face because they failed the examination." His poem read: "Heavenly peach blossoms are planted with dew, red apricots lean against clouds near the sun. Lotus flowers bloom on the autumn river, not complaining to the spring wind for not opening." He maintained a humble and simple disposition without the desire for rapid advancement, and was recognized and praised by high-ranking officials. Before this, Hu Zēng had a poem that said, "When will the imperial academy stop marrying off daughters? When will articles stop giving birth to sons?" The new laurel branches of the imperial garden bloom year after year, but they do not allow ordinary people to pick even a single branch. Lóu Yìng also often expressed criticism and resentment, which was resented by those in power, leading to his recognition. I once read Li He's collection of poems and admired his extraordinary talent and risky style, although I often doubted that his reasoning was lacking and hesitated to openly discuss it with my contemporaries. I saw Du Zimei's comment in the collection of Qizhang Gong (a historical figure): "If Li He had added more reasoning to his poetry, he could have been as great as the greatest poets." This shows that the general consensus is that his work is not far from the ideal. Wei and Du's bold and dignified style [attached: Li Pín] Du Xúnzī once wrote a couplet: "Old clothes are covered with gray fluff, new wine is filtered through bamboo sieves." At the time, Wei Xiangguo, the Right Vice Minister of the Ministry of Works, was living in Jingzhou. Someone told Wei, "I say that the seal will be locked with golden locks and the curtain will be hung with jade hooks, indicating a grand promotion. This bold and dignified style is already evident in the poem." Someone else recounted Li Pín's poem to Qian Shangfu, saying, "He used his five-character verses to express his deepest thoughts and feelings." Qian replied, "It is a pity that this heart was not used more widely and was only expressed in poetry."

Liang Zhen had no luck. In the Tang Dynasty, the Deputy Governor of Jingnan, Dong, and the official from Jingzhao, Du Wu, who was the son of Du Tao, the Grand Attendant of Huatiao, along with Liang Zhen from Shu, all claimed to be jinshi and went to see Cheng Zhongling, hoping to be recommended for a position. There was a lesser official named Xue, who had been transferred from Shu to Zhugong, where the three scholars visited him. One day, Xue told Dong, "You and the official from Jingzhao should not discuss seeking fame; you will definitely not succeed." Du also would not live long. Only the great sage would suddenly be bound by others, reaching a high rank like Liang Zhen, but this attempt would be futile, and he would not receive any official position after passing the examination. Later, as predicted, Liang Zhen thought about returning to Shu and revisited Zhugong, but the journey was blocked, and he did not reach the west before the Huai army invaded Jiangling. The Prince of Bohai invited him to his mansion, asking him to draft a proclamation. He refused to serve in the government, and the prince respected his decision. Liang Zhen lived in seclusion for twenty years, wearing only coarse clothing. Did Xue's words prove true? Xiahousheng told a story about Liu Puyue. Liu Puyue, a high-ranking official, was often expected to become a chancellor. He believed that once he left his position, he would immediately go to Luofu Mountain to live as a recluse. Xiahousheng, a person from Pengcheng, told Liu about his future. Liu asked about his actions and whereabouts in the next five years, and Xiahousheng said nothing would be unverified. It turned out that Liu died on the way back to the capital, and Xiahousheng's predictions were all accurate. This was because fate determined his life and death.

Liang Zhen Met with Misfortune During the Tang dynasty, Dong Yu, the regional judge of Jingnan, and Du Wusheng of Jingzhao, who was the son of Du Tao of Huetai, the former Attendant-in-Ordinary, along with Liang Zhen of Shu, all claimed to be jinshi degree holders and visited Cheng Zhongling, hoping to be recommended for official appointments. At that time, there was a minor official named Xue Shao Yin, who had fled from Shu and arrived at Zhu Gong. The three scholars visited him. One day, Xue Yin said to Dong Yu, "You and the official from Jingzhao should not even consider seeking fame, for it will surely not succeed. Du Wusheng will not live long. Only a great sage, suddenly bound and restrained, may rise to high rank—such as Liang Scholar here. But this endeavor will surely fail, and after passing the examination, he will not receive even the first official appointment." Later events proved his words true. Liang Zhen then wished to return to Shu and revisited Zhu Gong, but the river routes were blocked, and before he could travel west, the Huai River army attacked Jiangling. The Lord of Bohai invited him to his government office and asked him to draft a proclamation. He wished to appoint Liang Zhen to his administrative staff, but Liang Zhen remained steadfast in his refusal to take office. The Lord of Bohai respected his decision, and for twenty years, Liang Zhen lived in seclusion, still wearing a plain hemp robe. Did Xue Yin's words indeed prove true? The Story of Xiahou Sheng and Liu Pushe Liu Pushe of Guangnan had long been regarded as a potential minister of state. He once expected that after retiring from office, he would directly ascend to Luofu Mountain to live as a hermit. Xiahou Sheng, a reclusive scholar of virtue, was respected by Pengcheng. Liu Pushe consulted him about his future. Xiahou Sheng said he would not become a minister, and after three thousand miles, an unforeseen event would occur. Later, when Liu Pushe returned to the capital on his journey back, he fell ill and died along the way. Liu Shanfu also verified Xiahou Sheng's words regarding the events of the next five years, and all were accurate. This shows that one's fate is predetermined, like the portion of food and drink one is destined to receive.

Cao Xiang's Dream of Being Tonsured as a Monk --- Cao Quan, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, was expected to become a high-ranking official in the government. However, he had a dream of being tonsured as a monk, which he found deeply unpleasant. There was a man who could interpret dreams accurately, and Cao Quan summoned him to explain the dream. The man said that the previous Shilang He would soon be promoted, and the person who had been tonsured (出家者) was called "剃度." Soon after, Du was sent to govern Jiangxi, and Cao Quan was promoted to a high position. Wang Shenzhi, the ruler of the Min Kingdom, wanted to construct a harbor but was hesitant due to the difficulties involved. During the Gannin era of Emperor Zhaozong of Tang, Wang Shenzhi had a dream in which he saw a golden-armored deity who claimed to be Wu An Wang and offered to help in the construction of the harbor. After the dream, Wang Shenzhi told his officials about the dream, and they decided to perform a sacrifice to the deity. Liu Shanhua, an official who served in the Min Kingdom, was sent to perform the sacrifice. During the ceremony, Liu Shanhua witnessed a miraculous event where a creature resembling a fish or dragon appeared, and after three days of storms, a new harbor was revealed. The emperor then granted the harbor the name "Gan Tang Port." Liu Shanhua, a noble family from the central court, wrote a work titled "Jinxi Xiantan" (闲谈, or "casual talk") containing various stories and anecdotes, including this one. I once briefly read the work, but unfortunately, the original text has been lost, and no one has been able to find it. The story is far off in a distant coastal area and is impossible to search for. What remains today is what I have gathered, heard from Liu Shanhua, which is the story of that event. Out of ten details, only three or four are remembered. Alas.

### Final Translation **Liu Daoji's Dream by the Secluded Window** During the Guanghua era, the scholar Liu Daoji stayed at Guoqing Temple on Tiantai Mountain. In his dream, a woman led him into a space beneath a window where there was a side柏 tree and a sunflower, and they became a harmonious couple. Afterward, they frequently met in dreams, but he did not understand the reason. Soon after, in an ancient temple in Fenghua County, Mingzhou, he saw a window with a side柏 tree and a sunflower, just as in his dream. There was a guest official staying there, and his daughter was beautiful but poor and unmarried. She was suffering from a heart ailment, and the person Liu Daoji met was her soul. It is said that when a woman reaches the age of fifteen and has not married, it is the fault of her father and elder brothers. Another man from Pengcheng, Liu Sheng, dreamed of entering the home of a courtesan, where he drank with others in a familiar manner. Afterward, whenever he dreamed, he would go to that place, doubting that it was not a dream. The fragrance of the woman he met often clung to his clothes, which he believed was caused by a wandering or perverse heart. This was reported to Liu Shanfu. --- **Miscellaneous Notes from the North, Volume Seven** **Imperial Library Collection** **Miscellaneous Notes from the North, Volume Eight** **By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty** **Letter from Li Taibei to Duan Shaichang** During the Tang dynasty, Li Taibei, after being demoted to Zhuya, wrote forty-nine essays detailing his life's aspirations. He once sent a letter to Duan Shaichang, Chengshi, saying: "Since arriving in Zhuya, I have been fortunate to remain healthy. The locals often raise chickens, which sometimes fly into government offices. For now, I will content myself with being a chicken-wishing old man. I humbly report that Ji Fu, the chancellor, was appointed to govern Zhongzhou and traveled upstream to Zigui, where there is a place called Yunju Platform in the middle of the river. Zang Wu was born there, and his nickname was Tai Lang, named after the place." --- This translation aims to maintain the original meaning and cultural context while ensuring readability in English.

Straw Hat Servant Pouring Wine [with Pei Di attached] During the Tang Dynasty, Sun Fangzong, the Servant-General, was the great-grandfather of Sun Wuo, the Chancellor. At a gathering in his home, he invited close relatives and distant kin to a banquet. One of his nephews or cousins heard that court officials were arriving later and, upon reaching the main gate, saw a high-ranking official in red robes with wine stains on his chest. The official hurriedly left without being recognized. Later, at the banquet, he explained to the host that he had seen this official, and everyone was surprised because there was no such official. After some thought, it was realized that during the wine service, someone had carelessly poured wine on the steps, splashing it everywhere. From then on, whenever wine was poured, Sun's family would bow sideways in a respectful manner, pouring only once. This custom began with Sun's family, and it is incorrect for people today to pour wine three times. There was a person named Pei Di, a cousin of the Chancellor Zhi, who lacked literary talent. He served under General Zhao Si in Jingnan and became a junior officer under Yu Zong. When entertaining guests, Pei Di would bow sideways and pour wine once. General Zhao did not understand the etiquette of the court and found it very strange, even punishing Pei Di with seven strokes. How unfortunate! Two Court Officials Teasing Each Other with Names During the Tang Dynasty, Zhang Xi, the Minister of the Secretariat, was highly respected and known as a member of the elite class. He had a good relationship with Chancellor Wei Baoqing. Wei once told his colleagues that Zhang's name, Xi, meant "uncovered" or "torn" clothing. The poem also says, "Clothed in Xi, Xi is the swaddling clothes of a child, which are wrapped and bound." Zhang intended to change his name but before he could, Chancellor Wei was exiled and never achieved a high position. Chancellor Wei once asked Zhang about the origin of his name. Zhang said that he had been an orphan since childhood and was misled by his relatives and family members. Later, during the Tang Dynasty, there was a Chancellor named Yao Ji who was good at speaking and had many debates. His cousin, Cui Yi, the Secretary of the Board of Works, teased him by saying, "Ji means meat broth, why is your name like that?" Yao Ji had no reply, but he also said that although names can bring ridicule, there are many examples in ancient times. Some names can hinder matters, such as "Xianke" (Immortal Guest), "Xiantong" (Immortal Child), "Qi Qiu" (Qiu's Use), "Yong Li" (Use Li), and "Xi Xue" (Learn Xue). These names are not suitable. When renaming, one should follow the meaning of the name, and it is better to use a single name that is easy to remember. Zhang Xi had five sons, Yi, Xian, Wen, and Wei, who became famous. Wen later achieved high office.

Zhang Rengui, secretly blamed. During the Tang dynasty, Zhang Ti, the Shangshu (Minister of State), was in charge of the storage of his beloved courtesan outside Jin Prefecture. She gave birth to a son, and his wife, Su, who was called "Dust Beyond," was jealous and did not dare to bring the child back. Instead, she lived with her favorite scholar, Zhang the recluse, and their son in Jiangjin. She often sent letters to inquire about his well-being and provided him with money and silk. As he gradually grew up, she taught him to read. Someone told him that he was not the recluse's son and that his father was a high-ranking official in the court. He then secretly took his father's and the recluse's letters and fled back to the capital without telling anyone. By the time he arrived, Zhang Ti had already passed away. When he reached the gate of the house, the servants did not recognize him, but they said he was the Jianghuai Langjun (a title for a nobleman). His brothers were all surprised. His legitimate mother, Su, the Lady Dust Beyond, wept and said to her children, "If there is such a son, I know him. I was young and caused the eternal separation of his father and son; my sins are many." The family gathered and wept, and they took him into the house, placing him among the brothers. He was named Zhang Rengui. He had a literary nature and was fond of learning and composing poetry. He passed the imperial examination and served as a censor. On a mission to Jiangzhe, he committed suicide by hanging at an inn, and no one knew what happened. Before that, the recluse had died in sorrow and regret, and there must have been some complaint or accusation that led to this calamity. The censor was the beloved son-in-law of Yang Ju, the Attendant Minister.

The Right Minister Pei's Advancement After Passing the Imperial Examinations The Right Minister Pei, during the reign of Emperor Wenzong of Tang in the year Taihe, passed the imperial examinations under the examination of Li Han, the Censor-in-Attendance. Despite his success, he felt that his scholarly achievements were not yet refined enough to deserve such an honor. He did not bow deeply to his examiner and instead declined to return to official duties, retreating to his villa in Hu county for three years of intense study. During this time, he secluded himself and worked diligently, and his literary style transformed. Upon returning to the capital, he resubmitted his work to his benefactor, and the elegance and refinement of his writing were praised by all at court. He eventually rose to a high-ranking position, becoming a renowned minister of his time. Indeed, in the world, those who seek official rank first need to gain a reputation through passing the imperial examinations. Once they achieve this, few continue to strive and remain diligent. Only those who are deeply devoted to learning and mindful of their obligations can attain such heights. The Right Minister Pei's journey to the heights of government demonstrates his continuous improvement and dedication to virtue.

After passing the imperial examination, the Minister of State Péi Xiangguó furthered his studies. In the eighth year of the Tahe era, he passed the examination under the supervision of Secretary Li Han. Believing his scholarly attainments were not yet refined, he felt unworthy of the honor and did not bow deeply in gratitude to his examiner, but instead returned to his villa in Hù County, where he studied diligently for three years without entering the city. During the seasons, he sent only formal letters to his benefactors. He largely avoided contact with his fellow examinees, closing himself off in seclusion and working with great diligence. His literary style thus changed. Only after returning to the capital did he once again present his writings to his former examiner, whose elegant and classical style was praised throughout the court. Later, he was appointed to a high-ranking official post and became a renowned statesman of his time. Indeed, in the world, those who seek official rank first rely on passing the examination; once obtained, few are not prone to slackness or decline. Unless one is deeply committed to scholarship and mindful of the responsibilities of office, how could one achieve what Péi Xiangguó accomplished? His time in the imperial court clearly demonstrated his unceasing moral and scholarly progress.

Hou Yong Offended Lord Doulu During the Xiantong era of the Tang Dynasty, Hou Yong, a scholar who was well-known and respected, lacked courtesy and prudence. Doulu Zhuo, a former chancellor who now held the position of Puyue, visited a temple during his leisure time, leaving his servants behind and sitting alone as an elderly man. Hou Yong entered the temple, paid no attention, and arrogantly sat on a bed, addressing the old man: "Are you a high-ranking official?" The old man replied, "No." Hou Yong then asked, "Are you a magistrate or recorder?" The old man again denied it. "Are you a distant prefect?" The old man said, "It is somewhat higher." "Are you a junior official?" The old man replied, "It is even higher." Hou Yong became startled and felt uncomfortable, suspecting it was a high-ranking official, and quickly left. At the door, he saw his servants and a sedan chair arriving, and only then realized it was Lord Doulu. After returning, Hou Yong felt guilty and wrote a long letter apologizing, relying on a previous introduction to get it delivered. Doulu Zhuo acknowledged the mistake and forgave him. Doulu Zhuo invited him to drink, offering ten cups, which was a mild punishment. He also warned him to be more careful in the future, and Hou Yong felt deeply ashamed. Previously, Doulu Zhuo's family drank only clear wine, while Hou Yong's family served elaborate meals. On this day, every time Doulu Zhuo drank, he picked up a text from the table and read it, almost spilling his drink, which was considered a sign of refinement.

Lu Hang, the censor of Shan Prefecture in the Tang Dynasty, was highly regarded in the examination hall. Once, he encountered Emperor Xuanzong on a private tour at an inn by the Chan River. Thinking he was a noble person, Lu Hang bowed and stepped aside. The emperor bowed and greeted him. Lu Hang then introduced himself as a scholar named Lu Hang. The emperor asked for his poetry scroll, put it in his sleeve, and left on a donkey. On another day, when speaking to the chancellors, he mentioned Lu Hang and ordered the chief examiner to promote him. Lu Hang felt uneasy and feared the shame of presumption. The chancellors asked Lu Hang what his connection with the emperor was. Lu Hang then fully explained the reasons. At the time, no one was surprised because his articles were not obtained through favoritism. Later, Lu Hang was promoted from censor to chief examiner. He encountered a yellow bandit who attacked the capital, and the examination was not completed. Zhao Chong, the doctor, joked, "Born out of the womb of the chief examiner." Initially, the Lu family had never known the chief examiner, and Lu Xiang carried the shame of not being promoted to the position. Jia Dao, a famous Tang Dynasty poet, met Emperor Xuanzong on a private tour. The emperor asked for the scholar's name, and the scholar replied, "Jia Dao." The emperor said, "I have heard of your poetry for a long time." Jia Dao asked, "How did you know?" Later, the emperor spoke to the chancellors and demoted Jia Dao to the position of vice-magistrate of Changjiang, saying, "He did not recognize a noble person."

### The Rebirth of Gu Feixiong Gu Kuang, a Tang Dynasty Literary Censor and known as Bo Weng, was fond of mocking court officials. He was exiled to the south of the Yangtze River and often associated with monks and Taoist priests. In his later years, he lived on Mount Mao and had a son who was the previous incarnation of Gu Feixiong. One day, Gu Kuang suddenly died. His grief was so profound that he could not bear to speak of it, and he recited: "An old man mourns his beloved son, Crying until tears turn to blood at sunset. The old man is seventy years old, We won't part for much longer." Gu Feixiong, in the mourning period, heard of it and was very sad and nostalgic.
