He then reported his feelings to the mourning officials, who all pitied him. They discussed and decided to bring him back to life in Gu's family. At three years old, he could already speak. In the mourning period, he heard his father's painful recitation and sought to understand the details of his rebirth. When he grew up, he passed the imperial examination to become a Jinshi. If court officials asked, he would cry and tell them. Wang Dingbao said in his *Miscellaneous Notes* that people said that both Gu and his son had encountered something but didn't know where to go. Therefore, it is credible. ### The Young Mourning of Zhang Shu Zhang Yi, a Tang Dynasty Langzhong, was highly respected in the court. He had a beloved concubine who died early, and he mourned endlessly. Because he went to court and did not return, his nephew, Zhang Shu, who was a Right Censor and talented and charming, increased the grief of the younger brother. He then composed a poem titled "Huan Xisha," the words said: "The pillow, incense burner, and embroidered curtain are separated. For two years, they longed for each other every day. Only the gentle wind and bright moon could understand. Where in heaven or on earth have you gone? Old joys and new dreams awaken when you realize. At dusk, with light rain, the curtain hangs down." He placed the poem on the table. The elder brother, after court, leaned against the table in boredom. Suddenly, he saw this poem and could not help but mourn deeply. He said, "It must have been written by Ahui." Ahui is the nickname of Zhong Jian. However, in terms of moral education, one cannot excuse the similarity in age and appearance between uncle and nephew. A proverb says, "Nephews and uncles chasing and playing pranks are inevitable."

Zhang Xi, the Shangshu, was without any taboos. During the Tang Dynasty, Zhang Xi, the Shangshu, relied on his talent and followed the straight path, yet still possessed a profound nature. After passing the imperial examination, he returned to the Eastern Capital. One day, he vaguely saw his deceased parents and they said, "You can go." He then prepared and traveled to the capital, and indeed entered the imperial register, not deviating from the hidden warning. In the Eastern Capital, at Baipo, he had a manor with many tall and grand buildings. The central courtyard had a mound of earth resembling a tomb. People said there was music from beneath it. The owner tried to sell it but couldn’t. The Eight Seats (high officials) did not believe, but bought it at a good price and rushed to have it excavated. What was found beneath was just barley malt, which was used to mix with mud and plaster the walls and houses of the courtyard, without needing anything else. This shows that strange phenomena arise from human actions. If one were to doubt and mistake it for a supernatural event, then there would be something relying on it, and it would certainly become the material for village shamans’ feasts. An honest and upright person cannot be deceived. The story of Jing Shisan娘, the righteous and heroic woman. Jinshi Zhao Zhongxing lived in Wenzhou and made a living as a hero and侠义 (righteous and heroic). Traveling to Suzhou and staying at Zhi Mountain Zen Temple, in a monk’s room, there was a female merchant named Jing Shisan娘, who set up a major memorial service for her deceased husband. Because of admiration for Zhao【or perhaps at dusk】, she traveled together back to Yangzhou. Zhao spent Jing’s money carelessly without any concern. His friend Li Zhenglang, the thirty-ninth【or perhaps his younger brother】, loved a courtesan. His parents took her and gave her to Zhu Ge Yin. Li Zhenglang was deeply resentful. At that time, Zhu Ge Yin and Lü Yongzhi were deceiving Gao Taiv, exercising arbitrary power and favor. Li Zhenglang feared disaster and could only cry in secret. Told Jing Shisan娘 by chance, Jing Shisan娘 was also angry and sorrowful. She said to Li Zhenglang, the thirty-ninth, "This is a small matter; I can avenge you. Just cross the river to Ruzhou’s Beigushan Mountain on the sixth day of the sixth lunar month at noon to wait for me." Li Zhenglang followed her. On the appointed day, Jing Shisan娘 put the courtesan and the courtesan’s parents’ heads in a bag and returned them to Li Zhenglang. Later, she and Zhao Jinshi entered Zhezhong together, and their whereabouts are unknown.

The Soul of Li Dang's Deceased Daughter During the Tang Dynasty, Li Dang, the Shangshu, was stationed in Baocheng County, where there lived a recluse scholar named Chen Xiufu, known as Chen Qi Zi. He was overly familiar with gamblers and acted in an unusual manner. The high-ranking officials, the "Eight Seats," considered him a fraud and publicly humiliated him in the marketplace. Another Chen Xiufu died in prison, and his body was quickly buried by the prison authorities. After this, Chen Qi Zi seemed to reappear in Baocheng, and the officials were surprised and hesitant to investigate. One day, Li Dang's beloved daughter suddenly died, and his wife grieved to the point of illness. One of the officials suggested to Li Dang that Chen Qi Zi, the recluse scholar, must have some minor deity's method and could possibly help. Li Dang agreed and began to respect and invite Chen Qi Zi. Chen Qi Zi said, "This is a minor matter. On the first night, set up a room covered with a curtain and light some lamps. Draw a door and invite the lady to enter, holding her breath. At midnight, the deceased daughter will enter the room through the drawn door. The lady will be deeply moved and cry, and the soul will disappear suddenly." Then, he warned and advised the lady to suppress her emotions. The officials became even more respectful of Chen Qi Zi. From the "Běi Mèng Suǒ Yán" Volume Eight <Department of Literature, Novels, Miscellaneous Stories, Běi Mèng Suǒ Yán>

The *Complete Library of the Four Treasuries* *Dreams of the North: Trifles, Volume Nine* Tang dynasty, by Sun Guangxian Sheng Hongwei, the Imperial Censor, was impetuous and unreliable. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, he once addressed the emperor informally, saying, "Your Majesty, how could you not know of my existence? Why don’t you summon me through written commands?" The emperor was furious and said, "Who are you? I have never even heard of you." The next day, the emperor told the prime minister, "This man is impetuous and seeks the position of Hanlin Scholar too easily." The prime minister then demoted Sheng Hongwei, a small punishment to show his disapproval. Sheng Hongwei was also quick to anger and often quarreled with his younger brother, who accidentally fell into a well. Public opinion was outraged, and Sheng Hongwei wrote to his friends, saying, "I was halfway down the well when the rumors began to spread. It’s as if a small puddle could create a towering wave, a common occurrence throughout history." He lived near Zheng Feng, who was a magistrate in the southern provinces. One day, the wall between their houses collapsed, and Sheng Hongwei encroached upon Zheng Feng’s property by about six feet. Zheng Feng filed a complaint, and Sheng Hongwei responded, "The magistrate of the southern provinces has a small matter to attend to. When handling such matters, please be careful not to let them fall to the ground." Yang Zao, the Chancellor, reported to Yang Yuanjie. During the Tang dynasty, Yang Zao, the Chancellor, was exiled to the southern provinces and died there. At that time, Zheng Yu, the Minister of War, was governing the southern provinces. One day, a messenger came and said, "Chancellor Yang is waiting to see Minister Zheng." The Minister was shocked, as Yang Zao had recently been exiled. He received Yang Zao and asked, "Chancellor, how is it possible for you to be here?" Yang Zao replied, "I was slandered by Yang Yuanjie and unfortunately met my demise. Now, I have been granted divine retribution, and I have come to seek vengeance. I wish to entrust you with the task of rewarding me and borrowing ten million strings of coins." The Minister agreed to the request but declined to lend the money, citing the busy affairs of the military. Yang Zao said, "These are not copper coins. Please handle them with care and do not let them fall to the ground." <|endofresponse|>

Imperially Approved *Siku Quanshu* *Northern Dreams: Trivial Sayings, Volume Nine* Compiled by Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty **Meng Hongwei's Rashness** During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang, Meng Hongwei, a Langzhong (Officer of the Ministry of Rites), was reckless and unbound by convention. On one occasion, during a court session, he said, "Why does the Emperor not know of me? Why does he not summon me through literary talent?" The Emperor was furious and replied, "Who are you, sir? I have never heard of you at all." The next day, the Emperor told the prime ministers, "This man is rash and arrogant. If he desires to become an Academician of the Hanlin Academy, it will be very easy for him." The prime ministers then returned to the Ministry of State Affairs and demoted him, giving him a minor punishment. On another occasion, Meng was filled with resentment and jealousy, and he pushed his younger brother into a well. When public opinion became heated, he wrote to his relatives and friends, saying, "I am half-hanging over the well, and rumors are boiling like a storm. A small ripple becomes a great wave—this is a common occurrence in ancient and modern times." Meng lived next to Zheng Feng, who served as an official in Nanhai. When the wall collapsed, a *zhonglang* (or *langzhong*) encroached upon the boundary by five to six *chi* (or *zhang*). The person in charge of the estate submitted a petition requesting the return of the encroached land. The judge ruled on the case, saying: "An official stationed in the southern frontier rarely returns alive. The terrain is steep and sloped, and walls naturally shift inward. His conduct in life has always been like this. To not be expelled would be fortunate enough." **Yang Xiangguo's Vengeance Against Yang Yuanjia** During the Tang Dynasty, Yang Xiangguo, the Chancellor, was exiled and died in the southern frontier. At that time, Minister Zheng Yu was governing Nanhai. One day, a messenger suddenly arrived and announced, "Chancellor Yang is waiting in the guest hall and wishes to see Minister Zheng." The eight high-ranking officials were greatly alarmed, for it was said that Yang had recently received an imperial decree from the capital—how could he possibly have come here? They welcomed him nonetheless. Chancellor Yang said, "I was falsely accused by Yang Yuanjia, the Commandant of the Army, and unfortunately met my death. Now I have received permission from Heaven and have been granted an army of spirits to avenge myself. I wish to entrust you, Minister Zheng, with a banquet in my honor and also to borrow ten thousand strings of copper coins." Zheng of Xingyang agreed, but when it came to the money, he excused himself, saying that the military affairs were too numerous, and offered only half. Chancellor Yang said, "These are not copper coins. When you burn them, please be careful not to let them touch the ground." Zheng replied,

If this is the case, then it is inevitable that one would casually bow and eliminate the magistrate of Xingyang in the northern suburbs, preparing food, drink, and white money to offer as a sacrifice. Yang's nephew, who was the governor of Shouyang, saw the prime minister riding a white horse, wearing a red bow, and holding a red arrow, with an official in red clothes controlling the horse and telling him, saying, "Heaven has allowed me to kill Yang Yuanjia as revenge. I will shoot his foot, and he will certainly die." Soon after, Yang, the military officer, suddenly contracted a foot disease and died. The person from the Mao family, a descendant of the Mao family, who served diligently for the Zheng Shangshu, heard this. D Liu Shanfu wrote about the Heavenly King During the Tang Dynasty, Liu Shanfu, a prominent family of the central court from Pengcheng, had ancestors who served in the Lingnan region and accompanied the court northward, docking their boat at Qingcao Lake. He went ashore and saw the Northern Vaisravana Heavenly King, so he went to see him. He saw the temple in ruins and the incense and lamps not lit. From that point on, the deity had no response. How can prosperity and decline be determined by people? That night, he dreamed of being blamed by the Heavenly King, who said to himself, "I am not the Heavenly King or the Southern Mountain God. I am responsible for this pond [or place]. Why do you insult me?" Soon after, he awoke in shock as the wind and waves rose, the masts fell, and the ropes broke; they were about to sink. He immediately got up, repented, ordered the removal of the poem, and then it was over. Liu Shanfu wrote an autobiographical note.

### The Virtues and Deeds of Chancellor Wei [With Commentary by Chen Fang] In the Sichuan road, the White Wei Ridge is known for its tigers and leopards that prey on people. There was a candidate from Jingzhao named Wei, whose name is unknown. During the Guanghua period of the Tang Dynasty, he was appointed as the governor of Banan. He often recited the Diamond Sutra. On his way to take office, he reached Mudichuan where he encountered a woman dressed in a red robe carrying two children. They walked together up the mountain, but at the top of the ridge, the travelers stopped and shouted. They saw that the woman was actually a large red panther. After a short while, she and Wei went their separate ways. Wei was unaware of this, as it was the power of reciting the sutra that protected him. Chen Wei, a person from Guangdu County in Chengdu, had been reciting the Diamond Sutra since his youth. He had a grudge against a man named Ma, who belonged to the surname Xu. One day, Chen went into hiding after an incident. Ma publicly announced that he would pursue and capture Chen. In response, Chen sharpened a dagger and carried it with him at all times, swearing that if he met Ma, he would definitely stab him and would not allow himself to be caught. One day, as Chen was walking through a dense forest path, Ma suddenly attacked him. Chen drew his dagger and swung it, and Ma fell backward. Chen realized that what he had swung was the scabbard, not the blade. Upon returning, he found that the blade was still intact, and Ma was also unharmed. How strange this was! ### The Monk Who Wrote Sutras with Blood During the Xiantong period of the Tang Dynasty, a monk named Fajin from Western Sichuan wrote sutras using his own blood. He gathered people to teach at the Temple of Teaching. The authorities reported this to High Yan Public, who issued a judgment saying: "A person who has cut off their arm is already dangerous; writing sutras with blood is not a good deed. The leaves of the Bodhi tree should not be defiled by the dust of the world; how can a vulgar person's body bear the stench? It is appropriate to order him to leave the country and not deceive people. With a rope, he was sent out to the eastern boundary." The authorities did not understand the meaning of the rope and gave him a thousand coins, sending him out of the eastern city gate. Fortunately, he escaped by mistake and later died at the Jade Spring Temple in Jingzhou.

The Story of General Cheng Throwing the Divination Sticks During the Tianfu period of the Tang Dynasty, General Cheng Rui was ordered by imperial decree to lead an army to rescue Jiangxia. The grandeur of his fleet was rarely heard of in recent times. Having already decided the departure date, he did not listen to advice. His troops camped at Gongan County, where there were two Golden Warrior Gods, locally known as the "Two Saints," who were also very powerful. General Cheng anchored his boat and paid homage to them, burning incense and praying sincerely for divine guidance. He used the divination sticks to seek auspicious signs, but after thirty throws, all were inauspicious. He then turned to his trusted official, Yang Shihou, and said, "The divination is not auspicious. What shall we do?" Yang replied, "General, you have spent years building ships and raising banners. If we retreat halfway, how will we face our soldiers and civilians?" Thus, General Cheng, having no choice, advanced, and ultimately suffered a defeat in the battle, dying and having his family destroyed. This was not an accident. If Yang had noticed the general's hesitation and spoken up, the Cheng family might have been spared. The White Lotus Maiden Bewitches Su Changyuan During the Zhonghe period of the Tang Dynasty, a scholar named Su Changyuan lived in a small village near Suzhou, ten miles from the official road. In the water-rich region of Wu, lotus flowers were abundant. One day, Su suddenly saw a maiden dressed in white with a red face, whose beauty and intelligence seemed otherworldly, like a goddess. From then on, he became intimate with her, using the small farm as a secret meeting place. Su was deeply bewitched by her. Once, he gave her a jade ring as a token of his affection. One day, he saw a white lotus bloom in front of the fence, unusually beautiful. He bent down to admire it and noticed something inside the flower. Upon closer inspection, it was the jade ring he had given her. He picked it up, and the enchantment ended. Ghosts and spirits, though invisible, must rely on objects to manifest. The power of such enchantments is beyond the capabilities of mere lotus flowers. This story was told by Liu Shanfu. Liu Pengju Lures the Five-Stringed Courtesans Liu Pengju lured the five-stringed courtesans.

During the Longqing period of the Tang dynasty, a scholar named Liu Pengju traveled to Hangzhou to escape the rain and took shelter at the temple of Wu Xiang. There, he saw a woman holding five strings (the character "" is unclear and may be a typo or obscure term; it will be marked as uncertain). She claimed to be a servant of the wealthy family of Qiandai. Liu Pengju was attracted to her and lured her away, hiding in a boat with her. They were captured by a local official, and her servant committed suicide by hanging. On one day, the servant returned to Liu's place. Liu also knew that she had passed away and was surprised by her appearance. The servant fully explained the situation, and Liu met with her spirit after some time, leaving in casual conversation. The story of Yun Fangzi's spirit is as follows: During the year when Emperor Xizong fled to Shu, a scholar-official named Li Yin, a native of Xiangzhou, fled to the home of a commoner in the southern mountains. There, he met a palace attendant and scribe named Yun Fangzi, who had talent and thoughts. She traveled with Li to Shu and fully described the affairs of the palace, including that she had once written poems on red leaves that flowed down the imperial moat. When they reached Mianzhou, they met the court official Tian, who recognized her and said, "How can a palace attendant be here?" He forced her to mount a horse and left with her. Li was very unhappy and had no recourse. The palace attendant and scribe had deep affection for Li, and at the inn ahead, she committed suicide by hanging. Her spirit pursued Li, fully explaining her feelings of longing. For several years, Li Yin became ill and emaciated. A Taoist said that he had an evil aura. Yun Fangzi explained that humans and ghosts have different paths and bade farewell, leaving. This was heard by Liu Shanfu. The story of the spirit of a straw effigy is as follows: During the Wude period of the Tang dynasty, a minor official named Zhang, whose name is forgotten, lived among the young nobles of Suzhou. He frequented a beautiful woman who favored him for a long time and began to suspect her. Soon, he became ill and emaciated. He met the Taoist Wu Shouyuan of the Kaiyuan Temple, who gave him a talisman. Indeed, a soulless servant girl with the name Hong Ying written on her back was found in the empty house, in a column hole. He burned her, and the evil spirit was thus dispelled. This was heard by Liu Shanfu.

**Translation:** The Divine Pen of Lord Yan There was a small general from Huaihai, surnamed Zhu, whose name is forgotten. He had a daughter who, before her marriage, was tormented by ghostly things. She often called out to Han Lang, who came and went like a living person, though only not seen in form. She observed the proper etiquette towards her father-in-law and mother-in-law, and claimed that the Heavenly Court's God Zhu, due to extraordinary events, dared not keep secret, so he informed the head of the government, High Lord Yan. The lord only wrote a name and ordered Zhu to return and post it on the girl's room door. The evil spirit came to see, sighed, said goodbye, and left, and this was reported to Liu Shanfu. Fish Xuanji [with Xu Yueying appended] Fish Xuanji, a Tang dynasty female Daoist, also known as Huilan, was very talented. During the Xiantong era, she served as a concubine to Li Yi, holding a broom. After her love waned, she left the mountain and became a female Daoist at the Xianyi Temple. She wrote a poem expressing resentment towards Li Gong, saying, "It is easy to seek an invaluable treasure, but it is hard to find a man with a heart." She also wrote, "Huilan fades and returns to the spring pond, willows on both sides accompany the guest boat." Letting her heart go, she became a courtesan. Finally, she was killed by the Jingzhao Governor Wen Zhang for killing a servant girl. Her collection was published in the world. In the Jianghuai region, there was Xu Yueying, also a courtesan. Her poem for sending someone off said, "Longing for human affairs that have been long absent, two people go together, one returns. Hating the water in front of Pingwang Pavilion, enduring the sight of mandarin ducks flying apart." [One version also says, "Tears before the pillow and rain before the steps, dripping through a quiet window until dawn."] She also had a collection of poems. In Jinling, the sons of the Xu family favored a camp prostitute, who was burned to death. Xu Yueying accompanied the funeral and said to Xu Gong, "This woman's lifelong charm even in death carries a flame." At that time, she was called the beautiful play. At the end of the Tang dynasty, there was a record of the Northern Market, among which were the accounts of Sun Shangshu's collection of several virtuous men and their licentious entertainments. Some say it was written by Sun Qi, the Shangshu.

**Translation:** **Li's Daughter** During the Guangming era of the Tang dynasty, when Huang Chao rebelled and the imperial court fled to Shu, the aristocracy was scattered, and bandits roamed freely. A daughter of a general from the Western Palace was forced to flee with others, eventually reaching Xingyuan, where her family was broken apart and she had no support. She coincidentally met General Dong Sima of Fengxiang, who, to conceal his family background, took her under his protection. She was very intelligent and quick-witted, skilled in serving and pleasing others, and thus managed to reach Shu. After searching for her relatives, she learned that they were still traveling. She then said to General Dong, "In the midst of chaos, women are weak and cannot save themselves. I am fortunate to have been taken care of by you until now. Losing my virtue in such circumstances is not a misfortune. Everyone has their own partner, and it is difficult to stay together for life. Please allow me to leave." General Dong was startled and she descended from the mountain. Those who understood the situation said that the intelligence of this woman was worthy of praise, as recorded in Liu Shanfu's idle talk. **Feng Zao's Admiration for Fame** Feng Zao, a retainer of the Tang dynasty, was the son of Feng Su, the uncle of Feng Juan. His family had a long history of passing the imperial examinations, but Feng Zao himself had little literary talent. He was obsessed with fame and had taken the exams fifteen times. A Taoist priest he knew said, "I once observed your predecessor and saw that this life would not bring you fame, but only an official position." Feng Zao did not believe this and continued to take the exams for another ten times, totaling twenty-five attempts. His relatives advised him to stop taking the exams and to seek an official position. Feng Zao replied, "If I have never succeeded in my life, I will swear to take five more exams, but I will still not succeed. I finally took the exams thirty times and obtained an official position, serving as a high-ranking official, a governor of Xiang, and eventually a high official in the imperial court. How much the empty fame attracts, and how lightly official positions are treated, is evident in my case."

Li Fu, the Imperial Councillor, Revised the *Qieyun* Li Fu, a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, was the son of Li Fu, who passed the imperial examination based on the *Kaifeng Rituals*. He wrote short essays and enjoyed writing, and the court valued his extensive learning and knowledge of rituals and music. People called him the repository of Zhou rituals, relying on ancient texts. Before the *Guangming* era, the *Qieyun* often used Wu dialect sounds, and the characters for 'clear' and 'blue' did not need to be distinguished. Li Fu revised the *Qieyun*, completely removing Wu dialect sounds. As he was advancing, he informed the chancellor, and all agreed. Soon after, the rebel forces breached the palace gates, and thus the reform was put to rest. To this day, no one dares to think about phonology. However, I have seen the *Yunquan* criticized, and correcting Wu dialect sounds is very appropriate. I wonder why the Eight Seats are preventing this. Alas, in ancient times, the paper used for inscribing names and titles was called 'name paper'. During the *Dazhong* era, Xue Baosun, the leader of the examination, set an example by writing *menzhuang* (visiting cards). Later, he listed his thoughts in the *qishi* (petition) and presented it at the chancellor's door, calling it *menzhuang* and *menqi*. Though more complex than name paper, it was convenient for the time. The letter says, "I respectfully await the arrival of the Chief Secretary, followed by [the next official]." To this day, it remains unchanged. If the court were to change it, it would be a good thing.

**Minister Li Fu’s Reform of the Qieyun** Minister Li Fu, son of Li Fu Xiang of the Tang dynasty, passed the imperial examination based on the *Opening Rituals* (Kaiyuan Li) and, although [one version reads "not"], was known for writing small essays and enjoying scholarly composition. The imperial court valued his extensive knowledge of rites and music, and officials often consulted him on such matters. At that time, he was known among his contemporaries as the "Repository of Zhou Rites," as he drew upon ancient texts. Before the *Guangming* era, the *Qieyun* (a rhyme dictionary) largely used the Wu pronunciation, and the characters *qing* and *qing* were not necessarily distinguished. Li Fu revised the *Qieyun* [one version includes "its upper tone"], completely removing the Wu pronunciation. When the time was right, he presented his revision to the prime ministers, who all approved. However, shortly after, the rebel Chao attacked the imperial palace, and thus the reform was abandoned. To this day, no one dares to concern themselves with phonology. Yet I have seen the *Yunquan* (a later rhyme book), which is biased and flawed, yet its correction of the Wu pronunciation is quite accurate. It is a pity that the eight high-ranking officials (the *Ba Zuo*) did not impose any restrictions on this matter. Alas! In ancient times, the practice of writing one’s name on a sheet of paper, inscribing one’s surname and title, was called *mingzhi* (name paper). During the *Dazhong* era, Xue Baoxun became a prominent figure in the examination hall, and others followed his example, composing *menzhuang* (door notes). Later, people continued to list their intentions in petitions, delivering them to the gates of high officials, which came to be called *menzhuang*. Though *menzhuang* and *menqi* (door petitions) are more elaborate than *mingzhi*, they each suited their time. The *Book of Documents* says: "With respectful greetings, the official of the Office of the Emperor’s Daily Activities, [such-and-such official]." This means that the title *qiju lang* (official of the Office of the Emperor’s Daily Activities) comes first, followed by the official's rank. Yet to this day, the order is reversed, and no one has corrected it. If the court were to make this change, it would be a fine thing.

**The Unfortunate Fate of the Mu and Li Families** During the Tang Dynasty, Li Hang, a censor, had a son named Li Meim, who was well-regarded and influential in his time. After the Yellow Turban Rebellion, Li Meim supported and cared for the virtuous in their return to their villa in the Eastern Capital. He traveled with the censor Mu Yanhui and stayed overnight at the public hall in the state of Guo. The next day, they paid respects to the governor of the prefecture, Zhang Cun, who was a subordinate of Wang Gong. Zhang Cun told the official in charge of guests, "I have received a favor from the Mu family. Now that the censor Mu is passing through, I must show my gratitude." The official went to the inn and spoke to Mu, who then wrote a letter to express his thanks. Governor Zhang became very angry and said, "Who said this?" He answered that it was the official in charge of guests. Zhang then ordered the execution of the official. Mu was surprised and disappointed and returned to the inn. Soon after, he sent someone to harm him. The censor Li did not understand what was happening and was soon also killed to eliminate any witnesses. At the time, Li Gong Yi, who was staying near the villa of the virtuous, had no means to save himself and thus shared the same fate. Later, his brothers complained about the injustice. In a dream, Hang told his family members, "Zhang Cun has already asked God and will die soon; indeed, he was killed by Wang Gong." The Keeper of Light once read in Li Zhao's *Supplement to the National History*: "Li Gong Yi once released a condemned prisoner who later met him on the road and was grateful. He invited him home, and his wife discussed what to give him. She thought the gift was too small, and the man said, 'If the gift is too small, it's better to kill him.' Li quickly ran away and met a hero who saved him from this disaster. He often thought this was a fairy tale." Strange indeed.

Wang Zuo, the Right Attendant, was known for his uprightness and integrity. A man from a distinguished family, he took pride in his own firmness and honesty, and he set an example for his descendants by emphasizing the importance of uprightness. He disliked people who were gentle or lacking in strength. He had always been adept at accumulating wealth and resources through diligent effort and business acumen. Before Huang Chou, a military leader, had once governed Changzhou, the capital city was in a state of chaos and disorder, and Wang Zuo had been wandering in exile, often in a state of uncertainty. However, he had gained considerable reputation and was summoned back by an urgent imperial edict. His return preparations were lavish, and he transported his belongings by both water and land in separate batches. He arrived at Gan Tang, where Wang Gong, the governor of the region, was in power. Wang Gong ignored the king's orders and acted with cruelty and violence. When Wang Zuo heard that Wang Gong was expecting him, he believed that he would be treated as a junior member of the family, implying a position of respect but not equality. Wang Zuo was welcomed and served with great diligence, but Wang Gong looked down on military men and did not treat him with the same respect. Wang Gong then held a grand banquet in the inner hall, arranging precious treasures and beautiful objects in rows. Musicians and entertainers were placed in the center, and Wang Gong's wife also appeared. Wang Gong assumed a serious and respectful demeanor and addressed Wang Zuo, saying, "Although I am a mere military officer, I have been honored with this position. Today, I am fortunate to meet you, a nobleman passing through. I would not dare to neglect your presence." Wang Zuo replied, "I would be honored to be treated as a junior member of your family." Wang Gong refused, insisting that Wang Zuo accept his respect. Wang Gong became furious and said, "The king's schedule is limited, and I cannot delay your departure. I will immediately dismiss the banquet and arrange for your departure." He secretly instructed his subordinates to order the destruction of Wang Zuo's family. The entire family, including the servants, were thrown into the Yellow River. Wang Zuo's belongings were found in several hundred bags, which were lost when they drowned while traveling by boat. The incident was reported to the court, but due to the many pressing matters, it was ignored. Wang Zuo's son, who was traveling to Xiangzhou at the time, drowned in a well without any apparent reason. Although Wang Gong was known for his violent and oppressive behavior, he did not understand the will of heaven. Bao Guangzi commented, "Uprightness has its place in action, and there is a time to use and a time to set aside. To balance strength with gentleness is the right approach. In times of decline and disorder, one must act according to the appropriate time. If Wang Gong had accepted Wang Zuo's respect, what harm could it have done? Refusing bribes is a sign of integrity, but treading on the tail of a tiger leads to one's own destruction. It is a tragic outcome."

**裴杨操尚** **Moral and Conduct of the Pei and Yang Families** - **唐杨収段文昌皆以孤进贵为宰相率爱奢侈杨相女适裴坦长子嫁资丰厚什器多用金银坦尚俭闻之不乐一日与国号及儿女辈到新妇院台上用碟盛果实坦欣然视碟子内乃卧鱼犀遽推倒茶台拂袖而出乃曰破我家也他日収相果以纳赂竟至不令宜哉** - **Yang Shou and Duan Wenchang, both humble-born chancellors, were fond of luxury. Yang Shou's daughter married Pei Tan's eldest son, and the dowry was rich, with many utensils made of gold and silver. Pei Tan, who preferred frugality, was unhappy when he heard about it. One day, he and his family went to the new bride's room, where a dish containing a sleeping fish-shaped rhinoceros was placed. He immediately pushed over the tea table and left in anger, saying, "Ruining my family!" Later, Yang Shou was indeed implicated in bribery and was dismissed from office.** **出腹不生养卢侍郎** **Lu Wo, the Scholar-Official** - **卢氏衣冠第一歴代未尝知举干符中卢携在中书歉宗人无掌文柄乃擢羣从陜虢观察使卢渥知礼闱是嵗十二月黄巢犯阙僖皇播迁举子星散迨収复京都裴贽连知三举渥有羡色赵崇大夫戏之曰閤下所谓出腹不生养主司也** - **The Lu family, which has been the first among the gentry for generations, has never known to hold a position. In the year 874, Lu Xie, who was in the Central Secretariat, felt sorry for his relatives who did not hold positions, and he promoted his distant relatives to be the governor of Shaanxi and Guo. Lu Wo, who knew the examination hall, in the twelfth month of that year, Huang Chao attacked the imperial palace, Emperor Xizong fled, scholars scattered, until the capital was recaptured, and Pei Zhi was responsible for multiple examinations. Lu Wo envied him, and Zhao Chong, a scholar-official, joked, "You are the examiner who does not raise or nurture those born from the womb."**

**Zhang Xingshi and the Monk at the Gate** During the Tang Dynasty, Chancellor Zhang Jun had two sons. One was named Zhang Ge, with the courtesy name Ge, who served as a chancellor in the Later Shu state. The other son was named Zhang Xingshi, who later became a general named Li, with the courtesy name Yan. Both brothers followed in their father's footsteps and were known for their outstanding qualities and somewhat cunning nature. One day, Zhang Xingshi left his house and saw a monk at the gate. He did not remember the monk's name but recalled that the chancellor had ordered the monk to be beaten seven times. The monk had committed some unknown offense, but no one knew what it was. Soon after, the chancellor summoned the monk to sit and comfort him. When the chancellor asked the monk why he had been beaten, the monk replied that he had been told to do so by the chancellor. The chancellor was shocked and apologized, saying, "May the teacher be compassionate." This story is the nineteenth entry in the text and is similar to the story of Lu Wo, the governor of Shaanxi and Guo, and Lu Hang, the inspector of Shaanxi. It is likely that Lu Wo and Lu Hang are the same person, as noted by Sun Shixian.

Di Guichang, the Right Chancellor, despised purple-robed monks. Whenever he visited temples, he would often recite a line from a poem by his predecessors: "As I passed through the bamboo courtyard, I met a monk and had a brief conversation, gaining a moment of leisure in this fleeting life." However, he would distance himself from those who wore purple robes. Zheng Gu, the Langzhong, also loved monks and compared them to Sichuan tea, saying, "Sichuan tea and monks are not necessarily all good, but I do not wish to abandon them." Monk Luan had exceptional talent but was not bound by conventional rules. In his early years, he was known as "Qingyu" and served the noble family of Xue in the state of Jiaozhou. The eight officials there, due to his unconventional nature, found it difficult to accept him as a scholar, so they made him enter the monastic life. He shaved his head and donned robes in front of a hundred-foot Buddha statue but refused to take a teacher from among ordinary monks. Later, he entered the capital and served as a literary attendant, granted the purple robe. Liu Pin, the Grand Councillor, greatly admired his talent, and Zhang, the Prime Minister of Rent and Labor, also held him in high regard and spoke highly of his potential. As a result, Monk Luan returned to his original state, took the name Xianyu Feng, and presented a letter to Liu Gong. Liu Gong despised him and did not receive him. He then went to see Zhang, the Prime Minister, who also rejected him. Disappointed, he became the Magistrate of Jiangxi under Li Chuan and later served as a junior general in the West, eventually meeting his demise in Huangzhou. Zhang Ao was arrogant. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty, Chen Fan, the governor of Suzhou, who had risen through military ranks and was known for his strict and decisive nature, held a banquet. Zhang Ao, a scholar who relied on his talent and was arrogant, criticized Chen Fan's favorite concubine, Zhang Xiaotai, and became angry. Chen Fan was even more furious and asked, "According to this, how many strokes should he receive?" Zhang Ao replied, "Only these two lines are enough to receive three or five strokes. To seek a smile, it would be worth spending a thousand or ten thousand gold pieces. With a snap of the golden whip, he was dead at thirteen." It is a pity that he perished due to his arrogance and talent. This story is recorded in Liu Shanfu's *Idle Talks*, but many details are not included. The governor of Mianzhou, Sichuan, named Li, was known as "Kekou" and rose to his position through military merit. He was fond of...

Among the guests, there was a wine companion named Li Jianbai, who had a rough talent for writing. Li, the guest, asked him, "Why is your name Jianbai?" Li Jianbai replied, "Do not change it; 'Shiyuanliang' and 'Junxiong' are power, after all." There was also Jiang Yigong, who enjoyed mocking and singing, and often suffered greatly from this, yet still could not change his ways. Scholars in Shu liked to wear socks and trousers. Jiang said to them, "If you are a scholar, why do you wear a loose hat instead of an official hat?" Jiang's feet were all like this. [Though Jiang enjoyed mocking and singing, his conversation was always refined and scholarly. Anyone who mocked or ridiculed him was considered a vulgar person, and thus he was disliked by the gentry. Recently, he heard that he had become an official and died, which was fortunate.] **Dong Li's Nephew** During the Tang dynasty, Liu Zhan, the chancellor, was known for his integrity and fame. He and his brother Aichu had both attained the Dao and entered the records of immortals. He married into the Li family and had a son named Liu Zan. After the chancellor died, Liu Zan became an orphan and was very foolish. He was taught to read, but he never remembered. His uncle, Li Yinheng, the Censor-in-Chief, said that because the Liu family must have an heir, he often beat him with a stick. Li's wife, out of compassion, could not bear to see him suffer and lamented his fate. One day, without warning, he left, and there was no way to find him, so she remembered him and cried like a rope tied around her heart. No one knew if he was alive or dead, but after several years, he returned, and mother and son were reunited. He said, "I entered Mount Song and met a white-clad old man who said, 'I will open your heart, and you will surely become intelligent in the future.' From then on, he read one volume every day and developed literary talent, passing the imperial examination and becoming a scholar in the Imperial Academy, joining the ranks of the talented. His uncle, Li Yiheng, was sent as an envoy to Fanyu, where he was detained by the King of Yue and died as the governor of Guang. I am familiar with Liu Zan's nephew, Qu, who said that there should be one person in our family who has attained the Dao, just like the white-clad old man."

**Translation:** Li Peng Encounters Sang Dao Mao During the Tang dynasty, the magistrate of Shengtang County, Li Peng, encountered Sang Dao Mao, who said to him, "Your position is limited to this one county alone. Your two sons, the elder will reach the highest rank of the court, and the younger will hold several prefectures. Their descendants will follow this prophecy. The eldest son, named Shi, will become a general and enter the government. His descendants will pass the imperial examinations for two generations, without interruption, and will eventually reach high ranks such as Shangshu, Gujian, and Junmu. They will have many grandsons, all of whom will hold high positions, maintaining the family's scholarly reputation. How remarkable is this foresight!" Kong Shilang Borrows an Oil Cloth During the Tang dynasty, Kong Zheng, a Shilang, was on his way back from court when it rained heavily. He did not bring an oil cloth and sought shelter under the eaves of an old man's house. The rain was pouring down, and it was already past noon. The old man thought he might be hungry from the morning and invited him in for a meal. Soon, an old man wearing a black hat and silk scarf came out to greet him with great respect. He prepared a meal with exquisite delicacies, better than those of a noble family. Kong Zheng felt ashamed and thanked him, asking for an oil cloth. The old man said, "I don't go out in the cold, wind, or rain, so I never keep an oil cloth. However, I have already sent someone to get one for you." Kong Zheng was so impressed that he forgot his official duties. Later, he told his colleagues about this wonderful experience. In ancient times, the wealthy were compared to feudal lords. According to the *Hong Fan* (Great Pattern), one of the five blessings is wealth. The sages valued wealth as a means to achieve nobility. Are these people not examples of this? There was another county magistrate who was in a hurry and went to an empty house. He entered the toilet area, which was a large, empty room. Suddenly, a performer appeared, and the magistrate felt ashamed and thanked him. The performer said, "If you are in a hurry, just come here." People who heard about this found it extremely amusing.

**Translation:** Playful Anecdotes of the Sages During the Tang Dynasty, Pei Jinfu, a prominent statesman, was not conventionally handsome. He wrote a self-praise that read: "My body is not tall, my appearance is not tall. Why should I lead, and why should I serve as an advisor?" A subordinate in his camp humbly praised him, saying, "The Right Honorable One is superior as an advisor." Pei laughed and said, "The scholars all like to believe in humility." The staff all felt shocked and left. Li Guang, from Bohai, was known for his literary talent, as were his brothers. During a journey, he stayed in a village by the river at a commoner's house and saw a lead and tin Buddha on a cover. He used clever words to praise it, saying, "It just hasn't been blessed yet because the temple is far away." The commoner replied, "Why must it be a monk? I can do it too." Li Guang then lit incense and offered sincere prayers, bowing and speaking clearly: "Lead and tin Buddha, soft and gentle World Honored One. On the cover, it is dignified and has ten measures of merit. Reciting the Great Wisdom that leads to the Other Shore." Zhaolin, an official, served as an attendant to Peitan in the southern region. He was very ugly, and Peitan joked with him, saying, "Originally, Zhao Gong was not ugly. When he was a baby, the wet nurse often patted him and said, 'Make him ugly, make him ugly,' and thus he became so." Zhaolin laughed loudly. Xue, the Attendant Lang, had a certain temperament.

A Dull Appearance and Thick Lips: Du Mu, Wen Tingyun, and Others Du Mu, known for his dull appearance, and Wen Tingyun, whose lips were thick, both had nicknames that did not match their talent and reputation. Wen Tingyun was nicknamed "Wen Zhong Kui" (one version says "Wen Fang"). Xue Zhi, a scholar-official, before passing the imperial examinations, went to a shop to buy shoes. The shopkeeper asked, "Which pair is for the excellent scholar?" Xue replied, "It was made for Zhaowei, but never established a rank." Du Deyang, a scholar-official, and his brothers were in financial difficulty and needed to borrow money to help with urgent expenses. They summoned wealthy people from the same neighborhood to their house and asked, "How much is the principal and interest?" One of them left by brushing off his sleeves. Kong Zhaowei, after receiving an official position, was visited by actors and musicians from the music bureau, each seeking profit. Shi Yezhu, a performer, arrived first and received a gift from the official. The official said, "There is a lot lacking in the house, so I cannot provide a generous gift. If there are any pigs, please do not mention it." Another performer arrived later, and the official asked for his flute, calling him near and pointing to the flute's hole and asking, "Which one is *Huan Xisha*?" Kong Longzi, a performer, laughed at it. Daoist priest Chen Zixiao, while ascending the upper platform of Mount Hua, accidentally fell. Yuwen Han, a scholar-official, wrote a letter to tease him, saying, "Do not know if he can ascend, and find it strange." Xuan Si and others were all clear, virtuous, and playfully gathered together, what harm is there?

The Bell-Pulling Crow of Jingzhao Prefecture During the Tang Dynasty, Wang Zhiang served as the Prefect of Jingzhao and was known for his strictness and willingness to punish. The people of the capital feared him. One day, he heard the sound of a bell being pulled. He ordered officials to look under the eaves but found no one. The sound was heard three times before a crow appeared. The Prefect said, "This must be someone stealing the crow's chick and complaining about injustice." He ordered the officials to follow the crow wherever it went and catch the thief. The crow circled and led the officials to a tree outside the city where indeed someone was stealing its chick, which was still resting under the tree. The official then arrested him and sent him to the prefecture. The case of a bird complaining about injustice is unusual, so the person who caught the chick was killed and the matter reported. The Heavenly Emperor Summons a Chess Player During the reign of Emperor Xizong of Tang, a scholar named Hua Neng, known for his exceptional chess skills, rarely found an equal opponent. One day, a young man named Zhang, aged only fourteen, came to visit and requested a game, asking for a concession of one move. Hua Neng's thoughts were slow, and he pondered for a long time before placing a piece. Zhang responded immediately and did not seem to care. He continued to play, taking turns with Hua Neng. One day, when the Yellow Bandits attacked the palace, Emperor Xizong fled to Shu. Hua Neng, serving as a scholar, planned to follow the emperor and intended to take the route through Jin Prefecture. As he prepared to leave, Zhang said, "There is no need to go further. I am not a chess player. The Heavenly Emperor has summoned me to play chess with you. Please give me instructions on how to manage your household." Hua Neng was shocked, his wife and children wept, and he died suddenly. Later, the people of the capital all knew about this. In the past, Confucius and Ziyuan were known as scholars of the underworld, and Li He was summoned by the emperor to compose poetry. Is this similar to what is said? The Heavenly Emperor referred to here is not the Northern Pole Emperor. According to the *True Scripture*, it is not the Northern Black Emperor or the Daoist Lord. This is the Northern Emperor of the City of the Dead, also known as the Ghost Emperor. Those who have great virtue can be summoned by the Northern Emperor to request the Four Bright Scholars. Is the command to summon the chess player from the Emperor of the City of Punishment? It is similar to what is said in the *True Scripture*, so I will briefly mention it.

The Physician Liang Zhao Yi In ancient times, there were physicians who could diagnose diseases without relying on pulse-taking and immediately knew the cause, and such patients would certainly be cured. During the Tang Dynasty, Duke Cui of Wei was stationed at the residence of a wealthy merchant in the town of Zhu. One night, the merchant died suddenly. By dawn, his breath was still not completely gone. A physician from Wuling, known as Liang Xinhua (or Liang Xingong), heard about this and went to examine the body. He said, "This is a case of poisoning from food. Was it not from external food?" The servant replied, "The master rarely leaves the ship and does not eat food from others." Liang Xinhua asked, "What does he usually eat?" The servant answered, "He loves eating bamboo partridges, and he consumes hundreds of them each year. Recently, he bought bamboo partridges to be used as food." Liang Xinhua said, "Bamboo partridges eat pinellia, so it must be pinellia poisoning." He ordered the servant to pound ginger and squeeze out the juice, then pour it down the master's throat. The master then revived. Duke Cui of Wei heard of this and found it remarkable. He called Liang Xinhua to his office, comforted him, and praised him. He provided him with servants, horses, money, and silk, and sent him to the capital to write letters to officials. His reputation greatly increased, and he rose to the position of Imperial Physician. A court official visited him, and Liang Xinhua was highly regarded.

A person asked, "Why wasn't this shown earlier? The wind disease has already become severe. Please return quickly to handle family matters and submit to fate." The court officials heard and hurriedly withdrew, riding their horses back home. At that time, there was a horse doctor from Fu Prefecture named Zhao E, who had just arrived in the capital. He posted his name and claimed to be skilled in medicine on a busy street. This court official dismounted and informed him. Zhao E also said that the illness was already critical, agreeing with what Mr. Liang had said. He advised, "There is only one method: please eat more pears to relieve the condition. Without limit, chew them or squeeze out the juice to drink, perhaps there is a chance." This court official then rode his horse back, using a bamboo tube to store the pears and eating them while riding. Upon arriving home, he ate pears for ten days and suddenly felt refreshed; his illness did not return. He later visited Mr. Zhao and expressed his gratitude, then visited Mr. Liang and explained that he received advice from Mr. Zhao. Mr. Liang was surprised and said, "A great country must have someone who follows in succession." He then summoned Mr. Zhao, providing him with servants, horses, money, and silk to widely spread his fame. Mr. Zhao eventually became a High-ranking official.

**Final Translation:** Treating Doubtful Illnesses The learned doctor Yuan Wan spoke of a woman during the Tang dynasty who, among her in-laws, had once mistakenly eaten a bug and often doubted it. As a result, she fell ill, and despite frequent treatments, she did not recover. A physician from the capital, whose name was forgotten, realized that her condition required a specific treatment. He asked the cautious and discreet servant of the family to warn her in advance: "Now, with the medicine, you will vomit and have diarrhea. Hold it in a tray or basin. When you vomit, just say that a small frog has gone away. But do not let the lady know that this is a lie." The servant followed the instructions, and the illness was permanently cured. He also said that there was a young man who often saw a small mirror in his eyes. The physician Zhao Qing diagnosed him and arranged to meet the young man the next morning to serve him raw fish slices. The young man arrived as agreed and was invited into a small room. He was told to wait calmly until the guests left before proceeding. Soon, a table was set up, and a bowl of mustard vinegar was placed on it, with no other dishes. Zhao Qing had not yet appeared, and by midday, the young man had waited for a long time. He was very hungry and, smelling the vinegar, could not help but take a small sip. He then took another sip and felt a sudden clarity in his chest, but his vision became blurry. He drank the entire bowl. Zhao Qing, realizing what had happened, finally appeared. The young man apologized for drinking the vinegar, saying he was embarrassed. Zhao Qing replied, "Young master, you ate too much raw fish. Without vinegar, it would not taste good. There was also fish skin in your chest, which is why your vision was blurred. The mustard vinegar I prepared was only meant for you to drink because you were hungry." The young man's illness was cured. The use of mustard vinegar as a treatment was a temporary deception, and Zhao Qing suggested, "Please step back and plan for other wonderful meals." Such cases are not within the realm of ordinary physicians. All those who wish to seek sustenance through the art of medicine should strive diligently.

Unintentional Disaster During the Tang Dynasty, Du Yanlin, a court official, was riding one day when his horse suddenly reared and fell, its hoof deeply embedded in the stirrup. He could not pull his leg free and was dragged along, taking a step at a time, until he died. As the ancients said, "In riding a horse, one must always be cautious, for there is a risk of death at every moment." It is known that one should always be mindful when riding a horse. Du Shentong, the brother of the Prime Minister, also held a position in the imperial court. One day, while riding his horse into the palace gate, he was struck by the lintel, causing his neck to bend downward, his head to tilt forward, and his shoulders to rise above his head, a condition that persisted for the rest of his life. The Prince of Jingzhou, a lover of horses, was willing to spend thousands of gold pieces on them, but never found one to his liking. Emperor Zhou ordered the eunuch Li Tingyu to give a horse to Prince Nanping and asked what kind of horse he preferred. The prince replied, "Among ten thousand good horses, there is not one as swift as this. If it can be ridden comfortably and without the trouble of controlling it, then it is suitable for me." Such a horse would not bite or need to be restrained with a bit or mouthpiece, and in battle formation, ten thousand riders would act as one. If the horse had not been castrated, it would be filled with chaotic and fierce energy, and the rider, clad in armor, would struggle to control it, let alone turn left or right, release the reins, and command the troops. Since then, horses in Jiangnan and Shu have often been castrated for easier riding. Thus, those who show off their agility are admired by the eyes, while those who are gentle are convenient for the body. The difficulty of encountering a true gentleman is second only to the challenge of finding someone who can perform the delicate task of castrating horses.

Cui Shu Eats the Dragon's Child Cui Shu, a minor official of the Tang Dynasty, had a fish brought up from a well by his household. He was fond of fresh food and thought it was a mistake by the cook who had dropped the fish into the well. He ordered it to be cooked and ate it. Suddenly, he dreamed of being led by a guard official who read a judgment that said: "A low-ranking official in the human world has eaten the dragon's child; all official positions and titles shall be stripped away." One year later, Cui Shu died. Despite having a good reputation, he did not rise to a high position. Was it fate that he made this mistake? Execution by Secret Force In the Tang Dynasty, Xue Zhun served as a Langzhong (a low-ranking official). After a period of chaos, he neglected to care for his step-mother and wandered around the Jianghuai region. He stayed at Taoist temples and traveled to the southern part of China, reaching the Geshao Temple in Jizhou. There, he encountered a Taoist priest performing a Huangluo ritual. Xue Zhun joined the ceremony and suddenly let out a painful cry, saying, "I am hit by an arrow! Quickly bring me a brush and ink!" He then composed a poem on the spot: "The deep kindness of the state is hard to repay, Furthermore, leaving the step-mother to go to another state. Who knows if the anger of heaven can be appeased? Accumulated evil throughout life, begging for the end of one's life." He died shortly after. In the year Xinyou of Tianfu (a year in the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches system), this man must have committed some evil deeds that led to his secret execution. In ancient historical records, those who were known for their extreme filial piety toward their step-mothers were recorded in the biographies of notable individuals. This is because it is difficult for ordinary people to practice such virtue, but those who do so can be trusted to treat their biological mothers with the same respect. How can one treat a step-mother as a stranger? Xue Zhun's sudden death can serve as a warning to others.

Cui Yong Eats His Son's Meat [With Li Kei and Su Xun Attached] During the Xintong era of the Tang Dynasty, Pang Xun rebelled in Xuzhou. At that time, Cui Yong was the governor of Hézhōu and was captured by Pang Xun and brought to Pénmén. Cui Yong was skilled in charming conversation and humble words, using these to comply with Pang Xun, hoping to alleviate his own misfortune. Pang Xun also treated him very well. His son, Shaòjùn, who was carefree and close to Pang Xun, often engaged in drinking, gambling, and playing with a fan. Cui Yong was worried about the family's honor due to his son's association with the rebels. He told his son, "You should be familiar with him and find an opportunity to kill him. Everyone dies, but if one dies in the right place, what do I have to regret?" His son received the order and secretly carried a sharp knife. Suddenly, he changed color and trembled, which made Pang Xun suspicious and surprised. Pang Xun then searched his pockets and found a dagger. He ordered the son to be cooked. The next day, Pang Xun summoned Cui Yong to a drinking party. After the meal, he asked, "Is the meat delicious?" Cui Yong replied, "The taste is precious and filling." Pang Xun said, "This is your noble son's meat." He also ordered Cui Yong to be killed. Later, Huang Cháo entered Guangzhou and captured the governor, Li Kēi, bringing him to Jingzhou. Huang Cháo ordered Li Kēi to draft a memorial stating his intentions. Li Kēi said, "My relatives are everywhere in the court, and generations have received national favor. Even if I cut off my wrist, I will not write the memorial." Soon, Li Kēi was killed at Jiangjin. During the Tang and Song dynasties, Su Xún, a Shangshu, flattered and acted opportunistically. Liáng Tài Zǔ despised him. Once, Su Xún went to Píngmén to pay respects to the Prince of Jin. At that time, Zhang Chéngyè was just thinking about restoring the rightful ruler. Su Xún suddenly presented a pair of painted imperial brushes to the Prince of Jin. Zhang Chéngyè despised him even more. Compared to Cui Yong and Li Kēi, Su Xún's character was indeed far apart.

### Final Translation **Wang Di's Encounter with the Ox-Carts** Wang Di, a Shèrén (high-ranking official), was known for his early talent and diligence, yet his future success was uncertain. One day, he visited the Prime Minister Du, the Taivéi (a high military rank), at his residence, where the crossroads street was slightly narrow. Two ox-drawn carts were approaching from east and west, and Wang Di's horse was caught in between. The horse was startled, fell down, and lay on its side, with its nose being run over by the cart wheels, more than an inch deep, but the toes remained unharmed. Three days later, he entered the Hanlin Academy to pay respects. Although he was fortunate to escape, it was also divine assistance. **Du Ruxiu and the Blue Lotus Flowers** Du Ruxiu, the nephew of Han Wengong Yu of the Tang Dynasty, had a strange story about planting flowers in a novel. Du, the Jigshi Ruxiu, was in charge of Huzhou. There was a dyeing household's pond where blue lotus flowers grew. The governor sent a letter to ask the dyeing worker, saying, "In my family, there are three generations of people who have been treating indigo jars, always soaking lotus seeds at the bottom of the jars, waiting for years before planting them. If the blue lotus seeds are planted, they will turn red. It is still the original nature, so what is there to be surprised about?" He then sent the soaked lotus seeds to him. The Taoist priest Tian Kuangtu personally saw this flower and told me about it. I see that nowadays, people mix chicken manure with soil to cultivate peony flower clusters, and the light red ones all turn deep red. The dyeing worker's words are even more credible. **Omens of White Flowers** When the first ruler of Shu was about to pass away, that year, all the bodhi trees on Mount Emei bloomed with white flowers. Several years before the Xian Wang of Jingzhou passed away, all the ditches, canals, and city gates bloomed with white lotus flowers. One is due to the influence of qi, and the other is a sign of an omen. What could this mean?

Yan Junrong, a eunuch of the Tang Dynasty, was known for his benevolence among his peers. He claimed to have served as the Northern Marshal and wore the attire of a ceremonial official, attending to matters without formal protocol. He also mentioned that the Imperial Military Commissioner had a small office with three rooms, used primarily for bookshelves, and had no official duties, only issuing public orders after adding notes to official documents. This was a reference to Yang Fugong, who had seized the power of the chancellor. Since then, Yan Junrong often thought of retiring. One day, he suddenly went mad, dancing with his hands and feet, and his family was surprised. Beside him was a cat and a dog. The cat said to the dog, "The eunuch has changed his usual behavior; he has gone mad." The dog replied, "Don't worry about him; let him be." Soon, he stopped dancing, shocked and laughing at himself, finding the cat and dog's words strange. When Emperor Zhaozong was exiled to Fengxiang, Yan Junrong requested retirement. The Shu army conquered Xingyuan from the region of Liangzhou or Liangzhou and moved to Jian'nan, relying on Wang Xianzhu. He built a villa to live in at Qingcheng Mountain. He lived to be over eighty and died. He was loyal, upright, modest, and restrained, and he was the peer of Xiemen Jiyuan. At that time, the purge of eunuchs was carried out, but only Shu did not obey the decree, thus avoiding disaster. His family had a record of the governance and disorder of the Northern Office, titled "Eight Volumes of the North Office," which detailed the loyalty, deceit, and good or evil of eunuchs. I have heard of this text but have not yet seen it. It is said that eunuchs like Yan Junrong are not all evil; it is due to the excessive suspicion of officials from the south, leading to discord, which is indeed a misfortune for the state. Before this, Lu Yan, the chancellor, was moved from Chengdu to Zhubing Palace. The horse he was riding suddenly spoke in human language, saying, "The reed and bulrush flowers bloom, and after they bloom, there is no home for the road." Soon, he faced disaster. Is it due to some supernatural influence? The speaking stone in Jin is similar to this.

Duan Family's Wine and Roasting Establishment Duan Gong, a respected scholar of the Tang dynasty, came from a family that rarely produced famous officials. His grandfather, Jing, the former Shilang (a high-ranking official), was not adept at managing his livelihood and was in dire straits. Within the capital city, there was a piece of vacant land adjacent to a powerful eunuch. The eunuch desired this land, but it was only worth five or six hundred thousand coins. Duan Gong happily offered this land to the eunuch without mentioning the price, saying, "What is convenient for the general, I dare not disobey." He explained that he had a reason to travel to Jiangsu and Anhui and sought protection from three or four places. The eunuch was pleased and wrote a letter, and in total, Duan Gong received three thousand strings of cash, thus being fortunate to be saved. In the eastern market, there was another piece of vacant land that was low-lying and stagnant. Duan Gong bought it at a low price and had the maids bring a tray of fried cakes to attract children. If a child threw a brick or tile, they received a paper ticket for one cake. Children ran around throwing bricks and tiles, gambling for fried cakes. Soon, six or seven out of ten were filled with tickets. Then, they filled the land with good soil and built a shop. They stopped Persian merchants, earning one string of cash daily. Others followed suit, and thus, Duan Gong became wealthy. He invited guests and officials, and at the time, they called him frivolous, dubbing the place the "Wine and Roasting Establishment." He could also help others obtain official ranks, gathering people with food and drink, and seeking profit as one means of doing so. Duan Hui and Duan Yong had no literary talent but gained fame due to the power of drinking and eating. This account was obtained from the Yuan period, recorded by several virtuous officials of the Office of Censorate. The story of Pei Mingli buying a house is the same as the Duan family, and it is suspected that the Duan family imitated Pei Mingli's actions.

Li Changfu's Praise of Maidservants During the Xiantong era of the Tang dynasty, Li Changfu, a candidate for the imperial examinations, was known for his poetic talent but had not yet passed the exams. He often neglected the preparation of his examination scrolls, so he came up with a clever idea. He wrote fifty poems about maidservants and circulated them among the high-ranking officials. One of his poems read: "Chunniang loves to go to a tavern on the upper floor; she doesn't mind coming back late and doesn't leave. She claims that the lady of the house is asleep and asks to stay until she finishes combing her hair." Another poem said: "Regardless of whether it's autumn chrysanthemums or spring flowers, each can drink tea on an empty stomach. Don't let them enter the house too often. One needs a bit of idle time." All the poems hit on the taboo topics of maidservants. Within ten days, the capital was filled with the circulation of his poems. The maidservants and their like were furious and criticized him fiercely, all wanting to slap his face. That year, he passed the examination. Although the story of the peach stick and tiger boots is different, using a clever method is the same. The Effectiveness of the Knowledge of Fate Pill for General Zhong During the Tang dynasty, General Zhong, a military officer under the Guangnan Circuit Governor, whose name is unknown, wandered and stayed in Lingzhou in his later years. He often stayed in temples. County Magistrate Ouyang Shu, who felt sorry for General Zhong's old age, often invited him to stay. During the hot summer days, he fell ill with abdominal pain and lay in Ouyang Shu's house. He did not eat for over a month. County Magistrate Ouyang was worried that he would die soon and wanted to submit a report to the state office to get a statement from General Zhong about his actions. General Zhong said, "I am sick, but I am not going to die. Since I am already troubled, what harm is there in reporting?" He reported it to the officials. After that, his illness improved. Bao Guangzi, who was an assistant to the prefect at the time, kindly visited and asked about the reason for the report. He said, "I once encountered a battle in Xiangtan and did not advance. With several fellow merchants, we held a vegetarian meal at Yue Lu Temple. The temple monk had newly prepared the Knowledge of Fate Pill. He also said that after taking this medicine, one would..."

After taking the seaweed decoction or when the appointed time is near, there is a slight pain in the ribs. Once the pill is excreted, one must arrange family matters in preparation for the end. Then each paid a string of coins to swallow a pill. Later, when entering Shu and arriving at Le County, they met a merchant who had also taken the pill and was staying in Le County. They were able to reminisce about the past and discuss the great efficacy of the medicine. Soon after, this gentleman reported a pain in his ribs. The pill soon passed, and he quickly arranged his family affairs. Twenty days later, he died. I was amazed by the medicine, which was taken with a seaweed decoction. He bathed in fragrant water and then swallowed it. The medicine from yesterday had not yet passed, so I knew he was not dead. He also showed me the medicine. However, Zhong Gong had a ruddy complexion, drank strongly, and seemed to benefit from the medicine's power. I do not know what happened to him afterward, but because it was effective in predicting his fate, I recorded it. In the Juexing Temple in Chengdu, there was a monk who compounded this medicine and sold it. Many people took it. The Northern Dream Trivia Volume Ten The Northern Dream Trivia Volume Eleven Tang Dynasty, Sun Guangxian, compiled Zhang Zhifang Praises Pei Xiu In the Tang Dynasty, Zhang Zhifang, a great general of the Gold and White Guards, was a distinguished and talented military officer. He was known for his hospitality to guests, musicians, and singers, and his musical instruments were the best among his peers. When Pei Xiu, the Chancellor, was about to take the imperial examination, he wore mourning clothes and was preparing to take the test. Zhang Zhifang admired his demeanor and sought to meet him through Pei. Pei treated him with special courtesy. Later, in the court, Pei highly praised Pei Xiu's literary talent, which surprised the court officials. The Chancellor was concerned about potential complications and could not find a suitable place to accommodate him. From then on, Zhang Zhifang dared not visit his house. Pei Xiu frequently invited him, but he did not go. Someone said, "Pei Xiu is planning to advance in his career and is concerned about gaining fame, so he is not being humble." One day, he was summoned again and told, "If it is not inconvenient, please come and offer him a recommendation." Pei Xiu was even more apprehensive.

**Xue Zhaowei, the Vice-Minister, Wrapped a Falcon in Paper** During the Tang Dynasty, Xue Zhaowei, the Vice-Minister, was proud due to his talent and position, often showing arrogance. He considered himself worthy of the highest office and was eager for a high-ranking position. At that time, Zhu Wen, the founder of the Later Liang dynasty, had already controlled four commanderies, and his military power was growing. Zhu Wen had ambitions to seize the throne and was eager to transfer power. Xue Zhaowei was sent on a mission to the state of Liang. Zhu Wen invited him back and told the messenger, "The lord has given an order that cannot be refused. Please return quickly." Upon arriving at the western gate, Zhu Wen had no choice but to greet him personally. Seeing Xue Zhaowei's distinguished appearance and eloquent speech, Zhu Wen changed his attitude and treated him with cordiality. One day, Zhu Wen spoke about hawks and falcons, and Xue Zhaowei praised their excellence. Zhu Wen was delighted and told him, "You have also handled these matters before." After returning to his lodging, Zhu Wen sent a messenger to deliver a hawk. Xue Zhaowei wrote a letter of thanks and instructed his servants, "This hawk must be cherished. It should be wrapped in paper and placed in a pouch." The messenger laughed, and this was reported to Zhu Wen's court. **The Examination Board Imposed a Penalty on Cui Zhaoju, the Top Scholar** In the Tang Dynasty, Cui Zhaoju, the top scholar, was part of a group of successful candidates. The officials of the examination board imposed penalties for mistakes. One day, a mistake was made, and the top scholar whipped one of the officials. The official apologized and submitted himself to the ground in front of the other candidates, saying, "Cui Fifteen does not deserve to face the officials before. Please impose a penalty of a certain amount." The people of Boling were speechless and unable to respond.

程贺为崔亚持服 唐代，崔亚郎中治理眉州时，程贺因地方事务被差遣担任防子。他的弟弟曾在州里担任过小书吏。崔公见到程贺的风度有些像儒生，于是问他：“你父亲读过书吗？”程贺下阶回答说：“略懂一些文学。”崔公指了一件物品让他赋诗，程贺的诗作颇有意思，崔公于是命令他回去准备选日装写所业，并且对他进行了高度赞扬。程贺因此被称为进士，完全依赖崔公的门路，没有其他途径。他参加了二十五次科举考试才及第，每次进京都会住在博陵的府邸，常常感激崔公的提拔之恩。崔公去世后，程贺为他服丧三年，人们都称赞他的孝行。 髙太尉骈请留蛮宰相事 唐代，南蛮侵犯西川，苦于缺乏防御设施。自咸通年间以后，剑南地区深受其害。牛丛尚书担任镇守时，面对蛮寇的侵袭无能为力。髙公从东平调任成都，蛮酋攻打蜀城，掌武先选精锐士兵前来救援，有人背负神符一道，蛮人得知后望风而逃。后来，僖宗访问蜀地，深怀疑心作梗，于是同意与南蛮联姻，许配公主。蛮王因为与大国联姻而感到幸运，因此命令宰相赵隆眉、杨竒鲲、段义宗前来朝见，并迎接公主。髙太尉从淮海地区迅速上书，指出南蛮的核心人物只有这几人，请求停止联姻并暗中除掉他们。直到僖宗返回京城，南方才得以安定。杨竒鲲等人皆有文学才华，途中作诗云：“风里浪花吹又白，雨中岚色洗还青。江鸥聚处窗前见，林狖啼时枕上听。此际自然无限趣，王程不敢暂留停。”诗作清新优美。

Xia Houxiang died due to his technique. After attaining wealth and status, Chancellor Xia Hou Gongzi acquired Peng Su's technique, which greatly benefited him. He was dispatched to Puzhong and pleased with a courtesan, but she was unable to serve him properly, leading to a significant loss and ultimately his death. There was a man named Xia Hou Changguan, originally a former monk, who had once relied on the chancellor's protection. After the chaos, he took his family to settle in the mountains of Fengzhou, where he later passed away, leaving only a widow and a son. The old woman revealed this technique to the high-ranking official Man Cun, who greatly benefited from it. His son, named Ji, learned to compose poetry and entered the Western Sichuan region, relying on a distinguished minister to serve as an attendant. People called him Xia Hou Lizi, continuing the family's reputation for debauchery. I heard about this from Qiang Shanren, who explained it in great detail. I once knew Ji, and his son-in-law Luo Qiao, who also knew me, had many concubines. I suspect he was influenced by the Xia Hou family's ways. Could Xia Hou Changguan not have been the chancellor's teacher?

### 张金吾威势取术 唐金吾大将军张直方有一天举办宴会，邀请朝中官员观看干水银的防制过程，众人无不赞叹羡慕，认为张公曾遇到过高人。过了许久，张公大笑道：“这东西已非我所能拥有，它自会来。”不久后，他担任桂府团练使，遇到一位道士，道士掌握了这种有利的术法。张公前去求取，道士始终不肯传授。于是，张公命令健壮的士兵将道士绑在山中，以死相威胁。道士惊恐万分，只说如果给他药物，他就会多献一些术法，但术法不会传授，只有死路一条。因此，张公得到了药物，放任它去其他地方。今天，他只呈上成丹，说这并非他自己的能力。 ### 蔡畋虚诞【何法成附】 唐代高骈镇守成都时，非常喜爱方术。有一位隐士蔡畋，以黄白术（炼金术）为业。他取来一片瓦，研磨一粒半的丹药，涂在瓦上，然后点燃，烧成半截紫磨金，这是一件奇事。蔡畋因此受到人们的尊敬，被认为是一位地仙。燕公（高骈）求他传授术法，但始终未能如愿。后来，蔡畋的真实情况被揭露，原来他从别人那里得到了药物，并以此炫耀。最终，他被元戎（高骈）鞭打致死。在王先主（王建）时期，有一个叫何法成的小人，以卖符药为业。他的妻子稍有姿色，住在北禅院旁边。左院有一位毳衲者（僧人），与何法成相识，经常出入他家，让他卖药银，就在他家吃饭。

### Final Translation Only the final translation is provided below: --- ### Final Translation Afterward, Fa Cheng used his wife as bait to obtain the monk's method. The monk, secretly cherishing it and procrastinating, did not wish to pass it on. He then instructed his wife to adorn herself and meet him. When Fa Cheng returned home, he tied her up and intended to report the matter to the patrol officer. The monk, in fear, mistakenly taught him the method and gave him a few doses of the medicine, then released her bindings and fled. Upon hearing of this method, Fa Cheng went mad and boasted to others about the money-making technique. Soon, the method became known to the Later Shu Emperor, who summoned him into the garden and granted him a military position, though it was not fully the monk's method. Later, when the medicine ran out, he suddenly changed his ways and became lonely and destitute, narrowly escaping punishment for false claims. Peng Tao Guang, a close neighbor of He Sheng, learned the story and told it to me. ### The Misfortune of Shentu the Magistrate Gao Pian, stationed in Weiyang, had a magistrate named Shentu who possessed a secret technique. Lü Yongzhi slandered him, and he was exiled. Yan Gong ordered officials to search for him with a long-term warrant. They captured him at a Zen temple in Xiangzhou, where he was imprisoned. Shentu told the jailer that he wished to see the postal inspector, Wei Gong. The jailer informed Wei, who hurriedly met him in private. Shentu said, "I have a potion that can turn gold. Would you like to offer it to the military governor, Liu Gong Junrong?" Wei agreed and, at an unusual time, went to see Liu. He was able to present half a tile of the potion, which Liu admired and kept, using it to judge cases. Emperor Xizong, while in Shu, sent an envoy to Xianshan Mountain, where Liu was drunk and took the potion. ---

Jin夸衒 was heard by the imperial messenger, who then informed Tian Zhongwei and Liu Sike. They went together to the imperial court with Shen Sheng, but Jin夸衒 was not allowed to go elsewhere. Tian Zhongwei, the military commander, harbored resentment against Jin夸衒 and had him killed at Daojiang Manor. Liu and Shen were both unfortunate in this matter. Jin夸衒 had a son named Shen Sima, who lived in Langzhou and still possessed the mercury medicine. Jingnan's provincial judge, Liu Sike, obtained four pills of the mercury medicine from Shen Sheng and paid a hundred thousand coins to satisfy his desire for something rare. [During the Wang Shu period, there was a scholar wearing a green cloth shirt who often lingered in the streets and stayed at inns. The patrol commander Xiao Huaiwu wanted to learn his art but was firmly refused. As a result, he was beaten to death in the horse stable, which was due to his inability to handle the situation carefully.] **Zong Xiaozhi's Medicine Demon** In the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Zong Xiaozhi in Chang'an who practiced alchemy and was known for his skills in transforming base metals into gold and silver. He spent his time in Pingkang, enjoying the company of women, and was acquainted with Chen Jingxuan, the governor of Sichuan, during Chen's early days. They lost favor due to a matter of jealousy. Later, Chen Jingxuan rose to power and was appointed as the governor of Chengdu. During the turmoil of the kingdom, Emperor Xianzong fled to Sichuan. Zong Xiaozhi also fled to Jinjiang but feared that Yin川 would find out, so he traveled to Zizhong County, where he remained silent and hidden, fearing that others would know about him.
