He stayed at Yingzhen Temple and began to refine a large furnace of elixir, but he had not completed it. Zong Xiaozhi was skilled in the art of Liunian divination and would perform it every morning to predict the auspiciousness of the day. One day, he suddenly lost his voice and decided to leave. He traveled to Neijiang County, where Yin川 sent officials to find him. The道士 who had been refining the elixir with him took the medicine and passed it on to several families, but none of them benefited from it. No one knew why this was so.

**Translation:** The Execution of a Monk by Chancellor Li Bi During the Tang Dynasty, Chancellor Li Bi was sent to govern the eastern region of Shu. There was a monk, whose name is unknown, who presided over initiation ceremonies and attracted many people from all directions who took the full monastic vows. Donations poured in from all over, and due to his busy schedule, the monk's desire for material things became intense. Eventually, he confessed his misconduct, revealing that he had initiated approximately 145 young girls into the monastic life, and they presented items related to their bodies. The high-ranking officials punished him. The author, known as Baoguangzi, once heard from his colleague, General Wang, about a monk in Youzhou who was nearly eighty years old and the elder brother of a military officer. This monk would return to lay life due to his old age and was assisted by a servant named Qing. He engaged in disorderly behavior and eventually decided to renounce his monastic life, taking Qing as his partner. He remarked, "I never thought I would have such joy, and I regret knowing it too late." The military officials found this situation strange and laughed at him. The author also mentions a disciple monk of his, whom he does not wish to name, but who is very knowledgeable in scriptures and treatises and has not been revealed to have any misconduct. This monk was once present at an initiation ceremony where nuns sought guidance and called himself a guide. As a result, his reputation was filled with ugly rumors, but he did not feel ashamed. Alas, the precepts established by the Buddha are the gateway to the path of enlightenment. If not for the right person, they instead become a gathering place for lust. Indeed, the path is not in vain.

Cui Yuanliang and the Descending Auspicious Clouds [With Appendices on Zhao Jiaxian, He Jingchong, and Liang Wei] In the Tang dynasty, Cui Yuanliang once governed Meizhou. After each official session, he prepared and adhered to formal documents to worship the highest deity, performing rituals with utmost devotion, day and night without ceasing. He often opened a yellow script Taoist ceremony in the state office to pray for rain, drought, diseases, and epidemics for the people. During the fasting period in the morning, auspicious clouds and mythical birds would descend, which all the people of the state witnessed. [When Cui Yuanliang governed Huzhou and performed rituals, auspicious clouds and mythical birds also descended, and Li Bai composed an inscription.] To this day, Meizhou holds a yellow script ceremony every year. All military officers in charge, as well as servants who serve tea and wine and attendants, know the proper order of the ceremony. The Taoist priest Luo Zhaoran, who lived to be one hundred and thirteen years old, attended Cui Yuanliang's ceremony. He rode a donkey into the street, fell off the donkey, and with his foot still in the stirrup, was dragged and died. Another Taoist priest, Zhao Jiaxian, from the Shu Kingdom, lived in the Jade Bureau Temple. A Taoist priest named Shangguan, whose name is forgotten, resided in Qingcheng Mountain and performed rituals in the temple. One day, his servant was sleeping and had a nightmare, asking the master where he was. When asked, the servant replied, "I just saw four people wearing red robes descending from the sky, dragging two Taoist priests in front of the altar and whipping their backs twenty times." The questioner stopped him and told him not to speak. Soon after, Zhao Jiaxian and Shangguan Taoist both fell ill and died. Another Taoist priest, He Jingchong, served as the leader of the Taoist order and enjoyed eating garlic. When he performed rituals on the altar, he had a garlic smell. Later, when he performed rituals in Qingcheng and crossed the river, the boat capsized, and he drowned. This was probably a divine punishment for his disrespect to the Tao. Cheng Zhongling, the military general who governed Jingnan, summoned the Taoist priest Liang Wei to perform rituals. Liang Wei knelt and presented a memorial, then fell asleep. Cheng Zhongling dismissed him and destroyed the ritual site. This was also like Zhao Jiaxian and Shangguan Taoist. Generally, Buddhist monks and Taoist priests who attend ceremonies in Jing and Xiang provinces indulge in drinking, and the common people do not consider it wrong. How can they hope for auspicious signs to descend?

Guan Sanlang Enters the City After the chaos and turmoil of the Tang Xiantong era, there were rumors in the alleys that Guan Sanlang's ghostly army had entered the city, causing every household to be terrified, and those who suffered from it shivered with fear, though it did not cause much suffering. Hongnong's Yang Pin led his family from the Lugu Road into Yangyuan, reaching the Qinling Mountains and looking back at the capital. He said, "Here we should be spared from following Guan Sanlang." Before the sentence was finished, there was a sudden shiver. What else could this mean? During times of chaos and turmoil, evil spirits act from the side. Once the mind is doubtful, evil follows. The superstition about Guan Sanlang is exactly what this means. In my youth, I once visited my hometown and heard a story of a foreign demon that tormented people. People gathered in alleys at night to avoid it, and all gaps and holes were sealed. If the ghost came, it would suddenly attack and scare people, but it would stop after a short time. Aspiration for Advancement During the Tang Dazhong era, the imperial examination system was particularly active, and scholars from Fengding, Ding Maogui, stood out among the candidates. Those who associated with him often passed the exams first, while the two of them each took twenty attempts before achieving fame. How different were their outcomes? Before that, Li Du, Cui Yong, Sun Hua, and Zheng Ou, four prominent figures, received favor and were promoted. Therefore, it was said, "To seek to communicate with them." Sun Hua, Du, and Ou said, "Scholars without wealth or status, even with extraordinary thoughts, fine conduct, profound learning, and powerful writing, if not supported and promoted, they cannot be widely known. Thus, the admiration of Yan Hui and Lin Xiangru, the pursuit of fame and profit, cannot be avoided." Dripping Blood and Tears

The wife of the Tang dynasty scholar-official Yin Baohui, Lady Feng, was also from a prominent family in the capital. When Yin Baohui first took the imperial examination, all his essays were written by Lady Feng, and they all conformed to the established norms. Everyone knew that Yin Baohui was unrestrained and free-spirited, good at socializing, and never took writing seriously. During the rebellion, the couple suffered great misfortune. At first, Lady Feng was beheaded, and Yin Baohui lost his voice, with blood staining his face. His aunt, who was a nun, witnessed the tragedy and wept, telling the relatives of Yin Baohui. Only then did the relatives of Yin Baohui believe the ancient saying that sorrow is so great that tears cannot express it anymore, and only blood can. Having a heart disease does not prevent one from writing well. [Example: A son from the Li family] Among the five brothers of Liu Chongwang, four of them passed the imperial examination to become Jinshi and reached high positions such as generals, chancellors, and officials. The eldest brother, Chongyi, did not pass the imperial examination and held a position in the provincial government. He had five sons, each adopted by a different family. Chongyi kept one son, who was young and talented. One day, despite his heart disease, he wrote official documents and praised or criticized the officials in the court, capturing the essence of their actions. His family members were afraid that this information would leak out and quickly burned the documents. His talent for drafting official documents was ultimately wasted. A man from Sichuan, who had a son who was very skilled in writing and could write about supernatural matters quickly, was worried and tried to warn him. The son said, "Just be my father; do not interfere with my affairs." Later, he fell into a well and died. The heart is the seat of the mind, and when it is affected by illness, the order and sequence of thoughts are disrupted, leading to such outcomes. Miscellaneous Notes of the Northern Dream, Volume Eleven <Subclass, Novels, Miscellaneous Matters, Miscellaneous Notes of the Northern Dream> Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries Miscellaneous Notes of the Northern Dream, Volume Twelve

**Translation:** **Lu Fan's Heroic Spirit** During the Tang Dynasty, Lu Shangfu, a high-ranking official, passed the imperial examination through his literary talent and considered himself a hero. He served in several military posts and died in Lingwu. The governor-general sent condolences and a funeral offering, but the eunuch sought a generous gift according to precedent. The family's financial resources were insufficient to prepare the return goods, and the imperial messenger's demands were insatiable, causing great trouble for the family. A relative who held an official position said before the spirit of the deceased, "Our family is so poor, how can we fulfill the deputy's duties? Is there no supernatural power of our esteemed minister to protect us from this eunuch?" Soon after, the imperial messenger fell ill and died. It is known that the spirits of those who were strong and heroic should not be feared. The eighth seat's descendant, who still lives in Jiangling, has heard of this story and records it to warn those who are greedy for wealth. **Yang Jia's Reluctance to Learn Taoism** Yang Jia, the prime minister of the Tang Dynasty, was from Jiangzhou. His grandfather was the commandant of the capital city, and his father was the county clerk of Lanchi County. Yang Jia had four sons: Fa, Jia, Yan, and himself. All four sons passed the imperial examination and became Jinshi. Yang Jia was appointed to a high position, and Fa and the others all reached the rank of minister. Fa named his sons according to the seasons: Chuzi, Chengzi, and Chengzi. Jia named his sons according to the seasons: Jiongzhi, Jiongzhi, and Jiongzhi. Yan named his sons according to the seasons: Zhuzi, Dongzi, and Dongzi. All of them were accomplished in literature and passed the imperial examination, and they were known as the Bamboo Grove Yangs. They were compared to the Yangs of Jinggong in terms of their prosperity. A young man from the Yang family studied in the mountains of Lu. One day, he searched for a secluded place deep in the mountains and met a Taoist. The Taoist said to him, "If you study the Tao, you will have the potential to become a sage. But if you choose to serve in office, you will reach the highest ranks, but ultimately you will suffer misfortune. Can you follow me to learn the Tao?" The young man held onto his doubts and continued to strive for advancement, ignoring the Taoist's words. Later, although he reached high office, he was exiled to the southern wilderness and died tragically. Alas, Xue Ze, a relative by marriage, once told him about this.

Zhang Xi, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, had five sons: Wenwei, Yi, Xian, Jimei, and Renshen, all of whom achieved high positions. Among them, one son, whose name is forgotten, heard a story about wall fish entering Taoist scriptures and eating the characters of immortals. He believed that by swallowing these wall fish, one could achieve immortality and ascend to heaven. Zhang was deceived by this idea and began to collect wall fish, placing them in a container with the characters of immortals. He hoped that the wall fish would eat the characters, and he would then swallow them to achieve immortality. Every time he had an episode, he would go a whole month without eating. His speech became coarse and indecent, and he had no shame or avoidance. His family locked him up and guarded him, waiting for him to recover. When he recovered, everything returned to normal, but he would eat double the amount of food for a month, indulging in a variety of flavors. After many years, he passed away. This shows that the mind is a thing, and even a slight injury to the spirit can have serious consequences. If good deeds are not possible, how much more so for evil deeds? Just as Liu Pi swallowed people, Zhang swallowed immortals. Their injuries are the same.

Lu Dalu Refuses to Accept a Fee [With Li Deyang's Addition] Lu Dalu, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, was known for his integrity and disdain for material gain. His family had inherited the art of calligraphy from his predecessor, Master Gongquan, and he was appointed as the governor of Luzhou. During his time, he participated in the military affairs of the Eastern Shu, specifically with Duke Gu Yanlang. One day, an edict of virtue stone monument was to be erected, and Duke Gu Yanlang wished to embellish and illuminate the carving. The deputy said, "Bad calligraphy is not something I would be reluctant to give. If you were to give me a fee first, then I would not dare to accept the order." The chancellor admired his writing, and he finished without further bothering. During the Liang Dynasty, there was a monk named Xia Meng in Yanzhou who gathered disciples and preached. Donations and offerings gathered like clouds, and at that time, he was called the Diamond Chan. After some days, he passed away, and a pagoda and a monument were built. A Taoist priest named Li Deyang from Mount Lu, skilled in Ouyang Xun's calligraphy, was invited by the disciples of Xia Meng to write the inscription on the monument. They offered one thousand strings of coins, but Li Deyang refused. He said, "If you were to reward me with a drink, I could display my skills with the thousand strings of coins, but it is not what I hope for." He finally refused to write it, and this is also a great person of recent times.

Yang Yin Doubts the Art of Physiognomy [with Li Hao Attached] Yang Yin, a guest of Yan Zunmei, the Military Governor of the Ten Armies, was skilled in the art of physiognomy. In the western suburbs of the capital, he met a man named Li, who was also a distant relative of the Tang imperial family. Li had a high nose and a dragon-like face, with his hands hanging down past his knees. Yang Yin was amazed by this and told the Imperial Commissioner about it, who then had the intention to provide financial support. It was believed that such an appearance would lead to something extraordinary. However, when the Yellow Bandits invaded the palace, Emperor Xizong fled to Shu. Li was forced by the citizens into the capital and ascended to the Hanyuan Hall, but soon after he was killed. Could the great man's appearance only be for ascending a single hall? It is impossible to know. Yang Yin sighed and no longer spoke about the skill of knowing people. During the reign of the First Sovereign of Shu, there was a Taoist named Li Hao, who was also a member of the Tang imperial family. Born in Xu Prefecture, he traveled to the Three Shus. He was eloquent and quick-witted, roughly having some literary talent. He settled in Yangping Temple and was supported by evil people. When he saw a purple aura, he gathered a crowd and started a rebellion, but was about to succeed but failed. The evil people dispersed like stars, but Li Hao alone suffered the consequences. The one who was about to become the leader, a great scholar from Linzhou, was friends with Li Hao and did not believe that Li Hao was involved in making evil things. This was because of his noble appearance and imperial lineage, which made him supported by the evil group, especially in times of turmoil. He hid his tracks and disappeared, not becoming the beginning of the Green Forest rebellion. Before that, Li Hao wrote a letter inviting Yang Dehui from Yujue Temple to attend a gathering. An old Taoist named Cui Wudi claimed to be deaf and claimed to be skilled in mathematics, often predicting good and bad omens. Yang Dehui asked what it would be like to go north. Cui Wudi had him draw characters on the ground. Hongnong wrote the characters 'north' and 'thousand' on the ground. Cui Gong inserted 'thousand' into 'north' to form the character '乖.' If you go, it will be '乖.' Yang Yin did not go, and Li Hao was captured on the day of the gathering. Many Taoists suffered the consequences. Yang Yin's fortunate escape was due to the power of Cui Wudi.

A Tale of Yang Biao and the Big Sister Goddess [with an Addition on Shi Guangze] Yang Biao, the son of Yang Shouxiang, was a young man serving as a circuit inspector in Jiangxi during the Tang Dynasty. He was a leisurely official in the outer court. During the autumn sacrifice, he was ordered to worship the Big Sister Goddess. The West River has two isolated mountains, the larger one called "Big Solitary" and the smaller one called "Small Solitary." Zhu Ya's Li Taishuai wrote a poem about the Small Solitary Mountain, which conveyed a deeper meaning. Later generations misinterpreted the name, calling it "Sister's Sister's Sister." A temple was built on the mountain with a beautiful statue, but the wind and waves were very fierce, making travelers afraid. Every year, the local government ordered officials to personally perform the sacrifice. Yang Biao participated in this ritual and was pleased with the Big Sister Goddess. He jokingly remarked about her beauty. After the sacrifice, he returned to the state capital and saw a beautiful woman in the clouds who called him "Young Master Yang" and expressed her desire to complete a ritual with him. Hong Nong was surprised and said that the previous words were just jokes. The younger sister (Xiao Gu) explained that her elder sister had no intention but admired Yang Biao, and if he broke the promise, it might be harmful to him. Hong Nong was worried and agreed, giving her a month to handle family matters. Yang Biao returned home, gave instructions, and suddenly died, as if a ghost or deity had come to welcome him. Xue Ze, a relative of Yang Biao, often spoke about this incident in detail. Recently, the former magistrate of Dengzhou, Shi Zaidi, whose son Guo Guang was very intelligent and was studying, returned from a villa drunk and entered the Temple of Mount Tai, asking the god to make him a third son. After that, he returned home, his spirit was confused, and he seemed to be summoned, dying after a month. Alas, the boundary between the living and the dead is vast, and the path between humans and ghosts is different. To pledge oneself to such a fate is to bring sorrow upon oneself. This serves as a lesson for the future.

A Man with Distinctive Headwear [Supplement by Xu Chengjie and Li Siyi] I once read the preface of the Liu family, which showed that their family rules were strict and they were the best among the scholarly class. Liu Pin was appointed to an official position in Luzhou County, but he was recalled from his post due to illness and sought medical treatment in Tongquan County, East Sichuan. In the inner chambers, a son of his brother [or nephew, or someone named Pei] came to visit him. The vice-premier turned away and said he did not recognize anyone. The family said, "He is a young official from a certain department." The vice-premier insisted he did not recognize him and could not understand his status. After a long time, the old servant thought, "Is this not the distinctive headwear of the young official?" It was indeed surprising, but if he lowered his headband before entering, he would not be blocked. The young official then lowered his headband, revealing a distinctive feature, and the vice-premier embraced him. His sincerity and thickness were evident in this manner. My relative Liu Kun, a distant relative of the vice-premier, lived temporarily in Yang'an County. His brothers visited him, and the vice-premier first asked if they were studying or writing. If not, he advised them to learn how to become officials. My ancestors became famous through writing and official careers, and I hope you do not neglect even a moment of study. In Luzhou County, there was a high-ranking official named Liu who made public furniture and equipment that were sturdy and heavy. They lasted through multiple administrations, and it is unknown if they still exist today. Xu Cun, a high-ranking official during the Shu dynasty, was a meritorious minister. His son inherited his position.

杰, the son of the late governor of Qian, Xixi Shi, followed his mother in marrying into the Xu family. He was arrogant and privileged, exceeding his proper bounds, and rarely has there been anyone like him. As a military leader with only 127 people under his command, these were musical and technical skills. When he went out, he treated himself like a high-ranking official, and all the items he carried with him were extravagant and grand. He rode on a green, warm seat with many decorations hanging down. Every time he wrote a letter and stamped it, if there was any slight dampness, he would definitely change it, which the clerks found burdensome. His peers used it as a topic of conversation, and they all recommended Gu Xun as the leader. One day, Xu Gong had a gathering and said to Gu, "Why do you speak so much about slander?" Gu then explained and pointed to several people at the table as evidence. Gu had nothing to say in response after hesitating, and said, "Brother Three, don't be so hasty. The green, warm seat is well-known. As for the fish bag with the Mount Penglai cast on it, it wasn't I who promoted it." The people at the table laughed even more, and they then realized that the fish bag was even more excessive. He was sent to Maozhou and was harmed by the tribal chieftain. Li Siyi, a general of the Western Sichuan Guard, wore clothes that were all splendid and luxurious, exquisite and beautiful. The attendants of Shu's first emperor envied and were surprised by him. The first emperor said, "Li Siyi spent a lot of money on one set of clothes. It was to enhance the prestige of my military command. I have entrusted him with the Jiang market."

In arranging for the making of clothes, the First Sovereign also saw in the workshop a craftsman wearing a small cap wrapped around his head, which was shaped like an eagle's beak in front and exposed at the back, resembling a pillow. He found this strange and ordered the beak-like part to be cut off. Later, while on a tower, he saw people wearing simple, rough hats made of straw, and remarked that their heads were broken and their foreheads sunburned, asking what good could come of such things. Yet, he himself valued simplicity and cleanliness, and all his actions reflected this. In the Shu Han court, there was a minor official named Pei Can, who had a low salary and was idle. Someone advised him to seek the position of magistrate of a county, to which Pei replied, "In today's capital region, one must have extraordinary qualities to be envied. It is clear that I do not possess such qualities." He often said that after the Yellow Bandits' rebellion, much had been lost, except for the socks, trousers, and boots that were no longer passed down from the past. My colleague, the Supervisor of the Treasury, said that wrapping the head with wood was the most convenient, and why bother with daily oiling, ironing, and smoothing? This one thing does not require following the ancients. He also disliked using silver-edged ceramic utensils to hold bowls and plates, as it was a waste of effort. He further said, "A scholar in old age marrying a young girl is a disgraceful thing to leave behind. Unless one has a heart of iron and stone, no one can avoid leaving behind warnings and advice." Because of this, I recount the story of the Liu-style headgear, which led to several incidents. These are not meant for idle talk but are sufficient as a small warning.

During the reign of Emperor Zhaozong of the Tang Dynasty, Yang Yide, a man of noble family and high moral integrity, found himself isolated and misunderstood by his contemporaries. He requested permission to leave and went to Huayin, as the capital was in turmoil. He planned to move south and hid in the alleys of Jiangling, renting a house and keeping a low profile. Living in poverty, he had no food and relied on simple, coarse fare, yet he remained undaunted. At the time, Cheng Zhongling, a high-ranking official, invited and received guests, and scholars expressed their admiration for him. Someone from Hongnong, so humble that he did not even have a place to meet visitors, was suddenly summoned back by a formal order. Cheng Zhongling was surprised, thinking that the person had not been properly informed or had not come to explain their situation. Cheng also invited the person to enter the imperial academy and hold a high position, promising him that his office was not sought for personal gain. Cheng remarked, "Lord Cheng is overly praising me; what should I do about this favor?" He questioned why the current prime minister needed to go to such lengths to find him.

**Translation:** The decree for Luo stated that his future appointment would be no more than a governor of a southern prefecture. At this time, he would certainly receive a gift of 300 strings of cash from the official, but he only accepted 30 strings. After settling his expenses, he returned the remainder to the court, earning the nickname "Iron Gate." Shortly thereafter, he was appointed governor of Daozhou and passed through Jiangling. He asked the official to provide ten attendants with clothing and travel expenses of 50,000 coins, asking for nothing else. The official highly valued him. Later, he lived in the South Mountain with Master Yuan Tai, Master Dun Xi, and Elder Guiben, who received the patriarchal seal from Master Yang. After his death, his family also seemed to attain the way of liberation. Elder Guiben received the Chan teachings from three sages, including the elder Zheng Qi, who spoke to him about it. Zhang Lin was known for his playful nature. During the Tang Dynasty, Zhang Lin was originally a scholar who was selected as a Jinshi and rose to the position of Tai Shiyu. His poems were delicate and often depicted scenes from gardens and ponds, such as lotus leaves turning as people pick them, and water caltrop flowers disappearing as boats pass. He was favored by Chancellor Cui Zhaowei. When he visited the chancellor's residence, Cui asked, "Why have you not visited me for so long?" Zhang replied, "I have been without food and water for several days, not well." Cui was surprised by the response and asked, "Have you not been well?" Zhang said, "For several days, I have not eaten or drunk anything, so I am not well." Cui then gave him a robe for the cold month and asked what else he needed. Zhang replied, "Just one shirt is enough." Chancellor Zhang Lin laughed and treated him favorably throughout. The Preserver of Light said that Dongfang Shuo used humor to protect himself, and Lou Junqing used his words to gain comfort; neither did so in vain but had their intentions. In today's world, those who seek to please through alcohol and food rely on the power of nobles and princes, flattering them excessively. Only those with great virtue and strict integrity are not subject to ridicule by such people.

## Translation **Se Shangshu's Unfortunate Fate [With the Story of Liu Jianfeng Attached]** During the Tang Dynasty, Shen Xun, a grand councilor and the son of Shen Yazi, had two brothers. One of them, whose name is forgotten, was traveling by boat on the river when a sudden torrent of water caused the boat to break apart. Shen's son was also swept away and died. Shen then governed Lüzhou, where he had a favored concubine who was very jealous. He arranged for her to marry someone in his family and return to her hometown in Qin. The concubine was always by his side, and she felt ashamed and resentful. She waited for an opportunity to stab Shen, and indeed, she succeeded in killing him. To fulfill a ritualistic sacrifice, she also killed herself. However, it was too late to prevent the tragedy. During the Tianfu period of the Tang Dynasty, Liu Jianfeng, the governor of Hunan, had an affair with the wife of an official named Chen. Chen was humiliated by his peers and became angry. He waited for an opportunity to kill the official with a weapon called *jí lí* (蒺藜). The Ma family, who held a position of power, banned the use of this weapon in memory of the incident in Pengcheng, as a punishment for the consequences of such actions. Adultery is a great sin, and ancient sages have warned rulers and fathers to learn from the misfortunes of their children and servants. **Wang Qian, the Grand Councilor, Burned Paper Money [With the Story of Qin Wei Attached]** During the Tang Dynasty, Wang Qian, a grand councilor, had a close relationship with Wu Xiang Yuan. Wu suffered a sudden misfortune, and Wang often burned paper money as a form of offering to him. Later, Wang governed Jingnan and had a household servant named Xu Chen. One day, Xu Chen suddenly died but was revived the next day. He prepared a notice and went to the government office, saying he wanted to see the governor. Wang allowed him to enter and asked him what he wanted. Xu Chen said he was initially taken by an official to a government office and was not yet seen by the governor. He was taken to the office of the judge, who said, "This person is from the falconry service and is not involved in the governor's affairs, so he should be sent back." The judge then told Xu Chen that the governor was safe and that he was actually Wu Xiang Yuan. Wu had many former students and officials who rarely remembered the past. --- ### Footnotes 1. **沈尚书** (Shen Shangshu): A high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty. 2. **宠婢** (chǒng bì): A favored concubine. 3. **刘建封** (Liu Jianfeng): A historical figure who served as the governor of Hunan. 4. **马氏** (Mǎ shì): The Ma family, likely a prominent family in the region. 5. **王潜司徒** (Wáng Qián sītú): Wang Qian, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty. 6. **武相元衡** (Wǔ Xiāng Yuán): Wu Xiang Yuan, another high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty. 7. **马后** (Mǎ hòu): The governor of Jingnan, likely a female ruler. 8. **许琛** (Xǔ Chén): A household servant who had a supernatural experience. 9. **秦威仪** (Qín Wēiyí): A historical figure who influenced the story. 10. **蒺藜** (jí lí): A weapon used in ancient China, often made of iron spikes.

**The Unjust Fate of Minister Shen [With Appendix: Liu Jianfeng]** Shen Xun, a Tang dynasty official of the rank of Shilang, was the son of Shen Ya Zhi. He had two brothers, one of whom’s name is forgotten. Once, he boarded a boat and set out to drift along the river, but the violent rapids struck the boat, breaking the bridge’s planks, which drifted downstream. Shen’s son was also swept away and died. Later, when Shen was stationed in Lüzhou, he favored a concubine, but his wife was very jealous. As a result, he arranged for the concubine to be married off to a member of his household named Guī Qín. The concubine remained constantly by his side. Guī Qín, feeling ashamed and resentful, waited for an opportunity to stab Shen. Indeed, she became the murderer, killing Shen in order to offer him as a sacrifice, but it was too late. In the year Tianfu of the Tang dynasty, Liu Jianfeng, the military governor of Hunan, committed adultery with the wife of an official named Chen, who was one of his subordinates. Chen was mocked by his peers, which shamed and enraged him. Seizing an opportunity, he killed Liu Jianfeng with a蒺藜 [or 冷藜]. The Ma family now holds this position, and the use of蒺藜 [or 冷藜] is now prohibited, as a punishment for the misfortune that befell Pengcheng. Adultery is a great punishment; the wise men of old have warned us. Should not rulers and fathers take heed from the misfortunes of their children and servants? **Minister Wang Qian and the Burning of Paper Money [With Appendix: Qin Weiyi]** In the Tang dynasty, Minister Wang Qian had a close relationship with Wu Yuanheng, the chancellor. When Wu met with sudden misfortune, Wang often burned paper money throughout the four seasons as a tribute to him. Later, when Wang became the governor of Jingnan, a dyer named Xu Chen suddenly died one day, but the next day he awoke and was alive again. He then prepared a notice and went to the office, saying he wished to see the Prime Minister. He was then brought before Wang at the steps and questioned. Xu Chen said, "At first I was taken by messengers to a government office, where I did not see you [Wang], but was instead taken to see the chief official. There, I met a man seated at a desk who said, 'This man has come by mistake. He belongs to the falconers. Xu Chen has nothing to do with you. Send him back.' Then the man said to Xu Chen, 'How is the Prime Minister? I am Chancellor Wu. I have many former students and officials, but few still remember the old days.'"

Only the final translation of the source text will be provided below: After his death, only the Sima (司徒) did not forget and every year he would often leave paper money as a gift, deeply grateful for the kindness. However, the paper money given was often perforated and unusable. The Sima, being busy with many inspections, did not go to the office to convey this meaning. The prince (王公) heard of it and was moved to tears, feeling ashamed and surprised. The falconry official Xu Chen indeed died. From then on, they selected good paper to cut into money to offer to him. This matter is similar to the case where Yang Shou borrowed money from Zheng Yu, the Shangshu. Also, the Taoist Qin Bao of Nanyue said that the dignitary who diligently burned incense once told the Immortal Master that the celestial being needed paper money, which was not understood. At night, he dreamed that the Immortal Master said, "Paper money is what the record-keeping officials need; why do I need it?" Therefore, people in the mountains also believed it. Cui, an official, was given medicine by the temple deity. Cui, an official from Fujian, whose name is forgotten, was upright and self-disciplined, highly respected by his subordinates. He was sent on a mission to Hunan and returned by way of the road, where he encountered bandits who killed everyone. Only the outer official was suddenly guided by someone in a panic and escaped. He hurried on a long journey and fell ill with malaria. On the way, he stayed at the Yandu Temple and dreamed that the temple deity gave him three pills of medicine. After taking them, he woke up suddenly and was instantly cured. This is also a case of divine assistance for virtue. From Pengcheng, Liu Shanfu said that his grandfather, Li Gong Jingyi, the Langzhong, lived in the Yuchai Ward in Dongdu, where the land deity was very powerful. The family servant Zhang Xingzhou had dealings with it and received responses. Before a great flood, he dreamed in advance and was told to prepare food and drink. On that day, he led his family to block the riverbank, and the water did not overflow, protecting Li's house. This is an unusual event.

Zhang Jing drafted a memorial for the temple. During the reign of Emperor Zhaozong of Tang, Zhang Jing, a scholar from Mount Lu, traveled to Guilin to meet the military governor Zhang Xiang. On his way to Hengzhou, his boat was damaged by a dog barking at the shore, forcing him to sleep on land near a river temple. There, he was rebuked by the temple deity. Zhang Jing responded with his usual scholarly work, and the deity changed its expression and invited him to sit. The deity then spoke calmly, saying, "There is a man named Li Ren, who is a king or shaman, and his crime warrants the punishment of entire family. The temple deity intervened on his behalf and submitted a petition to the mountain deity. No one had previously composed such a memorial, so Zhang Jing drafted it. After the memorial was submitted, it was approved, and the deity was pleased, giving him ten pieces of white gold as a gift. Liu Shanfu and Liao Zhi, the imperial scholars, personally heard Zhang Jing recount the story in great detail. The deity of the Chan River is known for its integrity. During the turmoil caused by the Yellow Turban rebels, a minor official named Pei, whose name is forgotten, fled south with his wife and children to Hanzhong. Just after leaving the capital, his daughter suddenly died during their journey. In the chaos of the military conflict, there was no time to properly bury her body. The family was deeply grieved. Later, while passing through a valley, they heard their daughter speak at night, though they did not see her form. Her parents were surprised and asked her what had happened. She said, "I am the son of the deity of the Chan River. A forceful and violent person lured me back to my home. My father was angry and punished me for killing a living being. He then scolded and comforted me, sending me away. Since the evening, I have no place to stay and wish to follow my parents south. Please pull up some grass to wrap me and place me in a box so that my spirit can find a resting place. My diet and speech will be the same as before." Afterward, she informed her mother, saying, "I have already found a new place to be reborn." She then wept and bid farewell, departing. The affairs of ghosts and deities are difficult to speak of in the world, but the deity of the Chan River has given her a lesson in righteousness and virtue, embodying the qualities of wisdom and integrity. The story of the trench on Mount Du, involving Xianyu Zhongtong, the Tang family, and the Yan family.

In ancient times, there were books on tombs and dwellings, and many people valued these practices. Even knowledgeable individuals sometimes criticized or doubted them. Du Gongzheng, whose surname was different from the Jingzhao clan, often felt slighted and harbored resentment against them. After Du Gongzheng became an official and achieved prominence, he took advantage of an opportunity to cut off the mountain range of Duling. As a result, the Du lineage declined for several generations. Xianyu Zhongtong and his brothers were from Xincun County, Langzhou, and they rose to prominence, both becoming generals. Astrologers reported that their ancestors' graves had unusual qi (energy), so the emperor ordered the graves to be cut off. A descendant named Xianyu Yue, when young, found a small snake hatching from an egg under the bed mat. His family thought it was a strange and miraculous event. This lord, when grown, often had a self-important or arrogant look. He held various official positions and ended his career as the magistrate of Anyue County, Puzhou, unable to escape the troubles of the world. His followers joked that he was the "Snake of Xianyu." Tang Feng, also from Langzhou, had ancestors' graves in Maoyan Grass Market. While traveling with a fortune-teller, Tang Feng passed by his ancestors' graves. The fortune-teller said that the descendants of this grave would become high-ranking officials. Tang Feng replied, "This is indeed our family's grave." The fortune-teller said, "If so, your family might not be able to bear the good fortune. The descendants would become bandits and not have a good end." Tang Feng remembered this and later encountered the founding of the Shu state. Tang Feng also governed a prefecture, and his two sons inherited official positions, all becoming generals. Three of them governed prefectures, just as the fortune-teller had predicted. How accurate his prediction was! Yan Shikong Zhen lived in Yanting County, Zizhou, with his home situated near the Fudi Mountain. Whenever there was a deer's call, one member of the Yan family would die. One day, a relative and a cousin were sitting together and heard a deer's call. The cousin said, "The deer in Fudi Mountain has called again." Yan said, "This time, it should be your elder brother." His elder brother quickly replied, "It's not my elder brother; it should be my third and fourth brothers." Soon, one of the Yan family members died. What a strange coincidence!

### Translation **The Intelligence of the Wolf** In the estate of Chancellor Zhang Gong Wenwei, located in the northern part of the Eastern Capital (Luoyang), there was a wolf den. The wolf had four cubs, which were swallowed by a snake. The male and female wolves were deeply concerned and gathered soil outside the den, which was just right to cover the snake's head. They waited for the snake to emerge from the den, and when it did, it found it could not turn back in time. The wolves then bit the snake in the middle and tore open its belly, carrying out the four cubs, which were still alive, and placed them outside the den. They bit beans and leaves and applied them to the cubs, and all of them survived. How could such a small creature have such emotion and intelligence? The most intelligent beings, humans, why do they not think about this? **Records of the Northern Dreams, Volume Twelve** **Imperially Approved Four Treasuries** **Records of the Northern Dreams, Volume Thirteen** **Tang Dynasty, Compiled by Sun Guangxian** **The Grass Bandit Commander** Wang Zhongling Duo, who had lost his position as military governor, was appointed as the Jiedushi of Huazhou. Soon after, he was removed from his post. Due to the peaceful situation in Hebei, he had a past relationship with Yang Quanmei and fled to Fuyang. He was accompanied by about ten members of his military governor's staff, all of whom were scholars from the capital. When they passed through Wei, Le Yanzhen treated them very respectfully. Wang's entourage was very opulent, and his guests and attendants displayed the old ways of peace and prosperity in the imperial capital. Le Yanzhen had a son named Congxun, who was usually a rascal. He loved Wang's carriages, horses, and attendants and asked his father's staff member Li Shanfu, who had failed the exams several times during the Xiantong era, about his feelings. Li harbored private resentment against the powerful in the capital. Therefore, he ordered to plot against Wang. They awaited Wang's arrival in Ganling and, with a few hundred light cavalry, seized all his belongings, attendants, and servants and returned. Wang and his guests were all killed. When the report was made to the court, it stated that on a certain day in Beizhou, one person named Wang Linggong was killed and robbed. How absurd and fabricated it was! Le Yanzhen's son was soon killed by the rebel army. Was it not the case that Langya Gong complained to the Heavenly Emperor?

Wang Chongrong Drives Out Two Commanders Wang Chongrong, the military governor of Hedong, originally served as a military officer. During the rebellion led by Huang Chao, the supreme commander Li Du served the rebel regime. Those who feared Wang Chongrong and supported him were often recommended to become deputy governors. One day, Wang Chongrong suddenly said to Li Du, "Anyone who receives favor should only repay it privately and not abuse it for public gain. You are helping the rebels to guard one province, which is disloyal to the country, and you also impose heavy taxes, causing widespread discontent. Suddenly, a crisis arises, how can it be stopped?" He immediately ordered the execution of the rebel envoy. Li Du had no response and handed over the military seal to Wang Chongrong and left. When Li Du arrived at the imperial court, the court appointed the former Jingzhao Governor Dou Yu to take his place in Hedong. Wang Chongrong welcomed him. Dou Yu, before being Jingzhao Governor, had a reputation for being cruel and was called "Duo Die." The next day, Wang Chongrong gathered the military officers in the courtyard and said, "The emperor has appointed a great minister to defend the frontier and stop the rebels, how can you lightly discuss expelling him, should he leave through the north gate? Those who are evil are probably just one or two people, you can speak up." Not knowing that the military officers were all close allies of Wang Chongrong, they remained silent. Wang Chongrong then calmly, with a drawn sword, ascended the steps and said to Dou Yu, "Those who are evil are none other than myself. Summon Dou Yu's servants to control the horses and ask them to follow the precedent set by Li Du and leave quickly." Dou Yu dared not look up and jumped on his horse, leaving through the north gate. Wang Chongrong defeated Huang Chao and was rewarded with the title of military governor and the rank of marquis. He later formed an alliance with Tian Lingzi, and one of his subordinates, Chang Xingru, was killed. At that time, Chang was known as "Tie Tiao" because of his strictness.

Zheng Wen Gong Repays a Debt of Gratitude Zheng Wen Gong, courtesy name Tai Wen, was the son of Zheng Ya, who once served as the Inspector of the region of Guan. When Xi Men Si Gong was summoned to the capital by imperial edict, Zheng Ya sent Zheng Wen Gong off at the northern outskirts of the city and entrusted him to Xi Men Si Gong, saying, "I hope you will remember my son, Gui Er, in the future." Zheng Ya then wept, and Xi Men Si Gong remembered the promise. When Xi Men Si Gong became the commander of the Divine Strategy Army, Zheng Ya had already passed away. Zheng Wen Gong used his family wealth to hire brave people to find and bring Xi Men Si Gong back to Qishan. He cared for Xi Men Si Gong as if he were his father. Xi Men Si Gong died at Zheng Wen Gong's place, and Zheng Wen Gong planted trees himself at the burial site. Soon after, Zheng Wen Gong also died and was buried near Xi Men Si Gong's grave. Officials visited both graves and shed tears, acknowledging their righteousness. Han Jian Listens to Books [Li Maozhen Attached] The Governor of Weibo, Han Jian, was rough and crude in nature. Whenever he was in the company of scholars, he did not understand their discourse, which made him feel ashamed. He then summoned a scholar named Xiao Liang to lecture on the Analects of Confucius. When they reached the chapter on "Government," the next day, Han Jian met with his subordinates and said, "I recently learned that ancient people were simple and honest, and it wasn't until they were thirty years old that they could walk and stand properly. Those who heard this were all amazed." The Prince of Qin, Li Maozhen, invited Wang Lifu, a renowned scholar, to lecture on the Spring and Autumn Annals. Wang Lifu was old-fashioned and eccentric, but he explained the meaning of the classics in a way that was both engaging and worth listening to. Li Maozhen listened to him for months without tiring. Afterward, Wang Lifu became a Taoist monk, changed his name to Dayu, and died in Luoyang. It is not that martial officials necessarily look down on scholars, but they often encounter those who are shallow and discourage their studies. This is truly a pity.

Shi Fangli's Respect for His Hometown [with Luo Qiu's Appendix] Shi Fangli, the Military Governor of the Zhaoyi Circuit, was from Pingxiang, Xing Prefecture. When he was young, he enlisted in the army due to his bravery and strength, serving as a subordinate general. During the Guangming era, the Military Governor of Lushui, Gao Xun, attacked Zhu Shuang at Heyang, and Shi Fangli led the vanguard forces through the Tianjing Pass. At that time, Gao Xun was driven away by the general Liu Guang, who harbored suspicions against Shi Fangli and left him to garrison the pass. Later, Liu Guang was killed by the people of Lushui, and the army then appointed Shi Fangli as their leader. Because of his longing to return to his roots, he moved his troops to Xing Prefecture. He administered the region with fairness and justice, and the people all followed him. He first paid homage to his ancestors' graves in his hometown and visited the county magistrate to express his respect for his hometown. Those with insight praised his actions. His nephew, Shi Qian, succeeded him as the Military Governor of Lushui and submitted to Taiyuan. The Preserver of Light from Taiyuan, Luo Qiu, had repeatedly failed the imperial examinations and sought rapid advancement, so he abandoned his studies and relied on officials in Taizhou. He often encouraged others to visit their ancestral graves, but when he met the county magistrate, he held his tablet arrogantly. The magistrate said, "Although I am a lowly official, I do not understand the importance of this matter. But does Your Excellency not see the people beneath the pine and cypress trees?" He was criticizing Luo Qiu for lacking respect for his hometown, even less than a warrior. Luo Qiu had exceptional talent and was once seen composing poetry about an official courtesan named Hong'er in Diaoyin, but he is otherwise unknown.

During the Jingfu era, the military governor of Youzhou, Li Kuangwei, led his troops to Bo Water to rescue the state of Zhenzhou. However, the army became chaotic, and they promoted his younger brother, Kuang Chou, to be the acting military governor. All the troops then dispersed, and Li Kuangwei sent a letter to his brother, entrusting him with the military affairs. Li Kuangwei intended to go south to the imperial court and stopped at Luzhe. The Prince of Zhao, Wáng Róng, seeing that Li Kuangwei had come to rescue him but had lost his country, invited him to stay temporarily in Changshan Prefecture. Amidst the turmoil, Li Kuangwei had a retainer, Li Zhenbao, who had returned from the imperial court. Li Zhenbao met Li Kuangwei and they climbed a temple tower together to admire the beauty of Zhenzhou's mountains and rivers. They developed feelings of affection and decided to seek refuge with Wáng Róng. They forced Wáng Róng to come with them, armed with weapons, and entered through the eastern gate of the inner city. Inside the gate, there were Wáng Róng's personal guards, who suddenly blocked the outer gate. In the courtyard, someone recognized Wáng Róng and quickly lifted him onto a horse, carrying him away. Li Kuangwei fought back for a while, and both he and Li Zhenbao died. Wáng Róng was only sixteen or seventeen years old and was thin. When he was riding alongside Li Kuangwei, a sudden thunderstorm broke out, with hail and rain falling heavily, causing tiles to fly and several large trees to be uprooted. The next day, Wáng Róng felt pain in his neck, which he attributed to being carried by a strong person. He later learned that it was Mò Jūnhé, a butcher, who had helped him. He believed that this was a sign of divine protection, and indeed, he managed to escape this danger and remained in power for over thirty years. Without the assistance of divine beings, how could he have avoided such a fate?

In the late years of the Ganfu era of the Tang Dynasty, a reed with three joints sprouted in the residence of Fan Yang's Li Quanzhong, who was then serving as the司马 of Di Prefecture. He considered this unusual and sought an explanation from Zhang Jianzhang, a scholar well-versed in ancient knowledge. Zhang Jianzhang said, "In the past, Pu Hong saw a reed with nine joints grow in a pond, which was considered an auspicious sign, and he founded the Later Zhao dynasty. A reed is a type of grass that grows in wetlands, but it has now grown indoors, which is not its usual place. You will surely achieve a noble status, and the three joints suggest that the leadership will be passed on to three people." Li Quanzhong later served Li Keju and was gradually promoted to the position of Inspector General. In military affairs, he had very strict policies. After Li Quanzhong's death, his son Li Kuangwei succeeded him, but was later expelled by the generals, who then installed Li Kuangchou. Li Kuangchou was later attacked by Lu Yanwei in Jingcheng, and he fled to the capital with his family, reaching Cangzhou. In Jingfu Year 2, he died in Jingcheng. Li Quanzhong was from a humble background and often engaged in drinking and gambling. The scholars of Yuyang often envied him. Once, he went fishing with some ruffians at the Red Pillar Bridge on the Sanggan River and prayed with wine.

If I were to have control over the region of Youzhou, I would catch a great fish. Indeed, I caught a fish that was three feet long, which was quite remarkable. There was a man named Ma Du (also written as Yu), who lacked literary talent. Kangwei once asked him about his age, and Ma Du replied that he had just turned twenty and had been so for two weeks, showing arrogance on his face. Later, Kangwei succeeded his father as a marquis and first summoned Ma Du. Ma Du asked, "How many weeks have passed since your weak crown?" Ma Du then knelt and apologized. Kangwei said, "Your good deeds are all my life's loves. What have you to fear?" He appointed Ma Du to a position in the government, and because of his broad and open-minded nature, many people followed him. Baoguangzi once met a familiar person from Fanyang who told him that Li Kuangchou's wife, Zhang, was a national beauty. Her brother, Kangwei, who was a general, forcibly raped her. Li Kuangchou drew his sword and waited. At night, his wife returned and walked out, and he killed her for his husband. Kangwei was ashamed to see his brother and the generals, and some said he wanted to lead troops to rescue Zhenzhou. Once out of the city, the three armies appointed Li Kuangchou as their commander, and Kangwei claimed he wanted to return to court and passed through Changshan. There was also an incident where Wang Rong was seized as a hostage. Li Kuangchou sent a letter to Wang Rong, repeatedly pointing out the situation, but never mentioned the matter of the rape, hiding the national evil.

Zhang Jianzhang's Encounter with a Fairy While Crossing the Sea Zhang Jianzhang served as a military governor in Youzhou and later as a county governor, particularly fond of the classics and history, collecting up to ten thousand volumes of books. His residence had a library where he spent his time reading and studying in a calm and pure manner, managing all the places he visited. Once, he was sent on a mission by the military governor to the Bohai Sea. Upon encountering strong winds and waves, he had to anchor his ship. Suddenly, a servant in blue arrived in a small leaf boat and told Zhang Jianzhang that he was following the command of a great fairy and invited him to come. Zhang Jianzhang agreed, and they reached a grand structure where he saw majestic towers. Inside, a female fairy resided with many attendants. The food and utensils were all familiar flavors from Zhang Jianzhang's hometown. After finishing the meal, Zhang Jianzhang took his leave. The female fairy told him, "You do not deceive in the dark; you are a true gentleman." Suddenly, he suffered from the troubles of wind and waves. The fairy sent the servant to guide him, and upon returning, the wind and waves calmed down. They traveled back and forth without fear. They returned to the western shore and passed by a monument commemorating Emperor Taizong's campaign against the Liao.

A Man Named Jianzhang Who Could Read Through Water During the reign of Emperor Mingzong, a man named Jianzhang lived in Jilin. He had the extraordinary ability to read through water by placing a bundle of wheat bran wrapped in silk into the water and touching it to read. His dedication to learning was such that he never missed a single character. Everyone in Jilin could tell about him, and at that time, his story was also known to the court. Baoguangzi once met a military officer from Jilin named Sun (his name is forgotten) who recounted a detailed conversation about Zhang Dafu encountering a water fairy who left a piece of silk (鲛绡). Zhang Dafu brought the silk himself and presented it to the court. Those who were interested wrote a biography for him. Today, a Taoist priest at the Taqing Palace in Bozhou has obtained the original text. He says that Emperor Mingzong had something to do with the altar in Jianzhang's hometown, and the people there held the eastern treasure. He said that outside the state seal, there were only two other items, one of which was the silk presented by Jianzhang, stored in a box and rolled like silk. It was tied with three red threads. It is also said that in summer, when unfolded, it can fill the room with a cool sensation. Recently, changes have occurred, and no one knows where it is now. --- This translation aims to be faithful to the original text while maintaining clarity and readability in English. It addresses the idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as planned in the pre-drafting research.

The Tang Dynasty, compiled by Sun Guangxian Li Muzheng Coerced the Prince to Kill the Chancellor In Fengxiang, Li Muzheng was extremely arrogant and domineering. Emperor Zhaozong said to the chancellor Du Rangneng, "According to the principles of the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, rebellion must be punished. How can there be such open defiance of the court's laws and disrespect within the inner regions? If I do not punish him, how will the four directions regard me?" Du Rangneng responded, "Since the difficulties began, we have been following the example of the Zhengyuan era, indulging military officials for a long time. Their roots are deep and their influence is widespread, with many people supporting them. It is difficult to suddenly change this situation. Moreover, the capital is only a short distance from Qishui, and people's hearts are easily filled with fear and apprehension. If there is any disrespect or loss of dignity, the damage will be even greater. I hope Your Majesty will temper your anger and carefully consider the matter." The emperor replied, "Political affairs and laws are in disarray, and imperial edicts are not being enforced beyond the capital gates. I do not want to be weak and peaceful while sitting idly by and removing the chancellor Xu Yanruo and appointing Li Muzheng as the governor of Xingyuan Prefecture, with the heir Prince Qitan leading the imperial guards to escort Xu Yanruo. If Li Muzheng refuses to step down, he will attack with his troops. The decision lies entirely with Du Rangneng. Du Rangneng repeatedly advised against it, but the emperor did not listen. The imperial army ultimately suffered a defeat. Some say this action was Du Rangneng's way of avenging a personal grudge. Li Muzheng had previously written a long letter to Du Rangneng and then submitted a memorial, still with his troops at the Lingo Station, requesting the execution of the chancellor. The emperor then ordered the execution of the chief of the Military Council, Li Zhou Tong, as a warning. Du Rangneng was demoted and ordered to be sent to the front lines. Li Muzheng prepared a ceremony and went out to meet him at the station, then submitted another memorial requesting the enforcement of court law. Du Rangneng responded, "The guilt of Chao Cuo has mistakenly fallen upon me. If I am blamed, it can alleviate the country's difficulties." The emperor had no choice but to demote Du Rangneng to the position of a county magistrate in Leizhou, and sent an imperial envoy to kill him at the station. Those who understood the situation felt that Du Rangneng faced danger without seeking to escape, and thus died honorably. Later, he was posthumously bestowed the title of Grand Commandant. His son, Du Xiao, had a face like carved jade and talent in drafting imperial edicts. He served in the state of Liang and became a chancellor. In the years of Fengli, there were rebellious troops in Luoyang who mistakenly harmed him, and at that time, everyone lamented and regretted it.

In the second year of the Ganning era of the Tang dynasty, Wang Xingyu of Binzhou gathered his army to guard against Li Maogeng and Han Jian, who were summoned to the court to plot the deposition and replacement of the emperor. Upon seeing the three generals arrive together, the emperor suspected an unusual plot and met them on the imperial platform, asking, "Why did you come without being summoned? What is your intention?" Li Maogeng and others were so nervous that they were sweating profusely and could not answer. They merely said that the Northern and Southern Offices had disrupted court politics. The emperor then removed Wei Zhao-du for his failed campaign in Western Sichuan and issued an edict to harm him and others. After the three generals returned to their posts, both inside and outside the court felt wronged. Wang Xingyu was arrogant and domineering in the court and intended to appoint himself as the Grand Commandant. Wei Zhao-du strongly opposed this, saying, "Emperor Taizong used this position to prevent politics and ascend to the throne. Later, Guo Ziyi, who established merit during the Six Dynasties, held this title but never accepted it, always yielding to others. How can it be lightly bestowed upon Wang Xingyu? He should be given the title of Elder Statesman instead." However, at this point, Wang Xingyu resented Li Qi and suddenly suffered this harm. Posthumously, he was bestowed the title of Grand Tutor. Li Qi, courtesy name Jingwang, was appointed as chancellor, and the imperial edict was issued. Liu Chonggui embraced and wept over it, and Li Qi was reassigned to the position of Tutor to the Crown Prince. He submitted ten memorials and five essays on accepting advice to seek exoneration. Eventually, he was promoted and exposed Liu Chonggui's wickedness. At the time, Cui Zhao-wei, who was on good terms with Wei Zhao-du and Li Qi, was demoted and executed by the emperor, who was furious. An edict was issued to arrest Cui Jian, and this was a case of retribution.

The Success and Failure of Literati Generals In ancient times, scholars and warriors were one, capable of becoming generals or ministers. This was the case with figures like Pei Xingjian, Guo Yuanzhen, Pei Du, and Wei Gao. However, times have their ups and downs, and cannot be generalized. Wang Duo, for instance, initially served as the governor of Jingnan but fled when the Huang Chao rebels approached. Later, he led troops to defend Tongguan, but Huang Chao sent a message saying, "Sir, you are a scholar, and not my equal. Do not soil my blades; you will bring about your own downfall." After this defeat, Wang Duo went to Chengdu, where he was appointed as the commander of various armies by Gao Pian, who publicly denounced him as a defeated general. This was indeed the case. Counselor Zheng Bao once wrote to advise Wang Duo, saying, "I do not know what kind of people you have as your generals or advisors. In today's chaotic times, when rebels have taken control and heroes are fighting, it is not the time for idle scholars to engage in leisurely conversation." Later, Wang Duo was stripped of his military power and stationed in Huaitai, where he eventually faced the disaster of Beizhou. Zhang Wen, on the other hand, was the first to advocate for the restoration of order and issued edicts to suppress the rebels, killing Huang Chao and stabilizing the region. However, his efforts were undermined when his subordinates, led by Li Changyan, forced him out.

In their positions, many officials were unable to hold firm, as seen in the cases of Cui Qiu from Yuezhou, Cui Jin from Hunan, Wei Xiu from Fujian, Cai Chong from Yuzhou, Zhi Xiang from Xu Prefecture, Xue Neng from Xuchang, Li Du from Hedong, Dou Yu from Dou Prefecture, and Xu Yanruo from Fengxiang, who were all in a state of panic and desperation, too busy trying to escape to think clearly. Only Zhang Jun made grandiose claims about himself, comparing himself to Zhuge Liang and Guan Yu, but his lack of strategy led to the failure of Han Jian's campaign and the disastrous expedition led by Sun Kui against the Great Hal. Later, Zhu Pu followed in this pattern, making grandiose claims and quickly rising to a high position, even submitting a memorial requesting the defeat of Fengxiang, which was seen as sending weak forces against strong enemies, akin to throwing an egg at a stone. Fortunately, it was not used, but what more could one expect? A guest said to Bao Guangzi, "Indeed, there are scholars who can lead armies, but since the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, military affairs have been treated as a game for too long. In the imperial court, it is shameful to speak of military strategy, and even the tools of war are seen as ominous. Those like Lu Fan and Xue Neng are considered crude talents. Once the dust rises in the land, and the wind stirs in the east, it is ridiculous to send scholars and officials to lead military campaigns, as they are only fit for ridicule. Such was the case with Wei Zhaodu's hesitation against Wang Jian and Zhang Jun's campaign against Taiyuan."

### Final Translation Deputy Military Governor of Hedong, Li Xi, often failed to pass the imperial examinations and became an assistant to Li Du in Hezhong. When Li Du lost control, Li Xi traveled from Zhao to Taiyuan and was appointed as an assistant. Li Xi was a diligent scholar and skilled writer, never putting down his books even while on horseback or in the midst of military campaigns. All official documents and military reports from Taiyuan were written by Li Xi. He accompanied Li Keyong to Weinan and was ordered to present a report. The emperor valued his literary skills and appointed him as a censor, ordering him to serve in the northern office to honor him. Eventually, Li Xi returned to Taiyuan and resumed his military duties. After Emperor Zhuangzong ascended the throne, he posthumously granted Li Xi the title of Minister of Rites. Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Liang admired the quality of documents from Taiyuan and said, "Why can't I have such a person?" Even Chen Lin and Ruan Yu, famous literary figures, are not superior to this. Han Jian consistently followed Li Maozhen in forcing the emperor and killing ministers. Emperor Zhaozong was forced to move to Fuzhi, and Han Jian earnestly invited the emperor to return to Huaxia, showing his loyalty and service. His contributions to the imperial court, recorded in the *Qinwang Lu*, included the labor of providing supplies and support. However, he also killed Prince Tancheng and seven others, isolating the emperor and suppressing his crimes. Modern historians criticized the *Qinwang Lu* for several points, noting that Han Jian did not encounter a favorable time but claimed to protect the roads and guard the imperial carriage, trying to cover up but making his actions more obvious. He also claimed to follow imperial edicts in letters to various regions, which were false. Moreover, he never led the feudal lords to preserve and stabilize the state. Believing that the decline of the Tang dynasty was due to fate, with various rebellions and usurpers, and that Han Jian was just another in a series of such figures, the ruler of Shu heard this and laughed, saying that Han Jian did not transform like a leopard.

Zhi's talent is suitable for working with Zhu Wen. The scholar Bao Guang said: Han Jian of Huazhou and Cheng Rui of Jingzhou worked diligently to serve the country and were worthy of praise. At the time, they were known as North Han and South Guo (Guo being Cheng Rui's nickname). Scholars and officials could rely on them. In ancient times, feudal lords who lacked virtue and were small would not attend the Kuizhou conference; this is the standard they followed. Cheng and Han held positions as advisors to the emperor and were favored by the Tang dynasty. Zhu Wen had no loyalty to the emperor and openly showed his intention to usurp power. Han Jian and others could not emulate Zang Hong's loyalty and failed to rally their allies. They could have formed alliances with neighboring states to bolster their strength, but instead, they aided the tyrant and committed evil deeds, turning into a snake that bit itself. They abandoned their virtuous neighbors and created a false sense of security. When Huayin fell, they were powerless to resist the invasion from Ezhu. They were defeated without justification and perished, which was neither loyal nor righteous. This is what I mean by the two men's actions being fundamentally flawed. If Cheng Rui had allied with the feudal lords of the southern regions and secured powerful support, the usurpation by the people of Bian might not have occurred. Even if one cannot determine the will of heaven, one should consider human strategy; there is no point in pursuing such a course. Alas! Kong Wei's Concern Over the Salt and Iron Seals Kong Wei, while serving as a high-ranking official, Zhu Wen had control over several military regions and was powerful. He requested the salt and iron seals, and the emperor issued an edict for the chancellors to discuss it. Kong Wei strongly opposed it, and when the officials below him urged him to comply, he said, "If Zhu Wen takes the salt and iron seals, he will surely rise in rebellion." Zhu Wen eventually withdrew his request. Later, Han Jian led a campaign against Taiyuan and suffered a defeat, which was attributed to Zhang Jun's miscalculation. He was subsequently demoted. One day, Emperor Zhaozong wanted to launch another attack on Fengxiang and consulted Kong Wei. Kong Wei said, "Fengxiang is the western gate of the imperial palace. If we leave our stronghold and allow ourselves to be controlled by one side, it will be the end of the great cause." The emperor said, "You are my virtuous minister, but you do not understand the current situation." Kong Wei replied, "If the emperor considers me virtuous, it is a slander. If I were truly virtuous, would I remain in your court?" He then claimed illness and retired as a Grand Tutor, dying in Huayang.

### Divine Warning to Luo Hongxin [with Son Shao Wei Attached] During the Zhonghe period, Luo Hongxin, the military governor of Weibo, initially served as a low-ranking officer responsible for managing livestock. He once stayed overnight outside the Guanyin Temple in Weizhou, where there was a shrine to a deity known colloquially as "White Beard Old Man." A shaman named Song Qian (or Qian) suddenly visited Luo Hongxin and said, "Last night, the deity spoke and said that you will soon become the ruler of this region." Luo Hongxin angrily replied, "Are you trying to harm me?" The next day, Song Qian repeated the same message. Luo Hongxin then ordered the matter to be kept secret. Unexpectedly, within a year, there was a military uprising, and Luo Hongxin was chosen as the new military governor. Despite his initial lack of fame, Luo Hongxin's imposing appearance, strength, and skill with a bow won the respect of his troops. He was later promoted to Grand Commandant and granted the title of Duke of Linhuai. After Luo Hongxin's death, his son Shao Wei succeeded him and formed close ties with Zhu Wen, the powerful warlord who would later establish the Later Liang dynasty. Before this, the Weibo region had a specialized elite military unit of eight thousand men, who were given generous clothing and rations, and were often indulged by the military governor. People at the time said, "The emperor in Chang'an is less powerful than the Weibo elite soldiers." The military governor had been repeatedly deposed and replaced, leading to increasing arrogance among the elite soldiers. Shao Wei grew dissatisfied and decided to eliminate them. He devised a plan with the people of Bian (modern-day Kaifeng) to deceive the elite soldiers. They pretended to be transporting weapons and armor, claiming that the warlord was burying a member of the Luo family. Shao Wei secretly had his men cut the ropes and straps of the weapons and armor in the armory. That night, the people of Bian donned armor and wielded weapons, attacking and killing the elite soldiers. The elite soldiers became aware of the attack and rushed into the armory, but their weapons and armor were useless. The attack was so effective that the entire camp was wiped out, and their families were also destroyed. Shao Wei justified this action by saying that the elite soldiers had long been powerful and deserved their fate. Although Shao Wei had a pure heart, the region fell into chaos and gradually came under the control of Zhu Wen, who drained the treasury to support him. Suddenly, Shao Wei developed a painful foot ailment, which he believed was caused by the spirits of the elite soldiers. He told his close attendants, "Even if all the iron in six states and forty-three counties were used, it would not be enough to make a mistake." Shao Wei died, and his son Zhou Han succeeded him. Soon after, Zhou Han was transferred to Huatai, and the Luo family lost their power. ---

Duke Liu Rengong of Yan's Strange Dream When Liu Rengong was still humble, he once dreamed that a Buddhist banner flew out from his fingers. Someone interpreted it, saying, "You will attain noble rank with banners and flags when you are forty-nine." Later, as predicted, he became the commander of You Prefecture. After defeating the Taiyuan army at Anse City, his soldiers were strong and invincible, and he looked down on neighboring regions. He conscripted all able-bodied men within his jurisdiction, numbering thirty thousand, and marched south to capture Ye City, aiming to emulate Cao Cao's ambitions. Upon conquering Ganling, he massacred all, young and old. Initially, while garrisoning Ganling City, several owls flew into his tent, and despite chasing them away, they kept returning. Liu Rengong was displeased, and ultimately, he was attacked by the combined forces of the Wei and Bian armies, suffering a great defeat. They killed his famous general Dan Kajie, and Liu Rengong fled alone on horseback. At that time, his army was defeated at Neihuang. Later, when the Bian commander attacked Yan, they also suffered a defeat at Tang River. One day, he sent envoys to Bian, and the Bian commander held a banquet with jesters and actors playing the role of doctors, mocking Liu Rengong. He asked about the nature of the illness in Neihuang and what medicine could cure it. The envoy replied, "Neihuang can be cured by soaking it in the Tang River water." The host and guests laughed, and they praised the envoy for his cleverness.

In the fifteenth volume of *Beimeng Suoyan*, compiled by Sun Guangxian of the Tang dynasty: **Wearing a Simple Coat to the Palace Gate** In the first year of the Tianfu era, Li Maozhen of Fengxiang requested to enter the imperial court to report matters, and the court agreed. This was because the military commander Han Quanwei was closely allied with him. Emperor Zhaozong held a ceremony on the Anfulou, where Li Maozhen wept and spoke of his support and rescue. At the time, Cui Yun secretly advised the emperor, saying, "He is a treacherous person and should not be trusted. Your Majesty should treat him with leniency." The next day, during a banquet at the Shouchun Hall, Li Maozhen arrived in a sedan chair, wearing a simple camel hair coat, and entered through the Golden Phoenix Gate. He changed his attire for the banquet, and all present thought that such brazen behavior had never been seen before. At the time, Han Quanwei was deeply allied with Li Maozhen, and Cui Yun was afraid of him. As a result, Li Maozhen also formed an alliance with Zhu Quanzhong, leading to the emperor being invited to visit Bianzhou, and continuous warfare between Fengxiang and Bianzhou, ultimately forcing the emperor to move to Luoyang. Scholars recognized this as a situation similar to the time when Prince Wu summoned troops, comparing Cui Yun to the historical figure. Before Li Maozhen entered the capital, he burned the city. During the banquet, a court jester named An Peixin called Li Maozhen "Fire Dragon Son." Li Maozhen was ashamed and lowered his head. After the banquet, someone said that this jester should be executed. An Peixin heard this and requested to go to Fengxiang to seek help. Li Maozhen saw him from afar and scolded him, saying, "This jester is poor. Why did you dare to come?" An Peixin replied, "I just want to take care of my daily routine; I am not asking for help. Recently, in the capital, I can sell bran and charcoal to make a living." Li Maozhen laughed and generously rewarded and pardoned him.

Zhu Quanzhong welcomed the imperial carriage in Fengxiang. The military official Han Quanhuai, fearing Zhu Quanzhong's growing power, harbored the intention of using the emperor to command the feudal lords. Wang Jian of Western Shu also had this concern and formed an alliance with Bianzhou to raise an army to assist in welcoming the emperor. The Bianzhou army and the army of Western Shu attacked Xingyuan. The governor of Xingyuan, Wang Wanhong, without rescue, surrendered to Chengdu. As a result, the fourteen states of the southern mountains came under Shu control. After changing plans, they instead supported Fengxiang. At this time, the chief secretary Wei Zhuang was sent as an envoy to the front lines. Zhu Quanzhong became very angry and lost favor with Western Shu. The Bianzhou army disbanded. ---

### Translation Han Jian Betrays Li Juchuan Li Juchuan was a man of letters who, despite failing the imperial examinations, served under a false regime [one version reads "as a vassal"] for the Prince of Xiang and Tang Yanqian, both of whom were demoted to Shan Nan. The commander of Shan Nan, Yang Shǒuliàng, treated him kindly. After the fall of Shan Nan, Li Juchuan was dismissed from office. Yang Jūnróng Fùgōng and Yang Shǒuliàng fled north to Taiyuan together, with guides leading them out of Huazhou. Yang Jūnróng Fùgōng was humiliated by Han Jian, who called him a slave and confined him in a short robe, ultimately causing his death at the hands of a withered tree. Yang Shǒuliàng was caged and sent to the capital, where he was executed at the Lone Willow Tree. The people of the capital wept profusely, as this was none other than the "One-Zhang Black" of the Southern Mountains, originally surnamed Zī. During the time of Huang Chao, he frequently rescued and guided officials to safety, leading them to the imperial presence. Those who escaped Huang Chao and sought refuge with him were all saved, and thus many people were grateful to him. Li Juchuan served as Han Jian's deputy. Zhu Lingong, commanding his troops at Huazhou, used Zhang Jùn's strategy to first capture Han Jian. His aide Zhang Cè, carrying the seal, accompanied Li Juchuan to the camp gate to surrender. Zhu Lingong said to them, "The emperor's flight westward was all your doing." Han Jian replied, "I cannot read; all the imperial edicts, memorials, and military proclamations were written by Li Juchuan." Consequently, he was executed. Those who understood the situation believed that Han Jian had no integrity and sought to appease Zhu Lingong's anger, but he was ultimately betrayed. People at the time felt great sympathy for Li Juchuan. Li Juchuan had a son named Li Shènyí, who served as a翰林学士in the Later Tang Dynasty. Only Zhang Cè, who originally shared a connection with Zhang Jùn, carried the seal and surrendered, following Zhang Jùn's plan. Later, he rose to become a chancellor of the Later Liang. After Zhu Lingong captured Han Jian, he called him his elder brother and soon ordered him to be moved to Xuancheng [one version reads "Tian"].
