In the early years of the Later Liang's Feng era, he also met his end.

Emperor Dezong loved to compose poems to grant to meritorious ministers, such as Dai Shulun of Rongzhou. Emperors Wenzong and Xuanzong also granted poems to their ministers. Emperor Zhaozong stayed in Huazhou and granted songs to Han Jian, and poems and lyrics about willow branches to Zhu Quanzhong. The grants were essentially the same, and those who received them either out of respect or fear could not help but seek the meaning. Zhu Gong, the governor of Bianzhou, besieged Fengxiang again and fought against Maowen's army at Huayin Station west of Guoxian, defeating the army of Qizhou. Bodies lay scattered without end, and the stench reached ten miles. Then, Emperor Zhaozong killed twenty-two eunuchs including Han Quanhui, whose heads were shown to Maowen. Zhu also beheaded his adopted son Ji Jun and sent his head. Thus, the imperial entourage returned to the palace. Zhu Gong bowed before the horse and begged for forgiveness, tears streaming, leading the emperor's horse for a thousand steps. The emperor was moved. Upon reaching the capital, Chancellor Cui Yun was appointed to command the six armies, and an edict was issued to execute 710 eunuchs including the fifth Duke Ke Fan. Also, more than a hundred officials including Prime Minister Lu Guangqi were ordered to commit suicide. In the third year of Tianfu, the people of Bianzhou raised arms and killed.

In the late Tang Dynasty, Chancellor Cui Yun and Jingzhao Governor Zheng Yuan Gui forcibly relocated the imperial carriage to Donglu (Luoyang). Upon arriving in Huazhou, the common people shouted "Long live the emperor!" The emperor wept and told the people, "Do not shout 'Long live the emperor!' I am unable to be your ruler." Along the way, there were words of longing for the imperial city, and he said, "On the top of Heda Mountain, the birds freeze to death. Why don't they fly away to a place where they can be happy? How much more so in this uncertain journey, I do not know where I will end up." After speaking, he wept bitterly. When they arrived at the imperial banquet in Shaanfu, the Empress personally presented a jade bowl to Zhu Quanzhong, and the imperial concubines sang. Zhu Quanzhong was about to drink when Han Jian stepped on his foot, and Zhu Quanzhong, fearing for his life, pretended to be drunk and withdrew. He later killed the imperial concubines and 500 attendants at Gu River, leaving the emperor isolated. Emperor Zhaozong was assassinated. After moving the capital to Luoyang, those around him were all people from Bianzhou (Kaifeng). Although he had a lofty title, it was empty and powerless, like a bird in a cage, feeling depressed and unhappy. Zhu Quanzhong believed that all the feudal lords had the intention to restore order and feared that the emperor might plan to escape. At one point, Zhu Youliang and others gathered troops in the palace courtyard, complaining about insufficient food and clothing. The emperor was trying to comfort and persuade Zhu Youliang, but Zhu Youliang said, "I dare not be disrespectful; I am following the orders of the commander-in-chief." The emperor rushed into the kitchen and killed several people with the cook's knife, but he was ultimately killed by the rebel soldiers. The imperial concubines Li Jianrong and Pei Zhengyi also killed the emperor and died by the sword. Zhu Shucong, Zhu Youliang's brother-in-law, incited the soldiers, and Zhu Quanzhong requested that Zhu Shucong be executed and that the blame for Cheng Ji's crime be placed on Zhu Youliang and others. On the verge of execution, Zhu Shucong cried out to heaven, "If there is a heaven, one day you will also treat me as you do now." Zhu Quanzhong ascended the throne but was later killed by his son Zhu Yougui, just as Zhu Shucong had predicted.

**Translation:** Request to Execute the Prince of De Upon ascending the throne, Emperor Zhaozong held a grand feast for the Prince of De, Yu, and his descendants. They were all secretly killed by Zhu Quanzhong. The Prince of De was the emperor's older brother who had once been enfeoffed as the crown prince. Liu Jishu and others deposed Emperor Zhaozong and enthroned him as emperor. After Liu Jishu and others were executed, the emperor ordered the Prince of De to return to the Shaoyang Palace. Zhu Quanzhong, seeing that the Prince of De had clear and elegant features and was gradually maturing, grew jealous of him and requested that Cui Yun secretly advise the emperor that the prince had once usurped the throne, and that justice demanded his execution. The emperor refused to comply. One day, while the emperor was at the Fuxian Temple, he said to Jiang Yuanhui, the chief of the Imperial Secretariat, "The Prince of De is my beloved son. Why do you keep having me depose him, and now you want to kill him?" After speaking, tears fell from his eyes, and he bit his middle finger, causing blood to flow. Upon hearing this, Zhu Quanzhong ordered all the princes and their descendants to be killed after the feast. Conspiracy to Harm the Literati After Emperor Zhaozong ascended the throne, during the Tianyou period, Zhu Quanzhong, seeing that officials from the former court were still in high positions, planned to seize power. He first had them demoted and reduced in rank, gradually eliminating them. Former prime ministers Pei Shu, Dugu Sun, Cui Yuan, Lu Yi, Wang Pu, and other ministers Zhao Chong and Wang Ti were all ordered to commit suicide at the White Horse Inn in Huazhou. At the time, Chancellor Liu Can, who was cunning, greedy for power, and deceitful, hated that the former prime ministers were above him in status. He was close to Zhu Quanzhong's inner circle and secretly colluded with Jiang Yuanhui, the chief of the Imperial Secretariat, and Zhang Tingfan, the Grand Master of Rites, to harm the former prime ministers. Soon after, Zhang Tingfan was torn apart, and Jiang Yuanhui and Liu Can, along with his brothers Yao and Jian, were executed one after another. Prior to this, the entire family of Zhang Jun, the former prime minister, was killed, and their bodies were thrown into the Yellow River. Zhu Quanzhong's chief strategist, Li Zhen, had failed multiple times in the imperial examinations and resented the court officials. He said to Zhu Quanzhong, "These scholars should be thrown into the Yellow River to become part of the muddy waters forever." Zhu Quanzhong smiled and agreed. The massacre at Huayin, where the literati were not to blame, occurred. Soon after, Emperor Zhaozong abdicated and was bestowed the title of Prince of Jiyin, who died from poisoning in Caizhou. The Tang dynasty thus came to an end.

Accusing Empress Dowager He Zhu Quanzhong first appointed Jiang Yuanhui as the Chief of the Military Council to spy on the emperor's movements. After the assassination of Emperor Zhaozong, Empress Dowager He, fearing for her safety, often summoned Jiang Yuanhui personally to give him instructions and warnings about what she had requested. She spoke of ensuring the safety of mother and child after the future abdication, and her words were always accompanied by tears. Previously, Zhu Quanzhong had urgently demanded the abdication and summoned Jiang Yuanhui to Bianzhou, criticizing him for being too slow. Jiang Yuanhui argued that the abdication should first be granted the nine bestowals and then followed the proper order. Zhu Quanzhong became angry and said, "I don't want the nine bestowals; do you think I am an emperor?" Jiang Yuanhui returned to Luoyang and consulted with the chancellor, who was slandered by Zhao Yinhang, claiming that Jiang Yuanhui was conspiring with Empress Dowager He to prolong the Tang dynasty. As a result, Zhu Quanzhong ordered Zhao Yinhang to force the killing of Empress Dowager He and the palace women, and to execute Jiang Yuanhui. At the time, people felt great injustice towards this. Zhao Yinhang later changed his surname to Kong and took the name Xun, but no one knew his true identity. He served under Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang as an Envoy of the Imperial Household and was later appointed governor of Xuchang and Cangzhou. People knew him to be cunning and treacherous, and no one did not fear him.

**Translation:** Serving One's Aunt with Illness In the third year of the Tianyou era of the Tang Dynasty, Cui Zuan, an official appointed to assist in the compilation of historical records, submitted a petition stating that his aunt, residing in a private estate in Jiyuan, Mengzhou, was gravely ill and lacked brothers to care for her. There were no close relatives nearby to provide support, and with her approaching seventy years of age, she was far from home, unable to receive proper medical care and nourishment day and night. Therefore, he requested leave to personally attend to her illness. The emperor granted his request, and the people admired him. Some said that avoiding misfortune while enjoying the name of righteousness was also a form of wisdom. Qin Zongquan Accused of Not Rebellious After the defeat of Huang Chao, Qin Zongquan, who continued to rebel in Caozhou, had strong and sharp military forces and again claimed imperial authority. The various states of Shandong suffered greatly from his actions. Over ten years, he slaughtered and plundered the local population. The commander of Bianzhou, Zhu Quanzhong, finally managed to subdue him. Qin Zongquan was captured by his subordinate Shen Cong, who broke his leg and bound him. Zhu Quanzhong submitted a report detailing the capture, and Qin Zongquan was sent in a cage to the capital. The governor of the capital region, Sun Kui, inspected him. Qin Zongquan raised his head in the cage and said, "I did not rebel." The governor felt pity for him, and the people found it amusing. Li Mohun, Who Threw His Bowl to Become a Soldier Li Hanzi was from Heyang. As a young man, he was a disreputable Buddhist monk who was not accepted anywhere. He once begged for food in Suzhou, Suozuo County, but no one gave him anything from morning until evening. In frustration, he threw his bowl to the ground, destroyed his monk's robe, and joined the army of Zhu Geshuang in Heyang. Li Hanzi took the name of the monk he had been and became known as Li Mohun. He was naturally strong and often got into fights, sometimes beating the left cheek of his opponent, sometimes the right, causing them to bleed. Zhu Geshuang soon appointed him as a low-ranking officer. Whenever he was sent to fight rebels, he captured them all. In the north of Pu and Jiang, there was a mountain called Moyun, where a fortress was built and named Moyun Camp. No one could capture it before Li Hanzi, who eventually did so. From then on, he was known as Li Mohun. He served in various positions, including governor of several counties, governor of Henan, and a high-ranking official. He died in Bianzhou, and his fate was similar to that of Cheng Rui of Jingnan. [From the Tang to the Later Liang, the text is incomplete.]

**Translation:** Trivial Notes from the North Dream, Volume Fifteen *<Subclass: Miscellaneous, Trivial Notes from the North Dream>* Authorized Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature Trivial Notes from the North Dream, Volume Sixteen By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty Causing Disaster Through Alcohol When Liang Zong was planning his rise to power, Jiang Yantong, the governor of Shouzhou, surrendered his territory to him. Liang Zong then sent his trusted official Zhang Conghui to reward Jiang Yantong for his efforts. However, Zhang Conghui was a good-for-nothing. After drinking, he was accompanied by a drunkard named He Cangyao, with whom he became very close. Every matter was reported incorrectly to Zhang Conghui, who then gave orders to the city. Jiang Yantong held a grand celebration but was not invited. Instead, he had a meal with He Cangyao at the main general's house. Jiang Yantong became suspicious and fearful, thinking that the King of Bian (Liang Zong) was plotting against him. He executed the main general and dozens of others, then reported the matter to Liang Zong. Later, Jiang Yantong became even more suspicious and fearful, thinking that his close confidants were plotting against him. He committed suicide by hanging. Liang Zong became very angry and ordered Zhang Conghui to be executed by decapitation. He released He Cangyao and later had him executed in the market of Shouzhou. Preserving Light said: During the reign of Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang, Dong Zhang occupied Dongchuan and had rebellious intentions. At that time, a guest provincial envoy named Li Renju was sent to visit Zitong. Li Renju was originally a low-ranking officer who quickly rose to an important position. He had a fondness for debauchery. The military commander set up a banquet and invited Li Renju under the pretext of cold weather, but Li Renju did not come. Instead, he had a private banquet with a camp prostitute. Dong Zhang heard about this and became very angry, immediately wanting to harm Li Renju. Li Renju appeared at the door with his feet bound and dressed formally. Dong Zhang's anger slightly subsided. Later, Dong Zhang rebelled, and both provinces raised armies, which was due to Li Renju's advice to An Zhonghe.

The Envoy from Shu Who Drank Heavily When Emperor Zhu Wen of Later Liang first controlled four commanderies, he sent an envoy named Pan Wan on a mission. Pan Wan drank a large amount of wine without becoming disordered, and each time he drank, his demeanor became even more solemn. Emperor Zhu Wen admired him. When they were drinking, Emperor Zhu Wen asked, "Can you drink from a whole set of vessels?" Pan Wan replied, "I dare not." Then, the vessels were arranged on the table in order, and Pan Wan drank from them all. He remained even more composed. Emperor Zhu Wen thought that upon returning to his quarters, he would likely be too drunk to sleep, so he had someone spy on him. Upon returning, Pan Wan wore a hat made from bamboo shoots and weighed the wine vessels he had obtained, cleaned them, and stored them. On another occasion, Emperor Zhu Wen sent another envoy named Zheng Xu on a mission. Emperor Zhu Wen asked about the dangerous roads of the Jie Gate. Zheng Xu described them as extremely perilous. Emperor Zhu Wen said, "The wise host can be passed through." Zheng Xu replied, "If it is not reported, it might mislead the general's military plans." Emperor Zhu Wen laughed and said, "This is also an excellent example of a modern envoy." The Death of Brother by Brother When Zhu Jin held onto Yan Prefecture and Emperor Zhu Wen attacked him without success, his paternal uncle, Governor Qiong of Qi Prefecture, surrendered first. Qiong and Zhu Jin went together to the city wall to explain the situation. Zhu Jin then sent the chief of the guard, Hu Gui, to present a letter saying, "My elder brother has surrendered. Please spare my life and allow me to hand over the prefecture to the officials." Later, they opened the Yan寿 gate and placed the seal in a box, saying, "My elder brother has come and asked to present this first." Emperor Zhu Wen ordered Qiong to take it. Ge Congzhou suspected a trick and selected brave warriors like Sun Shaodi to guard Qiong with swords. Qiong said, "They are exhausted and not worth suspecting." Qiong approached to receive the seal, and Zhu Jin rode alone and said, "My elder brother has come alone to speak privately." As soon as they met, Zhu Jin ordered the brave soldier Dong Huaijin to pull and drag Qiong off his horse. Qiong then revealed a hidden blade and killed Qiong. Zhu Jin pulled and dragged him out and pulled him into the city. Soon, the city walls were filled with drums and shouts, and Qiong's head was thrown at the wall. Emperor Zhu Wen's army was shocked and he mourned deeply for a long time. He ordered the execution of the military strategist Xu Hou and appointed Qiong's younger brother Pin as the governor of Qi Prefecture, giving him special favor and courtesy. Zhu Jin eventually abandoned the city and fled to Yangzhou.

Ma Jing Set a Deception Zhu Wen stationed his troops in Qishan to welcome Emperor Zhaozong. The enemy's defenses were still strong, and in the morning, he thought of retreating. His personal attendant and commander, Gao Jichang, spoke out boldly, saying, "Those who have been watching this move for a year are now in a difficult position. Let us wait a little longer." Gao Jichang then secretly recruited people to enter Qishan as informants. There was a mounted soldier named Ma Jing who responded to the call. Because Zhu Youlun was in charge of the cavalry, and with the impending large-scale military action, Ma Jing asked for a horse to be given at the time, and mixed among the troops that were being sent out. About ten li away, he leaped on horseback and escaped to the west, knocking on the gates of Qishan to communicate the army's resentment and eastward retreat. He also said that the camps were hiding, as if there were no one, and that the enemy was still waiting for the evening to pass, suggesting that it was advisable to quickly attack them, as it would fall into their trap. If they missed this opportunity, there would be no way to survive. He wished to record his wife and children. Zhu Wen, with a sad expression, stopped his journey, but Ma Jing firmly requested, and Zhu Wen agreed. The next day, the army set out, and the camps hid, as if there were no one. Indeed, the cavalry retreated, and the Qishan people accepted them, not losing their expectations. The Qishan army opened the two doors, and all the troops came to us. Our army, having already fed the horses and fed the soldiers, beat the drum once from the central army, and all the camps advanced together, greatly defeating the Qishan army, with only about one-third or four-tenths remaining. Li Maozhen lost his courage, and Emperor Zhaozong issued an edict to return to the capital, finally succeeding in welcoming him. The credit goes to Gao Gong, and Ma Jing's wife and children were given double the concern and protection. Jie Yang upheld righteousness and loyalty, not caring about his own life, while Ma Jing used deception with his life, not for personal gain. The difficulty of the task was only possessed by Jing. The wife of Zhu Yanshou, a virtuous woman.

Tian Jun of Xuanzhou and Zhu Yanchou of Shouzhou planned to rebel against Yang Xingmi with their armies and asked Du Xunzhi to carry a letter to Huai Prefecture. Soon the plot was exposed, and Yang Xingmi mobilized all his troops to attack Wanling. Zhu Yanchou sent cavalry to join the fight, but both were killed by the Huai army. As Zhu Yanchou was about to leave, his wife Lady Wang encouraged him, saying, "I wish to send a messenger daily to soothe your mind. If one messenger is not sent, the situation is clear." She then organized the household servants and gave them weapons, quickly closing the city gates, but the pursuing cavalry had already arrived and could not enter. They gathered the household servants and opened the private treasury, lit a hundred torches to burn the houses and the city, and urgently knelt and bowed to report, saying, "I swear not to let my clear body be disgraced by the enemy." Then she threw herself into the fire and died. She was an example of a heroic woman from ancient times. Jupiter entered the constellation of the Dipper. During the Danyuan period, Wang Duo, the Governor of Jing Prefecture, later became the Commander of All Routes. When Jupiter entered the South Dipper and remained there for several nights, Wang Duo observed it and asked the astrologers about the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness. They all said that if the stars of metal, fire, or earth invaded the Dipper, it would bring disaster, but only wood should bring good fortune. Wang Duo agreed with this. At that time, there was a scholar named Bian Gang who was deeply knowledgeable in astronomy and skilled in calendrical calculations. He told Wang Duo, "The Dipper is the constellation of the emperor's palace, and only wood is the god of good fortune. It should be interpreted in terms of the emperor. Therefore, it is not a blessing for the present, but it will surely be verified in the future. I dare not say more." One day, Wang Duo dismissed his attendants and asked Bian Gang privately, "Jupiter entering the Dipper is a sign of imperial fortune. The character 'wood' (木) is inside the Dipper, forming the character '朱'. Wise people said there was an omen of a red-clothed person in the Tang Dynasty. Some said it would lead to a change in the dynasty, and it could be someone surnamed Pei or Niu. So Pei Jun and Niu Sengru often faced this slander. Li Weigong criticized the records of Zhou and Qin, which is related to this matter. How could it be destined for Zhu from Dangshan?"

Strange Writings in Wood During the Kai Ping era of the Later Liang dynasty, Li Si'an, stationed at the military front of the state of Lu, reported that in Shu Rang village of Hu Guan County, a tree had fallen and split into two pieces, revealing six characters written on it. These characters were all written from left to right and read "Tian Shi Si Zai." Shi Jin then drew a diagram of the inscription and presented it, which was subsequently entrusted to the historical archives. Later, after Emperor Zhuangzong of Tang ascended the throne from the Prince of Jin, he considered this inscription to be a response to the middle. In between, the Shi family received the country from Bing Gate and claimed the first court, and Ma Xifan of Hunan explained this character and reported it. Eulogy for Xue Yiju Xue Yiju, a high-ranking official of the Later Liang dynasty, was a son of a prominent family who passed the imperial examinations and rose to the rank of Grand Censor during the Tang dynasty. Previously, southern court officials feared and avoided communication with the northern service, and during the Tian Fu era, eunuchs were cut and killed. Xue Yiju had once collaborated with Han Quanhuai and others to write a eulogy, which was recorded on the walls of the inner service hall, leading to his demotion. Later, he carried the decree of the Tang emperor to abdicate to the Liang and rose to the position of chancellor. Chong Mo Camp Huang Chao fled from Chang'an back to his base and stationed his troops between Chen and Cai, setting up a camp on the Yin River, connected and named the Eight Mountain Camp. At that time, Qin Zongquan, the governor of Cai, feared Huang Chao and surrendered the city. The troops were already hungry and lacked resources, with nothing to plunder in the wild. They resorted to capturing people to eat, and when meat was gone, they followed it with bones, either pounded with a pestle or ground with a mill, all used to fill the hunger. The heavenly troops gathered from all sides, and Huang Chao's troops were at a disadvantage, causing his followers to be terrified and scatter. Frequent thunderstorms and heavy rains flooded his camp, and he then fled with his wife, children, and brothers to the Wolf Tiger Valley on Mount Tai. His nephew Lin Yan beheaded him, and his head was sent to Xu Zhou, where Shi Yi, a subordinate general, sent it to Chengdu, the imperial residence.

Emperor Zhu Wen Escapes a Crisis Emperor Zhu Wen personally led a military campaign to Yüan Prefecture, and while his army was stationed at Wèinan, they completed the construction of new fortifications. He then ascended and looked out, seeing birds perching on high walls and making loud noises. Deputy Commander Li Fan remarked, "These birds are an omen, and there will be an unfavorable event." Indeed, the front-line troops led by Zhu Youyu were attacked by Zhu Xuan and retreated southward. Our army did not know this and continued northward, encountering Zhu Xuan's troops who came to meet us. In a state of haste and confusion, Emperor Zhu Wen suddenly saw accumulated corn stalks inside a ditch, which he took as a path. He leaped over the ditch on his horse and thus escaped. However, Deputy Commander Li Fan and General Gao Xingsi were killed by the enemy. General Zhang Guiyu, as a rear guard, fought bravely with a spear and barely survived, being wounded by fifteen or sixteen arrows. Thus, it became known that the birds at Wèinan were an early warning. Dream of Ding Fang Ding Fang, the commander of the Zhaoyi circuit, often feared that Emperor Zhu Wen would suspect and doubt meritorious subjects. Suddenly, he said to Jing Xiang, "I dreamed that Ding Fang was waiting in front of me. I was about to ride a horse and leave, and the stable boy brought the horse to the platform. Suddenly, Ding Fang leaped onto it and left. In the dream, I shouted angrily several times and woke up in a panic, finding it very ominous. That month, Ding Fang led the people of Lushui to return to Hedong."

The Great Beam Collapsed In the later years of Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Liang, he frequently carried out executions. One night, a large beam in the imperial sleeping hall suddenly fell onto the emperor's bed. Initially, he heard soil falling onto the sleeping mat and was startled awake. After a while, he heard a small piece of wood fall onto the top of the canopy, and he hurriedly got out of bed before even leaving the hall, as the beam had fallen. Early the next morning, he summoned various princes and ministers to observe the incident. Fearing they might not see each other again, the emperor and his ministers wept together. He also said that during the time of fear and worry, it seemed as if someone had pulled their head into the door of the sleeping chamber and asked, "Is there no one inside?" This caused him to rush out hastily, wondering if it was not the spirit of the palace. On another day, while roaming in the Western Nine-Bend Pool of the Inner Palace and sailing a swan boat on the pool, he suddenly heard the boat tilt and fall into the pool. The court ladies and attendants jumped into the pool to help him onto the shore. After some time, he finally calmed down. Thereafter, he contracted a chronic illness and eventually suffered the disaster of being assassinated by his son, Prince Ying, who was a rebel. The boat capsizing and the beam breaking were not good omens. The Siege of Zaoqiang by Emperor Zhuangzong In the later years of Emperor Zhuangzong, his military campaigns were unsuccessful, and several towns in Hebei did not obey his orders. One day, restless and agitated, he insisted on leading a personal campaign and camped near the border of Shen Prefecture. He then ordered General Yang Shihou to divide the troops and attack Zaoqiang County. After half a ten-day period, they finally captured the stronghold. The town was strong, with moats and walls that were difficult to breach, and the people were fierce and wild. They attacked day and night until they were exhausted. On the day it was captured, all, regardless of age, were killed. A commoner came to join the army. Li Zhouyi was among the ranks and said to Zhouyi, "Please give me a sword, and I will lead the charge to capture the city." Before permission was given, he suddenly pulled out a tea ladle and struck Zhouyi on the head, hitting the brim and falling to the ground. The attendants then captured him. Originally sent from Zaoqiang, he had come to feign surrender with the intention of spying on the Liang army. The military commissioner, Yang Shihou, could not distinguish this person and mistakenly struck Zhouyi. Thus it is known that the people of the Hebei region were so brave and fierce.

### Translation Cui Yin and His Prophecies Cui Yin, the chief astronomer of the Later Liang, was skilled in divination and astrology. Whenever he foresaw good or bad omens, he dared not speak them plainly if they were likely to be displeasing to the emperor. He would keep such prophecies secret, and as a result, he was often penalized by having his salary reduced. This was due to his fear of Emperor Zhu Wen's (梁祖) penchant for violence. The Later Liang suffered several military defeats: the state of Zhao was lost, and General Fu Daozhang was captured. In Boling, General Wang Jingren suffered a significant defeat, all of which were attributed to the military prowess of Li Cunxu, the Prince of Jin, who was the successor to the throne of the Later Tang dynasty. The Later Liang was now concerned about the future, feeling that their misfortunes were due to ignoring Cui Yin's advice. --- ### Translation The Northern Dream Trivia, Volume Sixteen The Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature The Northern Dream Trivia, Volume Seventeen By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty Emperor Zhu Wen as a Laborer Emperor Zhu Wen was born in Wugou Village, Dangshan County, Song Prefecture. His original name was Wen, and he was later given the name Quan Zhong. After establishing the Later Liang, he changed his name to Hu. His family had been scholars for generations. His grandfather, Xun, and his father, Cheng, both made a living through teaching. Cheng died early, leaving behind three young sons. Their mother, Wang, took the children to live with Liu Chong, a fellow villager. Among the brothers, only Wen was cunning and unscrupulous. Liu's mother raised him, and although Liu's brothers sometimes scolded and beat him, Wen once stole a pot from Liu's home and ran away. Liu chased after him, and Liu's mother intervened to prevent punishment. Wen was good at hunting and often caught deer. Liu's mother also observed that Wen had a strange aura, like that of a dragon or snake. Later, Wen and his second brother, Cun, joined Huang Chao's rebellion, while his eldest brother, Yu, and their mother Wang remained with Liu's family. After Wen left, his family did not know his fate. When Wen became the governor of Bian Prefecture, he adorned his carriages and horses and sent people to fetch his mother from Liu's home. Wang was terrified and hid, not believing that Wen could achieve such success. She told others, "Zhu San was a vagrant with no morals. How could he become a bandit and die? How could he ever become wealthy and powerful?"

The General of Kaifeng is not my son. The envoy fully explained the reasons for leaving one's hometown and village, as well as the matters of returning to the country and establishing achievements. Wang was just crying and believed. That day, she and Chong's mother were welcomed back to Kaifeng with warm and grand ceremonies, which changed the perception of the officials. Chong, due to old favors, reached the position of a high-ranking official and became the governor of Shangzhou. Wang, because of Wen's rank, was enfeoffed as the Dowager of Jin State. Chong's second brother survived among the rebels but was hit by arrows and stones and died. Wen brought wine to his mother, who was very joyful, and they talked about family affairs. Wen said to his mother, "Zhu Wujing worked hard studying Confucianism but did not achieve any official rank. Now I have a son who is a military governor and has not disgraced our ancestors." His mother was not pleased for a long time and said to Wen, "You have reached this position; you truly believe that your noble conduct and righteousness may not be as good as your ancestors. Zhu Er and you entered the rebel army together, and he died in a remote border area. Orphaned boys and young girls have difficulty finding food and have no one to turn to. You do not have compassion for orphans. Even if you have noble qualities, it is not desirable." Wen shed tears and apologized, immediately ordered to summon all his brothers' children, and they all came to Kaifeng. You Lun and others all established military achievements and reached the position of regional military governors. Liang Zong's wife, the Hui Guo Fu Ren Zhang, was a wealthy woman from Dangshan. Her father, Fang, once served as the governor of Songzhou. During Wen's time, he heard that Zhang had a beautiful appearance and secretly admired her, with admiration for Li Hua. When Wen was in Tongzhou, he found Zhang among the soldiers and accepted her as a wife according to proper rites. Wen deeply respected and treated her differently because of their old acquaintance. Zhang was wise, virtuous, and polite. Although Wen had a heart like a tiger or wolf, he still respected her. He always consulted her first on military strategies and state affairs. Sometimes after troops had been dispatched, if something was not right, Zhang would request to return, and she would arrive on schedule. She was trusted and respected like this. At the beginning, when capturing Yanzheng and Yuying, he captured Zhu Jin's wife. Wen told him, "She has no support."

Zhang, staying in a carriage, sent someone to summon him. His wife bowed twice, and Zhang bowed in return, shedding tears and said to him, "Yan and Yun are countries of the same surname as the Duke of Si, and among brothers, a small matter led to the use of weapons, resulting in my sister-in-law's situation. If by misfortune Bianzhou were to fall, I would also be like my sister-in-law today." She shed tears again and was then ordained as a nun. Zhang Heng provided her expenses. After Zhang's death, the one who succeeded in favor was not a suitable person, and later usurped the title, greatly indulging in promiscuity. Family members engaged in promiscuity, and the curtains and screens were in a state of chaos, leading to the disaster of You Gui arising from a woman. Only then could gentle and mild virtue control a fierce heart, like Zhang. Is she not virtuous? The ancestors of the Zhu Ya clan: Li Keyong of Hedong, whose ancestors were people of the Uyghur tribe, held high positions in the Tang dynasty. Emperor Taizong established the Shatuo Fu in the northern desert to gather surrendered households. Later, Li Keyong's ancestor Zhu Ya Zhiyi, along with his father, relied on the Tibetans, but later defected to the Tang court. Emperor Dezong established the Yinshan Fu in Yanzhou and appointed Zhu Ya Zhiyi as its governor. Later, they moved to the villa in Shenwu River's Huanghuadui, which is now Yinzhou. Zhu Ya Zhiyi gave birth to Zhu Xincheng, who, after defeating the rebels in Xuzhou, was granted the surname Li and the title Li Guochang. Emperor Yizong asked about his ancestors' origin, and he replied that they were originally from Longxi Jincheng, relying on the Tibetans. The emperor said, "My ancestors and yours are from the same village; I have ordered you to be registered in the Prince of Zheng's lineage." He was first appointed as the military governor of Yunzhou and the Datong Army, then granted the three military governorships of Fuyan, Zhenwu, and Dainor. His nephew Li Kelang became a general, and his son Li Keyong, the most famous, was appointed as the military governor of Taiyuan for his victory over Huang Chao. His son Li Cunxu pacified Liang and Shu, eventually dominating the Central Plains. He was posthumously honored as...

执宜号懿祖国昌号献祖克用号太祖皇帝太祖在姙十三月载诞之夕母后甚危令族人市药于雁门遇神人教以率部人被介持旄击钲鼓跃马大噪环所居三周而止果如所教而生是日红【一作虹】光烛室白气充庭井水暴溢及能言喜道军旅年十二三能连射双鸟至于树叶针锋马鞭皆能中之曽于新城北以酒酹毗沙门塑像请与仆交谈天王被甲持矛隐隐出于壁间或所居帐内时如火聚如有龙形人皆异之甞随献祖征厐勋军阵出没如神号为飞虎子眇一目时号独眼龙功业磊落不可尽述【或云睛邪非眇也】 太祖皇帝，执宜号懿祖，国昌号献祖，克用号太祖。太祖在母腹中十三个月，诞辰之夜，母后甚危。族人奉命在雁门购药，遇神人指示，率部人披甲持旗，击鼓鸣钲，跃马大噪，绕所居三周而止。果然如神人所教而生。是日，红光【或作虹】照耀室内，白气充盈庭院，井水突然溢出。及至能言，喜谈军旅之事。年十二三，能连射双鸟，至于树叶、针锋、马鞭皆能命中。曾于新城北以酒祭毗沙门塑像，请求与仆交谈，天王披甲持矛，隐隐出现在壁间或所居帐内，时如火聚，如有龙形，人皆异之。尝随献祖征讨庞勋，军阵出没如神，号为飞虎子。眇一目，时号独眼龙，功业磊落，不可尽述【或云睛邪，非眇也】。 亲王拜蕃侯 唐干宁年间，凤翔李茂贞、华州韩建、邠州王行瑜拥兵胁君，诛戮宰辅，焚烧宫阙。初，帝西幸凤翔，昭宗出居石门莎城。太原克用领蕃汉兵马入京，三镇大惧。是年，破邠州，斩王行瑜。昭宗嘉奖倚赖，命延王丕、丹王允赍诏赐李公衣服，兼令二亲王设拜，以兄事之，近古未有也。仍封晋王以宠之。延王才识过人，聪悟辩慧，在晋阳留宴累月，每献酬乐作，必为晋王起舞。后为韩建所杀。

Li Xi Ji Fell into the Yellow River Li Keyong of Taiyuan, after returning from a campaign in the north of the Wei River, stopped at Xixian County. Wang Ke constructed a floating bridge on the ice for public use, but the horses' hooves caused the bridge to sink. Li Xi Ji fell from his horse into the river. Li Xi Ji clung to the ice, and the boatman rescued him, saving his life. Wang Ke, fearing Li Keyong, said to him, "Your relationship with me is not one of mutual support. What reason do you have to doubt me?" Li Xi Ji, known for his reputation, was not recognized by Li Keyong. They drank wine and laughed, then parted ways. Li Xi Ji was the descendant of the Right Chancellor Li Linfu, who failed the imperial examinations. After the rebellion of Huang Chao, he traveled in Hedong and was temporarily appointed as the magistrate of Yuci. Li Keyong appointed him to write official documents, and his skill was unmatched. Zhu Wen often praised his talent when reading the official documents from Hedong. He told Gu Jingxiang, "Li Gong is isolated in one corner of the map. How fortunate it is to have such a person! With his intelligence and strategy, combined with Li Xi Ji's literary talent, it is like a tiger with wings. He is greatly esteemed." Zhou Shi Resisted Zhu Wen Zhu Wen captured Xing Prefecture and attacked Wang Rong in Changshan. Among Wang Rong's advisors was Zhou Shi, who was bold and eloquent. He said to Wang Rong, "The situation is urgent. Make a quick decision on where to go. I am willing to go as an envoy." Zhou Shi went out to meet Zhu Wen. Zhu Wen said, "Prince Wang has allied with Bing and Fen and violated our alliance. His taxes are already due, and we have no choice but to continue the campaign." Zhou Shi replied, "Your Excellency is the Han dynasty's version of Duke Huan and Duke Wen. You should establish your hegemony through propriety and righteousness. Prince Wang now humbles himself and sends his son as a hostage, hoping to renew old friendships. Why do you want to exhaust your troops and destroy your allies? What will people say about you?" Zhu Wen laughed and took Zhou Shi's sleeve, saying, "I was just joking with you." Wang Rong immediately sent oxen, wine, and gifts worth tens of thousands of coins to reward Zhu Wen's army and ordered his son to be sent as a hostage to Bian, thus lifting the siege. Zhou Shi was compared to Lu Zhonglian of modern times.

Eunuch Conspiracy Emperor Zhaozong of Tang, due to the eunuchs' arrogance and indulgence in power, often harbored thoughts of exterminating them. The chancellor Cui Yun, who was particularly envious and resentful of them, was ordered by the emperor to submit all confidential reports sealed in a bag, without presenting them in the secondary palace. As a result, the eunuchs did not notice this instruction. Han Quanwei and others then sought to gather information about palace secrets by bringing dozens of beautiful women from the capital to the emperor. The emperor, however, did not realize the true intentions behind these actions. Cui Yun's plans gradually became known, and the eunuchs used heavy bribes and sweet words to persuade the regional lords to support them, viewing Cui Yun with intense anger. During festivals, the eunuchs would gather and cry together, bidding farewell to each other. Kou Tonghua, a person from Kaifeng, became aware of Cui Yun's plans, and Han Quanwei led the imperial guards to force the emperor to seek refuge in Fengxiang. Later, Cui Yun and Zhu Wen, the founder of the Later Liang dynasty, conspired to eliminate the eunuchs. However, the consequences of their actions soon caught up with them. On the day of Gengwu, the eunuchs were completely destroyed. Those who understood the situation blamed Cui Yun for the events. Prior to this, Cui Yun's younger uncle, An Qian, had once told close friends, "The one who will destroy our family is Zi'er," as "Zi'er" was a nickname for Cui Yun. Li Keyong, the Prince of Hedong, upon learning of Cui Yun's actions, said to his guests and friends, "The one who is helping the wicked do evil is Cui Yun. The destruction of the country and the ruin of families must be caused by this person."

The Jin Prince Li Keyong's wife, Lady Liu, often accompanied him on military campaigns, where she had significant influence and benefits in military affairs. Prior to this, at the Shangyuan Inn in Bianzhou, an incident occurred that deeply troubled the Jin Prince. He was furious and wanted to retreat and attack them. Lady Liu said, "Your mission is to fight for the country, and now you want to attack due to a personal grudge. If you were to attack the city, the fault would lie with us. It would be better to withdraw and let the imperial court handle the matter." Thus, the troops withdrew. During the Tiānfù period, General Zhou Dewei was defeated by the enemy forces, and the army was in disarray. The Jin Prince was in a state of panic and discussed with Zhou Dewei the possibility of retreating to Yunzhou for safety. Lady Liu said, "Cunxin was originally a shepherd boy from the north; how could he care about success or failure? The Jin Prince often laughed at Wang Xingyu, who lost his power and status and was humiliated by others. Now, he wants to follow his example. How far can he go? The Jin Prince had fled to the Dàdá tribe to escape danger a few years ago and narrowly escaped being harmed by them. Now, if he were to leave the city, there would be unforeseen dangers. How could he expect to achieve much?" The Jin Prince stopped his plans. After several days, the scattered soldiers returned, and the city was stabilized. This was due to Lady Liu's efforts.

Liu Xun's Loyalty to His Former Master Wang Shifan, when he was stationed in Qingzhou, had his subordinate general Liu Xun seize control of Yanzhou. Previously, the Bian general Ge Congzhou was stationed there. Taking advantage of Ge Congzhou's departure on a campaign, Liu Xun planned to take over Yanzhou. He disguised himself as a tea merchant, packaged armor, and built large shops, carving out large trees to hide soldiers. He then stealthily entered and gathered a thousand followers, taking control of the government offices. He ascended the hall to pay respects to Ge Congzhou's mother, still treating his wife, children, and descendants with propriety, and providing supplies as usual. Soon, Ge Congzhou attacked the city with a cloud of ladders and defenses. Liu Xun used a sedan chair to bring Ge Congzhou's mother to the city wall to persuade Ge Congzhou, saying, "General Liu treats me no differently than a son. His wife and concubines have not been deprived of their rightful places." Ge Congzhou, moved by emotion, withdrew his camp immediately. When the Qingzhou forces were defeated, Wang Shifan surrendered, and Liang Ancestor sent an envoy to instruct Liu Xun. Liu Xun said, "I know that Lord Wang has cultivated good relations with the Liang state, but I was originally commanded by Lord Wang to protect this state. Once I saw that his situation was dire, I took command without regard, which was not a sign of my full dedication to my duties. I await Lord Wang's command and am not too late to bow." Only then, upon Wang Shifan's instructions, did Liu Xun return the city to Liang Ancestor. Liang Ancestor praised his loyalty and promoted him beyond his rank, making him a famous general of the Liang state.

Refuting Emperor Zhaozong's Posthumous Name Emperor Zhaozong was initially given the posthumous name of "Shengmu Jingwen Xiaohuangdi" and the temple name of "Zhaozong." The court historian Su Kai and others argued against this, proposing that it should be changed to "Gongling Zhuangmin Huangdi" with the temple name "Xiangzong." Su Kai, the son of Su Xun, who served as the Minister of Rites, was a candidate for the imperial examination in the second year of the Ganning era. His talent had declined, and he lacked ability and virtue. Emperor Zhaozong detested his unqualified advancement and led the way in dismissing him, which caused Su Kai to harbor resentment and seek to exploit the country's misfortunes. His father, Su Xun, was known for his deceitful and flattering behavior, earning no reputation in his time. Thus, Su Kai sought advancement through flattery, but Zhu Wen, the founder of the Later Liang Dynasty, recognized his treacherous nature and was displeased with him. At the time, Su Kai was despised by Zhu Wen's advisors, Jing Xiang and Li Zhen. Zhu Wen issued an edict stating, "Su Kai, Gao Yixiu, and Xiao Wenli are all men of declining talent and should not be allowed to soil the ranks of officials. They are hereby removed from their posts and dismissed to return to their hometowns. Su Xun is hereby ordered to retire." The people of the Huai River region referred to Su Kai as a "scholarly local villain." The Prince of Jin Visits the Court and Receives a Decorative Wine Vessel At the age of eleven, Li Cunxu, the Prince of Jin, accompanied the Prince of Jin to suppress Wang Xingyu. Upon his initial visit to the court to present his success, Emperor Zhaozong was astonished and remarked, "This boy has extraordinary qualities." He then patted Li Cunxu's back and said, "My son will be the pillar of our nation in the future. Do not forget your loyalty and filial piety to our family." He then bestowed upon Li Cunxu a decorative wine vessel shaped like a bird and thirteen jade plates. Li Cunxu read the *Spring and Autumn Annals* and had a basic understanding of its principles. He was unparalleled in horsemanship and archery, with an open and broad-minded nature. He selected and recorded good words, listened to and accepted others' opinions, almost like Liu Cong. It was also said that Emperor Zhaozong remarked, "This boy can be second to his father," and people referred to him as "the younger son."

**Translation:** The Poems of the Prince of Ye Stolen from Jiangdong The Prince of Ye, Luo Shao Wei, was fond of literature and respected scholars. He often tasked his attendants with drafting letters and edicts for various regions, and if they were not satisfactory, he would tear them up and discard them, sometimes even tearing the paper himself to start anew. He also had a particular fondness for seven-character poems. In Jiangdong, there was Luo Yin, who served under Qian Liu. Luo Shao Wei expressed his respect for Luo Yin, who reciprocated by sending him his writings and poems. Luo Shao Wei greatly admired Luo Yin and named his collection of poems "Stolen from Jiangdong." Today, many people in Ye often recite these poems. The Military Forces of Wei and Bo Wei and Bo were wealthy and powerful, with their own territories. For three hundred years during the Tang dynasty, they were only tolerated. Luo Shao Wei was furious with the military forces and secretly informed the founder of the Later Liang about their plans, leading to their extermination. After Yang Shihou entered the city of Wei, he dismissed Luo Zhouhan and took his place. After Yang Shihou's death, the Later Liang appointed He Delun to lead the region, dividing its territory and establishing new prefectures in Xiang and Bei. This reduced the military's power, leading to a rebellion that forced He Delun to defect to the Later Jin. In his petition, he wrote, "Qu Yuan mourned Ying but was not a man of resentment; Lü Yi left Yan but was not a man of deceit." The Prince of Jin, upon reading this, personally led his troops and first accused Zhang Yan of threatening the ruler and oppressing the people, ordering his execution. He then took five hundred of Zhang Yan's loyal soldiers, fully armed, and rode around the city. The Prince of Jin wore loose clothing and a relaxed belt, showing no suspicion, which greatly impressed the troops. Later, he relied on the military strength of Wei and Bo to conquer Henan, proving their capability. After Luo Shao Wei's massacre, the military forces again pressured He Delun, who once more held his troops, holding Zhao Zai in rebellion. In the early Tiancheng era, they marched to the capital, where they killed another three thousand families, continuing their tradition of violence and cruelty.

**Tying Up a Donkey and Wearing a Flag** When the King of Jin, Li Cunxu, advanced into the territory of Liu Xun, who was stationed at Huanshui, the area was completely silent, as if no one was there. He ordered a more thorough investigation and found that the enemy had tied straw into the shape of a person, placed a flag on it, and had a donkey carry it along the battlements, creating the illusion of movement. Li Cunxu remarked, "Liu Xun has a new strategy for every step." He was cautious and ordered further investigation. Indeed, they had tied straw into the shape of a person, placed a flag on it, and had a donkey carry it along the battlements, causing the flags to flutter continuously. When he asked the old and weak people in the city, they said the army had left two days ago. It turned out that Liu Xun was heading towards Huangze, intending to attack Taiyuan, but heavy rain and flooding prevented him from advancing. His strategy was like this. --- **The Northern Dream Trivial Talk, Volume Seventeen** **The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries** **The Northern Dream Trivial Talk, Volume Eighteen** **By Sun Guangxian of the Tang Dynasty** **Yang Qianlang** After the execution of Guo Chongtao, his son-in-law and younger brother of Emperor Zhuangzong, Cunyi, was also put to death. After Guo Chongtao's execution, the court and the common people were shocked and saddened, and there was much debate. Emperor Zhuangzong ordered eunuchs to investigate external affairs, and they reported that Cunyi had appealed to the generals, claiming the innocence of the Guo family. His words were filled with resentment and anger. He also gathered with a sorcerer named Yang Qianlang, drank and feasted, and cried out in anger. Yang Qianlang was a lowly man from Weizhou who claimed to have learned the techniques of Mozi from his father-in-law. He could summon food and fruits from under his hat and always won in gambling, using a method to take anything he wanted. He also claimed to be able to transform into different forms, break locks, and was a mysterious official in the court, rising to the rank of Langguan and being granted the purple robe. His wife often entered and exited the palace, receiving favors and wielding influence. The younger brother of the emperor, Cunyi, often engaged in debauchery with her. In the end, Cunyi and Yang Qianlang both suffered the same fate.

A Fetus That Could Speak Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang, when he was in humble circumstances, accompanied the foreign general Li Cunxin on a border patrol and stayed at an inn in Yanmen Pass. The innkeeper's woman was pregnant, and when the emperor arrived, she was slow and did not prepare food properly. The child in her womb spoke to his mother, saying, "The emperor has arrived; you should prepare food quickly." The voice was heard outside, and the innkeeper was surprised. She hurriedly got up and personally prepared the food, serving with even greater respect and caution. The emperor asked her, saying, "Your guest is of high status; I cannot speak of it." He then asked about the reason and she fully explained the story of the fetus speaking in the womb. The emperor said, "The old woman spoke humbly out of fear that I would insult her." Later, as he said, it turned out to be true. Emperor Mingzong did not boast. When he first joined the army, he always focused on military matters and did not engage in production. He was heroic, martial, and humble, especially frugal with money. His family's wealth often ran out, but he remained calm and composed. Emperor Taizu wanted to test his honesty by inviting him to the treasury and allowing him to take whatever he wanted. He only took a few bolts of silk and a few strings of coins. He distributed any rewards or gifts he received among his subordinates. After winning a battle and returning triumphantly, his fellow soldiers boasted. The emperor calmly said, "Others fight with words; I fight with my hands." Everyone inwardly admired his abilities.

Unique Insight of Emperor Mingzong When Emperor Zhuangzong passed away, Emperor Mingzong, who was recommended by the generals and ministers, including Huo Yanwei and Kong Xun, was advised to change the dynasty's name due to the decline of the Tang dynasty. Emperor Mingzong told his close attendants in his private quarters, "Why should we change the calendar?" They replied, "The late emperor, being of the Xī clan, avenged the grievances of the Tang dynasty for Emperor Zhaozong. The current dynasty is still known as Tang. However, those who served the previous dynasty do not want Your Majesty to continue using the name 'Tang.' They ask for a new name." Emperor Mingzong wept and said, "I have served thirteen emperors, from the founding emperor to the late emperor, fighting bloodily to avenge the Tang dynasty. I am a member of the Xī clan, and the achievements of the martial emperor are my achievements. The late emperor's realm is my realm. There is no reason for me to be concerned about the succession. The rise and fall of fortune, I must bear it myself." Thus, he did not change the calendar, and people admired his independent judgment. The Brothers of Emperor Zhuangzong Met with Tragedy When Zhao Zaiyi rebelled, the generals forced Emperor Mingzong to enter the imperial court. Before he arrived, Guo Congqian, the commander of the cavalry, attacked the Xingjiao Gate. Emperor Mingzong's brother, Li Cunwu, fought alongside him. When the imperial carriage passed away, Li Cunwu and Empress Liu fled to Taiyuan. They were killed at Fenggu Valley by their own men. Empress Liu wanted to become a nun, but she was soon killed as well. Li Cunbai, another brother, was appointed as the governor of Beijing but traveled from Hedong to Taiyuan. The soldiers demanded that he be killed to pacify the people, but Fu Yanchao could not prevent it. At the time, Li Cunbai had already cut off his hair and put on monk's robes, seeking Fu Yanchao, wishing to become a mountain monk, but he could not escape his fate. Li Cunjie and Li Cuo hid in the home of a civilian in the southern mountains. Someone reported this to An Zhonghui, who said, "The emperor has already issued a decree to search for them. Your Majesty's benevolence and virtue will not harm them. It would be better to secretly protect them." However, they were both executed in the civilian's home. Later, when Emperor Mingzong heard of this, he severely reprimanded An Zhonghui and grieved for a long time.

Emperor Zhuangzong's Empress Beat Her Father Emperor Zhuangzong's Empress Liu was from Cheng'an, Huzhou, a place of humble origins. When the founding emperor attacked Huzhou and took Cheng'an, he obtained her when she was about five or six years old. She was brought back to the Jin Palace, where she served as a maid to the Empress Dowager and learned to play the flute. By the time she reached adulthood, her beauty surpassed all others, and she also excelled in music and performance. The Empress Dowager granted her to Emperor Zhuangzong, and he bestowed upon her the title of Korea Lady. After she bore Emperor Zhuangzong a son named Jiji, her favor grew daily. One day, a man from Cheng'an named Liu, who came to the Yeh Palace, claimed to be the father of the Empress. An eunuch named Liu Jianfeng recognized him as the former Yellow Fang's father-in-law, who was also the Empress's father. Liu was competing for favor with the principal wife, both boasting of their noble lineages. Liu was ashamed to be from a poor family and reported to Emperor Zhuangzong, saying, "When I left my hometown, my father died in the chaos of war. At that time, I surrounded his body and wept. How can this farmer deceive me now?" She then had him beaten at the palace gate. In fact, she was the eldest daughter of the old man. Emperor Zhuangzong enjoyed jesters and comedians. During leisure times in the palace, he would carry a bag of divination tools and a medicine box, letting Jiji follow with a tattered hat, resembling her father, Liu, who made a living as a doctor and fortune-teller. One day, the Empress was taking a nap during the day. Someone entered her bedroom and introduced himself as Liu, the district commissioner, looking for his daughter. The Empress was very angry.

In anger, he beat Jiji to the point of near-death, and treated the Empress Dowager disrespectfully. Because Lady Han was the legitimate wife, the ministers could not justify making Liu a queen. Those who debated the matter argued that Liu came from a lowly background and favored accumulating wealth. Initially in the capital city of Ye, she made people devise methods to supplement their income by selling various goods, also using the name of the queen. After assuming the position, all tributes first entered the inner palace, and only Buddhist scriptures were given to nuns. The troops under the palace were in dire straits, and even their wives and children were starving. The prime minister requested to distribute from the imperial treasury, and the queen will give out two silver basins as part of her dowry, and the princes Manxi and others three are ordered to sell them to support the army. One day, causing chaos, leading to the destruction of the country and the clan, no different from Bo Si and Da Ji. Previously, Emperor Zhuangzong made himself a clown or jester, naming himself Li Tianxia, mixed among the painted and messy, sometimes acting as a clown, being slapped and hugged, and finally becoming a foolish woman, corrupting the favoring of clowns, and those in power should take this as a warning. Empress Liu, with a bag containing four sets of gold and rhinoceros leather belts, intended to build a temple in Taiyuan to become a nun, preventing the road from reopening, and the royal brother Cunwo slept on the same bed. Emperor Mingzong heard about it and ordered her to commit suicide.

In the early days of Emperor Mingzong's reign, he executed several notorious officials. Among them were Kong Qian, the Commissioner of Rent and Labor; Yuan Xingqin, the Governor of the Guiwei Circuit; Wen Tao, the Governor of the Dengzhou Circuit; Duan Ning, the Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince; and Xin Tingwei, the official in charge of wine affairs in Bianzhou. Kong Qian, originally a clerk in the administrative office of Weizhou, had played a significant role in supporting Prince Zhuangzong's ambitions to become a hegemon by providing military rations. After becoming the Commissioner of Rent and Labor, he flattered the emperor's favorites, usurped the power of the chancellor, and focused solely on accumulating wealth, making exploitation the norm, which ultimately led to his execution. Yuan Xingqin, a favored general of Prince Zhuangzong, had unrestricted access to the palace and was implicated in harming Emperor Mingzong's son, Congjing, leading to his own execution. Duan Ning, who served the Later Liang dynasty through deceit and flattery, rose to the rank of a high-ranking military official but eventually surrendered to the Later Tang dynasty. Wen Tao, known for his cruelty and wickedness, was notorious for excavating the Western Capital, Chang'an.

The tomb of Emperor Zhuangzong, despite his role in the revival of the Tang dynasty, was not punished for his sins. Instead, he generously rewarded entertainers, eunuchs, and others, including bestowing the imperial surname. Some held banners and standards, symbolizing authority or command. Emperor Mingzong took public opinion into account and punished accordingly. Xin Tingwei, a subordinate officer under Wang Zan, the governor of Kaifeng, abused power and law during Zhu Youzhen's reign due to his reliance on Wang Zan's influence. The people of Bian (Kaifeng) disliked him. Li Jixuan, the son of Meng Shencheng, a general in Bian, fled to Emperor Zhuangzong. Empress Liu adopted him as her son, and rumors circulated about their relationship. These four notorious figures were disliked by the emperor when he was still a prince, leading to their dismissal. Emperor Zhuangzong restored the Tang dynasty's honor and was hailed as the reviver, but Wen Tao destroyed the tombs of various emperors, and they should be punished severely, including execution and the loss of their titles. This reveals that the notion of the Tang revival is flawed. The consorts Han and Yi, with an additional note about Xia, were introduced. Empress Han became a consort after being the emperor's principal wife, and Yi became a consort. When the Khitan entered Central China, the Shi family surrendered, and Chancellor Feng Dao honored the Khitan leader with a grand banquet. The Khitan leader's mother and consorts sat together at the banquet, compared to historical figures known for their beauty. Consort Xia, who was highly favored, later married into the Khitan and took the name Li Zanhua. The so-called Prince of Dongdan was the eldest son of Anba Jian, who first returned to the court and later became the governor of Huazhou. He was cruel, punishing even minor offenses with severe punishments. Consort Xia, who grew up in the palace, could not tolerate his cruelty and sought a divorce, returning to the home of Xia Luqi in Heyang, where she now lives as a nun.

No official post to reward merit. Since the time of chaos and disorder, titles and positions have been excessively awarded, likening it to attaching a dog's tail to a precious fur coat. In the early Tiansheng period, the Governor of Guizhou, Ma Er, who is the brother of Ma Yin from Hunan, holds a high rank despite his lack of merit. The imperial decree states that he is one of the four assistants and ranks above the three teachers. This is not a promotion in rank but a difficult situation to manage. This is a key point. Critics argue that the bestowal of titles and honors has become so casual that it leads to the kind of lamentation expressed by Jia Yi. Emperor Ming ordered the appointment of a chancellor. Emperor Ming entered the capital, and An Zhonghui wielded power, seeking advice from Kong Xun. The former chancellors Doulu Ge and Wei Shuo were removed. Kong Xun did not want a person from the Huai River region to be appointed as chancellor, and he strongly recommended Cui Xie. However, Ren Yuan argued against this, saying that Cui Xie, though somewhat literate, was often called the "wordless stele" by contemporaries. There was also Li Qi, who was highly talented and could rival a hundred of his contemporaries in learning and artistry. Adversaries skillfully obstructed and envied his abilities, and it was decided to appoint Cui Xie instead of Li Qi, as if discarding a precious perfume and choosing a dung beetle's dung. An Zhonghui laughed and stopped, but because of Kong Xun, the appointment was finalized. The emperor said, "Feng Ji, the former judge of the late emperor, is impartial and can be made chancellor." Thus, Dao and Cui Xie were appointed together, while Li Qi was passed over, and those who understood the situation lamented this. Emperor Ming's wise chancellor. Emperor Ming sent his son Cui Rong to govern Yedu. One day, the emperor told An Zhonghui, "There are false orders circulating around Cui Rong, claiming to be from me. He does not receive the advice of Confucian scholars, who are generally timid and might dull his resolve. I only recently learned of this and am greatly alarmed. Now that this young prince is governing a major province, I have selected learned and refined individuals to assist him. How could I have expected such treachery? I will punish those who spread these false orders. An Zhonghui said, "If we execute them immediately, we may also alarm the guests and followers, making it difficult to maintain stability. Instead, let us strictly warn them." The emperor agreed.

Emperor Mingzong particularly detested corrupt officials. During his reign, Tao Qi, the acting governor of Dengzhou, was accused by Cheng Guiren, the magistrate of Neixiang County, for imposing excessive taxes and levies. As a result, Tao Qi was demoted to the position of司马in Lanzhou. Wang Weiji, the chief secretary, was stripped of his previous titles and appointed to a long-term exile in Suizhou. Li Ye, the governor of Bozhou, was granted the right to commit suicide due to corruption. Chang Yan, the governor of Ruzhou, was directly warned by the emperor for his greed and brutality. In Bianzhou, a corrupt official was reported by Wang Jianli, a relative of Princess Shi Yinxun (who was also a former general and the father-in-law of Prince Shi Jingtao), hoping for a lenient punishment. The emperor said, "The law is impartial; how can it be bent for personal favor? Therefore, they were all executed." The emperor punished those guilty of filial impiety. Pei Yanwen, the magistrate of Goushi County, was executed for failing to care for his mother properly. Zhou Wei, a person from Xiangyi, was executed for not seeking justice for his father's murder, despite having evidence and a peaceful resolution. An Zhonghui unjustly killed Ren Yuan. Ren Yuan had five brothers: Yuan, Yuan, Tu, Hui, and Yong. They were all distinguished in appearance and personality. Ren Yuan was handsome, eloquent, and skilled in strategic planning. After pacifying Shu, he was appointed to govern Qiannan but did not take up the post. In the early Tiancheng era, he entered the imperial court and began to select and promote talented officials, cutting off the paths of favoritism. He was deeply concerned about the state as if it were his own family, driven by a strong sense of duty and ambition. However, An Zhonghui envied him and often kept him at his private residence. An Zhonghui had a beautiful courtesan who sang well, and he could not obtain her favor. This led to growing resentment between them. Soon after, Ren Yuan was dismissed from his post as head of the Three Departments and appointed as Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince, retiring to Cizhou. When Zhu Shouyin rebelled, Ren Yuan was immediately sent to deal with the situation. On the day he received his orders, he remained calm and composed, causing great sorrow among the people. In the Qingtai era, he was posthumously awarded the title of Right Chancellor.

**Trivial Talk of the North Dream, Volume Eighteen** Imperially Approved Four Treasuries **Trivial Talk of the North Dream, Volume Nineteen** Tang Dynasty, compiled by Sun Guangxian **The Jade Ruler** The retired Grand Tutor Zhao Guangfeng served in the Tang and Later Liang dynasties and passed away in Tiancheng. Renowned for his literary talent, moral integrity, and distinguished demeanor, he was known as the "Jade Ruler." He held high-ranking positions in the central government, served in the Hanlin Academy, and was the Imperial Censor. During the Later Liang period, he was a co-participant in state affairs, having twice served in the highest government offices and retired four times to private life. Adhering to the tenets of Confucian ethics, he was honest even in private, earning the respect of officials and scholars. **Zhou Yuanbao** Zhou Yuanbao, a native of Yan, was a young monk whose master had the ability to discern people's character. He spent ten years in close association with the master, enduring hardships without hesitation, and eventually transmitted the master's secret teachings. Returning to his hometown, he resumed secular life. Lu Cheng became a Taoist priest and, along with three like-minded individuals, sought out Zhou Yuanbao. Later, Zhou Yuanbao told someone, "The two gentlemen will bloom next year and both will be dead. Only that Taoist priest will be very esteemed in the future." The following year, the two men indeed saw their decline, while Lu Cheng rose to prominence. Later, Zhou Yuanbao returned to Jin Yang. Zhang Chengye still held him in high regard, and his predictions were often accurate. Zhang Chengye made Emperor Mingzong change clothes and stand among the lower-ranking officers, saying to others, "This is not right." Zhou Yuanbao pointed to Emperor Mingzong in the last seat and said, "His bone structure is unusual; this is the Grand Tutor of the Inner Court." Someone asked about his future, and he only said he would be a military governor in the end.

During the reign of Emperor Zong, the Queen Consort of the Western Xia (宗夏皇后) was performing the rituals of a newly married woman (方事巾栉) and occasionally offended (有时忤防), leading to severe punishment (大犯槚楚). Zhou Yuanbao (元豹) once said that this person would give birth to a noble son (此人有藩侯夫人之相当生贵子), and his words came true (其言果验). All predictions about good or bad omens (凡言吉凶) were accurate (莫不神中), but many of these events are not recorded (事多不载). After Emperor Ming (明宗) ascended the throne from his position as a military governor (自镇帅入纂), he told his attendants (谓侍臣曰) that Zhou Yuanbao had once predicted his affairs (周元豹昔曽言朕事), and if there were any signs (诸有征), he could be summoned from Beijing to the capital (可诏北京津置赴阙). Zhao Feng (赵凤) said (曰) that Zhou Yuanbao was particularly skilled in predicting the affairs of Xu (许之事元豹所长), but if he were summoned to the imperial court (若诏至辇下), people might ask about good or bad omens (即争问吉凶), which could be seen as bewitching or deceiving (恐近于妖惑). Therefore, the emperor ordered him to be granted gold and silk (乃令就赐金帛) and appointed him as an official (官至光禄卿), and he died at the age of eighty (年八十而终). It is also said that he once predicted auspicious signs for the founding emperor of Shu (蜀髙祖) and his successor (嗣主), even predicting the rise of high-ranking officials (云龙将相), and all his predictions were accurate (其言无不符验), indeed they were extraordinary (果异乎哉). --- ### **Growing Greed** The Minister of Rites, Cui Yisun (礼部尚书崔贻孙), was over eighty years old and constantly sought promotion (年过八十求进不休). He had always accumulated wealth (囊槖之资素有贮积) and had a nature of meddling in others' affairs, liking to receive small favors (性好干人喜得小惠). After being demoted (左降之后), his two sons fought over his property (二子争财), and none of them provided support for his medical care (甘防医药咸不供侍). He wrote a letter to his son saying (书责其子曰): "I lived under a wise ruler (生有明君真宰), and I will be judged in the afterlife (死有天曹地府). Do not act like a decrepit old man (无为老朽岂放尔邪)." This became a source of laughter among the literati (为缙绅之笑端). --- ### **Zhou Yuanbao Presents a Poem** Zhou Yuanbao, a Taoist priest (道士解元龟), was originally a lower-ranking military officer under a military commander in the western part of Shu (本西蜀节将下军校). After Emperor Ming ascended the throne, he said he came from the west (明宗入纂言自西来) and spoke in the private palace (对于便殿), praising the emperor's virtues (诗歌圣德) and calling himself a Taoist priest of Mount Taibai (自称太白山正一道士). He submitted a memorial requesting the position of governor of the western capital (上表乞西都留守) and also serving as the governor of three states (兼三州制置使), with the aim of renovating the palaces of the western capital (要脩西京宫阙). The emperor said to his attendants (上谓侍臣曰): "This old man came from afar to court (此老耄自逺来朝), and at this time he had different views (此期别有异见). He was very concerned about his reputation (乃为身名甚切), which was laughable (堪笑也)." At the time, he was called Mr. Zhi Bai (时号知白先生) and was granted a purple robe (赐紫), but he was a reckless and arrogant person (斯乃狂妄人也).

### Final Translation **Crushing the Relics** In the Tiancheng era, a monk returned from the Western Regions with a Buddha's tooth relic and ten particles of relics, which he presented to the emperor through the proper channels. The tooth was as large as a fist and was cracked and worn. Zhao Feng spoke to the prime ministers, saying, "I have heard that Buddha's teeth are indestructible even when hammered. Please try it." Under the axe, it broke. At the time, the palace had already destroyed several thousand relics upon hearing of the destruction, and they were discarded, as they said. This monk was known as Zhi Ming, from Youzhou, and I once knew him. **Cui Xie's Promotion** Emperor Mingzong asked the chancellor Feng Dao and Lu Zhi whether they had been drinking recently. Lu Zhi replied, "I once came to your residence and drank several cups. I advised him not to drink excessively." Just like drinking too much leads to problems, Cui Xie forced himself to speak at the meeting, saying, "I have heard that the *Shiyi Xinjing* (Book of Dietetics and Medicine) says that wine is extremely good and sufficient to calm the mind and spirit without the need for medicine." Those around him found his remarks shallow and could not help but smile. **Master of Subduing Dragons** A monk named Cheng Hui, who was known as the Master of Subduing Dragons, was from Mount Wutai. When a flood in Zhending destroyed the southern wall of the city, Cheng Hui said, "Since they lack faith, I will send a small dragon to warn them." He claimed to be able to control venomous dragons. At the beginning of the Tongguang era, he arrived at the imperial court, and the powerful and influential all bowed to him. Only Guo Chongtao knew his character and did not bow. When there was a drought in the capital, they invited him to Luoyang to pray for rain, but after several weeks, there was no response. Some people claimed to have heard the sound of fire and fled secretly. He returned to the temple, feeling ashamed and angry, and eventually died. A pagoda was built to honor him as the Master of the Dharma Rain. How mistaken it was.

Fish Eyes as Relics A monk from Zezhou named Hong Mi requested a stupa for relics. Hong Mi used Zen riddles to deceive the ignorant masses, claiming that he had produced relics from his body and had once visited Taiyuan, where wealthy civilians welcomed him and women knelt in worship. After Hong Mi left, the women picked up a hundred grains at the place where he had sat, which were later verified by reputable individuals to be dried fish eyes. Hong Mi claimed that he needed a thousand grains, and within half a day, he collected five hundred grains, thus deceiving people in this manner. Gracious Loyalty of Yao Hong [with Xia Luqi] Yao Hong, a commander of defense in Langzhou, served under Dong Zhang during the Later Liang period. When Dong Zhang planned to rebel, he frequently tried to persuade Yao Hong with appeals to righteousness, but Yao Hong refused. When the city fell and Yao Hong was captured, Dong Zhang scolded him, and Yao Hong shouted loudly. Dong Zhang said, "Old traitor, you have betrayed your benefactor. What gratitude do I owe you? I was a slave to Li Qilang, sweeping horse dung and receiving a small piece of roasted meat, which I was grateful for. Now the emperor has given me territory, and you form a clique and turn against me. You are a slave and have no shame. I, a loyal and valiant man, cannot do such a thing." Enraged, Dong Zhang ordered ten men to cut Yao Hong's skin and boil a cauldron in front of him, then took pieces of Yao Hong's flesh to eat. Yao Hong continued to shout loudly until his death. Emperor Mingzong heard of this and wept, placing Yao Hong's two sons in the imperial guard and granting them favors and rewards better than usual. Xia Luqi, who defended Suizhou, committed suicide by cutting his throat when the city fell, and both were hailed as loyal and valiant.

### Final Translation **The Chief Examiner and His Disciple Both Enter the Hanlin Academy** After Feng Shunqing served as the chief examiner during the Liang dynasty, his disciple Zheng Zhiyong was also appointed to the Hanlin Academy as a scholar. Zheng Zhiyong possessed remarkable talent, while Feng Shunqing's thoughts were slow and clumsy. When he was tasked with composing five essays, he found himself overwhelmed by the difficulty and, unable to cope, entrusted Zheng Zhiyong to write them on his behalf. At the time, critics believed that the chief examiner had disgraced himself by relying on his disciple. In the early years of the Tongguang era, Feng Shunqing retired from office. **Playful Incident Involving Xiao Xifu** Xiao Xifu, after passing the imperial examinations and becoming a Jinshi, was known for his literary talent and eloquence. He was also cunning and resourceful. During the Liang dynasty, when he was not favored, he abandoned his mother and wife, crossed the river, changed his name to Huangfu, and became a teacher. When Emperor Zhuangzong established his rule in Wei Prefecture, he summoned Xiao Xifu to serve as the official responsible for drafting imperial edicts. After Zhuangzong conquered Bian and Luo, Xiao Xifu was sent to comfort the regions of Qing and Qi. It was then that he learned his mother had died and his wife had remarried. He observed mourning in Wei Prefecture. At the time, critics playfully quoted a line from Li Ling's letter: "My mother has passed away, and my wife has left the house." Later, Xiao Xifu became a Censorial Adviser, known for his narrow-mindedness, impatience, and eagerness for advancement. He criticized the chancellors Doulu Ge and Wei Shuo, leading to their demotion and eventual death. He was also reprimanded for criticizing the prime minister and was demoted to serve as the magistrate of Lanzhou. **Emperor Mingzong Commends Feng Dao** Emperor Mingzong told his court officials, "Feng Dao is simple and thrifty. During his time at the De Sheng Camp, he lived in a thatched hut and ate from the same utensils as his subordinates. When he slept, he lay on a bundle of straw, and his heart remained calm. When he retired to his hometown due to his father's death, he cultivated the land and gathered firewood, mingling with farmers without regard for his former status. He is truly a genuine scholar-official."

Emperor Mingzhong Admonishes Prince Qin Emperor Mingzhong admonished Prince Qin Zhongrong, saying: "When I was young, I was in the midst of chaos and achieved fame on horseback, leaving no time to focus on classical texts. When I was a prince, I saw the judge discuss the meaning of the classics. Although I did not deeply understand it, it generally enlightened me. Now, there are upright and virtuous people in the court who can be befriended; perhaps it will be beneficial. I saw the late emperor, when he was a prince, love to compose songs and poems. A general's son's writing, not cultivated from childhood, cannot be fully refined. If recited by others, it might be secretly laughed at by scholars. I am old and cannot force myself to do it, but I still want to hear the meaning of books frequently. At that time, Prince Zhongrong was gathering various scholars and shallow youths who made poetry and recitation their business. The Emperor spoke these words to admonish him. One day, Prince Qin presented a poem, and the Emperor praised it to the clown Jing Xinmo. Jing Xinmo praised and said, 'Do not be surprised that Prince Qin's poem is good; his grandfather loved to compose poems all his life.' The Emperor laughed greatly." "Troubled by humor," the Emperor said. Chancellor Feng Dao had an ordinary appearance and spirit. Once he became chancellor, many scholars secretly laughed at him. Liu Yue and Ren Zan happened to be talking when they saw Feng Dao passing by and Ren Zan said, "Why did the new chancellor look back?" Liu Yue replied, "He must have forgotten to bring his *Ruyuan* book." Feng Dao's fellow townspeople in court heard about it and told him. Feng Dao then appointed Liu Yue as Secretary of the Secretariat and Ren Zan as Attendant of the Imperial Stables. In the northern villages, the *Ruyuan* book is often used to teach children. Thus, it was used to mock him. However, the *Ruyuan* book is in the style of Xu and Yu, not crude or vulgar. But because it is a book that many people have at home, it is often looked down upon. "Emperor Mingzhong did not enjoy riding horses," [with reference to Zhang Fangzhao].

The military governor of Jingyuan, Li Jinquan, had repeatedly served in frontier regions, where he oppressedively collected taxes and presented horses at inappropriate times. When asked about his governance, he did not focus solely on presenting horses, but he reluctantly accepted it, which displeased the emperor.
